topics.title: Signals and Signal Processing: Characterization and Classification of Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signals and Signal Processing: Characterization and Classification of Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Signal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition (Signals and Systems):**
> A **signal** is any physical quantity that varies with one or more independent variables (most commonly time) and contains information about the behavior, state, or characteristics of a physical phenomenon. Mathematically, it is represented as a function $x(t)$ for continuous-time signals or $x[n]$ for discrete-time signals.

A signal is therefore a carrier of information. In the context of **biomedical signal processing**, signals originate from biological sources (heart, brain, muscles) and are captured via electrodes or transducers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Independent Variables Used in KTU Syllabus:**
> - **Time ($t$):** The most common independent variable, measured in **seconds (s)**.
> - **Spatial coordinate ($x, y, z$):** Used in imaging signals (e.g., MRI, CT scans).
> - **Frequency ($f$ or $\omega$):** Used in transformed-domain representations.
> - **Sample index ($n$):** Used in discrete-time signals, dimensionless.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Signal Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Definition (Signal Processing):**
> **Signal Processing** is the systematic analysis, manipulation, transformation, and extraction of information from signals. It involves operations such as filtering, amplification, sampling, quantization, compression, and feature extraction to enhance the signal or derive meaningful diagnostic information.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (For First-Time Learners)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "News Channel" Model:**
> Imagine a TV news channel. The **signal** is the electromagnetic wave (or digital data stream) carrying the news content. The **independent variable** (time) is the broadcast schedule. The **signal processing** is what happens inside your TV — it decodes the signal, filters out noise (interference), amplifies weak parts, and displays a clean picture.
>
> In the same way, a raw ECG signal from your body is "noisy" and weak. The signal processor in the ECG machine filters out 50 Hz power-line interference, amplifies the small millivolt-level heart signals, detects R-peaks, and finally displays a clean heartbeat trace for the doctor.

## 1.4 Why Characterization & Classification Matter

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Board Insight:**
> In KTU university examinations, you will frequently be asked to "characterize" a given signal (e.g., $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$). Characterization means computing properties such as periodicity, energy, power, symmetry, and causality. **Misclassification** (e.g., calling a power signal an energy signal) is one of the **top three reasons students lose 3-4 marks** in Module 1 questions.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Standard Value / Use |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | **Hz** | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (Nyquist Criterion) |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_N$ | **Hz** | $f_N = 2 f_m$ |
| Heart Rate (Adult Resting) | $HR$ | **bpm** | $60 - 100$ bpm |
| ECG Signal Bandwidth | $BW$ | **Hz** | $0.05 - 150$ Hz |
| EEG Signal Bandwidth | $BW$ | **Hz** | $0.5 - 100$ Hz |
| EMG Signal Bandwidth | $BW$ | **Hz** | $20 - 500$ Hz |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Signals

Signals are classified along **seven independent axes** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. A given signal must be characterized along **all** axes for full marks in Part B questions.

### Axis 1: Continuity in Time
- **Continuous-Time Signal ($x(t)$):** Defined for all real values of $t \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **Discrete-Time Signal ($x[n]$):** Defined only at integer instants $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

### Axis 2: Continuity in Amplitude
- **Analog Signal:** Both time and amplitude are continuous.
- **Digital Signal:** Both time and amplitude are discrete (quantized levels).
- **Sampled Signal:** Discrete in time, continuous in amplitude.

### Axis 3: Determinism
- **Deterministic Signal:** Can be described by an exact mathematical formula (e.g., $x(t) = \sin(2\pi f t)$).
- **Random Signal:** Cannot be described exactly; characterized by statistical properties (mean, variance, PDF). Most biomedical signals contain random components.

### Axis 4: Periodicity
- **Periodic Signal:** $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ for all $t$, where $T_0$ is the fundamental period.
- **Aperiodic Signal:** No finite $T_0$ satisfies the periodicity condition.

### Axis 5: Energy vs. Power
- **Energy Signal:** Finite total energy ($0 < E < \infty$) and zero average power ($P = 0$).
- **Power Signal:** Finite average power ($0 < P < \infty$) and infinite energy ($E = \infty$).
- A non-zero signal **cannot** be both energy and power simultaneously.

### Axis 6: Symmetry
- **Even Signal:** $x(t) = x(-t)$ (symmetric about vertical axis).
- **Odd Signal:** $x(t) = -x(-t)$ (anti-symmetric about origin).
- **Neither:** Does not satisfy either condition.

### Axis 7: Causality
- **Causal Signal:** $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$.
- **Non-Causal / Anti-Causal:** $x(t) \neq 0$ for $t < 0$.

## 2.2 The "Why and How" Behind Each Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight (How to think about each axis):**
> - **Time continuity:** Ask "Is $t$ continuous or countable?" → ECG recorder output is continuous, but a digital ECG sample is discrete.
> - **Determinism:** Ask "Can I write $x(t) = ...$ as a closed-form equation?" If yes → deterministic.
> - **Periodicity:** Ask "Does the waveform repeat after some interval $T_0$?" A normal sinusoid is periodic; a single ECG heartbeat is aperiodic (though ECG as a train of beats is periodic).
> - **Energy/Power:** Compute the integral. If it converges → energy. If average power is finite → power. This is the **most-tested** axis in KTU exams.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas. They appear in 80% of KTU Module 1 questions.**

| Property | Continuous-Time $x(t)$ | Discrete-Time $x[n]$ | Remarks |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Total Energy ($E$)** | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Joules (J) |
| **Average Power ($P$)** | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Watts (W) |
| **Power of Periodic Signal** | $P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ | $P = \frac{1}{N_0} \sum_{n=0}^{N_0-1} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Single-period average |
| **Periodicity Condition** | $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ for all $t$ | $x[n] = x[n + N_0]$ for all $n$ | $T_0 > 0$, $N_0 \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ |
| **Even Component** | $x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)]$ | $x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}[x[n] + x[-n]]$ | Always even |
| **Odd Component** | $x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$ | $x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}[x[n] - x[-n]]$ | Always odd |
| **Reconstruction** | $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ | $x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$ | Universal decomposition |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU teaches this in Module 1:**
> - **Cardiac Monitoring:** The QRS complex in ECG is characterized as an **aperiodic energy signal** because each heartbeat is a transient burst.
> - **EEG Analysis:** EEG is modeled as a **random (stochastic) signal** because it cannot be expressed in a closed form.
> - **Pacemaker Design:** Engineers use the **periodicity** of ECG (average RR interval) to detect arrhythmias.
> - **Signal Compression:** Identifying **redundancy** (symmetry) helps in efficient storage of medical records.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy of a Rectangular Pulse (Canonical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** Compute the total energy of the signal $x(t) = A \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)$, defined as $A$ for $0 \leq t \leq T$ and $0$ elsewhere.

**Step 1:** Write the energy integral explicitly.

$$ E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt $$

**Step 2:** Substitute the piecewise definition. The integrand is non-zero only on $[0, T]$.

$$ E = \int_{-\infty}^{0} (0)^{2}\, dt + \int_{0}^{T} (A)^{2}\, dt + \int_{T}^{\infty} (0)^{2}\, dt $$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the non-zero integral in the middle.

$$ E = 0 + A^{2} \int_{0}^{T} dt + 0 $$

**Step 4:** Perform the integration.

$$ E = A^{2} \cdot [t]_{0}^{T} = A^{2}(T - 0) $$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$ \boxed{E = A^{2} T \quad \text{(Joules)} $$

**Step 6:** Compute average power.

$$ P = \lim_{T_{\text{obs}} \to \infty} \frac{E_{\text{obs}}}{2 T_{\text{obs}}} = \lim_{T_{\text{obs}} \to \infty} \frac{A^{2}T}{2 T_{\text{obs}}} = 0 $$

**Conclusion:** A rectangular pulse is a **finite-energy** signal with **zero average power** → it is an **Energy Signal**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Even-Odd Decomposition of a Mixed Signal

**Problem:** Decompose $x(t) = \sin(\omega_0 t) + \cos(\omega_0 t) + 1$ into its even and odd components.

**Step 1:** Recall the universal decomposition theorem.

$$ x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t) $$

**Step 2:** Apply the even-part formula.

$$ x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)] $$

**Step 3:** Compute $x(-t)$ using trigonometric identities.

$$ x(-t) = \sin(-\omega_0 t) + \cos(-\omega_0 t) + 1 = -\sin(\omega_0 t) + \cos(\omega_0 t) + 1 $$

**Step 4:** Add $x(t)$ and $x(-t)$.

$$ x(t) + x(-t) = [\sin(\omega_0 t) - \sin(\omega_0 t)] + [\cos(\omega_0 t) + \cos(\omega_0 t)] + [1 + 1] $$

$$ x(t) + x(-t) = 0 + 2\cos(\omega_0 t) + 2 = 2\cos(\omega_0 t) + 2 $$

**Step 5:** Divide by 2 to get the even part.

$$ \boxed{x_e(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t) + 1} $$

**Step 6:** Compute the odd part using $x_o(t) = x(t) - x_e(t)$.

$$ x_o(t) = [\sin(\omega_0 t) + \cos(\omega_0 t) + 1] - [\cos(\omega_0 t) + 1] $$

$$ \boxed{x_o(t) = \sin(\omega_0 t)} $$

**Verification:**
- $x_e(-t) = \cos(-\omega_0 t) + 1 = \cos(\omega_0 t) + 1 = x_e(t)$ ✓ (Even)
- $x_o(-t) = \sin(-\omega_0 t) = -\sin(\omega_0 t) = -x_o(t)$ ✓ (Odd)
- $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t) + 1 + \sin(\omega_0 t)$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy of the Causal Exponential Decay Signal

**Problem:** Determine if $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$ is an energy or power signal, where $a > 0$ and $u(t)$ is the unit step.

**Step 1:** Identify the signal as a one-sided decaying exponential. $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, so the energy integral starts at $0$.

$$ E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} (e^{-at})^{2}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2at}\, dt $$

**Step 2:** Integrate using $\int e^{-kt} dt = -\frac{1}{k} e^{-kt}$.

$$ E = \left[ -\frac{1}{2a} e^{-2at} \right]_{0}^{\infty} $$

**Step 3:** Evaluate limits. As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-2at} \to 0$ (since $a > 0$). At $t = 0$, $e^{0} = 1$.

$$ E = \left( 0 \right) - \left( -\frac{1}{2a} \cdot 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2a} $$

**Step 4:** Final energy value.

$$ \boxed{E = \frac{1}{2a} \quad (\text{finite, since } a > 0)} $$

**Step 5:** Compute average power.

$$ P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{0}^{T} e^{-2at}\, dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-2aT}}{2a} = 0 $$

**Conclusion:** Since $0 < E = \frac{1}{2a} < \infty$ and $P = 0$, the signal is an **Energy Signal**. The larger the decay constant $a$, the smaller the energy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Standard Test Signals (KTU High-Yield)

| Signal | Expression | Periodic? | Energy/Power | Symmetry | Causal? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Unit Step | $u(t)$ | No | Power | Neither | Yes |
| Unit Impulse | $\delta(t)$ | No | Energy | Even | Yes |
| Sinusoid | $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ | Yes (Period $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$) | Power ($P = 0.5$) | Odd | No |
| Cosine | $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | Yes (Period $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$) | Power ($P = 0.5$) | Even | No |
| DC Signal | $x(t) = C$ | Yes (Any $T_0$) | Power ($P = C^2$) | Even | No |
| Rectangular Pulse | $A \cdot \text{rect}(t/T)$ | No | Energy ($E = A^2T$) | Even (if centered) | No |
| Sinc | $\text{sinc}(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$ | No | Energy | Even | No |
| Exponential | $e^{-at}u(t)$ | No | Energy ($E = \frac{1}{2a}$) | Neither | Yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Classification Tree of Signals

```mermaid
graph TD
    SIG[SIGNAL X OF T] --> T1[BASED ON TIME DOMAIN]
    SIG --> T2[BASED ON AMPLITUDE]
    SIG --> T3[BASED ON DETERMINACY]
    SIG --> T4[BASED ON PERIODICITY]
    SIG --> T5[BASED ON ENERGY POWER]
    SIG --> T6[BASED ON SYMMETRY]
    SIG --> T7[BASED ON CAUSALITY]

    T1 --> T1A[CONTINUOUS TIME X OF T]
    T1 --> T1B[DISCRETE TIME X OF N]

    T2 --> T2A[ANALOG SIGNAL]
    T2 --> T2B[DIGITAL SIGNAL]
    T2 --> T2C[SAMPLED SIGNAL]

    T3 --> T3A[DETERMINISTIC]
    T3 --> T3B[RANDOM STOCHASTIC]

    T4 --> T4A[PERIODIC X T EQUALS X T PLUS T0]
    T4 --> T4B[APERIODIC]

    T5 --> T5A[ENERGY SIGNAL 0 LESS THAN E LESS THAN INFINITY]
    T5 --> T5B[POWER SIGNAL 0 LESS THAN P LESS THAN INFINITY]

    T6 --> T6A[EVEN X T EQUALS X MINUS T]
    T6 --> T6B[ODD X T EQUALS MINUS X MINUS T]
    T6 --> T6C[NEITHER EVEN NOR ODD]

    T7 --> T7A[CAUSAL X T EQUALS ZERO FOR T LESS THAN ZERO]
    T7 --> T7B[NON CAUSAL]
```

## 4.2 Biomedical Signal Processing Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    BIO[BIOLOGICAL SOURCE: HEART, BRAIN, MUSCLE] --> TR[TRANSDUCER: ELECTRODE, MICROPHONE]
    TR --> AM[AMPLIFIER: GAIN 1000X]
    AM --> FI[ANALOG FILTER: 0.05 TO 150 HZ]
    FI --> AD[ADC: SAMPLING AT 360 HZ]
    AD --> DP[DIGITAL PROCESSOR: FILTERING, FEATURE EXTRACTION]
    DP --> DSP[DISPLAY: ECG MONITOR, COMPUTER SCREEN]
    DP --> ST[STORAGE: DATABASE, EHR SYSTEM]
    DP --> AL[ALARM SYSTEM: ARRHYTHMIA DETECTION]
```

## 4.3 Even-Odd Decomposition Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[GIVEN SIGNAL X OF T] --> B{COMPUTE X OF MINUS T}
    B --> C[APPLY EVEN FORMULA: XE EQUALS X OF T PLUS X OF MINUS T DIVIDED BY 2]
    B --> D[APPLY ODD FORMULA: XO EQUALS X OF T MINUS X OF MINUS T DIVIDED BY 2]
    C --> E[VERIFY: XE OF MINUS T EQUALS XE OF T]
    D --> F[VERIFY: XO OF MINUS T EQUALS MINUS XO OF T]
    E --> G[RECONSTRUCT: X EQUALS XE PLUS XO]
    F --> G
```

## 4.4 Energy-Power Decision Tree (Most-Tested Concept in KTU)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[START: GIVEN SIGNAL X OF T] --> Q1{IS INTEGRAL OF X SQUARED DT FINITE?}
    Q1 -->|YES, 0 LESS THAN E LESS THAN INFINITY| E1[ENERGY SIGNAL]
    Q1 -->|NO, E EQUALS INFINITY| Q2{IS AVERAGE POWER P FINITE AND NON ZERO?}
    Q2 -->|YES, 0 LESS THAN P LESS THAN INFINITY| P1[POWER SIGNAL]
    Q2 -->|NO, P EQUALS INFINITY| NP[NEITHER ENERGY NOR POWER SIGNAL]
    E1 --> R[REMEMBER: P EQUALS ZERO FOR ENERGY SIGNAL]
    P1 --> R2[REMEMBER: E EQUALS INFINITY FOR POWER SIGNAL]
```

## 4.5 Schematic of Standard Test Signals (Waveform Concept)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[UNIT IMPULSE DELTA T: ENERGY EQUALS 1] --> A2[UNIT STEP U T: POWER SIGNAL]
    A2 --> A3[RECTANGULAR PULSE: ENERGY EQUALS A SQUARED T]
    A3 --> A4[EXPONENTIAL DECAY: ENERGY EQUALS 1 DIVIDED BY 2A]
    A4 --> A5[SINUSOID: POWER EQUALS 0.5]
    A5 --> A6[PERIODIC TRAIN OF IMPULSES: POWER EQUALS 1 DIVIDED BY T]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define a signal. Classify signals based on (i) continuity in time and (ii) continuity in amplitude, with one biomedical example for each type.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A signal is a physical quantity that varies with time (or space) and carries information about a phenomenon. It is mathematically represented as $x(t)$ for continuous-time or $x[n]$ for discrete-time.

**Classification (2 marks):**
- **Based on Time:** Continuous-time signals $x(t)$ are defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (e.g., analog ECG output from electrodes). Discrete-time signals $x[n]$ are defined at integer instants only (e.g., digitally sampled ECG stored in a Holter monitor).
- **Based on Amplitude:** Analog signals have continuous amplitude values (e.g., voltage from a microphone picking up heart sounds). Digital signals have discrete amplitude levels obtained after quantization (e.g., 12-bit ADC output of ECG).

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**Differentiate between deterministic and random signals. Give one biomedical example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Deterministic Signal | Random Signal |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Definition** | Can be described by an exact mathematical formula. | Cannot be expressed in closed form; characterized by statistics. |
| **Predictability** | Future values are exactly known from the equation. | Future values are uncertain; only probabilistic properties known. |
| **Biomedical Example** | Pure sinusoidal waveform from a function generator used for calibration. | Real EEG signal from a human brain. |
| **Repetition** | Repeatable across multiple measurements. | Varies randomly across measurements. |

(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for biomedical example)

---

## PART B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a) Classify signals in detail along all major axes with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Signals are classified along the following seven independent axes:

**1. Continuous-Time vs. Discrete-Time (1 mark):**
- Continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined for every instant of time. Example: the analog voltage output of an ECG electrode.
- Discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined only at discrete integer instants. Example: the sampled version of the same ECG stored in a digital patient monitor.

**2. Analog vs. Digital (1 mark):**
- An analog signal is continuous in both time and amplitude. Example: audio output of a stethoscope microphone.
- A digital signal is discrete in both time and amplitude (quantized). Example: the output of a 12-bit ADC in a digital ECG machine.

**3. Deterministic vs. Random (1 mark):**
- A deterministic signal is fully described by a known mathematical expression. Example: $x(t) = 5\sin(100\pi t)$.
- A random signal cannot be described exactly; it is characterized by statistical parameters (mean, variance, PDF). Example: real-world EEG, EMG signals.

**4. Periodic vs. Aperiodic (1 mark):**
- A periodic signal satisfies $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ for all $t$, with fundamental period $T_0$. Example: a healthy ECG train (with constant RR interval).
- An aperiodic signal does not repeat. Example: a single QRS complex in isolation.

**5. Energy vs. Power Signals (1 mark):**
- An energy signal has finite total energy ($0 < E < \infty$) and zero average power. Example: a single rectangular pulse.
- A power signal has finite average power ($0 < P < \infty$) and infinite energy. Example: a continuous sinusoid or a DC offset.

**6. Even vs. Odd Symmetry (1 mark):**
- An even signal satisfies $x(t) = x(-t)$. Example: $\cos(\omega_0 t)$.
- An odd signal satisfies $x(t) = -x(-t)$. Example: $\sin(\omega_0 t)$.

**7. Causal vs. Non-Causal (1 mark):**
- A causal signal is zero for $t < 0$. Example: $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$.
- A non-causal signal has non-zero values for $t < 0$. Example: $x(t) = e^{-|t|}$.

---

**Q. (b) Determine whether the signal $x(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$ is an energy signal or a power signal. Compute its total energy. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the nature of the signal. Since $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the signal is a one-sided exponential decay, defined only for $t \geq 0$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the energy integral.

$$ E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert e^{-3t} u(t) \vert^{2}\, dt $$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the unit step constraint. The integrand is non-zero only for $t \geq 0$.

$$ E = \int_{0}^{\infty} (e^{-3t})^{2}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t}\, dt $$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Integrate.

$$ E = \left[ -\frac{1}{6} e^{-6t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} $$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Evaluate limits.

$$ E = \left( \lim_{t \to \infty} -\frac{1}{6} e^{-6t} \right) - \left( -\frac{1}{6} e^{0} \right) = 0 + \frac{1}{6} $$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final energy value and classification.

$$ \boxed{E = \frac{1}{6} \text{ Joules (finite)}} $$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Average power and conclusion.

$$ P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{0}^{T} e^{-6t}\, dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-6T}}{6} = 0 $$

Since $0 < E = \frac{1}{6} < \infty$ and $P = 0$, the signal is an **Energy Signal**.

---

### **QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**Q. (a) Explain the major characteristics of biomedical signals. List the typical bandwidth and amplitude range of ECG, EEG, and EMG signals. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Characteristics of Biomedical Signals (4 marks):**

Biomedical signals exhibit the following properties:
1. **Low amplitude:** Most bioelectric signals are in the microvolt-to-millivolt range (e.g., ECG $\approx 1$ mV), requiring high-gain amplification.
2. **Low frequency:** Dominant frequency content lies in $0.05$ Hz to a few kHz.
3. **Non-stationarity:** Statistical properties (mean, variance) change with time (e.g., ECG morphology changes with exercise).
4. **Susceptibility to noise:** Strong interference from 50/60 Hz power lines, motion artifacts, baseline wander, and electrode-skin contact noise.
5. **Random/stochastic nature:** They cannot be expressed by a closed-form equation; statistical analysis is required.
6. **Complex morphology:** Contain multiple sub-components (P, QRS, T waves in ECG).

**Bandwidth and Amplitude Table (3 marks):**

| Signal | Origin | Amplitude Range | Frequency Bandwidth |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **ECG** | Heart (electrical) | $0.5 - 5$ mV | $0.05 - 150$ Hz |
| **EEG** | Brain (cortical) | $5 - 300 \ \mu$V | $0.5 - 100$ Hz |
| **EMG** | Muscles | $50 \ \mu$V $-$ 5$ mV | $20 - 500$ Hz |
| **PCG** (Heart sound) | Heart (mechanical) | — | $20 - 2000$ Hz |
| **PPG** | Blood volume pulse | — | $0.5 - 10$ Hz |

---

**Q. (b) Find the even and odd components of the signal $x(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t}$. Verify that $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Expand the complex exponential using Euler's formula.

$$ e^{j\omega_0 t} = \cos(\omega_0 t) + j\sin(\omega_0 t) $$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $x(-t)$.

$$ x(-t) = e^{-j\omega_0 t} = \cos(\omega_0 t) - j\sin(\omega_0 t) $$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the even part.

$$ x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)] = \frac{1}{2}[(\cos(\omega_0 t) + j\sin(\omega_0 t)) + (\cos(\omega_0 t) - j\sin(\omega_0 t))] $$

$$ \boxed{x_e(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)} $$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the odd part.

$$ x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)] = \frac{1}{2}[(\cos(\omega_0 t) + j\sin(\omega_0 t)) - (\cos(\omega_0 t) - j\sin(\omega_0 t))] $$

$$ \boxed{x_o(t) = j\sin(\omega_0 t)} $$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Verification by reconstruction.

$$ x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t) + j\sin(\omega_0 t) = e^{j\omega_0 t} = x(t) \quad \checkmark $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit step** when computing energy of $e^{-at}u(t)$: many students integrate from $-\infty$ instead of $0$, getting $E = \frac{1}{2a}$ only by *accident* (the negative half gives zero). Examiners check this. **Always state the limits explicitly.**
> 2. **Energy-Power confusion:** A signal cannot be both energy and power (except the zero signal). Do not write "it is both" — examiners will cut 1 mark.
> 3. **Periodic vs. Aperiodic ECG trap:** A single QRS complex is *aperiodic*, but a *healthy ECG train* (QRS-QRS-QRS) **is** periodic with period = RR interval. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **In even-odd decomposition**, always verify $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$ and $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$ at the end. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Signals and Signal Processing**

- [x] A **signal** is a function conveying information; **signal processing** is its analysis, transformation, and manipulation.
- [x] Signals are classified along **7 axes**: time continuity, amplitude continuity, determinism, periodicity, energy/power, symmetry, and causality.
- [x] **Energy signal:** $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$. **Power signal:** $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$.
- [x] **Even signal:** $x(t) = x(-t)$. **Odd signal:** $x(t) = -x(-t)$.
- [x] **Universal decomposition:** $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$, where $x_e = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)]$ and $x_o = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$.
- [x] **Rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and width $T$:** Energy $E = A^2T$.
- [x] **Exponential decay $e^{-at}u(t)$:** Energy $E = \frac{1}{2a}$ (finite, hence energy signal).
- [x] **Sinusoid $\sin(\omega_0 t)$:** Periodic with period $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$, power $P = 0.5$, odd symmetry.
- [x] **DC signal $x(t) = C$:** Periodic with any $T_0$, power $P = C^2$, even symmetry.
- [x] **Biomedical signal characteristics:** low amplitude (µV–mV), low bandwidth (0.05 Hz–few kHz), non-stationary, noise-prone, stochastic.
- [x] **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ to avoid aliasing. ECG sampled at 360–500 Hz; EEG at 256–512 Hz; EMG at 1–2 kHz.
- [x] **A non-zero signal can never be both an energy and a power signal** — this is a KTU board favorite for 1-mark conceptual questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Study Tip:** Before the KTU exam, write down the **classification table** (7 axes × 2 categories) from memory. If you can reproduce the even-odd decomposition formula and the energy/power integrals without looking, you are fully prepared for Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Signal Processing Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Signal Processing Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Signal Processing Operations** refer to the standardized, mathematically-defined procedures applied to raw biomedical signals (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, PPG, and PCG) in order to extract diagnostically meaningful information. These operations are categorized into **time-domain operations**, **frequency-domain transformations**, **filtering operations**, and **statistical operations**, forming the foundational pipeline of any Biomedical Signal Processing system.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these operations are classified under the broader umbrella of **linear and non-linear transformations** that map an input signal $x(t)$ to an output signal $y(t)$ through a defined functional relationship $y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$, where $\mathcal{T}$ is the operator.

### The Four Pillars of Biomedical Signal Operations:

| Pillar | Operation Type | Example in Biomedical Context |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Signal Acquisition Operations** | Sampling, Quantization, Encoding |
| 2 | **Transform Domain Operations** | Fourier, Laplace, Z, Wavelet Transforms |
| 3 | **Filtering Operations** | LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF, Adaptive Filtering |
| 4 | **Statistical Operations** | Averaging, Correlation, Power Spectral Density |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Stethoscope & The Doctor's Brain**
> Imagine a doctor listening to a patient's heart through a stethoscope in a noisy hospital ward. The raw sound reaching the doctor's ears is a chaotic mixture of heart sounds, lung sounds, ambient room noise, and electrical hum. Before the doctor's brain can interpret this sound as "lub-dub, lub-dub", it unconsciously performs a series of operations:
> 1. **Volume Adjustment (Amplification/Scaling)** — turning the stethoscope to hear faint murmurs.
> 2. **Noise Rejection (Filtering)** — mentally ignoring the air-conditioner hum.
> 3. **Pattern Recognition (Correlation/Template Matching)** — comparing the heard sound with thousands of stored heart-sound templates.
> 4. **Feature Extraction (Frequency Analysis)** — focusing on the S1 and S2 peaks (frequency content).
>
> **Biomedical Signal Processing replicates this exact workflow mathematically**, using processors and algorithms instead of the human brain.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Quantities

The following constants and metrics are fundamental to biomedical signal processing:

- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)**: Typically $\geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion). Standard values: **360 Hz** for ECG, **500 Hz** for EMG, **250 Hz** for EEG.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**: Measured in **decibels (dB)**, defined as $10 \log_{10}(P_{signal}/P_{noise})$.
- **Resolution**: **bits per sample** (e.g., 12-bit ADC gives 4096 discrete levels).
- **Power Line Interference Frequency**: **50 Hz** (India/Europe) or **60 Hz** (USA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus treats signal operations as the *toolkit* upon which all subsequent modules (filter design, feature extraction, classification) are built. Mastering this topic is the prerequisite for Modules 2, 3, and 4.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Operations by Linearity

> [!NOTE]
> **Linearity Test:** A system/operation $\mathcal{T}$ is **linear** if and only if it satisfies the **Superposition Principle**:
> $$\mathcal{T}\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a\, \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + b\, \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$$
> where $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary scalars. Biomedical signals are predominantly processed under linear-operation assumptions for tractability.

| Property | Linear Operation Example | Non-Linear Operation Example |
|---|---|---|
| Superposition | Convolution: $y(t)=x(t)*h(t)$ | Squaring: $y(t)=x^2(t)$ |
| Time-Invariance | LTI Filtering | Median Filtering |
| Stability | Bounded-Input Bounded-Output | Thresholding |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline (Logic Steps)

A biomedical signal processing chain follows a strict, ordered sequence of operations. Each step has a defined "Why" (purpose) and "How" (mechanism):

### Step 1: Signal Acquisition
- **Why?** The physical analog signal (e.g., electrical potential from the heart) must be converted to a form usable by digital processors.
- **How?** Through **transduction** (electrode → amplifier) followed by **sampling** $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ and **quantization** $Q(x) = \text{round}(x / \Delta) \cdot \Delta$.

### Step 2: Pre-processing (Conditioning)
- **Why?** Remove unwanted noise and artifacts (50 Hz line noise, baseline wander, motion artifacts).
- **How?** Apply **analog filters** (anti-aliasing LPF) and **digital filters** (notch filter at 50 Hz, HPF at 0.5 Hz to remove baseline wander).

### Step 3: Transformation
- **Why?** Convert from time-domain to a domain where diagnostically relevant features become prominent.
- **How?** Apply **Fourier Transform** for stationary signals, **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** or **Wavelet Transform** for non-stationary biomedical signals.

### Step 4: Feature Extraction
- **Why?** Reduce data dimensionality and extract clinically meaningful parameters.
- **How?** Compute **statistical moments** (mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis), **morphological features** (R-peak amplitude, QRS duration), and **spectral features** (dominant frequency, band power).

### Step 5: Analysis and Classification
- **Why?** Render diagnostic decisions.
- **How?** Apply correlation, template matching, or machine learning classifiers.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** The symbol $\vert$ is used below to denote absolute value / magnitude to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Do not confuse with the logical OR operator.

| # | Operation | Mathematical Formulation | Domain | Unit / Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time Shifting** | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ | Time | Delay $t_0$ in **seconds (s)** |
| 2 | **Time Scaling** | $y(t) = x(at)$ | Time | Compression $a > 1$, Expansion $0 < a < 1$ |
| 3 | **Time Reversal** | $y(t) = x(-t)$ | Time | Folding about $t=0$ |
| 4 | **Amplitude Scaling** | $y(t) = A \cdot x(t)$ | Time | Gain $A$ (dimensionless) |
| 5 | **Convolution** | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau) \, d\tau$ | Time | $*$ denotes convolution |
| 6 | **Correlation** | $R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) y(t+\tau) \, dt$ | Time | Cross-correlation |
| 7 | **Auto-correlation** | $R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) x(t+\tau) \, dt$ | Time | At $\tau=0$, equals signal energy |
| 8 | **Continuous Fourier Transform (FT)** | $X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j 2\pi f t} \, dt$ | Frequency | $f$ in **Hz** |
| 9 | **Inverse Fourier Transform** | $x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) e^{j 2\pi f t} \, df$ | Time | Existence requires Dirichlet conditions |
| 10 | **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | Discrete Freq. | $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$ |
| 11 | **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Frequency | $f_s$ in **samples/second** |
| 12 | **Quantization Step Size** | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{B}}$ | Amplitude | $B$ in **bits** |
| 13 | **SNR (Quantization)** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02B + 1.76$ | dB | Approximate formula |
| 14 | **Ensemble Averaging** | $\bar{x}[n] = \dfrac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} x_m[n]$ | Time | Improves SNR by $\sqrt{M}$ |
| 15 | **Parseval's Theorem** | $\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int \vert X(f) \vert^2 df$ | Energy | Time-frequency energy conservation |
| 16 | **Laplace Transform** | $X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-s t} \, dt$ | s-plane | $s = \sigma + j\omega$ |
| 17 | **Z-Transform** | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ | z-plane | $z = re^{j\omega}$ |
| 18 | **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** | $S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xx}(\tau)\}$ | Frequency | **W/Hz** |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These operations are not merely academic. They power every modern medical device:
> - **ECG Machines (Philips, GE Healthcare)**: Use ensemble averaging, 50 Hz notch filtering, and QRS detection.
> - **EEG Monitors**: Use band-pass filtering (0.5–40 Hz) and STFT for seizure detection.
> - **Pulse Oximeters**: Use PPG signal with adaptive filtering to extract SpO2.
> - **Cochlear Implants**: Use wavelet-based feature extraction for speech processing.
> - **MRI Systems**: Use 2D/3D Fourier Transform (k-space reconstruction) to form images.

---

## 2.4 Properties of Convolution (Most Tested Property Set)

For two continuous signals $x(t)$ and $h(t)$, the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ satisfies:

1. **Commutative**: $x * h = h * x$
2. **Associative**: $(x * h_1) * h_2 = x * (h_1 * h_2)$
3. **Distributive**: $x * (h_1 + h_2) = x * h_1 + x * h_2$
4. **Identity Element**: $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$ (where $\delta$ is the Dirac delta)
5. **Differentiation**: $\dfrac{d}{dt}[x(t) * h(t)] = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} * h(t) = x(t) * \dfrac{dh(t)}{dt}$
6. **Width Property**: If $x$ has duration $T_1$ and $h$ has duration $T_2$, then $x*h$ has duration $T_1 + T_2$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Convolution of Two Rectangular Pulses (A KTU-Favorite Problem)

> This derivation is exhaustively detailed. Every step is explicit with the governing logic.

### Problem Statement:
Let $x(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)$ (a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ and unit amplitude) and $h(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)$ (an identical rectangular pulse). Derive the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$.

### Step 1: Recall the Definition of Convolution

The continuous-time convolution is mathematically defined as:

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

> **Logic:** The variable $t$ represents the output time, while $\tau$ is a dummy integration variable swept across the entire real line. The operation is a "flip-shift-multiply-integrate" procedure.

### Step 2: Substitute the Rectangular Pulse Definitions

A rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ and unit amplitude is defined as:

$$
\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \vert t \vert \leq \frac{\tau}{2} \\
0, & \vert t \vert > \frac{\tau}{2}
\end{cases}
$$

Substituting into the convolution integral:

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{\tau}{\tau_0}\right) \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - \tau}{\tau_0}\right) d\tau
$$

> **Logic:** Here we use width $\tau_0$ to distinguish the pulse width from the dummy variable. The first term $x(\tau)$ is non-zero when $\vert \tau \vert \leq \tau_0/2$. The second term $h(t-\tau)$ is non-zero when $\vert t-\tau \vert \leq \tau_0/2$.

### Step 3: Identify the Integration Limits

For a given $t$, $h(t-\tau)$ is non-zero when:

$$
-\frac{\tau_0}{2} \leq t - \tau \leq \frac{\tau_0}{2}
$$

Rearranging both inequalities:

$$
t - \frac{\tau_0}{2} \leq \tau \leq t + \frac{\tau_0}{2}
$$

Combined with the non-zero condition on $x(\tau)$ (i.e., $-\tau_0/2 \leq \tau \leq \tau_0/2$), the effective integration limits are:

$$
\tau \in \left[ \max\!\left(-\frac{\tau_0}{2},\, t - \frac{\tau_0}{2}\right), \; \min\!\left(\frac{\tau_0}{2},\, t + \frac{\tau_0}{2}\right) \right]
$$

> **Logic:** The integrand is the product of two step functions. The product is non-zero only in the **intersection** of the two non-zero intervals, which is why we take the maximum of the lower bounds and the minimum of the upper bounds.

### Step 4: Evaluate the Integral for Each Region of $t$

**Case A: $t \leq -\tau_0$** (No overlap)
- Lower bound: $t - \tau_0/2 \leq -\tau_0/2$
- Upper bound: $t + \tau_0/2 \leq -\tau_0/2$
- The two non-zero regions do not intersect. Therefore:

$$
y(t) = 0, \quad \text{for } t \leq -\tau_0
$$

**Case B: $-\tau_0 \leq t \leq 0$** (Partial overlap, rising edge)
- Lower bound: $t - \tau_0/2$ (this is the binding constraint, since it is $> -\tau_0/2$)
- Upper bound: $\tau_0/2$ (this is the binding constraint, since $t + \tau_0/2 < \tau_0/2$)
- Integration:

$$
y(t) = \int_{t - \tau_0/2}^{\tau_0/2} (1)(1) \, d\tau = \left[ \tau \right]_{t - \tau_0/2}^{\tau_0/2} = \frac{\tau_0}{2} - \left(t - \frac{\tau_0}{2}\right) = \tau_0 - t
$$

$$
y(t) = \tau_0 - t, \quad \text{for } -\tau_0 \leq t \leq 0
$$

**Case C: $0 \leq t \leq \tau_0$** (Partial overlap, falling edge)
- Lower bound: $-\tau_0/2$
- Upper bound: $t + \tau_0/2$
- Integration:

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\tau_0/2}^{t + \tau_0/2} (1)(1) \, d\tau = \left[ \tau \right]_{-\tau_0/2}^{t + \tau_0/2} = t + \frac{\tau_0}{2} - \left(-\frac{\tau_0}{2}\right) = t + \tau_0
$$

$$
y(t) = t + \tau_0, \quad \text{for } 0 \leq t \leq \tau_0
$$

**Case D: $t \geq \tau_0$** (No overlap)
- The pulses have completely passed through each other.

$$
y(t) = 0, \quad \text{for } t \geq \tau_0
$$

### Step 5: Consolidate the Result

The convolution of two identical rectangular pulses of width $\tau_0$ is a **triangular pulse** of width $2\tau_0$ and peak amplitude $\tau_0$:

$$
\boxed{
y(t) = 
\begin{cases}
0, & t < -\tau_0 \\
\tau_0 + t, & -\tau_0 \leq t \leq 0 \\
\tau_0 - t, & 0 \leq t \leq \tau_0 \\
0, & t > \tau_0
\end{cases}
}
$$

> **Logic:** This is a foundational result: *the convolution of two rectangles is a triangle*. This is the time-domain counterpart of the fact that $\text{sinc}^2(f)$ in the frequency domain corresponds to a triangle in time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem Condition

> This is the cornerstone of digital biomedical signal processing.

### Step 1: Represent a Bandlimited Continuous Signal

Consider a strictly bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$:

$$
X(f) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert > f_{max}
$$

### Step 2: Express the Impulse-Train Sampling

Sampling the signal at intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$ is equivalent to multiplying $x(t)$ by an impulse train:

$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n T_s) \, \delta(t - n T_s)
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Modulation Property of the Fourier Transform

The Fourier Transform of the impulse train is itself an impulse train in the frequency domain:

$$
\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)\right\} = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(f - \frac{k}{T_s}\right) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)
$$

Using the **multiplication-in-frequency property** $\mathcal{F}\{x(t) \cdot p(t)\} = X(f) * P(f)$:

$$
X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

> **Logic:** Sampling replicates the spectrum $X(f)$ at every integer multiple of $f_s$, scaled by $f_s$.

### Step 4: Apply the Non-Aliasing Condition

For perfect reconstruction (no aliasing), the shifted replicas of $X(f)$ must not overlap. The most stringent constraint is between the baseband ($k=0$) and the first shifted replica ($k=1$):

$$
\frac{f_s}{2} \geq f_{max}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{f_s \geq 2 f_{max}}
$$

> **Logic:** The replicas are centered at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2 f_s, \ldots$. Each has bandwidth $2 f_{max}$. They are separated by gaps of size $f_s - 2 f_{max}$. For non-negative separation, we require $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ensemble Averaging SNR Improvement

### Step 1: Define the Noisy Signal

Let each measured signal epoch $x_m[n]$ be the sum of a clean, periodic biomedical signal $s[n]$ and zero-mean uncorrelated noise $\nu_m[n]$:

$$
x_m[n] = s[n] + \nu_m[n]
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Ensemble Average

$$
\bar{x}[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} x_m[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} \big[ s[n] + \nu_m[n] \big] = s[n] + \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M} \nu_m[n]
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Output Signal Power

Since $s[n]$ is deterministic and identical in all epochs, its average power is unchanged:

$$
P_s^{out} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n} s^2[n]
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Output Noise Power

Assuming each $\nu_m[n]$ has zero mean and variance $\sigma_\nu^2$, and the noise across epochs is independent:

$$
\sigma_{\nu}^{out\,2} = E\!\left[\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M} \nu_m\right)^2\right] = \frac{1}{M^2} \sum_{m=1}^{M} \sigma_\nu^2 = \frac{\sigma_\nu^2}{M}
$$

> **Logic:** The cross-terms vanish due to statistical independence of the noise across epochs.

### Step 5: Compute the Output SNR

$$
\text{SNR}^{out} = \frac{P_s^{out}}{\sigma_{\nu}^{out\,2}} = \frac{P_s}{\sigma_\nu^2 / M} = M \cdot \text{SNR}^{in}
$$

In decibels:

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}^{out}_{dB} = \text{SNR}^{in}_{dB} + 10 \log_{10}(M)}
$$

> **Logic:** Averaging $M$ epochs improves SNR by a factor of $M$ (or $10\log_{10}M$ dB). This is why **synchronous averaging** is used in evoked-potential EEG and ECG stress test analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Typical Biomedical Signal Processing Chain

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates the canonical pipeline of operations applied to a biomedical signal, from acquisition to clinical interpretation. Each block represents a typical signal processing operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SOURCE: Patient Physiological Activity] --> B[BIO-ELECTRODE / TRANSDUCER]
    B --> C[Analog Signal: x of t]
    C --> D[Analog Pre-Amplifier]
    D --> E[Analog Anti-Aliasing LPF]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    F --> G[Digital Sample Stream: x of n]
    G --> H{Digital Pre-Processing}
    H --> I[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    H --> J[High-Pass Filter 0.5 Hz]
    H --> K[Low-Pass Filter 40 Hz]
    I --> L[Conditioned Signal]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Feature Extraction Block}
    M --> N[Time Domain Features]
    M --> O[Frequency Domain Features]
    M --> P[Statistical Features]
    N --> Q[Feature Vector]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Classification / Diagnosis Block]
    R --> S[Clinical Output: Normal or Abnormal]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style O fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Taxonomy of Typical Signal Processing Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Typical Signal Processing Operations] --> B[Time Domain]
    A --> C[Frequency Domain]
    A --> D[Statistical Domain]
    A --> E[Transform Domain]
    
    B --> B1[Amplitude Scaling]
    B --> B2[Time Shifting]
    B --> B3[Convolution]
    B --> B4[Correlation]
    B --> B5[Ensemble Averaging]
    
    C --> C1[Fourier Transform]
    C --> C2[Power Spectral Density]
    C --> C3[Filtering]
    
    D --> D1[Mean and Variance]
    D --> D2[Skewness and Kurtosis]
    D --> D3[Histogram Analysis]
    
    E --> E1[Laplace Transform]
```
