# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions require **concise, definition-focused answers** with appropriate equations. Each answer should fit in 1–2 paragraphs of about 80–120 words.

---

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define convolution. State and prove any two properties of convolution for continuous-time signals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Convolution is a mathematical operation that combines two signals $x(t)$ and $h(t)$ to produce a third signal $y(t)$, defined as:

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau = x(t) * h(t)
$$

**Property 1: Commutativity (1 mark):**

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

Substituting $\lambda = t - \tau$, so $d\lambda = -d\tau$. When $\tau \to -\infty$, $\lambda \to +\infty$, and vice versa:

$$
y(t) = \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} x(t - \lambda) \, h(\lambda) \, (-d\lambda) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\lambda) \, x(t - \lambda) \, d\lambda = h(t) * x(t)
$$

**Property 2: Identity Element (1 mark):** The Dirac delta $\delta(t)$ is the identity element of convolution:

$$
x(t) * \delta(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \delta(t - \tau) \, d\tau = x(t)
$$

This uses the sifting property of the delta function: $\int x(\tau) \delta(t-\tau) d\tau = x(t)$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**State the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem. Why is it critical in biomedical signal processing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):** *A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies:*

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

The minimum allowable sampling rate, $f_s = 2 f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist rate**, and $f_s/2$ is the **Nyquist frequency**.

**Critical Importance in Biomedical Signal Processing (1 mark):** Biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) have known frequency bands. If sampled below the Nyquist rate, **aliasing** occurs—high-frequency components fold back into the low-frequency band, corrupting diagnostic features. For example, an EMG with $f_{max} = 500$ Hz must be sampled at $f_s \geq 1000$ Hz (typically 1 kHz–2 kHz clinically) to preserve signal integrity. The theorem also dictates the design of the **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** placed before the ADC.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **internal choice** (students answer either OR option). Each part-question is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define the Fourier Transform of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ and its inverse. Derive the relationship between time-shifting in time-domain and phase-shift in frequency-domain. (7 marks)

**(b)** An ECG signal is sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz. The signal contains significant frequency content up to $f_{max} = 100$ Hz, but is contaminated by 50 Hz power-line interference. Design a digital notch filter specification. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Fourier Transform Definition (2 marks):**

$$
X(f) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi f t} \, dt
$$

$$
x(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{X(f)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) \, e^{j 2\pi f t} \, df
$$

**Derivation of Time-Shift Property (5 marks):**

Let $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ be the time-shifted version of $x(t)$. We compute its Fourier Transform:

$$
Y(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t - t_0) \, e^{-j 2\pi f t} \, dt
$$

Substitute $\tau = t - t_0$, so $t = \tau + t_0$ and $dt = d\tau$:

$$
Y(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, e^{-j 2\pi f (\tau + t_0)} \, d\tau
$$

Separate the exponential:

$$
Y(f) = e^{-j 2\pi f t_0} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, e^{-j 2\pi f \tau} \, d\tau
$$

Recognize the integral as $X(f)$:

$$
\boxed{Y(f) = e^{-j 2\pi f t_0} \cdot X(f)}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A time shift of $t_0$ in the time domain corresponds to multiplying the spectrum by a complex exponential $e^{-j 2\pi f t_0}$, which has unit magnitude and a frequency-dependent phase:

$$
\vert Y(f) \vert = \vert X(f) \vert, \quad \angle Y(f) = \angle X(f) - 2\pi f t_0
$$

> The magnitude spectrum is **unchanged**; only the **phase spectrum** is linearly shifted by $-2\pi f t_0$ radians. This is why time delays cannot be detected from magnitude alone.

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Verify Nyquist Criterion (1 mark):**

$$
f_{Nyquist} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ Hz}
$$

Since $f_{max} = 100$ Hz $< 250$ Hz, the sampling theorem is satisfied. No aliasing will occur.

**Step 2: Design Goal of the Notch Filter (2 marks):**

A **notch filter** (band-stop filter) must:
- Strongly attenuate the 50 Hz component.
- Pass all other frequencies (0.5 Hz – 100 Hz biomedical band) with minimal distortion.

The filter must satisfy:

$$
\vert H(50) \vert \leq 0.01 \quad (-40 \text{ dB attenuation at 50 Hz})
$$

$$
0.99 \leq \vert H(f) \vert \leq 1.01 \quad \text{for } f \in [0.5, 49] \cup [51, 100] \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3: Bandwidth and Quality Factor (2 marks):**

Choose notch bandwidth $\Delta f = 2$ Hz (centered at 50 Hz, so passband is 49–51 Hz).

The quality factor of the filter is:

$$
Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f} = \frac{50}{2} = 25
$$

**Step 4: Digital Frequency Mapping (1 mark):**

The digital frequency corresponding to 50 Hz is:

$$
\omega_0 = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_0}{f_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{50}{500} = \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample}
$$

**Step 5: Difference Equation Realization (1 mark):**

A second-order IIR notch filter has the transfer function:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}
$$

with $r \approx 0.95$ for narrow bandwidth. The output samples $y[n]$ are computed via:

$$
y[n] = x[n] - 2\cos(\omega_0) x[n-1] + x[n-2] + 2r\cos(\omega_0) y[n-1] - r^2 y[n-2]
$$

with $\omega_0 = \pi/5$, $r = 0.95$. This filter achieves the desired 50 Hz suppression with negligible distortion of the ECG waveform.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define auto-correlation and cross-correlation functions for continuous-time signals. Derive the relationship between correlation and convolution. (7 marks)

**(b)** A clinician records 64 epochs of a noisy ECG signal for ensemble averaging. The SNR of a single epoch is measured to be 5 dB. Compute the SNR of the ensemble-averaged signal. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Definitions (3 marks):**

The **auto-correlation** of a signal $x(t)$ is:

$$
R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, x(t + \tau) \, dt
$$

The **cross-correlation** between two signals $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ is:

$$
R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, y(t + \tau) \, dt
$$

**Derivation of the Correlation-Convolution Relationship (4 marks):**

Compare the cross-correlation definition with the convolution definition:

$$
\text{Cross-Correlation: } R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, y(t + \tau) \, dt
$$

$$
\text{Convolution: } (x * y)(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, y(\tau - t) \, dt
$$

Substitute $u = -t$ in the convolution integral:

$$
(x * y)(\tau) = \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} x(-u) \, y(\tau + u) \, (-du) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-u) \, y(\tau + u) \, du
$$

Compare with the cross-correlation:

$$
R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, y(t + \tau) \, dt
$$

These are identical if and only if $x(u) = x(-u)$, i.e., $x(t)$ is an **even function**. In general:

$$
\boxed{R_{xy}(\tau) = x(-\tau) * y(\tau)}
$$

Alternatively, this is often written as:

$$
R_{xy}(\tau) = y(\tau) * x(-\tau)
$$

> **Implication:** Correlation is convolution with one signal **time-reversed**. For an even function $x(t) = x(-t)$, correlation and convolution become identical. This is why **white noise** (which is uncorrelated) convolved with any signal remains white noise, while correlated signals exhibit structure.

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Number of epochs: $M = 64$
- Input SNR: $\text{SNR}_{in} = 5$ dB

**Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to Linear Scale (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{in}^{linear} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{in}/10} = 10^{5/10} = 10^{0.5} \approx 3.162
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Ensemble Averaging Formula (3 marks):**

Ensemble averaging improves SNR linearly with $M$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{out}^{linear} = M \cdot \text{SNR}_{in}^{linear} = 64 \times 3.162 \approx 202.4
$$

**Step 3: Convert Output SNR to dB (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{out}^{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(202.4) = 10 \times 2.306 \approx 23.06 \text{ dB}
$$

**Alternative Shortcut (Direct dB Formula):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{out}^{dB} = \text{SNR}_{in}^{dB} + 10 \log_{10}(M) = 5 + 10 \log_{10}(64) = 5 + 10 \times 1.806 = 5 + 18.06 = 23.06 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_{out} \approx 23.06 \text{ dB}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** By averaging 64 epochs, the clinician achieves an SNR improvement of **18.06 dB**, which is the difference. This is sufficient to clearly visualize small-amplitude features like the P-wave and T-wave that were previously buried in noise, aiding in arrhythmia detection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in "Typical Signal Processing Operations":**
>
> 1. **Convolution vs. Correlation Confusion (–2 marks):** Many students write $R_{xy}(\tau) = x(\tau) * y(\tau)$. The correct relationship is $R_{xy}(\tau) = x(-\tau) * y(\tau)$. The time-reversal of one argument is **mandatory**.
>
> 2. **Sampling Theorem Inequality Direction (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often incorrectly write $f_{max} \geq 2 f_s$. The correct form is $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The sampling frequency must be **at least twice** the maximum signal frequency.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Units (–1 mark):** Always state the unit (Hz, dB, W/Hz, bits) when quoting a numerical answer. "SNR = 23" loses marks; "SNR = 23.06 dB" is complete.
>
> 4. **Ensemble Averaging Linear vs. dB Confusion (–2 marks):** The factor is $M$ in linear scale, but $10 \log_{10}(M)$ in dB. Mixing these yields a wrong answer.
>
> 5. **Convolution Integration Limits (–1 to –2 marks):** When convolving two finite-duration signals, students frequently forget to evaluate the **multiple cases** of overlap. Always draw the pulses and identify the overlap region graphically.
>
> 6. **Missing the Identity Element:** When asked for properties of convolution, the **identity element ($\delta$ function)** is the most-overlooked high-value point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bulletins:**

- ✅ **Convolution:** $y(t) = \int x(\tau) h(t-\tau) d\tau$. **Rectangle $*$ Rectangle $=$ Triangle.** Width: $T_1 + T_2$. Peak amplitude: $T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot A_1 A_2$.
- ✅ **Correlation:** $R_{xy}(\tau) = \int x(t) y(t+\tau) dt$. Equals convolution with one signal **time-reversed**.
- ✅ **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist rate is $2 f_{max}$, Nyquist frequency is $f_s/2$).
- ✅ **Quantization:** $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^B$. SNR in dB $\approx 6.02B + 1.76$.
- ✅ **Ensemble Averaging:** SNR improves by $M$ (linear) or $10\log_{10}M$ (dB).
- ✅ **Fourier Transform properties:** Time-shift → linear phase; Time-scaling → inverse frequency-scaling; Differentiation → multiplication by $j 2\pi f$.
- ✅ **Parseval's Theorem:** Energy in time = energy in frequency (conservation).
- ✅ **Linear vs. Non-linear:** A system is linear iff superposition holds.
- ✅ **Time-invariant:** Output delay = delayed output. If $y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$, then $\mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$.
- ✅ **Stable system:** Bounded input must give bounded output (BIBO).
- ✅ **Causal system:** Output at $t$ depends only on input for $\tau \leq t$.
- ✅ **Typical ECG sampling rate:** 360–500 Hz. **EMG:** 1–2 kHz. **EEG:** 250–500 Hz. **PCG:** 8–10 kHz.
- ✅ **Power-line notch:** 50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (USA). Notch Q-factor typically 25–50.
- ✅ **Ensemble averaging** is **synchronous** (triggered on R-peak for ECG).
- ✅ **DFT size $N$** is typically a power of 2 (radix-2 FFT): 256, 512, 1024, 2048.
- ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter** is a **low-pass filter placed BEFORE the ADC**, not after.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes: Typical Signal Processing Operations**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Biomedical Signals and Analog-to-Digital Conversion (Block Diagram Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Examples of Biomedical Signals and Analog-to-Digital Conversion (Block Diagram Only) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Biomedical Signals — Formal Definition A **biomedical signal** is a time-varying physical quantity that carries diagnostic or physiological information about a biological system. These signals originate from living organisms and are measured using specialized transducers and electrodes to monitor, diagnose, or treat medical conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Classification Snapshot:** > Biomedical signals are broadly classified into: > - **Bioelectric signals** (generated by cellular electrical activity) — e.g., ECG, EEG, EMG > - **Biomechanical signals** (displacement, pressure, flow) — e.g., blood pressure, respiratory flow > - **Bioacoustic signals** (sound emissions) — e.g., PCG (heart sounds) > - **Biochemical signals** (chemical concentrations) — e.g., oxygen saturation SpO₂, pH ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Heart as a "Drum":** > Imagine the human body as a large orchestra. Each organ (heart, brain, muscles, eyes) plays its own "instrument" producing unique rhythms. The **heart** is like a steady drum (ECG signal, ~1–2 Hz fundamental). The **brain** hums with continuous whispers in multiple "octaves" (EEG: 0.5–100 Hz). The **muscles** crackle like static when they flex (EMG: 20–2000 Hz). Biomedical signal processing is simply the act of placing a microphone in this orchestra and converting the music into a digital format that computers can analyze. ### 1.3 Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) — Formal Definition **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** is the process of converting a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital signal. This is the fundamental step that allows modern computers and DSP chips to process biomedical data. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU 2024 Statement:** > ADC transforms $x(t) \to x[n]$, where the time axis is discretized (**sampling**) and the amplitude axis is discretized (**quantization**). The block diagram is mandatory for the ESE examination. ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy for ADC > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — A Digital Camera Capturing a Painting:** > An analog biomedical signal is like a smooth oil painting (every color, every shade exists). A digital converter is like a **digital camera**: > 1. The **Anti-aliasing filter** removes high-frequency "noise" beyond the camera's capture limit (like wearing anti-glare glasses). > 2. **Sampling** takes snapshots at regular intervals (like clicking the shutter every fraction of a second). > 3. **Quantization** limits the number of available colors (like reducing a 16-million-color image to 256 colors). > 4. **Encoding** stores the final picture in a binary format (like saving as JPEG — ones and zeros). > The "rule of thumb": To capture a fast-moving object perfectly, you need at least **two snapshots per motion cycle** (Nyquist criterion). > > Standard Metric: ECG sampling rate is typically **360 Hz** (clinical) or **500 Hz – 1 kHz** (research-grade), with a quantization resolution of **12-bit** (4096 levels). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Biomedical Signals Biomedical signals can be classified along several axes: - **By Origin**: - *Non-invasive (external)*: ECG (surface electrodes), EEG, EMG. - *Invasive (internal)*: Intracardiac ECG, intra-cranial EEG. - **By Stationarity**: - *Stationary*: Steady-state EEG during sleep. - *Non-stationary*: ECG (its morphology changes beat-to-beat with heart rate variability). - **By Dimensionality**: - *1-D signals*: ECG, blood pressure waveform. - *Multi-dimensional*: 12-lead ECG, 3-D MRI voxel data. ### 2.2 Examples of Major Biomedical Signals | **Signal** | **Full Name** | **Source Organ** | **Typical Frequency Band** | **Amplitude Range** | |------------|---------------|------------------|----------------------------|---------------------| | ECG | Electrocardiogram | Heart | **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz** | **0.5 mV – 5 mV** | | EEG | Electroencephalogram | Brain | **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz** | **2 µV – 200 µV** | | EMG | Electromyogram | Skeletal Muscles | **20 Hz – 2000 Hz** | **50 µV – 5 mV** | | EOG | Electro-oculogram | Eye | **DC – 30 Hz** | **10 µV – 5 mV** | | ERG | Electroretinogram | Retina | **0.2 Hz – 200 Hz** | **5 µV – 1 mV** | | PCG | Phonocardiogram | Heart (sound) | **20 Hz – 2000 Hz** | Acoustic pressure | | BP | Blood Pressure | Cardiovascular | **DC – 60 Hz** | **0 – 300 mmHg** | | RESP | Respiratory Signal | Lungs | **0.1 Hz – 10 Hz** | Variable | > [!NOTE] > **Why EEG amplitude is so tiny (µV):** > The EEG signal is generated by millions of cortical neurons firing in synchrony. The signal is heavily attenuated by the skull (≈ 1000x) before reaching surface electrodes, hence the micro-volt range. ### 2.3 Functional Block Diagram of ADC — Detailed The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates that students draw the **block diagram** of an ADC. The system consists of four mandatory functional blocks in cascade: 1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-Pass Filter)**: - Removes frequency components above half the sampling frequency. - Prevents **aliasing** (frequency folding). - Typical cutoff: $f_c = f_s / 2$. 2. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit**: - Captures the analog voltage at sampling instants $t = nT_s$. - Holds the value constant during the A/D conversion interval (aperture time). - Output: $x(nT_s) = x(t) \big\vert_{t = nT_s}$. 3. **Quantizer**: - Maps continuous amplitude $x(nT_s)$ to nearest discrete level. - Number of levels $L = 2^n$, where $n$ = number of bits. - Introduces **quantization error** $e_q = x_q - x$. 4. **Encoder (Binary Encoder)**: - Converts quantized level index to binary code. - Output: $n$-bit binary word. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Master These for KTU 2024 ESE** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** | |--------------|-------------|--------------------------| | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = 1 / T_s$ | $T_s$ is sampling period (seconds) | | Nyquist Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | $f_m$ is the highest signal frequency (Hz) | | Number of Quantization Levels | $L = 2^n$ | $n$ is number of bits | | Quantization Step Size | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | $V_{FS}$ is full-scale voltage range (Volts) | | Quantization Error Bound | $-\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Maximum error in Volts | | RMS Quantization Noise | $\sigma_q = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Volts (RMS) | | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Full-scale sinusoid) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | Decibels (dB) | | ADC Resolution | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Volts per LSB | | Dynamic Range | $\text{DR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2^n) = 6.02\,n$ | Decibels (dB) | | Quantization SNR (general) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_q^2} = \dfrac{12 \cdot \sigma_x^2}{\Delta^2}$ | Linear / ratio | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** > - **Telemedicine** uses low-power ADCs (10–12 bit) to transmit ECG over phone networks. > - **ICU monitors** use 16–24 bit ADCs to capture subtle EEG micro-signals. > - **Pacemakers** use ultra-low-power, low-resolution ADCs (8-bit) since they only need to detect R-wave peaks. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist Sampling Theorem Condition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this derivation matters:** It is the most heavily asked derivation in KTU Biomedical Signal Processing papers. **Statement (Nyquist-Shannon Theorem):** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. **Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation:** Consider a bandlimited analog signal $x(t)$ whose spectrum $X(f)$ is zero for $\vert f \vert > f_m$. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: Express ideal sampling as multiplication by an impulse train} \\ &x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s) \\ &\text{Step 2: Take the Fourier Transform. Multiplication in time } \to \text{ convolution in frequency} \\ &X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s) \\ &\text{Step 3: The spectrum is replicated at multiples of } f_s. \text{ For no overlap (no aliasing):} \\ &\text{The replicas must not touch each other.} \\ &f_s - f_m \geq f_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_s \geq 2 f_m \\ &\text{Step 4: Define the minimum sampling rate (Nyquist Rate)} \\ &f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 f_m \\ &\text{Step 5: Reconstruction uses a low-pass filter with cutoff } f_c = f_s / 2 \\ &x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right) \\ &\text{Final condition:} \quad \boxed{\,f_s \geq 2 f_m\,} \end{aligned} $$ > **Reasoning:** If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas overlap, causing frequency components to fold back into the baseband — a phenomenon called **aliasing** — which permanently destroys information. ### 3.2 Derivation of Quantization Step Size and Error **Setup:** A unipolar analog input ranges from $0$ to $V_{FS}$ Volts, and the ADC uses $n$ bits. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: Number of discrete levels} \\ &L = 2^n \\ &\text{Step 2: Total range} \\ &\Delta_{\text{total}} = V_{FS} - 0 = V_{FS} \\ &\text{Step 3: Quantization step (LSB size)} \\ &\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{L} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \\ &\text{Step 4: For bipolar signal } -V \text{ to } +V \\ &\Delta = \frac{2V}{2^n} \\ &\text{Step 5: Maximum quantization error} \\ &\vert e_q \vert_{\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} \\ &\text{Step 6: Quantization error is uniformly distributed in } [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2] \\ &\text{PDF: } p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \text{ for } -\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e \leq \frac{\Delta}{2} \\ &\text{Step 7: Mean square error (variance)} \\ &\sigma_q^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot p(e) \, de = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} \\ &\sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot (\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} \\ &\text{Step 8: RMS quantization noise} \\ &\boxed{\,\sigma_q = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n \sqrt{12}}\,} \\ \end{aligned} $$ > **Reasoning:** Each quantization level has width $\Delta$. The worst-case error happens when a signal lies exactly between two decision levels, yielding an error of $\pm \Delta/2$. On average, the noise power is $\Delta^2/12$. ### 3.3 Derivation of Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) **Assumption:** The ADC is driven by a full-scale sinusoidal signal: $$ x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t), \quad \text{where } A = V_{FS}/2 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: Signal power (mean square)} \\ &\sigma_x^2 = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{(V_{FS}/2)^2}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8} \\ &\text{Step 2: Noise power} \\ &\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n} \\ &\text{Step 3: SNR (linear)} \\ &\text{SNR} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2/8}{V_{FS}^2/(12 \cdot 4^n)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} = \frac{3 \cdot 4^n}{2} \\ &\text{Step 4: Convert to decibels} \\ &\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3 \cdot 4^n}{2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 n \log_{10}(4) \\ &\text{Step 5: Numerical evaluation} \\ &\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 10 n \cdot (2 \log_{10} 2) \\ &\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 10 n \cdot (2 \cdot 0.3010) \\ &\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 n \\ &\boxed{\,\text{SNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76 \text{ dB}\,} \\ \end{aligned} $$ > **Practical implication (KTU favorite question):** Every additional bit of ADC resolution increases the SNR by **6.02 dB** (approx. doubles the dynamic range). ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example **Problem (KTU-Style):** An ECG signal has bandwidth **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz** with amplitude range **±5 mV**. Design a 12-bit ADC and compute its sampling frequency, step size, and SNR. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Given:} \\ &f_m = 100 \text{ Hz}, \quad n = 12 \text{ bits}, \quad V_{FS} = 10 \text{ mV} \\ &\text{Step 1: Minimum sampling frequency (Nyquist)} \\ &f_s = 2 f_m = 200 \text{ Hz} \\ &\text{(Typically oversampled to } f_s = 500 \text{ Hz or } 1 \text{ kHz in practice)} \\ &\text{Step 2: Number of quantization levels} \\ &L = 2^{12} = 4096 \\ &\text{Step 3: Quantization step size} \\ &\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10 \text{ mV}}{4096} = 2.441 \text{ μV/LSB} \\ &\text{Step 4: Maximum quantization error} \\ &\vert e_q \vert_{\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} = 1.221 \text{ μV} \\ &\text{Step 5: RMS quantization noise} \\ &\sigma_q = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{2.441}{3.464} = 0.705 \text{ μV} \\ &\text{Step 6: Signal-to-Noise Ratio} \\ &\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 (12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB} \\ \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Biomedical Signal Acquisition & ADC System This Mermaid flowchart represents the complete **biomedical signal acquisition chain** — from the human body to the digital processor — as mandated by the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus. ```mermaid graph TB BIO["Biological Source
Heart / Brain / Muscle"] ELECTRODE["Transducer / Electrode
ECG / EEG / EMG"] PREAMP["Pre Amplifier
Gain 1000 to 10000"] AAF["Anti Aliasing Filter
Low Pass Filter"] SHC["Sample and Hold
S and H Circuit"] QUANT["Quantizer
Discrete Levels 2 to the n"] ENC["Encoder
Binary Code Output"] DSP["Digital Processor
Microcontroller / DSP"] DISP["Display and Storage
Monitor / Cloud"] BIO --> ELECTRODE ELECTRODE --> PREAMP PREAMP --> AAF AAF --> SHC SHC --> QUANT QUANT --> ENC ENC --> DSP DSP --> DISP ``` **Block-Level Explanation:** - **Biological Source**: The physiological generator (e.g., sinoatrial node of heart for ECG). - **Transducer / Electrode**: Converts ionic current into electronic voltage. Common types: Ag/AgCl surface electrodes. - **Pre-Amplifier**: Boosts tiny biomedical signals (µV) to a processable range (mV to V). High **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)** is essential. - **Anti-Aliasing Filter**: A low-pass filter that band-limits the signal to $f_c = f_s/2$. - **Sample-and-Hold**: Maintains a constant voltage during A/D conversion. - **Quantizer**: Discretizes amplitude into $2^n$ levels. - **Encoder**: Outputs $n$-bit binary code. - **Digital Processor**: Applies filtering, feature extraction, classification. - **Display and Storage**: Monitor, smartphone, or cloud database. ### 4.2 ADC Internal Block Diagram (KTU Mandated) ```mermaid graph LR AIN["Analog Input
x of t"] LPF["Anti Aliasing LPF
Cutoff f sub s over 2"] SAMPLE["SAMPLING
Impulse Train Modulator"] HOLD["HOLD Circuit
Capacitor based"] Q["QUANTIZER
2 to the n Levels"] E["ENCODER
Binary Coder"] DOUT["Digital Output
x of n bits"] AIN --> LPF LPF --> SAMPLE SAMPLE --> HOLD HOLD --> Q Q --> E E --> DOUT ``` **Functional Description of Each ADC Block:** | **Block** | **Function** | **Key Parameter** | |-----------|--------------|-------------------| | Anti-Aliasing LPF | Remove frequencies > $f_s/2$ | Cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$, Order (e.g., 4th order Butterworth) | | Sampling | $t \to nT_s$ | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (Nyquist) | | Hold | Maintain constant voltage during conversion | Aperture time $\tau_a$ | | Quantizer | Discretize amplitude | $L = 2^n$ levels, $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$ | | Encoder | Convert level index to binary | $n$-bit word | ### 4.3 Signal Transformation — Continuous-to-Discrete Visual ```mermaid graph TB subgraph ANALOG["ANALOG DOMAIN"] AS["x of t
Continuous Time
Continuous Amplitude"] end subgraph SAMPLED["SAMPLED DOMAIN"] S["x of nT sub s
Discrete Time
Continuous Amplitude"] end subgraph DIGITAL["DIGITAL DOMAIN"] D["x of n
Discrete Time
Discrete Amplitude
n bit word"] end AS -- Sampling Process --> S S -- Quantization and Encoding --> D ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define a biomedical signal. List any four examples of biomedical signals with their typical frequency range.* **Model Answer:** A biomedical signal is a measurable time-varying physical quantity that originates from a biological system and carries diagnostic or physiological information. Four examples: | **Signal** | **Frequency Range** | |------------|---------------------| | ECG (Electrocardiogram) | 0.5 Hz – 100 Hz | | EEG (Electroencephalogram) | 0.5 Hz – 100 Hz | | EMG (Electromyogram) | 20 Hz – 2000 Hz | | EOG (Electro-oculogram) | DC – 30 Hz | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, KTU Model]** *What is the Nyquist sampling theorem? Why is the anti-aliasing filter placed before the sample-and-hold circuit in an ADC?* **Model Answer:** The **Nyquist sampling theorem** states that a bandlimited signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$ The **minimum sampling rate** is called the **Nyquist rate**: $f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 f_m$. The anti-aliasing filter is placed *before* the sample-and-hold circuit because **aliasing is irreversible** — once high-frequency components fold back into the baseband during sampling, the original information is permanently lost. The low-pass filter removes all frequency components above $f_s/2$ *before* they can alias, ensuring clean digitization. (3 marks) --- ### Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Style** **Q3. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the characteristics of ECG, EEG, and EMG signals with neat waveforms, frequency content, and typical clinical applications. **Model Solution:** **(i) ECG — Electrocardiogram (3 marks)** The ECG represents the electrical activity of the heart captured using surface electrodes on the limbs and chest. - **Origin**: Sinoatrial (SA) node → Atrial depolarization (P-wave) → Ventricular depolarization (QRS complex) → Ventricular repolarization (T-wave). - **Frequency band**: 0.5 Hz – 100 Hz (clinical diagnostic range 0.5 – 40 Hz). - **Amplitude**: 0.5 mV – 5 mV. - **Clinical uses**: Detection of arrhythmias, myocardial infarction, conduction blocks. **(ii) EEG — Electroencephalogram (2 marks)** The EEG captures electrical activity of the brain via scalp electrodes following the international **10-20 system**. - **Frequency bands**: - Delta ($\delta$): 0.5 – 4 Hz (deep sleep) - Theta ($\theta$): 4 – 8 Hz (drowsiness) - Alpha ($\alpha$): 8 – 13 Hz (relaxed wakefulness) - Beta ($\beta$): 13 – 30 Hz (active thinking) - Gamma ($\gamma$): 30 – 100 Hz (cognitive processing) - **Amplitude**: 2 µV – 200 µV. - **Clinical uses**: Epilepsy diagnosis, sleep disorder analysis, brain death confirmation. **(iii) EMG — Electromyogram (2 marks)** The EMG records the electrical potential generated by skeletal muscle fibers during contraction. - **Frequency band**: 20 Hz – 2000 Hz. - **Amplitude**: 50 µV – 5 mV. - **Clinical uses**: Neuromuscular disease diagnosis, prosthetics control, biofeedback. **Q3. (b) [7 Marks]** Draw the block diagram of an Analog-to-Digital Converter and explain each block in detail. **Model Solution:** ``` Analog Input → Anti-Aliasing Filter → Sample & Hold → Quantizer → Encoder → Digital Output x(t) (LPF, fc = fs/2) (S/H) (2^n Lvl) (Binary) x[n] (n-bit) ``` **Block 1: Anti-Aliasing Filter (LPF) (1.5 marks)** - A low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = f_s/2$. - Function: Removes frequency components above Nyquist frequency to prevent aliasing. - Typical: 4th-order Butterworth or Bessel filter for biomedical applications. **Block 2: Sample-and-Hold (1.5 marks)** - Captures analog value at $t = nT_s$. - Holds voltage constant during the A/D conversion time (aperture time $\tau_a$). - Output: discrete-time signal $x(nT_s)$ with continuous amplitude. **Block 3: Quantizer (2 marks)** - Maps $x(nT_s)$ to nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels. - Step size: $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$. - Introduces quantization error $e_q \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$. - Example: 12-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 10$ mV → $\Delta = 2.441$ µV. **Block 4: Encoder (1.5 marks)** - Converts quantized level index into an $n$-bit binary word. - Example: level 2048 in a 12-bit ADC → binary `100000000000`. - Output: digital word $x[n]$, ready for DSP processing. **Block 5 (Optional): Output Buffer/Register** (0.5 marks) - Latches the digital word for downstream processing. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q4. (a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the Analog-to-Digital Conversion process. State and explain the Nyquist sampling theorem. **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (4 marks):** ``` Analog Input x(t) │ ▼ ┌─────────────────┐ │ Anti-Aliasing │ (Low-Pass Filter, fc = fs/2) │ Filter │ └────────┬────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────┐ │ Sample & Hold │ (Captures x(nTs)) │ Circuit │ └────────┬────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────┐ │ Quantizer │ (2^n discrete levels) └────────┬────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────┐ │ Encoder │ (n-bit binary code) └────────┬────────┘ ▼ Digital Output x[n] (n bits) ``` **Nyquist Sampling Theorem (3 marks):** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with highest frequency component $f_m$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$ **Proof sketch**: The sampled signal spectrum $X_s(f)$ consists of replicas of $X(f)$ at multiples of $f_s$. For no overlap, the gap between replicas $f_s - f_m$ must be at least $f_m$, giving $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. Reconstruction is achieved by passing the sampled signal through an ideal low-pass filter of cutoff $f_s/2$. > **Nyquist Rate:** $f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 f_m$ > **Aliasing:** Violation of $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ causes frequency folding and irreversible information loss. **Q4. (b) [7 Marks]** A 10-bit ADC is used to digitize an analog signal with a range of 0 to 5 V. Calculate (i) the quantization step size, (ii) the maximum quantization error, (iii) the RMS quantization noise, and (iv) the signal-to-noise ratio for a full-scale sinusoidal input. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $V_{FS} = 5$ V, $n = 10$ bits. **(i) Quantization Step Size (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} \\ &\Delta = 4.88 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 4.88 \text{ mV/LSB} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Maximum Quantization Error (1.5 marks):** $$ \vert e_q \vert_{\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{4.88}{2} = 2.44 \text{ mV} $$ **(iii) RMS Quantization Noise (2 marks):** $$ \sigma_q = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{4.88 \times 10^{-3}}{3.464} = 1.41 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1.41 \text{ mV} $$ **(iv) Signal-to-Noise Ratio (1.5 marks):** $$ \text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 n + 1.76 = 6.02 (10) + 1.76 = 60.2 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Mistakes**: Writing $\Delta$ as 4.88 (dimensionless) instead of **4.88 mV** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing/wrong unit. > 2. **Forgetting $\sqrt{12}$**: Using $\Delta$ itself instead of $\Delta/\sqrt{12}$ for RMS noise — a classic 1-mark penalty. > 3. **Confusing Full-Scale with Peak**: Signal RMS is $V_{FS}/(2\sqrt{2})$, not $V_{FS}/\sqrt{2}$. The factor of 1.76 dB comes precisely from this distinction. > 4. **Sampling Theorem Confusion**: Many students write $f_s = f_m$ instead of $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. **Half the marks for sampling theorem questions are lost here.** > 5. **Block Diagram Order**: Reversing the S/H and Quantizer order. **Always**: Input → AAF → S/H → Quantizer → Encoder → Output. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1** - ✅ **Biomedical signals** originate from biological systems and carry diagnostic information. Major examples: **ECG (0.5–100 Hz)**, **EEG (0.5–100 Hz)**, **EMG (20–2000 Hz)**, **EOG (DC–30 Hz)**, **ERG (0.2–200 Hz)**, **PCG (20–2000 Hz)**. - ✅ **ECG amplitude**: 0.5–5 mV; **EEG amplitude**: 2–200 µV (much smaller because of skull attenuation). - ✅ **EMG** has the highest frequency content among common biomedical signals. - ✅ **ADC** converts $x(t)$ (continuous) → $x[n]$ (discrete). The two key operations are **sampling** (discretize time) and **quantization** (discretize amplitude). - ✅ **Nyquist Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ — must be drawn as a block and stated mathematically. - ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter** is **mandatory** before sampling to prevent irreversible aliasing. - ✅ **Quantization step**: $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$. - ✅ **Quantization error bound**: $\pm \Delta/2$. - ✅ **RMS quantization noise**: $\Delta/\sqrt{12}$. - ✅ **SNR formula**: $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ (sinusoidal full-scale input). - ✅ **Block diagram order (CRITICAL)**: Analog Input → Anti-Aliasing LPF → Sample & Hold → Quantizer → Encoder → Digital Output. - ✅ **Clinical sampling rates**: ECG = 360–500 Hz; EEG = 250–1000 Hz; EMG = 1000–2000 Hz (often 2 kHz minimum). - ✅ **Typical ADC resolutions**: 8-bit (pacemakers), 10–12 bit (ECG monitors), 16–24 bit (EEG/EP systems). - ✅ **Aliasing** is **irreversible**; prevention is mandatory during signal acquisition.

topics.title: Discrete-Time Signals - Typical Sequences and Sequence Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Signals - Typical Sequences and Sequence Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Discrete-Time Signal?

A **discrete-time signal** is a mathematical function defined only at discrete instants of time. Unlike continuous-time signals (which are defined for every real value of $t$), a discrete-time signal is defined only at specific time instants, usually equally spaced.

$$x[n] = \{x[n]\}, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

where $n$ is an integer index representing the sample number.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A discrete-time signal is a sequence of numbers $x[n]$, where $n$ is an integer index, representing the amplitude of the signal at the $n$-th sampling instant. Each value in the sequence is called a **sample**.

## 1.2 The Origins in Biomedical Context

In biomedical signal processing, almost all signals we encounter are **inherently continuous** (like ECG, EEG, EMG). However, for digital processing, we must convert them into discrete-time form via **sampling** at a rate governed by the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Biomedical Insight:** ECG signals with a highest frequency component of about **150 Hz** must be sampled at $f_s \geq 300$ Hz. In practice, a sampling rate of **360 Hz** or **500 Hz** is used clinically.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Staircase vs. The Slide**
> Think of a continuous-time signal as a **smooth slide** at a playground — you can stand at any point along it. A discrete-time signal is like a **staircase** — you can only stand on the steps. The "staircase steps" are the discrete samples. The "height" of each step corresponds to the amplitude $x[n]$, and the "step number" corresponds to the index $n$.

## 1.4 Why Discrete-Time Signals Matter in Biomedical Engineering

- **Digital storage:** Hospitals store ECG/EEG as digital samples, not continuous traces.
- **Algorithmic processing:** Filtering, feature extraction, and pattern recognition all operate on discrete samples.
- **Microprocessor implementation:** Embedded pacemakers and patient monitors use DSP chips that process discrete sequences.

## 1.5 Notation Conventions Used by KTU Examiners

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $x[n]$ | Discrete-time signal, dependent on integer index $n$ |
| $x(n)$ | Sometimes used interchangeably (context matters) |
| $n$ | Integer time index, dimensionless |
| $T$ | Sampling period in **seconds** |
| $f_s = 1/T$ | Sampling frequency in **Hz** |
| $\Omega$ | Normalized digital frequency in **radians/sample** |
| $\omega$ | Analog frequency in **radians/second** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Classification of Discrete-Time Signals

Discrete-time signals can be classified based on periodicity, symmetry, energy, and power:

### 2.1.1 Periodic vs. Aperiodic

A discrete-time signal is **periodic** with period $N$ if:

$$x[n+N] = x[n] \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

The smallest positive $N$ is called the **fundamental period**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrete-Time Periodicity Constraint:** A discrete-time sinusoid $x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic **only if** $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is a **rational number**. This is fundamentally different from continuous-time!

### 2.1.2 Even and Odd Symmetry

$$x_{even}[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$$

$$x_{odd}[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$$

Any signal can be decomposed uniquely: $x[n] = x_{even}[n] + x_{odd}[n]$.

### 2.1.3 Energy vs. Power Signals

**Energy** of a discrete-time signal:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

**Power** of a discrete-time signal:

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

## 2.2 The "Big Six" Typical Discrete-Time Sequences

These are the canonical sequences that form the building blocks of DSP. Every other sequence can be constructed as a combination of these.

### 2.2.1 Unit Impulse Sequence (Kronecker Delta) $\delta[n]$

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.2 Unit Step Sequence $u[n]$

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.3 Unit Ramp Sequence $r[n]$

$$r[n] = n \cdot u[n] = \begin{cases} n, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.4 Exponential Sequence

$$x[n] = a^n \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

### 2.2.5 Sinusoidal Sequence

$$x[n] = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$$

### 2.2.6 Rectangular Pulse Sequence

$$x[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & -N_1 \leq n \leq N_1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU exam paper for this module.**

| Sequence | Mathematical Definition | Value at $n=0$ | Value at $n=3$ | Graphical Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unit Impulse $\delta[n]$ | $\delta[n]=1$ if $n=0$ else $0$ | **1** | **0** | Single spike of height 1 |
| Unit Step $u[n]$ | $u[n]=1$ if $n \geq 0$ else $0$ | **1** | **1** | Right-side constant at 1 |
| Unit Ramp $r[n]$ | $r[n] = n$ for $n \geq 0$ | **0** | **3** | Linearly increasing from 0 |
| Exponential $a^n$ | $x[n]=a^n$, all $n$ | **1** | $a^3$ | Decays if $\vert a \vert < 1$ |
| Sinusoid $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ | $A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ | $A\cos(\phi)$ | $A\cos(3\omega_0+\phi)$ | Oscillates, periodic only for rational $\omega_0/2\pi$ |
| Rectangular Pulse | $1$ for $-N_1 \leq n \leq N_1$ | **1** | depends on $N_1$ | Finite-duration box |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

| Typical Sequence | Biomedical Application |
|---|---|
| Unit Impulse | Modeling a single QRS complex in an idealized ECG |
| Unit Step | Modeling a sudden onset of a stimulus in a nerve response |
| Exponential | Modeling nerve membrane voltage decay (RC circuit response) |
| Sinusoidal | Modeling arterial blood pressure waveform components |
| Rectangular | Modeling a finite-duration pacing pulse from a pacemaker |

## 2.5 Critical Relationship Between Impulse and Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sifting Property of Impulse:** Any signal can be reconstructed from its samples using the unit impulse:
> $$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k]$$
> This is foundational because it shows the unit impulse is the **atomic building block** of all discrete-time signals.

Also, the relationship between step and impulse is:

$$\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$$

$$u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k]$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Decomposition of a Generic Sequence into Impulses

**Statement:** Any discrete-time signal $x[n]$ can be expressed as a weighted sum of shifted impulses.

**Starting from the sifting property intuition:**

We observe that $x[n]$ evaluated at integer $n$ is a number. We want to write this as a sum of impulses, where the $k$-th impulse $\delta[n-k]$ is shifted to position $k$ and weighted by $x[k]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[n] &= x[\dots] \cdot \delta[n-\dots] + x[\dots] \cdot \delta[n-\dots] + \dots \\
&= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step verification:**

- For a fixed $n$, the term $\delta[n-k]$ is non-zero **only when** $k = n$.
- At that point, $\delta[n-k] = \delta[0] = 1$.
- All other terms vanish.
- Therefore, the entire sum collapses to $x[n] \cdot 1 = x[n]$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Step Sequence as a Sum of Impulses

We start with the relationship $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$, then rearrange:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u[n] &= u[n-1] + \delta[n] \\
u[n-1] &= u[n-2] + \delta[n-1] \\
u[n-2] &= u[n-3] + \delta[n-2] \\
&\vdots \\
u[0] &= u[-\infty] + \delta[0]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Telescoping summation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u[n] &= u[-\infty] + \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $u[n] = 0$ for $n \to -\infty$, we have $u[-\infty] = 0$. Substituting:

$$
u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k]
$$

This is a **causal summation** of impulses. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Periodicity Condition for Discrete-Time Sinusoids

**Question:** When is $x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ periodic?

For periodicity, we need $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for some integer $N > 0$:

$$
\cos(\omega_0 (n+N)) = \cos(\omega_0 n)
$$

This requires:

$$
\omega_0 N = 2\pi m, \quad m \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

Solving for $N$:

$$
N = \frac{2\pi m}{\omega_0}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** $N$ must be a **positive integer**. This is only possible when $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is **rational** (i.e., a ratio of two integers). For irrational $\omega_0 / 2\pi$, the sinusoid is **aperiodic**.

**Example:** $\cos(\pi n / 3)$ has $\omega_0 = \pi/3$, so $\omega_0/2\pi = 1/6$, giving $N = 6$ samples. Period is **6**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Fundamental Frequency of Discrete-Time Sinusoids

For periodic discrete sinusoids, the **fundamental digital frequency** is:

$$
\Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N} \quad \text{[radians/sample]}
$$

where $N$ is the fundamental period.

Note that $\cos(\omega_0 n) = \cos((\omega_0 + 2\pi k) n)$ for any integer $k$, which means **all frequencies differing by $2\pi$ are indistinguishable** in discrete time. This is called **aliasing in the frequency domain**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Properties of the Six Typical Sequences

| Property | $\delta[n]$ | $u[n]$ | $r[n]$ | $a^n$ | $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Causal? | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (bilateral) | ✗ |
| Energy | **1** (finite) | Infinite | Infinite | $\frac{1}{1-\vert a \vert^2}$ if $\vert a \vert < 1$ | Infinite (if periodic) |
| Power | 0 | 1 | Infinite | 0 (if $\vert a \vert < 1$) | $\frac{1}{2}$ |
| Periodic? | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Only if $\omega_0/2\pi$ rational |
| Even? | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Only if $a=1$ | ✓ |
| Odd? | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Only if $a=-1$ | ✗ |

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example: Sampling an ECG-like Signal

Consider a continuous-time signal $x(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 5 t)$ representing a 5 Hz component of an ECG-like waveform. Sample it at $f_s = 20$ Hz, so $T = 0.05$ s.

**Step 1:** Sampling instants are $t_n = nT = 0.05n$ seconds.

**Step 2:** The discrete-time signal becomes:

$$
x[n] = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 0.05 n) = 5\cos(0.5\pi n)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the first few samples:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[0] &= 5\cos(0) = 5 \\
x[1] &= 5\cos(0.5\pi) = 5 \cdot 0 = 0 \\
x[2] &= 5\cos(\pi) = -5 \\
x[3] &= 5\cos(1.5\pi) = 0 \\
x[4] &= 5\cos(2\pi) = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Periodicity check. $\omega_0 = 0.5\pi$, so $\omega_0/2\pi = 1/4$ — rational. Fundamental period $N = 4$ samples. The signal repeats every 4 samples. $\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Numerical Example: Energy of an Exponential Decay

Let $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$. Compute its energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^{2n} \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a geometric series with first term 1 and common ratio $0.25$:

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333 \text{ [Joules, normalized]}
$$

Since $E$ is finite, $x[n]$ is an **energy signal**, not a power signal. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Signal Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Discrete-Time Signal x of n] --> B{Energy Finite?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Energy Signal]
    B -->|No| D{Power Finite?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Power Signal]
    D -->|No| F[Neither]
    A --> G{Periodic?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Periodic Signal]
    G -->|No| I[Aperiodic Signal]
    A --> J{Causal?}
    J -->|Yes x n = 0 for n less than 0| K[Causal Signal]
    J -->|No| L[Non-Causal Signal]
    A --> M{Symmetric?}
    M -->|x n equals x -n| N[Even Signal]
    M -->|x n equals -x -n| O[Odd Signal]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The Six Canonical Sequences

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Typical Discrete-Time Sequences] --> B1[Unit Impulse delta n]
    A --> B2[Unit Step u n]
    A --> B3[Unit Ramp r n]
    A --> B4[Exponential a to power n]
    A --> B5[Sinusoidal cos of omega n]
    A --> B6[Rectangular Pulse P n]
    B1 --> C1[Atomic building block]
    B2 --> C2[Integral of impulse]
    B3 --> C3[Integral of step]
    B4 --> C4[Decays if a less than 1]
    B5 --> C5[Periodic if rational omega]
    B6 --> C6[Finite support sequence]
    C1 --> D[Sifting Property Reconstruction]
    C2 --> D
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Sequence Representation Methods

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[Discrete Sequence x n] --> R1[Functional Form]
    S --> R2[Tabular Form]
    S --> R3[Graphical Stem Plot]
    S --> R4[Set Notation]
    S --> R5[Closed Form Equation]
    R1 --> E1[e.g. x n = n for n greater than 0]
    R2 --> E2[e.g. n -2 -1 0 1 2]
    R2 --> E3[x n  4  1  0  1  4]
    R3 --> E4[Vertical lines with dots]
    R4 --> E5[Curly braces with arrow]
    R5 --> E6[e.g. x n equals 2 times sin of pi n divided by 4]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Conversion from Continuous to Discrete-Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Continuous Biomedical Signal ECG] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter LPF]
    B --> C[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Discrete Sequence x n]
    E --> F[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    F --> G[Processed Output e.g. filtered ECG]
    H[Clock at f s Hz] --> C
    H --> D
```

## 4.5 Schematic: Impulse-Step-Ramp Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Unit Impulse delta n] -->|Running Sum| B[Unit Step u n]
    B -->|Running Sum| C[Unit Ramp r n]
    A -->|Backward Difference| B
    B -->|Backward Difference| C
    C -->|Second Difference| A
```

## 4.6 Graphical Representation: Sample Stem Plots

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "sketch" a discrete-time sequence, always use **stem plots** (vertical lines topped with filled circles), not continuous curves. The x-axis must show integer indices, not continuous time.

**Description for x[n] = δ[n]:**
- A single vertical stem of height 1 at $n = 0$
- Zero amplitude everywhere else

**Description for x[n] = u[n]:**
- Stems of height 1 starting from $n = 0$ and extending to $+\infty$
- Zero amplitude for all $n < 0$

**Description for x[n] = (0.8)^n u[n]:**
- $x[0] = 1$, $x[1] = 0.8$, $x[2] = 0.64$, $x[3] = 0.512$, ...
- Exponentially decaying stems for $n \geq 0$
- Zero for $n < 0$

**Description for x[n] = 2cos(πn/4)u[n]:**
- $x[0] = 2$, $x[1] = \sqrt{2}$, $x[2] = 0$, $x[3] = -\sqrt{2}$, $x[4] = -2$, $x[5] = -\sqrt{2}$, $x[6] = 0$, $x[7] = \sqrt{2}$, $x[8] = 2$
- Period of 8 samples; oscillates with decreasing visible bounds
- Zero for $n < 0$

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define a discrete-time signal. List any four typical sequences used in discrete-time signal processing.**

**Model Answer:**

A **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$ is a sequence of numbers defined only at integer values of the index $n$, representing the amplitude of a signal sampled at discrete time instants.

**Four typical sequences:**

1. **Unit impulse sequence** $\delta[n]$: Defined as 1 at $n=0$ and 0 elsewhere.
2. **Unit step sequence** $u[n]$: Defined as 1 for $n \geq 0$ and 0 for $n < 0$.
3. **Exponential sequence** $x[n] = a^n$: Amplitude varies exponentially with index.
4. **Sinusoidal sequence** $x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$: Oscillates with discrete samples.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1/2 mark each for four sequences.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Check whether the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n)$ is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period.**

**Model Answer:**

For periodicity, $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for some positive integer $N$:

$$\cos(0.5\pi(n+N)) = \cos(0.5\pi n)$$

This requires $0.5\pi N = 2\pi m$ for integer $m$:

$$N = 4m$$

Smallest positive integer value: $N = 4$ (taking $m = 1$).

**Therefore, $x[n]$ is periodic with fundamental period $N = 4$ samples.** (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Define the unit impulse and unit step sequences. Derive the relationship between them, showing that $u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k]$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A discrete-time signal is given by $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$. Calculate (i) the energy of the signal, and (ii) the first 5 sample values. State whether it is an energy or power signal.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): [7 marks]

**Definitions:**

**Unit impulse sequence:**

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Unit step sequence:**

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Derivation of the relationship:**

We start with the observation that the unit step differs from a delayed version of itself by an impulse:

$$u[n] - u[n-1] = \delta[n]$$

Rearranging: $u[n] = u[n-1] + \delta[n]$.

Expanding recursively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u[n] &= u[n-1] + \delta[n] \\
&= u[n-2] + \delta[n-1] + \delta[n] \\
&= u[n-3] + \delta[n-2] + \delta[n-1] + \delta[n] \\
&= \dots \\
&= u[-N] + \sum_{k=-N+1}^{n} \delta[k]
\end{aligned}
$$

As $N \to \infty$, $u[-N] = 0$ (since step is zero for negative indices):

$$u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k]$$

This is the desired relationship. $\blacksquare$ **(7 marks: 2 for definitions, 5 for derivation)**

#### Part (b): [7 marks]

**(i) Energy calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert (0.5)^n \vert^2 \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^{2n} \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the geometric series formula with first term $a = 1$ and ratio $r = 0.25$:

$$E = \frac{1}{1-0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \text{ J}$$

**(ii) First 5 sample values:**

- $x[0] = (0.5)^0 = 1$
- $x[1] = (0.5)^1 = 0.5$
- $x[2] = (0.5)^2 = 0.25$
- $x[3] = (0.5)^3 = 0.125$
- $x[4] = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$

**Classification:** Since $E = 4/3$ is **finite** and power $P = 0$, this is an **energy signal**. **(7 marks: 3 for energy, 2 for samples, 2 for classification)**

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain with neat diagrams the representation of a discrete-time signal in (i) functional form, (ii) tabular form, and (iii) graphical (stem) form. Use the example $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ with arrow indicating $n=0$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** For the signal $x[n] = 2\cos(\pi n/6 + \pi/4)$, determine whether it is periodic. If so, find the fundamental period and compute the first 6 sample values.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): [7 marks]

Given the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ with the arrow at $n=0$, this represents $x[-2]=1$, $x[-1]=2$, $x[0]=3$, $x[1]=2$, $x[2]=1$.

**(i) Functional form:**

$$x[n] = \begin{cases} 3 - \vert n \vert, & -2 \leq n \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is a triangular pulse centered at $n=0$ with peak value 3.

**(ii) Tabular form:**

| $n$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x[n]$ | 1 | 2 | **3** | 2 | 1 |

**(iii) Graphical (stem) form:**

- Stems of height 1 at $n = -2$ and $n = 2$
- Stems of height 2 at $n = -1$ and $n = 1$
- Central stem of height 3 at $n = 0$
- Forms a triangular shape symmetric about $n = 0$

**(7 marks: 2 for functional, 2 for tabular, 3 for graphical explanation)**

#### Part (b): [7 marks]

The signal is $x[n] = 2\cos(\pi n/6 + \pi/4)$, with $\omega_0 = \pi/6$.

**Periodicity check:**

For periodicity, $\omega_0/2\pi$ must be rational:

$$\frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\pi/6}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{12}$$

This is rational ($1/12$), so the signal is periodic.

**Fundamental period:**

$$N = \frac{2\pi m}{\omega_0} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 1}{\pi/6} = 12 \text{ samples}$$

**First 6 sample values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[0] &= 2\cos(0 + \pi/4) = 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \\
x[1] &= 2\cos(\pi/6 + \pi/4) = 2\cos(5\pi/12) \approx 2 \cdot 0.259 = 0.518 \\
x[2] &= 2\cos(\pi/3 + \pi/4) = 2\cos(7\pi/12) \approx 2 \cdot (-0.259) = -0.518 \\
x[3] &= 2\cos(\pi/2 + \pi/4) = 2\cos(3\pi/4) = 2 \cdot (-\sqrt{2}/2) = -\sqrt{2} \approx -1.414 \\
x[4] &= 2\cos(2\pi/3 + \pi/4) = 2\cos(11\pi/12) \approx 2 \cdot (-0.966) = -1.932 \\
x[5] &= 2\cos(5\pi/6 + \pi/4) = 2\cos(13\pi/12) \approx 2 \cdot (-0.966) = -1.932
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The signal is **periodic with fundamental period $N = 12$ samples**. **(7 marks: 3 for period, 4 for sample values)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1-2 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Confusing continuous-time and discrete-time periodicity:** Students often state the period of $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ as $T = 2\pi/\omega_0$ seconds. This is **wrong** — in discrete time, period is in **samples**, not seconds. Always write $N = 2\pi m/\omega_0$ and verify it is an integer.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to check rationality of $\omega_0/2\pi$:** Many students declare a discrete sinusoid periodic without verifying that $N$ is a positive integer. This loses 1-2 marks.
>
> 3. **Using $t$ instead of $n$ in equations:** When writing discrete-time signals, use the integer index $n$ (not $t$). Writing $x(t) = 2^n$ would be a conceptual error.
>
> 4. **Improper stem plot:** Drawing continuous curves instead of discrete stems; forgetting to mark the integer values on the n-axis; using filled circles only at the data points.
>
> 5. **Misapplying the sifting property:** Writing $x[n] = \sum x[n]\delta[n-k]$ (with $n$ instead of $k$ inside the sum) — the index of summation must differ from the index in the body.
>
> 6. **Sign error in energy formula:** Using $E = \sum x[n]$ instead of $E = \sum \vert x[n] \vert^2$. For real signals both work, but for complex signals the modulus squared is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Items Before Every KTU Exam**

- [ ] **Definition of discrete-time signal** — sequence of numbers $x[n]$ at integer indices $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- [ ] **Unit impulse** $\delta[n]$ — value 1 at $n=0$, zero elsewhere; satisfies the sifting property.
- [ ] **Unit step** $u[n]$ — value 1 for $n \geq 0$, zero for $n < 0$.
- [ ] **Unit ramp** $r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$ — linearly increasing from $n=0$.
- [ ] **Exponential sequence** $x[n] = a^n$ — decays if $\vert a \vert < 1$, grows if $\vert a \vert > 1$.
- [ ] **Sinusoidal sequence** $x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ — periodic only if $\omega_0/2\pi$ is rational.
- [ ] **Sifting property:** $x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\delta[n-k]$
- [ ] **Step-impulse relation:** $u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k]$
- [ ] **Discrete periodicity formula:** $N = 2\pi m / \omega_0$ (must be a positive integer)
- [ ] **Energy formula:** $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$
- [ ] **Power formula:** $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$
- [ ] **Even-odd decomposition:** $x[n] = \frac{x[n]+x[-n]}{2} + \frac{x[n]-x[-n]}{2}$
- [ ] **Sampling rate for biomedical signals:** ECG ≥ **360 Hz**, EEG ≥ **256 Hz**, EMG ≥ **1000 Hz**.
- [ ] **Stem plot convention:** Always use vertical lines with filled circles at sample points; integer indices only on the horizontal axis.
- [ ] **Geometric series result:** $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \frac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$ — used heavily in energy computations.
- [ ] **Aliasing warning:** Frequencies differing by $2\pi$ in discrete time are **indistinguishable** — choose $\omega_0 \in [-\pi, \pi]$ or $[0, 2\pi]$ for unique representation.
- [ ] **Causality definition:** $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. The unit step, ramp, and right-sided exponentials are causal; the bilateral exponential and sinusoids are non-causal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete-Time Systems: Classification of Discrete-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Systems: Classification of Discrete-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Discrete-Time System

A **Discrete-Time System (DTS)** is a mathematical operator `T` that transforms an input discrete-time sequence `x[n]` into an output discrete-time sequence `y[n]`, where `n` is an integer-valued time index.

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 of **PBBRT504 – Biomedical Signal Processing** treats a discrete-time system as the fundamental processing block for biosignals such as **ECG, EEG, EMG, and PCG**. Understanding its classification is a direct prerequisite for filter design and biosignal feature extraction.

## 1.2 The Concept of Classification

Classification of a DTS is the systematic categorization of a system based on the **properties** of its operator `T`. Two systems described by the *same input-output relationship* may behave fundamentally differently — hence, classification lets us predict system behaviour **before** full implementation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line"

Think of a discrete-time system as a **smart factory assembly line**:

- **Input Conveyor Belt (`x[n]`)** → discrete items (raw ECG samples) arriving one-by-one.
- **Processing Unit (`T{ }`)** → workers + machines that handle the item.
- **Output Conveyor Belt (`y[n]`)** → finished product (filtered / processed signal).

> Each classification criterion simply asks: *"What special qualities do these workers and machines have?"*

| Classification Question | Real-World Factory Equivalent |
|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Static vs Dynamic | Worker who acts only on the *current* item vs. one who *remembers* the previous 5 items. |
| Linear vs Non-Linear | Workers that scale with input vs. workers that have a "saturation" limit. |
| Time-Invariant vs Variant | Same worker rules every day vs. shift changes (morning/evening) alter behaviour. |
| Causal vs Non-Causal | Worker who only uses *already-arrived* items vs. one who peeks at *future* deliveries. |
| Stable vs Unstable | A machine whose output never explodes vs. one that can blow up if input is large. |

## 1.4 The Six Major Classifications (Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> These six classifications are the **high-yield list** for any KTU 2024 Board Examination question on this topic.

1. **Static (Memoryless) vs Dynamic (Memory) Systems**
2. **Time-Invariant (TI) vs Time-Variant (TV) Systems**
3. **Linear vs Non-Linear Systems**
4. **Causal vs Non-Causal Systems**
5. **Stable (BIBO) vs Unstable Systems**
6. **LTI Systems** (combined Linear + Time-Invariant — the most analytically tractable class).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static vs Dynamic Systems

### Definition
- **Static (Memoryless):** Output at time `n` depends **only** on the input at the *same* time `n`.
- **Dynamic (with Memory):** Output at time `n` depends on input samples from *other* time instants (past or future).

### Mathematical Form
$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} \quad \text{... Static}$$
$$y[n] = f\big(x[n], x[n-1], x[n+1], \ldots\big) \quad \text{... Dynamic}$$

### Why It Matters (Engineering Utility)
A static system in ECG processing might compute instantaneous amplitude, while a dynamic system performs **moving-average filtering** to remove 50 Hz powerline interference.

## 2.2 Time-Invariant vs Time-Variant Systems

### Definition
A system is **Time-Invariant (TI)** if a time-shift in the input produces an identical time-shift in the output.

### Test Condition
$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad y[n-n_0] = T\{x[n-n_0]\} \quad \forall\, n_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$$

> If this holds for *every* `n₀`, the system is **TI**; otherwise it is **Time-Variant (TV)**.

### Engineering Utility
LTI (Linear + TI) systems are *completely* characterized by their **impulse response** `h[n]`, enabling convolution-based analysis — the foundation of digital filter design for biomedical signals.

## 2.3 Linear vs Non-Linear Systems

### Definition
A system is **Linear** if and only if it satisfies the **Principle of Superposition**:

$$T\{a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]\} = a\,T\{x_1[n]\} + b\,T\{x_2[n]\}$$

This is the union of two properties:
- **Additivity:** `T{x₁[n] + x₂[n]} = T{x₁[n]} + T{x₂[n]}`
- **Homogeneity (Scaling):** `T{a·x[n]} = a·T{x[n]}`

### Engineering Utility
Most practical biomedical filters (LPF, HPF, Notch) are designed under linear assumptions because non-linear systems cannot be analysed via the convolution sum.

## 2.4 Causal vs Non-Causal Systems

### Definition
A system is **Causal** if the output at time `n` depends only on present and *past* inputs, **never** on future inputs.

$$y[n] = f\big(x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots\big) \quad \text{... Causal}$$

A system is **Non-Causal** if `y[n]` depends on any `x[n+k]` with `k > 0`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-time** biomedical signal processing (e.g., ICU ECG monitoring) *requires* causal systems. Non-causal systems are permitted only in **offline** applications (e.g., post-operative PCG analysis from recorded data).

## 2.5 Stable (BIBO) vs Unstable Systems

### Definition — BIBO Stability
A system is **BIBO Stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output Stable) if every bounded input produces a bounded output.

$$\vert x[n]\vert \le M_x < \infty \quad \forall n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert y[n]\vert \le M_y < \infty \quad \forall n$$

### For an LTI System
BIBO stability is equivalent to the **absolutely summable impulse response**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n]\vert < \infty$$

## 2.6 LTI Systems — The "Golden Class"

A system is **LTI** (Linear Time-Invariant) if it is both linear *and* time-invariant. For such systems, the output is given by the **Convolution Sum**:

$$\boxed{\,y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} h[k]\,x[n-k] = x[n] * h[n]\,}$$

where `h[n] = T{δ[n]}` is the **impulse response** of the system.

### Special Sub-Classes based on `h[n]`
| Sub-class | Condition on `h[n]` | Length |
|------------|---------------------|--------|
| **FIR** (Finite Impulse Response) | `h[n] = 0` for `n < N₁` and `n > N₂` (finite) | Finite |
| **IIR** (Infinite Impulse Response) | `h[n]` extends to infinity on at least one side | Infinite |

---

## 📋 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Every formula below is **board-exam tested** in the KTU 2024 Scheme for `PBBRT504`. Memorize the table **verbatim**.

| # | Property | Test Equation / Condition | Type |
|---|----------|--------------------------|------|
| 1 | Static (Memoryless) | `y[n] = f(x[n])` only | Definition |
| 2 | Dynamic (Memory) | `y[n]` depends on `x[n-k], k≠0` | Definition |
| 3 | Time-Invariant | `T{x[n−n₀]} = y[n−n₀]` | Test |
| 4 | Time-Variant | Fails at least one `n₀` | Test |
| 5 | Linear | `T{a·x₁ + b·x₂} = a·T{x₁} + b·T{x₂}` | Test |
| 6 | Non-Linear | Fails additivity or homogeneity | Test |
| 7 | Causal | `y[n]` uses only `x[k] for k ≤ n` | Definition |
| 8 | Non-Causal | `y[n]` uses any `x[k] for k > n` | Definition |
| 9 | BIBO Stable (General) | Bounded input → Bounded output | Definition |
| 10 | BIBO Stable (LTI) | `Σ |h[n]| < ∞` | Test |
| 11 | LTI Output | `y[n] = Σ h[k]·x[n−k]` | Convolution |
| 12 | FIR | `h[n]` is finitely supported | Sub-class |
| 13 | IIR | `h[n]` is infinitely supported | Sub-class |

> **Engineering Real-World Note:** In ECG/EEG denoising pipelines, an LTI BIBO-stable causal FIR filter is the *most common* building block — combine properties 3, 5, 7, 9, 12 above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — BIBO Stability Condition for an LTI System

We will prove that for an LTI system, **BIBO stability ⇔ absolutely summable impulse response**.

### Step 1 — Start from the Convolution Sum

The output of any LTI system is given by:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} h[k]\,x[n-k]$$

### Step 2 — Take the Absolute Value of Both Sides

Applying the absolute value operator to both sides:

$$\vert y[n]\vert = \left\vert \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} h[k]\,x[n-k] \right\vert$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Triangle Inequality

The **triangle inequality** states that $\vert a+b\vert \le \vert a\vert + \vert b\vert$, generalized to a sum:

$$\left\vert \sum_{k} h[k]\,x[n-k] \right\vert \le \sum_{k} \vert h[k]\vert \cdot \vert x[n-k]\vert$$

Therefore:

$$\vert y[n]\vert \le \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[k]\vert \cdot \vert x[n-k]\vert$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Bounded-Input Assumption

The input is bounded: there exists a finite `M_x > 0` such that:

$$\vert x[n-k]\vert \le M_x \quad \forall n, k$$

Substituting into the inequality:

$$\vert y[n]\vert \le \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[k]\vert \cdot M_x = M_x \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[k]\vert$$

### Step 5 — Define the Stability Bound

We obtain a finite upper bound for `|y[n]|` provided the impulse response is absolutely summable:

$$\boxed{\;\vert y[n]\vert \le M_x \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[k]\vert = M_y < \infty\;}$$

This holds **iff**:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[k]\vert < \infty$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion

> **Theorem:** *An LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if its impulse response `h[n]` is absolutely summable.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Example: Linearity Test

Consider the system:

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} = n \cdot x[n]$$

We test whether the system is **linear**.

### Step 1 — Apply Scaled Input

Let input be `a·x₁[n]`. Output is:

$$y_1[n] = T\{a\,x_1[n]\} = n \cdot (a\,x_1[n]) = a\,n\,x_1[n]$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Linearity Condition (Homogeneity)

For linearity, the output must scale by the same factor `a`:

$$T\{a\,x_1[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} = a \cdot (n\,x_1[n]) = a\,n\,x_1[n]$$

The two results match. Homogeneity is satisfied.

### Step 3 — Apply Sum of Two Inputs

Let `x[n] = x₁[n] + x₂[n]`. Output is:

$$y[n] = T\{x_1[n]+x_2[n]\} = n(x_1[n]+x_2[n]) = n\,x_1[n] + n\,x_2[n]$$

### Step 4 — Compare with Sum of Individual Outputs

$$T\{x_1[n]\} + T\{x_2[n]\} = n\,x_1[n] + n\,x_2[n]$$

The two results match. Additivity is satisfied.

### Step 5 — Conclusion

By superposition, `y[n] = n·x[n]` **is linear** — even though it is *time-variant* (because the coefficient `n` depends on the time index).

> **Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse *linear* with *time-invariant*. The system `y[n] = n·x[n]` is **linear but TV** — a classic KTU trap question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Example: Time-Invariance Test

Consider the system:

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} = x[n] - x[n-1]$$

We test whether the system is **time-invariant**.

### Step 1 — Shift the Input First

Replace `x[n]` by `x[n−n₀]` and compute output:

$$y_1[n] = T\{x[n-n_0]\} = x[n-n_0] - x[n-1-n_0]$$

### Step 2 — Shift the Output

Original output is `y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]`. Shifted by `n₀`:

$$y_2[n] = y[n-n_0] = x[n-n_0] - x[n-1-n_0]$$

### Step 3 — Compare

$$y_1[n] = x[n-n_0] - x[n-1-n_0] = y_2[n]$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

The system is **time-invariant** (in fact, it is the discrete differentiator, a classic FIR LTI system).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — BIBO Stability Check of an FIR Filter

Let `h[n] = {1, 2, 3, 2, 1}` for `n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4` and `0` elsewhere.

### Step 1 — Compute Absolute Sum

$$\sum_{n=0}^{4} \vert h[n]\vert = \vert 1\vert + \vert 2\vert + \vert 3\vert + \vert 2\vert + \vert 1\vert = 9$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Stability Criterion

$$9 < \infty$$

### Step 3 — Conclusion

The system is **BIBO stable** and the output is bounded by `M_y = 9·M_x`.

> **KTU Insight:** Every FIR filter with finite coefficients is *automatically* BIBO stable. Only IIR filters require explicit stability checks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
