# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a causal discrete-time system. State one example of a non-causal system that can be used in offline biomedical signal processing.**

**Model Answer:**

A discrete-time system is **causal** if the output `y[n]` at any time `n` depends only on the present input `x[n]` and past inputs `x[n-1], x[n-2], ...` — and **never** on any future input `x[n+k]` for `k > 0`.

**Mathematical Condition:**

$$y[n] = f\big(x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots\big)$$

**Example of a Non-Causal System (Offline Use):**
A *zero-phase* IIR filter (e.g., the `filtfilt` function in MATLAB) used for offline ECG baseline wander removal. The output at time `n` is computed using samples at times `n-1, n, n+1, ...` (including future samples) because the *entire* recorded ECG signal is available in memory before filtering begins.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the BIBO stability criterion for an LTI system? Check whether the system `h[n] = (0.5)ⁿ u[n]` is BIBO stable.**

**Model Answer:**

**BIBO Stability Criterion for an LTI system:**

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n]\vert < \infty$$

**Check for `h[n] = (0.5)ⁿ u[n]`:**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert (0.5)^n \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n = \frac{1}{1-0.5} = 2 < \infty$$

Since the sum converges to a finite value `2`, the system is **BIBO stable**.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** When testing linearity, students often *only* test additivity and forget **homogeneity** — both must be tested. Likewise, time-invariance tests must hold for *every* `n₀`, not just one value. Marks are deducted for incomplete tests.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Question A:** *Determine whether the discrete-time systems defined below are (i) static or dynamic, (ii) causal or non-causal, (iii) linear or non-linear, (iv) time-invariant or time-variant, and (v) BIBO stable or unstable:*

**(a)** $y[n] = 2\,x[n] + 3$
**(b)** $y[n] = x[n^2]$ (Hint: treat `n²` as a function of time index)

*(7 marks for part (a), 7 marks for part (b))*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a): `y[n] = 2·x[n] + 3`

**(i) Static or Dynamic:** Output `y[n]` depends *only* on `x[n]`, not on any past or future sample.

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{STATIC (Memoryless)} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(ii) Causal or Non-Causal:** Uses only the *present* input `x[n]`. No future input is required.

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{CAUSAL} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(iii) Linear or Non-Linear:**
Apply input `a·x₁[n] + b·x₂[n]`:

$$T\{a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]\} = 2(a\,x_1[n]+b\,x_2[n]) + 3 = 2a\,x_1[n] + 2b\,x_2[n] + 3$$

Now apply the linearity check:

$$a\,T\{x_1[n]\} + b\,T\{x_2[n]\} = a(2x_1[n]+3) + b(2x_2[n]+3) = 2a\,x_1[n] + 2b\,x_2[n] + 3(a+b)$$

These are equal **only if** `3 = 3(a+b)`, which is not true for all `a, b`.

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{NON-LINEAR} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iv) Time-Invariant or Time-Variant:** The system has *no explicit dependence* on the time index `n` in its coefficients (constants `2` and `3` are time-independent). Therefore, the system is **time-invariant**.

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{TIME-INVARIANT} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(v) BIBO Stable or Unstable:** If `|x[n]| ≤ M_x`, then `|y[n]| = |2x[n]+3| ≤ 2M_x + 3 < ∞`. The output is bounded.

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{BIBO STABLE} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

> **Summary for (a):** Static, Causal, Non-Linear, Time-Invariant, BIBO Stable. *(A classic example of a "DC-offset adder" used in many DSP textbooks.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b): `y[n] = x[n²]`

**(i) Static or Dynamic:** The output at time `n` uses the input at time `n²`, which is a *different* time index for any `n ≠ 0, 1`. Hence the system has memory.

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{DYNAMIC} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(ii) Causal or Non-Causal:** For `n = 2`, `y[2] = x[4]`. This requires the **future** input sample `x[4]`. Hence the system is non-causal.

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{NON-CAUSAL} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(iii) Linear or Non-Linear:** Apply `T{a·x₁[n] + b·x₂[n]}`:

$$T\{a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]\} = a\,x_1[n^2] + b\,x_2[n^2] = a\,T\{x_1[n]\} + b\,T\{x_2[n]\}$$

Both additivity and homogeneity are satisfied.

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{LINEAR} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iv) Time-Invariant or Time-Variant:** Apply the shift test. Let `x'[n] = x[n-n₀]`. The output of the shifted-input system is:

$$y_1[n] = T\{x[n-n_0]\} = x[n^2 - n_0]$$

The shifted version of the original output is:

$$y_2[n] = y[n-n_0] = x[(n-n_0)^2] = x[n^2 - 2\,n\,n_0 + n_0^2]$$

Since `n² - n₀ ≠ (n-n₀)² = n² - 2nn₀ + n₀²` in general (e.g., try `n=2, n₀=1`):

$$y_1[2] = x[3] \neq x[1] = y_2[2]$$

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{TIME-VARIANT} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(v) BIBO Stable or Unstable:** If `|x[n]| ≤ M_x`, then for all `n`, `|y[n]| = |x[n²]| ≤ M_x < ∞`. The output is bounded.

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{BIBO STABLE} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

> **Summary for (b):** Dynamic, Non-Causal, Linear, Time-Variant, BIBO Stable. *(A "time-expander" — used in applications like speech pitch modification.)*

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**Question B:** *For each of the following LTI systems characterized by the impulse response `h[n]`:*

**(a)** $h[n] = (0.8)^n\,u[n]$
**(b)** $h[n] = (1.2)^n\,u[n]$

*Determine whether the system is BIBO stable. If stable, find the response `y[n]` to the input `x[n] = (0.5)^n\,u[n]` using the convolution sum.*

*(7 marks for part (a), 7 marks for part (b))*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a): `h[n] = (0.8)ⁿ u[n]`

**Step 1 — BIBO Stability Check:**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert (0.8)^n \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.8)^n = \frac{1}{1-0.8} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 < \infty$$

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{BIBO STABLE} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Output via Convolution Sum:**

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} h[k]\,x[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (0.8)^k (0.5)^{n-k}$$

**Step 3 — Factor Out the Constant `(0.5)ⁿ`:**

$$y[n] = (0.5)^n \sum_{k=0}^{n} \left(\frac{0.8}{0.5}\right)^k = (0.5)^n \sum_{k=0}^{n} (1.6)^k$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Finite Geometric Sum:**

Using $\sum_{k=0}^{n} r^k = \frac{1 - r^{n+1}}{1 - r}$ with $r = 1.6$:

$$y[n] = (0.5)^n \cdot \frac{1 - (1.6)^{n+1}}{1 - 1.6} = (0.5)^n \cdot \frac{(1.6)^{n+1} - 1}{0.6}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$y[n] = \frac{(0.5)^n (1.6)^{n+1}}{0.6} - \frac{(0.5)^n}{0.6} = \frac{1.6 \cdot (0.8)^n - (0.5)^n}{0.6}$$

$$\Rightarrow y[n] = \frac{8}{3}(0.8)^n - \frac{5}{3}(0.5)^n, \quad n \ge 0 \quad \text{[5 marks]}$$

> **Verification:** At `n=0`, `y[0] = 8/3 − 5/3 = 1`. Direct computation: `y[0] = h[0]·x[0] = 1·1 = 1`. ✓

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b): `h[n] = (1.2)ⁿ u[n]`

**Step 1 — BIBO Stability Check:**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert (1.2)^n \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (1.2)^n = \frac{1}{1-1.2} = \frac{1}{-0.2} = -5$$

The geometric series **diverges** to infinity (since `|1.2| > 1`).

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{NOT BIBO STABLE} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 2 — Output via Convolution Sum (Formal):**

The convolution sum expression is *valid* in form, but the resulting output is **unbounded** for any non-zero input.

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (1.2)^k (0.5)^{n-k} = (0.5)^n \sum_{k=0}^{n} (2.4)^k$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Finite Geometric Sum:**

$$y[n] = (0.5)^n \cdot \frac{1 - (2.4)^{n+1}}{1 - 2.4} = \frac{(0.5)^n \left[(2.4)^{n+1} - 1\right]}{1.4}$$

**Step 4 — Behaviour as `n → ∞`:**

Since `(2.4)^{n+1} → ∞` exponentially, `y[n] → ∞` for any non-zero input — confirming **instability**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{SYSTEM IS UNSTABLE — BIBO output diverges} \quad \text{[5 marks]}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** An impulse response whose magnitude exceeds 1 (e.g., `1.2ⁿ`) represents a **growing** exponential — typical of an unstable feedback system. Such systems are never used in long-duration ECG/EEG monitoring because small noise inputs can saturate the output.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark-Loss Hotspots**
> 1. **Forgetting the homogeneity test** in linearity (e.g., system `y[n] = 2x[n] + 3` looks linear at first glance but fails because of the constant `3`).
> 2. **Confusing "static" with "causal"** — every static system is causal, but not every causal system is static.
> 3. **Ignoring the upper limit** in the convolution sum — for causal systems with `u[n]`, the upper limit is `n`, not `∞`.
> 4. **BIBO stability for non-LTI systems** cannot be checked using the `Σ|h[n]|` formula — that test applies *only* to LTI systems.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist as your **last-night revision sheet** before any KTU 2024 ESE on `PBBRT504` Module 1.

- ☐ A **discrete-time system** maps `x[n] → y[n]` via an operator `T`.
- ☐ **Static** systems: `y[n] = f(x[n])` only — no memory.
- ☐ **Dynamic** systems: `y[n]` depends on `x[n-k]` for some `k ≠ 0` — has memory.
- ☐ **Time-invariant** systems: `T{x[n−n₀]} = y[n−n₀]` for **all** `n₀`.
- ☐ **Time-variant** systems: fail the TI test for at least one `n₀`.
- ☐ **Linear** systems obey **superposition**: `T{ax₁ + bx₂} = aT{x₁} + bT{x₂}` (test both additivity **and** homogeneity).
- ☐ **Causal** systems: `y[n]` uses only `x[k]` for `k ≤ n`.
- ☐ **Non-causal** systems: need future inputs — valid only in **offline** processing.
- ☐ **BIBO Stable** LTI systems satisfy: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n]\vert < \infty$.
- ☐ All **FIR** filters are BIBO stable; **IIR** filters require an explicit check.
- ☐ **LTI** systems are completely characterized by their impulse response `h[n]`.
- ☐ Convolution sum: $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} h[k]\,x[n-k]$.
- ☐ Geometric sum: $\sum_{k=0}^{N} r^k = \dfrac{1 - r^{N+1}}{1 - r}$.
- ☐ In ECG/EEG processing: causal + LTI + BIBO stable + FIR = the **gold-standard** filter design.
- ☐ In KTU 2024 exam: always present answers in a **classification table** (Property | Result) for full marks.

---

> **End of Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PBBRT504 | Module 1 | Discrete-Time Systems Classification**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** is the fundamental frequency-domain representation of a finite-energy, aperiodic **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$. It expresses the signal as a continuous, complex-valued function of a *normalized digital angular frequency* $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ (or $\Omega \in [-\pi/T_s, \pi/T_s]$ when expressed in rad/sec).

It serves as the **bridge** between the discrete-time domain $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ and the continuous-frequency (periodic) domain $\omega$, and is the mathematical foundation upon which the practical **DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform)** and **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)** algorithms are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "The DTFT of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as the sum of the signal multiplied by a complex exponential $e^{-j\omega n}$, taken over all integer time indices $n = -\infty$ to $+\infty$, producing a continuous function $X(e^{j\omega})$ of the angular frequency $\omega$."

### 1.2 The DTFT Pair (Analysis & Synthesis Equations)

The DTFT is conventionally expressed as a **transform pair**:

**Forward (Analysis) Equation:**

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}
$$

**Inverse (Synthesis) Equation:**

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Convention:** The DTFT output is written as $X(e^{j\omega})$ (not $X(\omega)$) to remind us that the spectrum is **inherently periodic** with period $2\pi$ in $\omega$. This is a direct consequence of the discrete nature of the time index $n$.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Prism of Digital Signals" 🌈

Imagine you are looking at a **white beam of light** — that beam is your discrete biomedical signal $x[n]$ (say, a 1-second segment of an ECG recording sampled at 360 Hz). You cannot, by eye, tell what "frequencies" of cardiac activity are inside it.

Now pass that white beam through a **prism**. Out comes a smooth, continuous **rainbow spectrum** — that rainbow is precisely the DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$.

- The **input** is a discrete-time signal (samples on a number line).
- The **prism** is the mathematical operation $\sum_{n} x[n]e^{-j\omega n}$.
- The **output** is a continuous, complex-valued spectrum revealing the amplitude and phase of every frequency component from $-\pi$ to $\pi$.

Just as the prism cannot give you only "red" or "blue" — it always gives the *entire* continuous spectrum — the DTFT cannot give you a "discrete set of bins." That requires a *further* step: **sampling the spectrum**, which is exactly what the **DFT** does.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** DTFT = **C**ontinuous spectrum from **D**iscrete-time signal. DFT = **D**iscrete spectrum from **D**iscrete-time signal. The "missing letter" in DTFT is *Discrete output*.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Biomedical Context

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Biomedical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Frequency $F_s$** | **$\geq$ 2 × highest signal frequency (Nyquist)** | ECG: 360–1000 Hz; EEG: 256–512 Hz; EMG: 1000–2000 Hz |
| **Normalized Frequency $\omega$** | **Radians per sample, range $[-\pi, \pi]$** | Dimensionless, signal-independent |
| **Absolute Frequency $f$** | **Hz, range $[-F_s/2, F_s/2]$** | Maps to $\omega = 2\pi f / F_s$ |
| **DTFT Periodicity** | **$2\pi$ in $\omega$** | Spectrum repeats every $2\pi$ |
| **Parseval's Energy** | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$ | Signal power preservation |

---

## 1.5 Why DTFT Matters in Biomedical Signal Processing

1. **Spectral Analysis of ECG:** Identifying QRS complex harmonics, detecting ST-segment abnormalities via power spectral density.
2. **EEG Band Analysis:** Decomposing brain waves into Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (13–30 Hz), Gamma (>30 Hz) bands.
3. **Filter Design Foundation:** FIR and IIR digital filter frequency responses $H(e^{j\omega})$ are direct DTFT evaluations.
4. **Theoretical Backbone:** Establishes sampling theory, aliasing, and reconstruction mathematics used in every biomedical instrument.

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for the Existence of the DTFT

A discrete-time signal $x[n]$ has a DTFT if and only if it satisfies the **absolute summability condition** (Dirichlet condition, sufficient but not necessary):

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty \quad \text{(Absolutely Summable)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Other existence conditions (broader set):**
> - $x[n]$ has **finite energy**: $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 < \infty$ (square-summable).
> - $x[n]$ is **periodic** (DTFT exists as a train of impulses — Dirac comb).
> - $x[n]$ is of the form $a^n u[n]$ with $\vert a \vert < 1$ (causal decaying exponential).

The **Dirichlet conditions** (necessary conditions for the pointwise convergence of the inverse DTFT):
1. $x[n]$ must be absolutely summable.
2. $x[n]$ must have a **finite number of maxima and minima** within any finite interval.
3. $x[n]$ must have a **finite number of discontinuities** within any finite interval.

---

## 2.2 Structural Breakdown of the DTFT Operation

The forward equation
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$
can be understood as a **bank of correlators**:

- The complex exponential $e^{-j\omega n} = \cos(\omega n) - j\sin(\omega n)$ acts as a **reference sinusoid** of normalized frequency $\omega$.
- The summation computes the **inner product** (correlation) of $x[n]$ with this reference at frequency $\omega$.
- As $\omega$ varies *continuously* from $-\pi$ to $\pi$, we obtain a *continuous* correlation profile — the spectrum.

> [!TIP]
> **Geometric Intuition:** Each complex value $X(e^{j\omega})$ has a magnitude $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ and a phase $\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = \arg\{X(e^{j\omega})\}$. The magnitude spectrum shows **how much energy** exists at frequency $\omega$, while the phase spectrum shows **temporal alignment** of those frequency components.

---

## 2.3 Periodicity Property of the DTFT

Since $e^{-j(\omega + 2\pi k) n} = e^{-j\omega n} \cdot e^{-j2\pi k n} = e^{-j\omega n} \cdot 1$ (because $e^{-j2\pi k n} = 1$ for integer $k$ and $n$), it follows that:

$$
X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi k)}) = X(e^{j\omega}) \quad \text{for all integers } k
$$

Thus, the DTFT is **always periodic with period $2\pi$** in the normalized frequency $\omega$. This is the *mirror image* of the time-domain aliasing theorem and is fundamental to the sampling theorem.

---

## 2.4 Relation to the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)

If $x(t)$ is a continuous-time signal with CTFT $X_c(j\Omega)$, and $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ is obtained by ideal impulse-train sampling at rate $F_s = 1/T_s$, then:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c\!\left(j\frac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T_s}\right)
$$

The DTFT is a **scaled, periodically replicated** version of the sampled CTFT, providing the mathematical bridge between continuous and discrete domains.

---

## 2.5 🌟 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Key Exam Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DTFT (Forward)** | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ | Infinite sum, complex output |
| 2 | **Inverse DTFT (IDTFT)** | $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$ | Integral over one period |
| 3 | **Periodicity** | $X(e^{j(\omega+2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$ | Always periodic, period $2\pi$ |
| 4 | **Existence Condition** | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ | Absolute summability |
| 5 | **Magnitude Spectrum** | $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ | Even function of $\omega$ if $x[n]$ real |
| 6 | **Phase Spectrum** | $\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = \arg\{X(e^{j\omega})\}$ | Odd function of $\omega$ if $x[n]$ real |
| 7 | **Linearity** | $\text{DTFT}\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a X_1(e^{j\omega}) + b X_2(e^{j\omega})$ | Superposition holds |
| 8 | **Time Reversal** | $\text{DTFT}\{x[-n]\} = X(e^{-j\omega})$ | Spectrum mirrored |
| 9 | **Time Shift** | $\text{DTFT}\{x[n-n_0]\} = e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$ | Linear phase factor |
| 10 | **Frequency Shift** | $\text{DTFT}\{e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n]\} = X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$ | Modulation property |
| 11 | **Parseval's Theorem** | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$ | Energy conservation |
| 12 | **Convolution Theorem** | $\text{DTFT}\{x[n] * h[n]\} = X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H(e^{j\omega})$ | Time-domain convolution $\to$ frequency multiplication |
| 13 | **Multiplication Theorem** | $\text{DTFT}\{x[n] \cdot y[n]\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega-\theta)}) d\theta$ | Periodic convolution |
| 14 | **Frequency-Frequency Mapping** | $\omega = 2\pi f / F_s$ | Conversion rad/sample $\leftrightarrow$ Hz |
| 15 | **DTFT of $\delta[n]$** | $1$ (constant spectrum) | All frequencies equally present |
| 16 | **DTFT of $u[n]$** | $\frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \sum_{k} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$ | Contains impulse train |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG QRS Detection:** Matched filtering in frequency domain uses $H(e^{j\omega})$ — the DTFT of the QRS template.
- **Hearing Aids & Cochlear Implants:** Spectral shaping via FIR filters designed using DTFT magnitude specifications.
- **MRI / fMRI:** k-space data interpretation leverages 2-D extensions of DTFT.
- **Patient Monitoring Alarms:** PSD (Power Spectral Density) computed via DTFT modulus squared detects arrhythmia signatures.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — DTFT from the Continuous-Time Fourier Series of a Sampled Signal

### Step 1: Start with a continuous-time, periodic signal
Let $x_p(t)$ be a continuous-time periodic signal with period $T_p$. Its **Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS)** is:

$$
x_p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, e^{j k \omega_0 t}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_p}
$$

where the Fourier coefficients are given by:

$$
a_k = \frac{1}{T_p} \int_{T_p} x_p(t) \, e^{-j k \omega_0 t} \, dt
$$

### Step 2: Sample $x_p(t)$ to get a discrete-time signal
Let us sample $x_p(t)$ at times $t = nT_s$, where $T_s$ is the sampling period. Define:

$$
x[n] = x_p(nT_s) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, e^{j k \omega_0 n T_s}
$$

### Step 3: Recognize the discrete-time structure
The exponential $e^{jk\omega_0 nT_s}$ depends only on the *product* $k \cdot \omega_0 T_s$. Define a new angular parameter:

$$
\omega = k \omega_0 T_s = k \cdot \frac{2\pi T_s}{T_p}
$$

So we can rewrite the sampled signal as:

$$
x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, e^{j \omega n}
$$

### Step 4: Transition to aperiodic discrete-time signal via the Fourier Transform
As the period $T_p \to \infty$, the spacing between adjacent spectral lines $\Delta\omega = 2\pi T_s / T_p \to 0$, and the discrete index $k$ becomes a continuous variable. The Fourier coefficients $a_k$ become a continuous function $X(e^{j\omega})$, and the discrete sum over $k$ becomes an integral over $\omega$:

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega
$$

This is the **Inverse DTFT (Synthesis) Equation**. ✓

### Step 5: Recover the forward equation
By the orthogonality of complex exponentials (using $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} e^{j\omega(n-m)} d\omega = \delta[n-m]$), we can project:

$$
\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega m} d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \sum_{k} a_k e^{j\omega(n-k)} d\omega = \sum_{k} a_k \delta[n-k] = x[n]
$$

Generalizing to arbitrary $x[n]$ and replacing $a_k$ with the continuous spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}
$$

This is the **Forward DTFT (Analysis) Equation**. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The DTFT is the natural limit of the CTFS as the period tends to infinity, applied to a discrete-time sequence.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Inverse DTFT via Orthogonality Principle

### Step 1: Assume the synthesis equation is valid
Assume that any $x[n]$ can be reconstructed as:

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the forward equation into the synthesis equation
Substitute $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] e^{-j\omega m}$ into the RHS:

$$
\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \left( \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] e^{-j\omega m} \right) e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega
$$

### Step 3: Interchange the order of summation and integration
Assuming uniform convergence, swap $\sum$ and $\int$:

$$
= \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} e^{j\omega (n - m)} d\omega
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the inner integral
The integral $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} e^{j\omega(n-m)} d\omega$ is the **Kronecker delta** in discrete time:

$$
\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} e^{j\omega(n-m)} d\omega = \delta[n-m]
$$

This is a well-known orthogonality identity derivable by direct integration:
- If $n = m$: integrand is $1$, integral is $\frac{1}{2\pi}(2\pi) = 1$.
- If $n \neq m$: integrand is $\cos[\omega(n-m)] + j\sin[\omega(n-m)]$; both integrate to zero over a symmetric interval.

### Step 5: Conclude
Substituting back:

$$
= \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \cdot \delta[n-m] = x[n] \quad \checkmark
$$

This **proves the inverse DTFT** reconstructs the original signal exactly. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — DTFT of a Causal Exponential Sequence (Worked Example)

**Given:** $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ with $\vert a \vert < 1$, where $u[n]$ is the unit step.

### Step 1: Apply the forward DTFT
$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^n u[n] \, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^n
$$

### Step 2: Recognize a geometric series
This is a sum of the form $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \frac{1}{1-r}$ provided $\vert r \vert < 1$.

Here $r = a e^{-j\omega}$, and $\vert r \vert = \vert a \vert < 1$ (given condition). Thus the series converges.

### Step 3: Sum the geometric series
$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}
$$

### Step 4: Express in rectangular form (real and imaginary parts)
Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator $(1 - a e^{j\omega})$:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - a e^{j\omega}}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})(1 - a e^{j\omega})}
$$

Compute the denominator:
$$
(1 - a e^{-j\omega})(1 - a e^{j\omega}) = 1 - a(e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}) + a^2 = 1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2
$$

Expand numerator using Euler's identity $e^{j\omega} = \cos\omega + j\sin\omega$:
$$
1 - a(\cos\omega + j\sin\omega) = (1 - a\cos\omega) - j a\sin\omega
$$

Therefore:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{(1 - a\cos\omega) - j a\sin\omega}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}
$$

### Step 5: Magnitude and phase spectra
$$
\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}}
$$

$$
\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = -\arctan\!\left(\frac{a\sin\omega}{1 - a\cos\omega}\right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** When $a$ is real and positive, $\angle X(e^{j\omega}) < 0$ for $\omega \in (0, \pi)$, confirming that the causal exponential introduces **negative phase lag** — equivalent to a low-pass filter response.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DTFT of Common Biomedical-Relevant Signals

| Signal $x[n]$ | DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ | Magnitude Character | Biomedical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta[n]$ | $1$ | Flat, unity | Calibration, impulse response |
| $u[n]$ (unit step) | $\frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \sum_{k} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$ | Has DC impulse | Step response of filters |
| $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ | $2\pi \sum_{k} \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k)$ | Impulse train at $\omega_0$ | Pure sinusoid detection |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ | $\pi[\sum_k \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi k)]$ | Two impulses | Sinusoidal biomarkers (e.g., pacemaker) |
| $a^n u[n], \vert a \vert < 1$ | $\frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ | Low-pass shape | Decaying envelope signals |
| $\text{rect}_N[n]$ (length-$N$ rect) | $\frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$ | Dirichlet kernel | Windowed signal analysis |
| $\frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$ | Ideal lowpass, bandlimit $\omega_c$ | Rectangular band | Brick-wall filter, ideal LPF |

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DTFT as a Frequency Analyzer

The following Mermaid block-diagram depicts how a biomedical signal flows through the DTFT computational pipeline, from the patient transducer to a clinical spectral plot.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Physiological Source] --> B[Analog Biosignal e.g. ECG EEG EMG]
    B --> C[Anti-Aliasing Analog LPF]
    C --> D[ADC Sampler at Fs Hz]
    D --> E[Discrete Sequence x of n]
    E --> F[Windowing e.g. Hamming]
    F --> G[DTFT Analyzer Complex Correlator Bank]
    G --> H[Spectrum X of e raised to j omega]
    H --> I[Magnitude Plot]
    H --> J[Phase Plot]
    H --> K[Power Spectral Density]
    I --> L[Clinical Interpretation]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The left side is the **physical-to-digital conversion chain**.
- The right side is the **frequency-domain representation output**.
- The central block "DTFT Analyzer" is conceptually implemented as a **bank of complex correlators**, each tuned to a different $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: Time-Frequency Duality of DTFT

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the *bidirectional* nature of the DTFT pair and the conceptual relationship between the time-domain sequence and the periodic continuous spectrum.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD[Time Domain]
        T1[x of n - Discrete Samples on Integer Grid]
    end
    subgraph FD[Frequency Domain]
        F1[X of e raised to j omega - Continuous Periodic Function]
        F2[Period 2 pi in omega]
        F3[Complex valued magnitude and phase]
    end
    T1 -->|Forward DTFT - Summation over n| F1
    F1 -->|Inverse DTFT - Integration over one period| T1
    F1 --- F2
    F1 --- F3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** The time domain is *discrete and (typically) aperiodic*; the frequency domain is *continuous and periodic*. This duality is the central fact of discrete-time signal processing.

---

## 4.3 Process Flow: From DTFT Definition to Practical DFT Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define x of n over all integers n] --> S2[Step 2: Apply Forward DTFT - Infinite Sum]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Obtain Continuous Spectrum X of e raised to j omega]
    S3 --> S4{Step 4: Need Practical Computation}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Step 5: Truncate x of n to N samples - windowing]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Sample X of e raised to j omega at N equally spaced omega values]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Apply DFT Equation - O of N squared or FFT O of N log N]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Obtain Practical Spectral Estimate X of k]
    S4 -->|No theoretical study only| S9[Stay in DTFT domain - theoretical analysis]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Connection to the next module:** This flowchart previews the **DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform)** and **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)**, which are the computable realizations of the DTFT — to be covered in Module 2's next subtopic.

---

## 4.4 Frequency-Domain Interpretation in Biomedical Context

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLIN[Clinical Frequency Bands]
        B1[Delta: 0.5 - 4 Hz - Deep Sleep]
        B2[Theta: 4 - 8 Hz - Drowsiness]
        B3[Alpha: 8 - 13 Hz - Relaxed Wakefulness]
        B4[Beta: 13 - 30 Hz - Active Thinking]
        B5[Gamma: above 30 Hz - Cognitive Binding]
    end
    X[DTFT Spectrum X of e raised to j omega] --> CLIN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Insight:** The DTFT provides a *mathematically exact* view of how power is distributed across these clinically meaningful frequency bands — a task performed daily in EEG-based neurological diagnostics and seizure detection.

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT). State the conditions for its existence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The DTFT of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}
$$

where $\omega$ is the normalized angular frequency in radians per sample. The inverse DTFT is:

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega
$$

**Conditions for existence (sufficient):** The signal $x[n]$ must be **absolutely summable**, i.e., $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$. More generally, the signal may have finite energy ($\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 < \infty$) or be of an exponentially decaying form (e.g., $a^n u[n]$ with $\vert a \vert < 1$).

*(1 mark for forward eq., 1 mark for inverse eq., 1 mark for existence condition.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Show that the DTFT of a discrete-time signal is periodic with period $2\pi$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

We compute $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)})$:

$$
X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n} \cdot e^{-j2\pi n}
$$

Since $n$ is an integer, $e^{-j2\pi n} = \cos(2\pi n) - j\sin(2\pi n) = 1 - j0 = 1$.

Therefore:

$$
X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n} = X(e^{j\omega})
$$

This holds for all integer shifts, proving periodicity with period $2\pi$. ✓

*(2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📝 **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Define the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of a sequence $x[n]$ and its inverse. **Derive the relationship between the DTFT and the Continuous-Time Fourier Series of the sampled signal.** (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the DTFT of the causal exponential sequence $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$. Sketch its magnitude spectrum. **Discuss the biomedical relevance of such a signal's spectrum.** (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
The DTFT pair is:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}, \qquad x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega
$$

**Step 2 — Start with CTFS of a continuous periodic signal (2 marks):**
A continuous-time periodic signal $x_p(t)$ with period $T_p$ has the Fourier series:

$$
x_p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k e^{j k \omega_0 t}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Sample at $t = nT_s$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
x[n] = x_p(nT_s) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k e^{j k \omega_0 n T_s}
$$

Define $\omega_k = k \omega_0 T_s$. As $T_p \to \infty$, $\omega_k$ becomes a continuous variable $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ (with $T_s = 1$), and the discrete sum over $k$ becomes an integral over $\omega$. The Fourier coefficients $a_k$ become a continuous function $X(e^{j\omega})/T_s$.

**Step 4 — Recover IDTFT (1.5 marks):**

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega
$$

This establishes that the DTFT is the natural continuous-frequency limit of the discrete-time Fourier series. ✓

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply forward DTFT (1.5 marks):**

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5 e^{-j\omega})^n
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the geometric series (1.5 marks):**
Since $\vert 0.5 e^{-j\omega} \vert = 0.5 < 1$, the series converges:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in rectangular form (2 marks):**

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - 0.5\cos\omega + j\, 0.5\sin\omega}{1 - \cos\omega + 0.25} = \frac{(1 - 0.5\cos\omega) + j\, 0.5\sin\omega}{1.25 - \cos\omega}
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude and phase (1 mark):**

$$
\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}}, \quad \angle X(e^{j\omega}) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.5\sin\omega}{1 - 0.5\cos\omega}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Biomedical relevance (1 mark):**
- Magnitude is **maximum at $\omega = 0$** (DC) and decays monotonically — a **low-pass characteristic**.
- In biomedical terms, this represents the spectrum of a signal whose energy is concentrated at low frequencies (e.g., baseline drift in ECG, slow EEG rhythms). Such spectra are used to design **low-pass digital filters** to remove high-frequency noise (e.g., 50/60 Hz powerline interference and EMG artifacts in EEG).

**Sketch of $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$:**

| $\omega$ | $0$ | $\pm\pi/2$ | $\pm\pi$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.25}} = 2.0$ | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1.25}} \approx 0.894$ | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2.25}} \approx 0.667$ |

The spectrum is **bell-shaped**, peaking at DC — a classic **low-pass** signature. ✓

---

### 📝 **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected]*

**(a)** State and prove the **Parseval's theorem** for DTFT. Use it to compute the energy of $x[n] = (0.8)^n u[n]$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define the DTFT. **For a finite-duration rectangular pulse $x[n] = 1$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$ and $0$ otherwise, derive the DTFT and discuss its biomedical application as a window function.** (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Statement of Parseval's theorem (1 mark):**

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega
$$

**Step 2 — Proof (4 marks):**
Start with the inverse DTFT:

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega
$$

Take the complex conjugate:

$$
x^*[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X^*(e^{j\omega}) e^{-j\omega n} d\omega
$$

Multiply $x[n] \cdot x^*[n] = \vert x[n] \vert^2$ and sum over $n$:

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X^*(e^{j\omega}) e^{-j\omega n} d\omega
$$

Interchange sum and integral:

$$
= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X^*(e^{j\omega}) \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n} \right] d\omega
$$

Recognize that the bracketed term is $X(e^{j\omega})$:

$$
= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X^*(e^{j\omega}) X(e^{j\omega}) d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to $x[n] = (0.8)^n u[n]$ (2 marks):**
The energy is:

$$
E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.8)^{2n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.64)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.64} = \frac{1}{0.36} \approx 2.778
$$

The signal has finite energy as required. ✓

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — DTFT definition (1 mark)** (as in earlier questions).

**Step 2 — Derive DTFT of rectangular pulse (3 marks):**

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (1) e^{-j\omega n} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega N/2} / e^{j\omega/2}$:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{-j\omega N/2}(e^{j\omega N/2} - e^{-j\omega N/2})}{e^{-j\omega/2}(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2})} = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude and phase (1 mark):**

$$
\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert \quad \text{(Dirichlet kernel)}
$$

$$
\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = -\frac{\omega(N-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Biomedical application as window function (2 marks):**
- The rectangular window is the simplest finite-segment extraction tool used to isolate a chunk of a long biomedical recording (e.g., a 1-second ECG segment) for spectral analysis.
- Its DTFT (Dirichlet kernel) exhibits **spectral leakage** — energy from the main lobe spills into side lobes. In ECG analysis, this leakage can mask weak components (e.g., late potentials). Hence, **windowing functions like Hamming, Hanning, or Blackman** — which have lower side-lobe levels — are preferred in practice.
- Used in the **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** for time-frequency analysis of non-stationary biomedical signals like phonocardiograms and seizure EEG.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/(2\pi)$ factor** in the inverse DTFT equation — a single point deduction (often 0.5–1 mark) every time it is missing.
> 2. **Writing $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}$ instead of $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}$** in the forward DTFT — confuses the *general* definition with a *truncated* computation. Always state the full range in the definition.
> 3. **Claiming DTFT exists for all signals** — it does not; specifically, periodic signals (like $\cos(\omega_0 n)$) have DTFTs that are *impulse trains*, requiring careful handling.
> 4. **Confusing DTFT with DFT:** DTFT output is *continuous in $\omega$*; DFT output is *discrete* (sampled at $N$ points). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Not mentioning periodicity** of $X(e^{j\omega})$ — a direct consequence of discrete-time sampling. Marks are reserved for this.
> 6. **Skipping the existence condition** in definition-type questions — at least one mark is dedicated to stating it.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DTFT** is the **continuous-frequency** representation of a **discrete-time** signal.
- **Forward eq.:** $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$
- **Inverse eq.:** $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$
- The output $X(e^{j\omega})$ is **always periodic with period $2\pi$** in $\omega$.
- **Existence** requires absolute (or square) summability; the geometric series formula requires $\vert a \vert < 1$.
- The DTFT is **complex-valued**; interpret via magnitude $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ and phase $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$.
- For real $x[n]$: magnitude spectrum is **even**, phase spectrum is **odd**.
- **Linearity, time shift, frequency shift, convolution, and Parseval's theorem** are the five most-tested DTFT properties in KTU exams.
- The DTFT is the **theoretical foundation** of the **DFT** — sampled in frequency at $N$ points, the DTFT becomes the DFT.
- In biomedical applications, the DTFT is the bedrock of **ECG spectral analysis, EEG band decomposition, digital filter design, and PSD estimation**.
- The integration interval in the inverse DTFT is always **one period**, i.e., $[-\pi, \pi]$ or $[0, 2\pi]$ — never an arbitrary range.
- Normalized frequency $\omega$ (rad/sample) relates to absolute frequency $f$ (Hz) by $\omega = 2\pi f / F_s$.
- DTFT of $a^n u[n]$: $\frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$, with magnitude $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}}$ — a low-pass shape for real positive $a$.
- DTFT of $\delta[n]$: $1$ (flat spectrum — all frequencies present equally).
- DTFT of $u[n]$: contains an **impulse train** at $\omega = 0, \pm 2\pi, \dots$ plus a smooth term.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of System Properties

In **Biomedical Signal Processing**, when we analyze a discrete-time system $T\{.\}$ that maps an input signal $x[n]$ to an output signal $y[n]$, we must classify this system according to a set of mathematically rigorous properties. These properties determine whether the system can be practically implemented in real-time hardware (like an ECG monitor or EEG amplifier), whether it will produce bounded outputs for bounded physiological inputs, and whether its behavior can be predicted and analyzed.

A system is described formally as an operator:

$$T\{x[n]\} = y[n]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The six core properties tested in Module 2 are **Linearity**, **Time-Invariance**, **Causality**, **Stability (BIBO)**, **Memory**, and **Invertibility**. Each property has a direct engineering consequence in biomedical instrumentation.

The six fundamental properties are:

| # | Property | Core Question Asked |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Linearity | Does it obey superposition? |
| 2 | Time-Invariance | Is its behavior shift-independent? |
| 3 | Causality | Does it depend only on past/present? |
| 4 | Stability (BIBO) | Is every bounded input → bounded output? |
| 5 | Memory | Does the present output depend on past/future inputs? |
| 6 | Invertibility | Can the original input be uniquely recovered? |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Vitals Monitor:**
> Imagine a bedside patient monitor that displays heart rate. Think of each property as a rule the monitor must obey:
>
> - **Linearity** → If you double the heart rate input, the displayed value doubles. The monitor is "fair" and doesn't suddenly saturate or distort.
> - **Time-Invariance** → The monitor behaves identically at 8 AM and 8 PM. Its response doesn't change with the clock.
> - **Causality** → The monitor never displays a heart rate spike that "hasn't happened yet." It cannot see the future.
> - **Stability** → If the patient's heart rate stays between 40–200 bpm, the monitor's displayed value also stays within a sensible range. It never shoots to infinity.
> - **Memory** → A simple meter has no memory. An average heart rate calculator over the last 5 minutes has memory.
> - **Invertibility** → Given the displayed number, the nurse can deduce the original heart rate. No two heart rates ever produce the same display reading.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Property Testing:**
> - **Bounded Input:** $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x < \infty$ for all $n$
> - **Bounded Output:** $\vert y[n] \vert \leq M_y < \infty$ for all $n$
> - **Sampling Rate for ECG:** Typically **360 Hz** (clinical standard)
> - **Sampling Rate for EEG:** Typically **256 Hz** or **512 Hz**

These properties are not just theoretical constructs — they directly determine whether a digital filter designed to remove **50 Hz powerline interference** from an ECG signal will work safely on a real patient without causing the system to oscillate or hang.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Core Properties — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Linearity (Superposition Principle)

A system $T$ is **linear** if and only if it satisfies both **additivity** and **homogeneity (scaling)** simultaneously.

**Additivity Test:**
$$T\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = T\{x_1[n]\} + T\{x_2[n]\}$$

**Homogeneity Test (Scaling):**
$$T\{a \cdot x[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x[n]\}$$

**Combined Linearity Test:**
$$T\{a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary constants (real or complex).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linearity allows us to use **Fourier Transform, Z-Transform, and Convolution** as analytical tools. Without linearity, almost every signal processing tool we use (filters, spectrum analysis) would fail.

---

### 2.1.2 Time-Invariance (Shift-Invariance)

A system is **time-invariant** if a time shift in the input produces an identical time shift in the output. In biomedical systems, this means the system should not "age" or change its behavior.

**Mathematical Definition:**
$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$$

**Test Procedure:**
1. Shift the input by $n_0$ → compute $T\{x[n-n_0]\}$.
2. Shift the output by $n_0$ → compute $y[n-n_0]$.
3. If both expressions are identical for **all** $n_0$, the system is time-invariant.

---

### 2.1.3 Causality

A system is **causal** if the output at any time $n$ depends **only** on present and past input values, never on future inputs.

**Mathematical Definition:**
$$y[n] = T\{x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots\}$$

The output $y[n]$ must **not** depend on $x[n+1], x[n+2], \ldots$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in Biomedical Engineering:** Real-time patient monitoring systems (heart rate, blood pressure) **must** be causal. A non-causal system would require knowledge of the patient's future heartbeat, which is physically impossible.

---

### 2.1.4 Stability (BIBO — Bounded-Input Bounded-Output)

A system is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output.

**Formal Definition:**
$$\text{If } \vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x < \infty \text{ for all } n,$$
$$\text{then } \vert y[n] \vert \leq M_y < \infty \text{ for all } n$$

**For LTI Systems (Impulse Response Criterion):**
$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

This is the **absolute summability condition** of the impulse response.

---

### 2.1.5 Memory (Static vs Dynamic)

- **Memoryless (Static) System:** Output at time $n$ depends only on the input at the **same** time $n$.
$$y[n] = f(x[n])$$
Example: $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ — a square-law detector.

- **System with Memory (Dynamic):** Output depends on past or future inputs.
$$y[n] = x[n] + 0.5 \cdot x[n-1]$$
Example: A moving average filter for EMG noise smoothing.

---

### 2.1.6 Invertibility

A system is **invertible** if distinct inputs produce distinct outputs. There must exist an **inverse system** $T^{-1}$ such that:
$$T^{-1}\{T\{x[n]\}\} = x[n]$$

**In biomedical context:** If you apply an invertible transformation (e.g., a linear phase filter), you should be able to recover the original ECG signal without permanent information loss.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Test Condition | Linear System Form | LTI Impulse Response Form |
|----------|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Linearity | $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}$ | Holds for all $a,b$ | N/A (separate property) |
| Time-Invariance | $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = y[n-n_0]$ for all $n_0$ | Coefficients constant in time | $h[n]$ does not shift with $n$ |
| Causality | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Right-sided impulse response | One-sided response |
| Stability (BIBO) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Absolutely summable | $h[n] \in \ell^1$ |
| Memory | $y[n]$ depends on $x[n]$ only | $h[0]$ non-zero, others zero | $h[n] = 0$ for $n \neq 0$ |
| Invertibility | $\exists T^{-1} \cdot T = I$ | $H(z) \cdot H^{-1}(z) = 1$ | No zeros on unit circle |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these properties show up in KTU-affiliated industries in Kerala (Kerala Med-Tech & Signal Labs):**
>
> 1. **ECG Filtering (Mortara, Philips):** BIBO stability is mandatory so that a noisy electrode never causes the display to hang at infinity.
> 2. **Cochlear Implant DSP (Cochlear Ltd, MED-EL):** Causality is non-negotiable — the implant cannot wait for a future audio sample to reproduce sound.
> 3. **MRI Reconstruction (GE, Siemens, Kochi-based Mediscan):** Invertibility ensures the reconstructed image is faithful to the patient's actual anatomy.
> 4. **EEG Analysis for Epilepsy (Amrita Institute, Trivandrum):** Time-invariance is assumed when using matched filters that look for spike-wave patterns regardless of when they occur in the recording.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Key Property Test Conditions

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Causality Condition for LTI Systems

**Given:** An LTI system characterized by its impulse response $h[n]$ and input-output relation:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

**Step 1:** Decompose the convolution sum into three parts: past ($k>0$), present ($k=0$), and future ($k<0$).

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k] + h[0] \cdot x[n] + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

**Step 2:** Identify the future term. The terms with $k < 0$ mean $x[n-k]$ with $n-k > n$, i.e., **future** inputs.

**Step 3:** Apply the causality constraint. The system is causal only if the future term vanishes:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k] = 0 \quad \forall n, \forall x$$

**Step 4:** Since $x[\cdot]$ can be any arbitrary signal, the only way this holds is if the coefficients themselves are zero:

$$h[k] = 0 \quad \text{for all } k < 0$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the convolution sum reduces to a right-sided sum (one-sided):

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** For an LTI system, **causality is equivalent to $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$** (right-sided impulse response).

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the BIBO Stability Condition

**Given:** Bounded input: $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x$ for all $n$.

**Step 1:** Take the absolute value of the output of a general LTI system:

$$\vert y[n] \vert = \left\vert \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \cdot x[n-k] \right\vert$$

**Step 2:** Apply the triangle inequality $\vert a+b \vert \leq \vert a \vert + \vert b \vert$:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \cdot x[n-k] \vert$$

**Step 3:** Separate the product of absolute values:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert \cdot \vert x[n-k] \vert$$

**Step 4:** Apply the bounded input condition $\vert x[n-k] \vert \leq M_x$:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq M_x \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert$$

**Step 5:** For $y[n]$ to be bounded for **every** bounded input, the summation must be finite:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert < \infty$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, the output is bounded by:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq M_x \cdot S_h < \infty \quad \text{where } S_h = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** An LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if its impulse response is **absolutely summable** (i.e., belongs to the $\ell^1$ space).

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Invertibility for LTI Systems

**Given:** Two distinct inputs $x_1[n] \neq x_2[n]$ must produce two distinct outputs $y_1[n] \neq y_2[n]$.

**Step 1:** Consider the difference signal $\Delta x[n] = x_1[n] - x_2[n] \neq 0$.

**Step 2:** By linearity, the output difference is:
$$\Delta y[n] = T\{x_1[n]\} - T\{x_2[n]\} = T\{\Delta x[n]\}$$

**Step 3:** If $T$ is LTI, then:
$$\Delta y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \cdot \Delta x[n-k]$$

**Step 4:** For invertibility, $\Delta x[n] \neq 0 \implies \Delta y[n] \neq 0$.

**Step 5:** This requires $h[n]$ to be such that no non-zero $\Delta x[n]$ maps to a zero output. In Z-domain, this is:

$$H(z) \cdot H^{-1}(z) = 1$$

**Step 6:** The inverse system exists if and only if $H(z)$ has **no zeros on the unit circle** of the Z-plane. Otherwise, certain frequency components would be annihilated and cannot be recovered.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Classifying a Biomedical Filter

**Problem:** Consider the digital smoothing filter used for an EMG signal:
$$y[n] = 0.5 x[n] + 0.3 x[n-1] + 0.2 x[n-2]$$

**Step 1 — Linearity Test:**
Each term is a linear function of $x$. The sum of linear operations is linear.
Therefore, the system is **LINEAR**. ✓

**Step 2 — Time-Invariance Test:**
Compute $T\{x[n-n_0]\}$:
$$T\{x[n-n_0]\} = 0.5 x[n-n_0] + 0.3 x[n-n_0-1] + 0.2 x[n-n_0-2]$$
Compute $y[n-n_0]$:
$$y[n-n_0] = 0.5 x[n-n_0] + 0.3 x[n-n_0-1] + 0.2 x[n-n_0-2]$$
Both match. The system is **TIME-INVARIANT**. ✓

**Step 3 — Causality Test:**
The output uses $x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2]$ — all present and past values. No future values.
The system is **CAUSAL**. ✓

**Step 4 — Stability Test:**
The impulse response is $h[n] = \{0.5, 0.3, 0.2\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2$.

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 < \infty$$

The system is **BIBO STABLE**. ✓

**Step 5 — Memory Test:**
$y[n]$ depends on $x[n-1]$ and $x[n-2]$, not only on $x[n]$.
The system has **MEMORY** (it is a dynamic system).

**Step 6 — Invertibility Test:**
The Z-transform is:
$$H(z) = 0.5 + 0.3 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2}$$
The inverse $H^{-1}(z)$ exists if $H(z)$ has no zeros at the unit circle. For typical coefficient sets, it is **INVERTIBLE**. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Property Testing Workflow

The following diagram shows the systematic KTU-recommended procedure for testing a discrete-time biomedical system against all six properties:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive System T{x}] --> B[Linearity Test]
    B --> B1{Additivity + Homogeneity?}
    B1 -->|Yes| B2[Linear]
    B1 -->|No| B3[Non-Linear]
    B2 --> C[Time-Invariance Test]
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1{Shift Invariant?}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[Time-Invariant]
    C1 -->|No| C3[Time-Variant]
    C2 --> D[Causality Test]
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1{h[n] = 0 for n < 0?}
    D1 -->|Yes| D2[Causal]
    D1 -->|No| D3[Non-Causal]
    D2 --> E[Stability BIBO Test]
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1{Sum of |h[n]| finite?}
    E1 -->|Yes| E2[BIBO Stable]
    E1 -->|No| E3[Unstable]
    E2 --> F[Memory Test]
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1{Depends on past or future?}
    F1 -->|No| F2[Memoryless Static]
    F1 -->|Yes| F3[Dynamic with Memory]
    F2 --> G[Invertibility Test]
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1{H(z) has no unit-circle zeros?}
    G1 -->|Yes| G2[Invertible]
    G1 -->|No| G3[Non-Invertible]
    G2 --> H[Final Classification Report]
    G3 --> H
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Property Mapping for a Biomedical ECG Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECG[Raw ECG Signal x n] --> F1[Analog Front End]
    F1 --> F2[ADC 360 Hz]
    F2 --> F3[Digital Filter T]
    F3 --> F4[Display Monitor]
    subgraph Properties of F3
        P1[Linear: Superposition]
        P2[Time Invariant: Constant Coefficients]
        P3[Causal: Real-time]
        P4[BIBO Stable: Bounded Heart Rate]
        P5[Dynamic: Past Samples]
        P6[Invertible: Reconstruction]
    end
    F3 -.-> P1
    F3 -.-> P2
    F3 -.-> P3
    F3 -.-> P4
    F3 -.-> P5
    F3 -.-> P6
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Property Dependencies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Discrete Time System T] --> A1[Linearity]
    Root --> A2[Time Invariance]
    A1 --> LTI[LTI System]
    A2 --> LTI
    LTI --> B1[Impulse Response h n]
    B1 --> C1[Causality h n = 0 for n less than 0]
    B1 --> C2[BIBO Stability Sum h n finite]
    B1 --> C3[Memory h n spans multiple n]
    B1 --> C4[Invertibility H z has no unit circle zeros]
    C1 --> Final[Complete System Classification]
    C2 --> Final
    C3 --> Final
    C4 --> Final
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a causal discrete-time system. State and prove the necessary and sufficient condition for an LTI system to be causal in terms of its impulse response.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A discrete-time system is **causal** if its output at any instant $n$ depends only on the present and past values of the input, and not on any future values. Mathematically, $y[n] = f(x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots)$.

**For an LTI system**, the input-output relation is given by the convolution sum:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

Splitting this sum into the future ($k<0$), present ($k=0$), and past ($k>0$) components, the future component involves $x[n+1], x[n+2], \ldots$. For the system to be causal, this future term must be zero for all $n$ and all possible inputs. This is only possible if the impulse response coefficients for negative indices are zero.

$$\boxed{h[n] = 0 \quad \text{for } n < 0}$$

This is the necessary and sufficient condition for an LTI system to be causal. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is BIBO stability? Derive the condition for BIBO stability of an LTI system in terms of its impulse response.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A system is **BIBO stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output stable) if every bounded input produces a bounded output.

For an LTI system, $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$.

Taking absolute values and applying the triangle inequality, followed by the bounded input condition $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x$:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq M_x \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert$$

For the output to remain bounded for every bounded input, the impulse response must be **absolutely summable**:

$$\boxed{\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty}$$

This is the necessary and sufficient condition for BIBO stability of an LTI system. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define linearity and time-invariance for a discrete-time system. For the system described by the difference equation $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-2]$, test the system for **(i)** linearity and **(ii)** time-invariance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital filter for ECG signal smoothing is given by $y[n] = 0.6 x[n] + 0.4 x[n-1]$. Test this filter for **(i)** causality, **(ii)** BIBO stability, **(iii)** memory, and **(iv)** invertibility. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a)(i) Linearity Test (3 marks):**
A system is linear if $T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a T\{x_1[n]\} + b T\{x_2[n]\}$.

Let $x_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$. Then:
$$T\{x_3[n]\} = n \cdot x_3[n] + x_3[n-2] = n[a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]] + [a x_1[n-2] + b x_2[n-2]]$$

$$T\{x_3[n]\} = a [n \cdot x_1[n] + x_1[n-2]] + b [n \cdot x_2[n] + x_2[n-2]]$$

$$T\{x_3[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$$

So the system is **LINEAR**. ✓

**(a)(ii) Time-Invariance Test (4 marks):**
Compute $T\{x[n-n_0]\}$:
$$T\{x[n-n_0]\} = n \cdot x[n-n_0] + x[n-n_0-2]$$
Compute $y[n-n_0]$:
$$y[n-n_0] = (n-n_0) \cdot x[n-n_0] + x[n-n_0-2]$$

These two expressions are **not equal** (the first has coefficient $n$ on the shifted input, the second has coefficient $(n-n_0)$). For example, at $n = n_0 = 1$, $T\{x[n-1]\}$ has coefficient 1 on $x[n-1]$, but $y[n-1]$ has coefficient 0.

The system is **TIME-VARIANT**. ✗

---

**(b)(i) Causality (2 marks):**
$y[n]$ depends only on $x[n]$ (present) and $x[n-1]$ (past). No future inputs.
The system is **CAUSAL**. ✓

**(b)(ii) BIBO Stability (2 marks):**
Impulse response: $h[0] = 0.6$, $h[1] = 0.4$, $h[n] = 0$ otherwise.

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.0 < \infty$$

The system is **BIBO STABLE**. ✓

**(b)(iii) Memory (1 mark):**
Since $y[n]$ depends on $x[n-1]$ (a past sample), the system **has memory** (dynamic). ✗ (not memoryless)

**(b)(iv) Invertibility (2 marks):**
The Z-transform is $H(z) = 0.6 + 0.4 z^{-1}$. To find the inverse, compute the zeros:

$$H(z) = 0 \implies 0.6 + 0.4 z^{-1} = 0 \implies z^{-1} = -1.5 \implies z = -\frac{2}{3}$$

The zero $z = -2/3$ is **inside** the unit circle, not on it. Therefore, the inverse system $H^{-1}(z) = \frac{1}{0.6 + 0.4 z^{-1}}$ exists.
The system is **INVERTIBLE**. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain with mathematical justification why an LTI system with impulse response $h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$ is causal and BIBO stable, but a system with $h[n] = (1.2)^n u[n]$ is unstable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system $y[n] = x[-n]$, test for **(i)** linearity, **(ii)** time-invariance, **(iii)** causality, and **(iv)** stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a)** For $h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$:

**Causality (2 marks):** Since $u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$, we have $h[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$. By the causality condition derived earlier, the system is **causal**.

**BIBO Stability (3 marks):**
$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = 2 < \infty$$

The impulse response is absolutely summable, so the system is **BIBO stable**.

For $h[n] = (1.2)^n u[n]$:

**Instability (2 marks):**
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (1.2)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 1.2} = \frac{1}{-0.2} = -5$$

The sum diverges (the geometric series with ratio $> 1$ does not converge). Therefore, $\sum \vert h[n] \vert = \infty$, and the system is **NOT BIBO stable**. A unit-step input $x[n] = u[n]$ would cause $y[n] = (1.2)^{n+1} - 0.2$, which grows without bound. ✗

---

**(b)** System: $y[n] = x[-n]$ (a time-reversal operation).

**(b)(i) Linearity (2 marks):**
$$T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a x_1[-n] + b x_2[-n] = a T\{x_1[n]\} + b T\{x_2[n]\}$$
The system is **LINEAR**. ✓

**(b)(ii) Time-Invariance (2 marks):**
$$T\{x[n-n_0]\} = x[-n - n_0]$$
$$y[n-n_0] = x[-(n-n_0)] = x[-n + n_0]$$
These are not equal (sign of $n_0$ differs). The system is **TIME-VARIANT**. ✗

**(b)(iii) Causality (1 mark):**
At $n = -1$: $y[-1] = x[1]$, which is a **future** input. The system is **NON-CAUSAL**. ✗

**(b)(iv) Stability (2 marks):**
If $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x$, then $\vert y[n] \vert = \vert x[-n] \vert \leq M_x$. The system is **BIBO STABLE**. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting both halves of Linearity:** Students often test only the *additivity* part and forget the *homogeneity* (scaling) part. **Both** are required to claim linearity. *(−2 marks penalty)*
>
> 2. **Confusing "Time-Invariant" with "Time-Independent":** A time-invariant system can still have a time-dependent input. The check is whether a *shift* in input produces an *identical shift* in output — not whether the system uses time at all.
>
> 3. **Causality for LTI systems only:** The condition $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ applies **only to LTI systems**. For non-linear systems, you must check the input-output definition directly. *(−1 mark penalty)*
>
> 4. **BIBO Stability Summation Direction:** Ensure you sum from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, not just one side. A two-sided impulse response like $h[n] = a^{\vert n \vert}$ must be summed across **both** positive and negative indices.
>
> 5. **Invertibility vs. Stability:** Students confuse $H(z) = 0$ at unit circle (loses information) with $H(z) = \infty$ at unit circle (system is marginally stable). These are two different conditions.
>
> 6. **Memory test trap:** A system can have memory but still be "memoryless" in the present value if the coefficients on $x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots$ are all zero. Always check $h[0]$ first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Properties of Discrete-Time Systems**
>
> - **Linearity:** Two-part test — Additivity **and** Homogeneity. Use arbitrary constants $a, b$.
> - **Time-Invariance:** Compare $T\{x[n-n_0]\}$ with $y[n-n_0]$ for general $n_0$.
> - **Causality (LTI form):** $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (right-sided impulse response).
> - **BIBO Stability (LTI form):** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ (absolute summability).
> - **Memoryless:** Output depends only on $x[n]$; $h[n] = 0$ for $n \neq 0$.
> - **Invertibility (LTI form):** $H(z)$ has no zeros on the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.
> - **LTI = Linear + Time-Invariant.** Once LTI is established, convolution, Z-transform, and frequency response are all valid tools.
> - **All real-time biomedical systems MUST be causal and BIBO stable** — these are non-negotiable design constraints in medical device engineering.
> - **KTU 2024 Most-Tested Properties:** Linearity + Time-Invariance + Stability (these three appear in nearly every question paper, often worth 7–10 marks combined).
> - **Memorize the impulse response form for each property** — it is the fastest way to answer in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Fourier Transform - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
