## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define poles and zeros of a discrete-time system transfer function. State the stability condition in terms of pole locations.

**Model Answer:**

A discrete-time system transfer function $H(z)$ expressed in pole-zero form is:

$$H(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{M}(z - z_i)}{\prod_{j=1}^{N}(z - p_j)}$$

- **Poles** ($p_j$): The values of $z$ for which the denominator equals zero, causing $H(z) \to \infty$. They indicate system resonances.
- **Zeros** ($z_i$): The values of $z$ for which the numerator equals zero, causing $H(z) = 0$. They indicate frequencies that are completely attenuated.

**Stability Condition:** A discrete-time LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if all poles lie **strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert p_j \vert < 1$ for all $j$.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

What is the Region of Convergence (ROC) of a Z-transform? Why cannot the ROC contain any pole?

**Model Answer:**

The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is the set of values of $z = re^{j\omega}$ in the complex plane for which the Z-transform $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ converges to a finite value.

The ROC cannot contain any pole because, by definition, at a pole the transfer function $H(z)$ becomes infinite. If the ROC contained a pole, the Z-transform would diverge at that point, contradicting the requirement of finite convergence. Therefore, the ROC is always bounded by the locations of the poles (typically forming an annular region $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

(a) For the discrete-time system described by the difference equation:
$$y[n] = 0.6 y[n-1] - 0.08 y[n-2] + x[n] + 0.5 x[n-1]$$
determine the transfer function, find all poles and zeros, plot them on the z-plane, and comment on the stability of the system. **(7 marks)**

(b) A digital bandpass filter for ECG signal processing is designed with poles at $p_{1,2} = 0.95 e^{\pm j \pi/4}$ and zeros at $z_1 = 1$ and $z_2 = -1$. Derive the transfer function in polynomial form and compute the magnitude response at $\omega = 0$, $\omega = \pi/4$, and $\omega = \pi$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Apply the Z-transform to the difference equation using the time-shift property:

$$Y(z) = 0.6 z^{-1} Y(z) - 0.08 z^{-2} Y(z) + X(z) + 0.5 z^{-1} X(z)$$

**Step 2:** Collect $Y(z)$ terms on the left:

$$Y(z) \left[ 1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2} \right] = X(z) \left[ 1 + 0.5 z^{-1} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Form the transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 + 0.5 z}{z^2 - 0.6 z + 0.08}$$

**Step 5:** Find zeros. Setting numerator to zero: $z^2 + 0.5 z = z(z + 0.5) = 0$, giving zeros at $z_1 = 0$ and $z_2 = -0.5$.

**Step 6:** Find poles. Setting denominator to zero: $z^2 - 0.6z + 0.08 = 0$. Using the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{0.6 \pm \sqrt{0.36 - 0.32}}{2} = \frac{0.6 \pm \sqrt{0.04}}{2} = \frac{0.6 \pm 0.2}{2}$$

This gives $p_1 = 0.4$ and $p_2 = 0.2$.

**Step 7:** Plot the pole-zero pattern. Both poles ($p_1 = 0.4$, $p_2 = 0.2$) lie on the positive real axis inside the unit circle. The zero at $-0.5$ lies on the negative real axis inside the unit circle, and a zero at the origin.

**Step 8:** Stability check: $\vert p_1 \vert = 0.4 < 1$ and $\vert p_2 \vert = 0.2 < 1$. Both poles are inside the unit circle, so the system is **BIBO stable**. The impulse response will be a sum of two decaying exponentials: $h[n] = A(0.4)^n + B(0.2)^n$ for $n \geq 0$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Express the transfer function in pole-zero form:

$$H(z) = \frac{(z - 1)(z + 1)}{(z - 0.95 e^{j\pi/4})(z - 0.95 e^{-j\pi/4})}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply out the denominator. With $\cos(\pi/4) = \sin(\pi/4) = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$:

$$(z - 0.95 e^{j\pi/4})(z - 0.95 e^{-j\pi/4}) = z^2 - 2(0.95)(0.7071) z + 0.9025 = z^2 - 1.3435 z + 0.9025$$

**Step 3:** The polynomial form of the transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 - 1}{z^2 - 1.3435 z + 0.9025}$$

**Step 4:** Magnitude at $\omega = 0$ ($z = 1$):

$$H(1) = \frac{1 - 1}{1 - 1.3435 + 0.9025} = \frac{0}{0.559} = 0$$

The DC component is completely nulled (zero at $z = 1$).

**Step 5:** Magnitude at $\omega = \pi/4$ (resonance, $z = e^{j\pi/4} = 0.7071 + j 0.7071$):

$$H(e^{j\pi/4}) = \frac{e^{j\pi/2} - 1}{e^{j\pi/2} - 1.3435 e^{j\pi/4} + 0.9025}$$

Numerator: $j - 1$, magnitude $= \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

Denominator: $(0.7071 + j 0.7071)^2 - 1.3435(0.7071 + j 0.7071) + 0.9025$
$= (0.7071^2 - 0.7071^2 + j 2 \cdot 0.7071 \cdot 0.7071) - 0.9499 - j 0.9499 + 0.9025$
$= j 1.0 - 0.9499 - j 0.9499 + 0.9025$
$= (j 1.0 - j 0.9499) + (-0.9499 + 0.9025)$
$= j 0.0501 - 0.0474$

Magnitude: $\sqrt{0.0025 + 0.00225} = \sqrt{0.00475} \approx 0.0689$.

$$\vert H(e^{j\pi/4}) \vert \approx \frac{1.414}{0.0689} \approx 20.52$$

**Step 6:** Magnitude at $\omega = \pi$ ($z = -1$):

$$H(-1) = \frac{1 - 1}{1 + 1.3435 + 0.9025} = \frac{0}{3.246} = 0$$

The Nyquist frequency component is also nulled (zero at $z = -1$).

**Step 7:** Interpretation. This filter has zeros at DC and Nyquist (acting as a bandpass) and a strong resonance at $\omega = \pi/4$ (resonance gain $\approx 20.5$), making it suitable for isolating a specific frequency band in the ECG signal.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

(a) Explain the geometric interpretation of frequency response computation from the pole-zero plot. Using this method, show that the magnitude response achieves its maximum near a pole and minimum (zero) at a zero location. **(7 marks)**

(b) Consider a discrete-time system with transfer function:
$$H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-2}}{1 - 1.2 z^{-1} + 0.72 z^{-2}}$$
Determine all poles, classify them, test for stability, and find the impulse response $h[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Geometric Method Setup.** When evaluating the frequency response, we set $z = e^{j\omega}$, which traces the unit circle in the z-plane. For any specific frequency $\omega$, the point $e^{j\omega}$ lies somewhere on this circle.

**Step 2: Vector Definition.** The frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ can be written as:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{M}(e^{j\omega} - z_i)}{\prod_{j=1}^{N}(e^{j\omega} - p_j)}$$

Each term $(e^{j\omega} - z_i)$ is a **vector** drawn from the zero $z_i$ to the evaluation point $e^{j\omega}$ on the unit circle. Similarly, $(e^{j\omega} - p_j)$ is a vector from the pole $p_j$.

**Step 3: Magnitude Computation.** The magnitude is the product of vector lengths:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert K \vert \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{M} \vert e^{j\omega} - z_i \vert}{\prod_{j=1}^{N} \vert e^{j\omega} - p_j \vert}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Near a Pole.** As the evaluation point $e^{j\omega}$ approaches a pole $p_j$ on the unit circle, the vector length $\vert e^{j\omega} - p_j \vert \to 0$, causing the denominator to vanish and the magnitude $\to \infty$. Even for poles inside the unit circle, the magnitude reaches a local maximum when the evaluation point is geometrically closest to that pole.

**Step 5: Minimum (Zero) at a Zero Location.** When the evaluation point $e^{j\omega}$ passes through a zero $z_i$, the vector $(e^{j\omega} - z_i)$ becomes exactly zero, forcing the entire magnitude to zero. This creates a **null** or complete attenuation at that frequency.

**Step 6: Application in Biomedical Filtering.** This geometric intuition is used in filter design: placing a zero at $z = e^{j 2\pi(50/360)}$ cancels 50 Hz powerline interference in ECG signals, while placing a pole close to the unit circle at the desired QRS frequency maximizes detection sensitivity.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$ to obtain positive powers:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 - 1}{z^2 - 1.2 z + 0.72}$$

**Step 2:** Find zeros. Numerator: $z^2 - 1 = (z-1)(z+1) = 0$, giving $z_1 = 1$ and $z_2 = -1$.

**Step 3:** Find poles. Denominator: $z^2 - 1.2 z + 0.72 = 0$. Using the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{1.44 - 2.88}}{2} = \frac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{-1.44}}{2} = \frac{1.2 \pm j 1.2}{2} = 0.6 \pm j 0.6$$

**Step 4:** Compute the magnitudes of the poles:

$$\vert p_{1,2} \vert = \sqrt{0.6^2 + 0.6^2} = \sqrt{0.72} \approx 0.8485$$

**Step 5:** Classify the poles. They are **complex conjugates** with magnitude $< 1$, located inside the unit circle at angles $\pm \pi/4$. The pole angle corresponds to a resonance frequency of $\omega_0 = \pi/4$ rad/sample.

**Step 6:** Stability check. Since $\vert p_1 \vert = \vert p_2 \vert = 0.8485 < 1$, the system is **BIBO stable**.

**Step 7:** Express poles in polar form. $p_{1,2} = 0.8485 e^{\pm j \pi/4}$.

**Step 8:** Express the transfer function in polar form. With $r = 0.8485$ and $\theta = \pi/4$:

$$H(z) = \frac{(z-1)(z+1)}{(z - r e^{j\theta})(z - r e^{-j\theta})}$$

**Step 9:** Perform partial fraction expansion. The transfer function can be rewritten as:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 - 1}{z^2 - 2r\cos(\theta) z + r^2} = \frac{z^2 - 1}{(z - 0.8485 e^{j\pi/4})(z - 0.8485 e^{-j\pi/4})}$$

The impulse response for causal ROC ($\vert z \vert > 0.8485$) with complex conjugate poles is:

$$h[n] = \frac{1}{\sin(\theta)} r^n \sin((n+1)\theta) u[n]$$

Substituting $r = 0.8485$ and $\theta = \pi/4$:

$$h[n] = \frac{1}{\sin(\pi/4)} (0.8485)^n \sin\left((n+1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right) u[n] = \sqrt{2} \cdot (0.8485)^n \sin\left(\frac{(n+1)\pi}{4}\right) u[n]$$

**Step 10:** Compute specific values:

$h[0] = \sqrt{2} \cdot (0.8485)^0 \cdot \sin(\pi/4) = \sqrt{2} \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 1$

$h[1] = \sqrt{2} \cdot (0.8485)^1 \cdot \sin(\pi/2) = \sqrt{2} \cdot 0.8485 \cdot 1 \approx 1.2$

$h[2] = \sqrt{2} \cdot (0.8485)^2 \cdot \sin(3\pi/4) = \sqrt{2} \cdot 0.72 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.72$

$h[3] = \sqrt{2} \cdot (0.8485)^3 \cdot \sin(\pi) = 0$ (since $\sin(\pi) = 0$)

**Step 11:** Interpretation. The impulse response exhibits **damped sinusoidal oscillations** with decaying envelope $(0.8485)^n$, consistent with complex conjugate poles inside the unit circle. The resonance frequency corresponds to $\omega_0 = \pi/4$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Poles:** When factoring $z^2 - 0.5z + 0.06$, students often write poles as $0.3$ and $-0.2$ instead of $0.3$ and $0.2$. Always re-verify by substituting back into the polynomial.
> 2. **Forgetting the ROC:** A pole-zero specification is incomplete without the ROC. The same $H(z)$ represents different sequences depending on the ROC choice. KTU examiners frequently deduct 1–2 marks for omitting the ROC.
> 3. **Confusing Stability with Causality:** A system can be stable but non-causal, or causal but unstable. Both properties must be checked independently. Always state the ROC explicitly.
> 4. **Miscounting Order:** The number of poles minus zeros affects the asymptotic behavior. Forgetting that $N \geq M$ for causal realizable systems costs marks.
> 5. **Geometric Method Errors:** When computing vector lengths, students often confuse the direction. The vector goes **from the pole/zero TO the evaluation point**, not the other way.
> 6. **Unit Confusion in Frequencies:** When converting between $\omega$ (rad/sample) and $f$ (Hz), always use $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$. Mixing these is a frequent deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Pole-Zero Form:** $H(z) = K \cdot \prod(z - z_i) / \prod(z - p_j)$ — the canonical representation.
- **Stability Golden Rule:** All poles **strictly inside** the unit circle ($\vert p_j \vert < 1$) for BIBO stability.
- **Zeros** are roots of the numerator; they cause complete signal nulling at their location.
- **Poles** are roots of the denominator; they cause resonance peaks when close to the unit circle.
- **Real poles** produce exponential responses ($a^n$); **complex poles** produce damped sinusoids ($r^n \sin(\omega_0 n + \phi)$).
- **ROC must exclude all poles** — it is the region where the Z-transform converges.
- **Causal system ROC:** exterior of outermost pole ($\vert z \vert > \max \vert p_j \vert$).
- **Anti-causal system ROC:** interior of innermost pole ($\vert z \vert < \min \vert p_j \vert$).
- **Vector method:** Magnitude response = product of zero-to-point vector lengths / product of pole-to-point vector lengths.
- **For biomedical ECG notch filters:** Place zeros at $z = e^{\pm j 2\pi f_{\text{noise}} / f_s}$ to cancel specific interference frequencies.
- **Bandpass filter design:** Use complex conjugate pole pairs near the unit circle at the desired center frequency.
- **Order of system:** $N = \max(M, N)$ where $M$ = number of zeros and $N$ = number of poles.
- **Difference equation → Transfer function:** Use time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = z^{-k} Y(z)$.
- **System with poles on the unit circle** is marginally stable (pure oscillator) — not BIBO stable.
- **Conjugate symmetry:** For real-coefficient systems, complex poles and zeros always appear in conjugate pairs.
- **Sampling rate matters:** A pole's physical frequency is $f = (\angle p) \cdot f_s / (2\pi)$ Hz.
- **Pole proximity to unit circle controls bandwidth:** Closer pole = sharper resonance = narrower bandwidth $\Delta f \approx (1 - \vert p \vert) f_s / \pi$.
- **Inverse Z-transform for simple poles** uses the standard pair $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow z/(z-a)$.
- **Check the gain constant $K$** — it scales the entire response and is often normalized to unity in design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inversion of the z-Transform Using Partial Fraction Expansion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inversion of the z-Transform Using Partial Fraction Expansion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Z-Transform Inversion** is the mathematical procedure of recovering the original discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ from its Z-domain representation $X(z)$. When the transfer function is expressed as a ratio of polynomials in $z^{-1}$ (or $z$), the **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)** method decomposes this rational function into a sum of simpler, first-order (or low-order) terms whose inverse z-transforms are already tabulated in standard transform pairs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** In Module 2, the focus is restricted to inversion using **Partial Fraction Expansion** (as opposed to long division, contour integration, or power-series expansion), which is the most frequently tested technique in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).

The general form of a rational Z-transform expressed in $z^{-1}$ is:

$$X(z) = \frac{N(z)}{D(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M}}{a_0 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}}$$

The PFE represents $X(z)$ as a sum of terms, each of the form $\dfrac{A_k}{1 - p_k z^{-1}}$, where $p_k$ are the poles of $X(z)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **complex smoothie (signal $x[n]$)** that is a blend of five different fruits. If someone hands you the chemical composition report (the z-transform $X(z)$), you want to know exactly **how much of each fruit was used**. 

Partial Fraction Expansion is like **chemically separating the smoothie back into its individual fruit components**. Each component (fruit) has a known "fingerprint" (standard z-transform pair), and once you know the **quantity (residue coefficient $A_k$)** of each component, you can reconstruct the original recipe by looking up the inverse transform table.

In engineering terms:
- The **numerator polynomial** = the "recipe proportions"
- The **denominator polynomial** = the "characteristic behavior" (defines the pole locations)
- The **residues $A_k$** = the "weights" of each exponential mode in the final signal

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is PFE the preferred KTU method?**
> 1. It yields a **closed-form expression** for $x[n]$.
> 2. It allows direct use of standard z-transform pairs.
> 3. It clearly reveals the **causality and stability** of the system from the pole locations.
> 4. It is the cleanest method for **KGTU-style analytical problems** requiring step-by-step residue calculation.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics in This Topic

- **Region of Convergence (ROC):** Denoted in **bold** as a critical boundary that determines causality.
- **Pole locations ($p_k$):** The roots of the denominator polynomial $D(z)$ — always in **bold** when emphasized.
- **Residues ($A_k$):** Complex-valued coefficients representing the strength of each pole.
- **Unit circle:** $\vert z \vert = 1$ — the boundary of stability for discrete-time LTI systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The PFE procedure follows a **rigorous sequence of six logical steps**. Each step has a clear "Why" and "How" explanation:

#### Step 1: Express $X(z)$ as a Proper Rational Function in $z^{-1}$

- **Why:** KTU standard convention writes transfer functions in negative powers of $z$ for causal sequences.
- **How:** If the numerator degree $\geq$ denominator degree, perform polynomial long division first to extract a direct (non-fractional) term $C(z)$ plus a proper fraction $X_{\text{prop}}(z)$.

#### Step 2: Locate the Poles (Roots of the Denominator)

- **Why:** Poles determine the natural modes of the system — sinewaves decaying/growing, or constants.
- **How:** Solve $D(z) = 0$ for all $N$ roots $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_N$.

#### Step 3: Choose the Form of Expansion

- **How:** Depending on pole types:
  - **Distinct real poles** → $\dfrac{A_k}{1 - p_k z^{-1}}$
  - **Repeated real poles (multiplicity $m$)** → $\dfrac{A_{k1}}{1 - p_k z^{-1}} + \dfrac{A_{k2}}{(1 - p_k z^{-1})^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{A_{km}}{(1 - p_k z^{-1})^m}$
  - **Complex conjugate pole pairs** → second-order real terms $\dfrac{B_0 + B_1 z^{-1}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$

#### Step 4: Compute the Residues ($A_k$)

- **Why:** Residues tell us "how much" of each mode is present.
- **How:** For distinct poles, the formula is given in the cheat sheet below.

#### Step 5: Apply Standard Inverse Z-Transform Pairs

- **How:** Use the lookup table to convert each PFE term back to the time domain $x[n]$.

#### Step 6: Determine the ROC and Establish Causality

- **Why:** A unique inverse requires specifying the ROC.
- **How:** For a **causal sequence**, the ROC is $\vert z \vert > \max \vert p_k \vert$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table verbatim. It is the **highest-yield reference** for KTU Module 2 problems.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inverse Z-Transform Symbol | $x[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{X(z)\}$ | Discrete-time index $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 2 | Proper Rational Form | $X(z) = \dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)}$ with $\deg(N) < \deg(D)$ | Dimensionless (normalized frequency) |
| 3 | Residue for Distinct Pole $p_k$ | $A_k = (1 - p_k z^{-1}) X(z) \Big\vert_{z=p_k}$ | Computed via residue theorem |
| 4 | Residue for Repeated Pole (Order $m$) | $A_{kj} = \dfrac{1}{(m-j)!} \lim_{z \to p_k} \dfrac{d^{m-j}}{dz^{m-j}}\left[(z - p_k)^m X(z)\right]$ | $j = 1, 2, \dots, m$ |
| 5 | PFE for Complex Pair at $r e^{\pm j\omega_0}$ | $\dfrac{A}{1 - re^{j\omega_0} z^{-1}} + \dfrac{A^*}{1 - re^{-j\omega_0} z^{-1}}$ | Combines to real form |
| 6 | Standard Pair: Causal Exponential | $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}\right\} = a^n u[n]$ | $\vert a \vert$ is the pole magnitude |
| 7 | Standard Pair: Right-sided Delayed | $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z^{-k}}{1 - a z^{-1}}\right\} = a^{n-k} u[n-k]$ | Introduces a delay of $k$ samples |
| 8 | Standard Pair: Causal Ramp | $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}\right\} = n a^n u[n]$ | Used for repeated pole terms |
| 9 | Standard Pair: Damped Sinusoid | $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1 - (r\cos\omega_0)z^{-1}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}\right\} = r^n \cos(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ | ROC: $\vert z \vert > r$ |
| 10 | Standard Pair: Damped Sinusoid (Quad) | $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{(r\sin\omega_0)z^{-1}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}\right\} = r^n \sin(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ | ROC: $\vert z \vert > r$ |
| 11 | Causal System Condition (Stability) | $\max \vert p_k \vert < 1$ | All poles **inside the unit circle** |
| 12 | ROC for Causal Sequence | $\vert z \vert > \max \vert p_k \vert$ | Exterior of the outermost pole |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **biomedical signal processing**, PFE-based z-transform inversion is used to:
- Decompose an **ECG/EEG filter transfer function** into its natural modes, identifying the dominant frequency components (e.g., QRS complex energy).
- Analyze **biomechanical impulse responses** from accelerometer data sampled at $f_s = \mathbf{500}$ Hz.
- Design **IIR digital filters** for noise cancellation in hearing aids, where pole locations dictate the resonance frequencies.
- Implement **adaptive filters** in pacemakers to reject motion artifacts — stability is enforced by ensuring all poles lie inside the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Case Study A: Distinct Real Poles (Worked Example)

> **Problem:** Find the inverse z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})}$, assuming a causal ROC.

**Step 1: Identify the poles.**
The poles are $p_1 = 0.5$ and $p_2 = 0.8$ (both real, distinct).

**Step 2: Write the partial fraction expansion form.**

$$X(z) = \frac{A_1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{A_2}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Compute residue $A_1$.**
Multiply both sides by $(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})$ and evaluate at $z^{-1} = 1/0.5 = 2$ (equivalently, $z = 0.5$).

$$A_1 = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) X(z) \Big\vert_{z = 0.5} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}} \Big\vert_{z = 0.5}$$

Substitute $z = 0.5 \Rightarrow z^{-1} = 2$:

$$A_1 = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 (2)} = \frac{1}{1 - 1.6} = \frac{1}{-0.6} = -\frac{5}{3}$$

**Step 4: Compute residue $A_2$.**
Multiply both sides by $(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})$ and evaluate at $z = 0.8 \Rightarrow z^{-1} = 1.25$.

$$A_2 = (1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) X(z) \Big\vert_{z = 0.8} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} \Big\vert_{z = 0.8}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5(1.25)} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.625} = \frac{1}{0.375} = \frac{8}{3}$$

**Step 5: Substitute back into the PFE.**

$$X(z) = -\frac{5/3}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{8/3}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 6: Apply standard inverse z-transform pairs.**
Since the ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.8$ (causal), we use the pair $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}\right\} = a^n u[n]$.

$$\boxed{x[n] = -\frac{5}{3}(0.5)^n u[n] + \frac{8}{3}(0.8)^n u[n]}$$

### 3.2 Case Study B: Repeated Real Poles (Worked Example)

> **Problem:** Find the inverse z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2 (1 - 0.25 z^{-1})}$, causal ROC.

**Step 1: Write the PFE form for a repeated pole.**

$$X(z) = \frac{A_{11}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{A_{12}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2} + \frac{A_2}{1 - 0.25 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 2: Compute $A_2$ (simple distinct pole).**

$$A_2 = (1 - 0.25 z^{-1}) X(z) \Big\vert_{z = 0.25}$$

At $z = 0.25 \Rightarrow z^{-1} = 4$:

$$A_2 = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5(4))^2} = \frac{1}{(-1)^2} = 1$$

**Step 3: Compute $A_{12}$ (the term that pairs with the highest power).**
Multiply both sides by $(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2$ and evaluate at $z = 0.5 \Rightarrow z^{-1} = 2$:

$$A_{12} = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2 X(z) \Big\vert_{z = 0.5} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25 z^{-1}} \Big\vert_{z = 0.5}$$

$$A_{12} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25(2)} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = 2$$

**Step 4: Compute $A_{11}$ (the term that pairs with the first power).**
Take the derivative of $(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2 X(z)$ with respect to $z^{-1}$ and evaluate at $z^{-1} = 2$. Alternatively, plug in an arbitrary $z$ value, say $z = 0 \Rightarrow z^{-1} = \infty$, but the cleanest method is:

$$A_{11} = \frac{d}{dz^{-1}}\left[(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2 X(z)\right] \Big\vert_{z^{-1}=2}$$

Let $u = z^{-1}$. Then:

$$(1 - 0.5u)^2 X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25u}$$

Differentiate with respect to $u$:

$$\frac{d}{du}\left[\frac{1}{1 - 0.25u}\right] = \frac{0.25}{(1 - 0.25u)^2}$$

At $u = 2$:

$$A_{11} = \frac{0.25}{(1 - 0.5)^2} = \frac{0.25}{0.25} = 1$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the PFE.**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{2}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2} + \frac{1}{1 - 0.25 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 6: Apply standard inverse z-transform pairs.**
Using the table:
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}\right\} = (0.5)^n u[n]$
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2}\right\} = (n+1)(0.5)^n u[n]$ (causal)
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1 - 0.25 z^{-1}}\right\} = (0.25)^n u[n]$

For the middle term: $\dfrac{2}{(1-0.5z^{-1})^2} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1-0.5z^{-1})^2}$, so its inverse is $2(n+1)(0.5)^n u[n]$.

$$\boxed{x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n] + 2(n+1)(0.5)^n u[n] + (0.25)^n u[n]}$$

### 3.3 Case Study C: Complex Conjugate Poles (Worked Example)

> **Problem:** Find the inverse z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 1.2 z^{-1} + 0.72 z^{-2}}$, causal ROC.

**Step 1: Find the poles by solving the denominator.**
Characteristic equation: $z^2 - 1.2 z + 0.72 = 0$.

Using the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{1.44 - 2.88}}{2} = \frac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{-1.44}}{2} = \frac{1.2 \pm j 1.2}{2}$$

$$p_{1,2} = 0.6 \pm j 0.6 = 0.848 \angle \pm 45^\circ$$

So $r = 0.848$ and $\omega_0 = \pi/4$.

**Step 2: Verify the standard form.**
$1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2} = 1 - 2(0.848)(\cos 45^\circ)z^{-1} + (0.848)^2 z^{-2}$
$= 1 - 1.2 z^{-1} + 0.72 z^{-2}$ ✓

**Step 3: Match to standard pair.**
Since $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$, and this matches the **damped cosine** form with $B_0 = 1$, $B_1 = 0$:

$$\boxed{x[n] = r^n \cos(\omega_0 n) u[n] = (0.848)^n \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{4}\right) u[n]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Method (Residues):** Express $X(z)$ as $\dfrac{A_1}{1 - p_1 z^{-1}} + \dfrac{A_1^*}{1 - p_1^* z^{-1}}$ with $p_1 = 0.6 + j0.6$. Compute $A_1$ and combine real/imaginary parts to obtain the same result. **KTU students must know both routes.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: PFE Algorithm Flowchart

The following Mermaid block describes the complete decision-flow for performing z-transform inversion using PFE:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given X of z rational in z to power minus 1"] --> B{"Degree of N greater than Degree of D"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Perform Polynomial Long Division to Extract Direct Term C of z plus Proper Fraction Xprop of z"]
    B -- No --> D["X of z is Already Proper: Proceed to PFE"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Find Poles: Solve D of z equals 0 to Get pk"]
    E --> F{"Pole Type Classification"}
    F -- Distinct Real --> G["Compute Residues Ak using Cover Up Method"]
    F -- Repeated Real Order m --> H["Compute A kj using Differentiation Formula"]
    F -- Complex Conjugate Pair --> I["Use Second Order Real Form B0 plus B1 z power minus 1 over Quadratic Denominator"]
    G --> J["Assemble PFE Expansion: Sum of Ak over 1 minus pk z power minus 1"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Apply Standard Inverse Z Transform Table Pairs"]
    K --> L{"ROC Specified"}
    L -- Causal outside outermost pole --> M["Right sided sequence: use u of n for all terms"]
    L -- Anti causal inside innermost pole --> N["Left sided sequence: use minus u of minus n minus 1 for all terms"]
    M --> O["Final Answer: x of n closed form expression"]
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"]
        XZ["X of z Rational Function in z power minus 1"]
    end
    subgraph DECOMP["Decomposition Stage"]
        P1["Pole Locator Module"]
        P2["Residue Calculator Module"]
        P3["PFE Assembler Module"]
    end
    subgraph LOOKUP["Lookup Stage"]
        TBL["Standard Z Transform Pair Table"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"]
        XN["Time Domain Sequence x of n"]
        ROC["Region of Convergence Specification"]
    end
    XZ --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> TBL
    TBL --> XN
    P3 --> ROC
    ROC --> XN
```

### 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot Schematic (Conceptual)

A typical causal discrete-time system with **two real poles** ($p_1 = 0.5$, $p_2 = 0.8$) and **two zeros** at the origin has the following pole-zero geometry on the complex $z$-plane:

```
                Im(z)
                  |
                  |
        __________|__________
       /          |          \
      /     . (0.5)          \
     |          ●             |
     |                          |
   --|---------+---------●------|------ Re(z)
   -1|         0       (0.8)    |  1
     |                          |
      \                        /
       \______________________/
                  |
                  |
                  |
```

> [!NOTE]
> Both poles are **inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$, confirming that the system is **stable and causal**. The ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole, i.e., $\vert z \vert > 0.8$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A.1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *State any **three** standard z-transform pairs required for inverting a rational $X(z)$ using partial fraction expansion, when the system is causal. Write the corresponding ROC for each pair.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Causal exponential:** $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}\right\} = a^n u[n]$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.
2. **Causal ramp (repeated pole):** $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}\right\} = n a^n u[n]$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.
3. **Damped cosine:** $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1 - r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}\right\} = r^n \cos(\omega_0 n) u[n]$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > r$.

*(Any three standard pairs with their ROCs are accepted; marking follows the key: 1 mark per pair + ROC.)*

#### Question A.2
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *What is the region of convergence (ROC) for a causal LTI discrete-time system whose transfer function has poles at $z = 0.3$ and $z = 0.9$? State the stability condition in terms of pole locations.*

**Model Answer:**

The ROC of a **causal** sequence is the **exterior of the outermost pole**:

$$\text{ROC}: \quad \vert z \vert > \max(0.3, 0.9) = 0.9$$

**Stability condition:** For a causal LTI system to be **BIBO stable**, all poles must lie strictly **inside the unit circle**:

$$\vert p_k \vert < 1 \quad \forall k$$

Since both $0.3 < 1$ and $0.9 < 1$, the system is **stable**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> Find the inverse z-transform of the following using partial fraction expansion, assuming a causal sequence. Show all residue calculations.
> 
> $$X(z) = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})}$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify poles.**
Poles are at $p_1 = 0.5$ and $p_2 = 0.8$ (both real, distinct).

**Step 2: PFE form.**

$$X(z) = \frac{A_1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{A_2}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Compute $A_1$ using the cover-up method.**

$$A_1 = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) X(z) \Big\vert_{z = 0.5} = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}} \Big\vert_{z = 0.5}$$

At $z = 0.5 \Rightarrow z^{-1} = 2$:

$$A_1 = \frac{1 + 0.5(2)}{1 - 0.8(2)} = \frac{1 + 1}{1 - 1.6} = \frac{2}{-0.6} = -\frac{10}{3}$$

**Step 4: Compute $A_2$ using the cover-up method.**

$$A_2 = (1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) X(z) \Big\vert_{z = 0.8} = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} \Big\vert_{z = 0.8}$$

At $z = 0.8 \Rightarrow z^{-1} = 1.25$:

$$A_2 = \frac{1 + 0.5(1.25)}{1 - 0.5(1.25)} = \frac{1 + 0.625}{1 - 0.625} = \frac{1.625}{0.375} = \frac{13}{3}$$

**Step 5: Substitute and invert.**

$$X(z) = -\frac{10/3}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{13/3}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

Since the ROC is causal ($\vert z \vert > 0.8$), the inverse is:

$$\boxed{x[n] = -\frac{10}{3}(0.5)^n u[n] + \frac{13}{3}(0.8)^n u[n]}$$

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 Key):**
- (a) Setting up PFE form & computing $A_1$: **7 marks**
- (b) Computing $A_2$ and writing the final inverse: **7 marks**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
> Using the partial fraction expansion method, determine the inverse z-transform of
> 
> $$X(z) = \frac{z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2 (1 - 0.25 z^{-1})}, \quad \text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > 0.5$$
> 
> Also verify the stability of the corresponding system.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify poles and their types.**
- $p_1 = 0.5$ is a **repeated pole** of order $m = 2$.
- $p_2 = 0.25$ is a **distinct real pole**.

**Step 2: Write PFE form for repeated + distinct poles.**

$$X(z) = \frac{A_{11}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{A_{12}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2} + \frac{A_2}{1 - 0.25 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Compute $A_2$.**

$$A_2 = (1 - 0.25 z^{-1}) X(z) \Big\vert_{z = 0.25} = \frac{z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2} \Big\vert_{z = 0.25}$$

At $z = 0.25 \Rightarrow z^{-1} = 4$:

$$A_2 = \frac{4}{(1 - 0.5(4))^2} = \frac{4}{(-1)^2} = 4$$

**Step 4: Compute $A_{12}$ (coefficient of $(1 - 0.5z^{-1})^{-2}$).**

$$A_{12} = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2 X(z) \Big\vert_{z = 0.5} = \frac{z^{-1}}{1 - 0.25 z^{-1}} \Big\vert_{z = 0.5}$$

At $z = 0.5 \Rightarrow z^{-1} = 2$:

$$A_{12} = \frac{2}{1 - 0.25(2)} = \frac{2}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{2}{0.5} = 4$$

**Step 5: Compute $A_{11}$ via differentiation.**

$$A_{11} = \frac{d}{dz^{-1}}\left[(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2 X(z)\right] \Big\vert_{z = 0.5}$$

Let $u = z^{-1}$. Then $(1 - 0.5u)^2 X(z) = \dfrac{u}{1 - 0.25u}$.

Differentiate with respect to $u$:

$$\frac{d}{du}\left[\frac{u}{1 - 0.25u}\right] = \frac{(1 - 0.25u)(1) - u(-0.25)}{(1 - 0.25u)^2} = \frac{1 - 0.25u + 0.25u}{(1 - 0.25u)^2} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.25u)^2}$$

At $u = 2$:

$$A_{11} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5)^2} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4$$

**Step 6: Assemble the PFE.**

$$X(z) = \frac{4}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{4}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2} + \frac{4}{1 - 0.25 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 7: Apply inverse z-transform pairs.**
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{4(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^{-1}\} = 4 (0.5)^n u[n]$
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{4(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^{-2}\} = 4 (n+1)(0.5)^n u[n]$ (causal form)
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{4(1 - 0.25 z^{-1})^{-1}\} = 4 (0.25)^n u[n]$

$$\boxed{x[n] = 4(0.5)^n u[n] + 4(n+1)(0.5)^n u[n] + 4(0.25)^n u[n]}$$

**Step 8: Stability verification.**
The poles are at $\vert p_1 \vert = 0.5 < 1$ and $\vert p_2 \vert = 0.25 < 1$. Since all poles lie **inside the unit circle**, the system is **BIBO stable**.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 Key):**
- (a) Setting up PFE for repeated + distinct poles and computing $A_2, A_{12}$: **7 marks**
- (b) Computing $A_{11}$ via differentiation, final inverse, and stability check: **7 marks**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PFE Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the initial long-division step** when $\deg(N(z)) \geq \deg(D(z))$ — this loses **2–3 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Sign errors in cover-up method:** When substituting $z = p_k$, students often forget to convert to $z^{-1} = 1/p_k$ correctly. Always cross-check by multiplying out and plugging back into the original equation.
> 3. **Missing the $u[n]$ causal step-function:** The inverse of $\frac{1}{1-az^{-1}}$ is $a^n u[n]$, **NOT** just $a^n$. A common oversight that costs **1 mark** per term.
> 4. **Repeated pole formula confusion:** The order-2 pair is $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{(1-az^{-1})^{-2}\} = (n+1)a^n u[n]$ for a **causal** system. Students sometimes write $n a^{n-1} u[n]$, which is wrong.
> 5. **Not stating the ROC explicitly:** KTU 2024 valuation keys mandate the ROC for the final 1 mark of stability/clarity.
> 6. **Complex pole errors:** When matching to the damped-sinusoid form, the cross-term must be $2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1}$ — students often drop the factor of 2.
> 7. **No sanity check on stability:** Always verify $\max \vert p_k \vert < 1$ and mention it explicitly in the final answer.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **PFE Goal:** Decompose $X(z)$ into a sum of first-order (or second-order real) terms.
- **Always begin** by expressing $X(z)$ in **negative powers of $z$** ($z^{-1}$ form).
- **If improper** ($\deg N \geq \deg D$), **perform long division first** to extract a polynomial $C(z)$ plus a proper fraction.
- **Poles** = roots of denominator — classify as **distinct, repeated, or complex** before choosing the PFE template.
- **Cover-up method (distinct poles):** $A_k = (1 - p_k z^{-1}) X(z)\Big\vert_{z=p_k}$.
- **Repeated poles:** use the differentiation formula $A_{kj} = \frac{1}{(m-j)!} \lim_{z\to p_k} \frac{d^{m-j}}{dz^{m-j}}\left[(z-p_k)^m X(z)\right]$.
- **Complex conjugate pair** at $re^{\pm j\omega_0}$: combine residues into a **real second-order** form $B_0 + B_1 z^{-1}$ over $1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}$.
- **Causality ↔ ROC:** ROC is $\vert z \vert > \max \vert p_k \vert$ (right-sided / causal sequence).
- **Anti-causality ↔ ROC:** ROC is $\vert z \vert < \min \vert p_k \vert$ (left-sided sequence), and inverses use $-u[-n-1]$.
- **Stability condition:** All poles strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$).
- **Standard causal pairs** (must be memorized):
  - $\frac{1}{1-az^{-1}} \leftrightarrow a^n u[n]$
  - $\frac{1}{(1-az^{-1})^2} \leftrightarrow (n+1)a^n u[n]$
  - $\frac{1 - r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}} \leftrightarrow r^n \cos(\omega_0 n) u[n]$
  - $\frac{r\sin\omega_0 z^{-1}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}} \leftrightarrow r^n \sin(\omega_0 n) u[n]$
- **Always verify** the PFE by recombining the partial fractions into a single fraction and matching it with the original $X(z)$.
- **Final answer must include** the $u[n]$ (or $-u[-n-1]$) multiplier and the ROC.
- **Biomedical relevance:** PFE is the workhorse for analyzing **ECG filter impulse responses** and **IIR notch filters** for **50/60 Hz powerline interference** removal in biomedical instrumentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Linear Time-Invariant Systems in the z-Domain - Transfer Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Linear Time-Invariant Systems in the z-Domain - Transfer Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) discrete-time systems**, the **Transfer Function $H(z)$** is defined as the **ratio of the z-transform of the output sequence $y(n)$ to the z-transform of the input sequence $x(n)$**, evaluated under the assumption of **zero initial conditions** (i.e., a relaxed system).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} \;=\; \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} h(n)\, z^{-n}$$

where $h(n)$ is the **impulse response** of the LTI system — the output produced when the input is a unit sample $\delta(n)$.

Equivalently, $H(z)$ is the **z-transform of the impulse response**:

$$H(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\{h(n)\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Check:** The phrase *"ratio of output z-transform to input z-transform with zero initial conditions"* is the **verbatim wording** KTU examiners expect in 3-mark and 12/14-mark questions. Memorize this sentence structure.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spectral Fingerprint" of a System

Imagine you are an audiologist using a stethoscope. Different stethoscopes modify the heartbeat sound differently — some emphasize low-frequency "lub-dub" thumps (useful for murmurs), while others amplify high-frequency components (useful for detecting valve clicks).

The **transfer function $H(z)$** is precisely this **"spectral fingerprint"** of the system. It tells us, in a single mathematical object:

- **How much** the system amplifies or attenuates each frequency.
- **How much** the system delays or phase-shifts each frequency component.
- **Whether** the system is stable, causal, or realizable.

In a **biomedical context** (ECG, EEG, EMG filtering), the transfer function is the engineer’s recipe — specifying the filter coefficients so that 50 Hz powerline interference is killed, baseline wander is removed, and the diagnostically important QRS complex is preserved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember in Bold:**
> - **Sampling Frequency ($F_s$):** in Hz — typically **360 Hz** for MIT-BIH ECG database, **250 Hz** for EEG.
> - **Nyquist Frequency ($F_s/2$):** the highest unambiguous representable frequency.
> - **Unit circle** in the z-plane corresponds to the **frequency axis** $|z| = 1$.

---

### 1.3 Why z-Domain and not s-Domain?

Although the Laplace transform $H(s)$ handles continuous-time systems, biomedical signals are **digitized (sampled)** after acquisition. The z-transform is the discrete-time equivalent, where the complex variable $z$ is related to the Laplace variable $s$ through:

$$z \;=\; e^{sT_s} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad s \;=\; \frac{1}{T_s}\ln(z)$$

Here $T_s = 1/F_s$ is the **sampling period**. The z-plane is therefore a **mapped (conformal) version** of the s-plane — the imaginary axis $j\Omega$ in s maps to the **unit circle** $|z|=1$ in z. This makes the z-domain the natural mathematical home for digital biomedical filters (e.g., notch filters, low-pass filters for EMG smoothing).

> [!TIP]
> **Visual mnemonic:** Left half of s-plane (stable analog) → **Inside the unit circle** in z-plane (stable digital). This single fact solves 80% of KTU stability problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: From Difference Equation to Transfer Function

Any causal LTI discrete-time system can be described by a **linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)** of the form:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\, y(n-k) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x(n-k)$$

with the convention $a_0 = 1$ (so we normalize the leading output coefficient).

**Step-by-step logic for converting to Transfer Function:**

1. Apply the z-transform to **both sides** of the LCCDE.
2. Use the **time-shift property**: $\mathcal{Z}\{y(n-k)\} = z^{-k} Y(z)$ (under zero initial conditions).
3. Factor $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$ out of the resulting polynomial equation.
4. Take the ratio $Y(z)/X(z)$ to obtain $H(z)$.

Applying the time-shift property to every term:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}\, Y(z) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}\, X(z)$$

Solving for $H(z)$:

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Result:** This *rational polynomial form* in $z^{-1}$ is the **canonical representation** of a digital filter. When asked to "express the transfer function," write it in this exact form first.

---

### 2.2 Poles, Zeros, and the Factorized Form

By the **Fundamental Theorem of Algebra**, the numerator and denominator polynomials can each be factored into linear terms. We can rewrite $H(z)$ as:

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{b_0}{a_0}\,\frac{\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{M}\left(1 - z_k\, z^{-1}\right)}{\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{N}\left(1 - p_k\, z^{-1}\right)} \;=\; K\,z^{N-M}\,\frac{\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{M}(z - z_k)}{\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{N}(z - p_k)}$$

- **Zeros** $\{z_k\}$: roots of the numerator. Frequencies that the filter **blocks or attenuates**.
- **Poles** $\{p_k\}$: roots of the denominator. Frequencies that the filter **amplifies or resonates at**.
- **Gain constant $K$**: overall system gain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Insight:** In a 50 Hz **notch filter** for ECG, the design places a **pair of complex-conjugate zeros exactly on the unit circle at the angle corresponding to 50 Hz**, while placing **poles slightly inside the unit circle** at the same angle. This combination gives a sharp, narrow notch that surgically removes only the powerline interference without harming the QRS complex.

---

### 2.3 Region of Convergence (ROC)

The ROC is the set of all values of $z$ in the complex plane for which the z-transform **converges** (i.e., the summation $\sum h(n) z^{-n}$ is finite).

**Key KTU Properties of ROC:**

| # | Property | Statement |
|---|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Bounded by poles | ROC is bounded **inwardly and outwardly** by poles (no pole may lie inside the ROC). |
| 2 | Causal system | ROC is the region **outside the outermost pole**: $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$. |
| 3 | Anti-causal system | ROC is the region **inside the innermost pole**: $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$. |
| 4 | Two-sided system | ROC is an **annulus** (ring): $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$. |
| 5 | Stability | ROC **must include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$. |
| 6 | Finite-length sequence | ROC is the **entire z-plane** (except possibly $z=0$ or $z=\infty$). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion (KTU repeated question):** A causal LTI system is **BIBO stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all $k$.

---

### 2.4 Frequency Response from Transfer Function

Substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$ (a point on the unit circle, where $\omega = \Omega T_s$ is the **normalized digital frequency** in radians/sample) into $H(z)$ gives the **frequency response**:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) \;=\; \left. H(z) \right|_{z = e^{j\omega}} \;=\; \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert\, e^{j\angle H(e^{j\omega})}$$

- **Magnitude response** $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ — describes **amplification vs. attenuation** of each frequency.
- **Phase response** $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$ — describes **time delay** introduced at each frequency.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** In designing a digital **ECG low-pass filter** with a cutoff of 40 Hz at $F_s = 500$ Hz, we evaluate $H(z)$ on the unit circle at the corresponding digital frequency $\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot 40/500 = 0.5027$ rad/sample. We adjust the filter coefficients until $|H(e^{j\omega_c})| = 1/\sqrt{2}$ (the **-3 dB point**).

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Transfer function definition | $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{h(n)\}$ | Filter design starting point |
| 2 | From LCCDE | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Deriving system equation |
| 3 | Pole-zero factor form | $H(z) = K \cdot \dfrac{\prod (z - z_k)}{\prod (z - p_k)}$ | Pole-zero plot analysis |
| 4 | Convolution property | $y(n) = h(n) * x(n) \;\Rightarrow\; Y(z) = H(z) X(z)$ | Output computation |
| 5 | s-to-z mapping | $z = e^{sT_s}$ | Analog-to-digital conversion |
| 6 | Frequency response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z)\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$ | Magnitude/phase plotting |
| 7 | Stability (causal system) | All poles $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ | BIBO stability check |
| 8 | ROC for causal system | $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$ | ROC determination |
| 9 | Digital frequency | $\omega = \Omega T_s = 2\pi f / F_s$ | Hz-to-radians conversion |
| 10 | Inverse z-transform | $h(n) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C H(z)\, z^{n-1}\, dz$ | Recovering impulse response |
| 11 | Linearity property | $\mathcal{Z}\{a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)\} = a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$ | Superposition analysis |
| 12 | Time-shift property | $\mathcal{Z}\{x(n - n_0)\} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | Deriving $H(z)$ from LCCDE |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: LCCDE to Transfer Function

**Problem (typical KTU setup):** A causal LTI system is described by the difference equation:
$$y(n) - \tfrac{1}{2} y(n-1) \;=\; x(n) + \tfrac{1}{4} x(n-1)$$
Determine the transfer function $H(z)$, its ROC, poles, zeros, and state whether the system is stable.

**Step 1 — Write the LCCDE in standard form** (move all $y$-terms to the left, $x$-terms to the right):

$$y(n) - \frac{1}{2} y(n-1) \;=\; x(n) + \frac{1}{4} x(n-1)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the z-transform term-by-term** using linearity and the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{w(n-k)\} = z^{-k} W(z)$:

$$Y(z) - \frac{1}{2}\, z^{-1}\, Y(z) \;=\; X(z) + \frac{1}{4}\, z^{-1}\, X(z)$$

**Step 3 — Factor $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$ on their respective sides:**

$$Y(z)\left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right) \;=\; X(z)\left(1 + \frac{1}{4} z^{-1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the transfer function $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:**

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{1 + \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to positive powers of $z$** by multiplying numerator and denominator by $z$:

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{z + \tfrac{1}{4}}{z - \tfrac{1}{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Find poles and zeros:**

- **Zero:** Solve numerator $= 0 \Rightarrow z + 1/4 = 0 \Rightarrow z_1 = -1/4$.
- **Pole:** Solve denominator $= 0 \Rightarrow z - 1/2 = 0 \Rightarrow p_1 = 1/2$.

**Step 7 — Determine the ROC.** Since the system is causal and has a single pole at $p_1 = 1/2$:

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \tfrac{1}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Check stability.** The pole magnitude is $\vert p_1 \vert = 1/2 < 1$, so the pole lies **inside the unit circle**. The unit circle is therefore inside the ROC.

> **Conclusion:** The system is **causal** (ROC is exterior) **and stable** (ROC includes the unit circle). ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Inverse z-Transform via Partial Fractions

**Problem:** Given
$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{1}{\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right)}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \tfrac{1}{2}$$
Find the impulse response $h(n)$.

**Step 1 — Perform partial fraction expansion:**

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{A}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} \;+\; \frac{B}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for A.** Multiply both sides by $(1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1})$ and set $z^{-1} = 2$:

$$A \;=\; \left.\frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1}}\right|_{z^{-1} = 2} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{2}{3}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1/3} \;=\; 3$$

**Step 3 — Solve for B.** Multiply both sides by $(1 - \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1})$ and set $z^{-1} = 3$:

$$B \;=\; \left.\frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}}\right|_{z^{-1} = 3} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{3}{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{-1/2} \;=\; -2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the partial fraction form:**

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{3}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} \;-\; \frac{2}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the standard z-transform pair** $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ for $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$. Since ROC is exterior, the system is **causal** and the inverse transform gives a right-sided sequence:

$$h(n) \;=\; 3\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n u(n) \;-\; 2\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^n u(n)$$

> **Verification:** At $n = 0$, $h(0) = 3 - 2 = 1$ which matches the impulse response definition. ✓

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: System Response to a Specific Input

**Problem:** For the same $H(z)$ as in Derivation #1, find the output $y(n)$ when the input is $x(n) = \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)^n u(n)$.

**Step 1 — Write the input z-transform:**

$$X(z) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}, \quad \vert z \vert > \tfrac{1}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply to obtain $Y(z)$:**

$$Y(z) \;=\; H(z)\, X(z) \;=\; \frac{1 + \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}{\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Perform partial fraction expansion.** Letting $H_1(z) = \dfrac{1}{\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}\right)}$:

$$H_1(z) \;=\; \frac{A}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} \;+\; \frac{B}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}$$

Solving:
- $A = \dfrac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4}\cdot 2} = \dfrac{1}{1/2} = 2$
- $B = \dfrac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 4} = \dfrac{1}{-1} = -1$

So $H_1(z) = \dfrac{2}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} - \dfrac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}$.

**Step 4 — Multiply the numerator factor** $\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}\right)$ across:

$$Y(z) \;=\; \left(1 + \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}\right)\left[\frac{2}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}\right]$$

Expanding:

$$Y(z) \;=\; \frac{2}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}} + \frac{\tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} - \frac{\tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Take inverse z-transform term by term** using $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ and $\mathcal{Z}\{a^{n-1} u(n-1)\} = \dfrac{z^{-1}}{1 - a z^{-1}}$:

$$y(n) \;=\; 2\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n u(n) \;-\; \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)^n u(n) \;+\; \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n u(n-1) \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)^{n-1} u(n-1)$$

> **Interpretation:** The first two terms are the **zero-state response** (from $H(z)$ alone), and the latter terms arise because $x(n)$ contains its own poles. Since initial conditions are zero, there is **no zero-input response** component.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for KTU Problems

```
START: Given an LTI discrete-time system
  │
  ├──► Is the system given as a difference equation?
  │      YES → Apply z-transform, solve for H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)
  │      NO  → Move to next check
  │
  ├──► Is the system given as a block diagram?
  │      YES → Identify gain blocks, delay elements (z⁻¹), summers
  │            Write y(n) in terms of x(n), take z-transform
  │
  ├──► Question asks about stability?
  │      YES → Find all poles, check if |p_k| < 1
  │
  ├──► Question asks about causality?
  │      YES → Check if ROC is exterior (|z| > r_max)
  │
  ├──► Question asks for impulse response h(n)?
  │      YES → Partial fraction expand H(z), inverse z-transform
  │
  └──► Question asks for output y(n) to a given x(n)?
         YES → Compute Y(z) = H(z) X(z), partial fraction, inverse z-transform
```

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic LTI System in z-Domain

The flow of signals through a discrete-time LTI system when viewed in the z-domain:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[X of z] --> mul1[H of z]
    mul1 --> outNode[Y of z]
    inNode -.->|"ROC: R_x"| mul1
    mul1 -.->|"ROC: R_y"| outNode
    classDef inputBox fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef systemBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class inNode inputBox
    class mul1 systemBox
    class outNode outputBox
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- $X(z)$ — z-transform of the **input biomedical signal** (e.g., raw ECG).
- $H(z)$ — the **transfer function block** (filter transfer function).
- $Y(z)$ — z-transform of the **filtered output** (e.g., clean ECG).
- The relationship $Y(z) = H(z)\, X(z)$ is the fundamental **multiplicative law** of z-domain LTI analysis.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Direct Form I Realization

The Direct Form I is the most direct hardware/software implementation of a difference equation, mapping one-to-one to the coefficients $\{a_k, b_k\}$:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["x of n"] --> s1((+))
    inp --> dly1["z super -1"]
    dly1 --> m1["b sub 1"]
    m1 --> s1
    dly1 --> dly2["z super -1"]
    dly2 --> m2["b sub 2"]
    m2 --> s1
    s1 --> outp["y of n"]
    outp --> fdly1["z super -1"]
    fdly1 --> fm1["minus a sub 1"]
    fm1 --> s1
    fdly1 --> fdly2["z super -1"]
    fdly2 --> fm2["minus a sub 2"]
    fm2 --> s1
    classDef sigNode fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef dlyNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef coefNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef sumNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    class inp,outp sigNode
    class dly1,dly2,fdly1,fdly2 dlyNode
    class m1,m2,fm1,fm2 coefNode
    class s1 sumNode
```

> **Interpretation:** The top feed-forward path (with $b_0, b_1, b_2$) processes the **input samples**, and the bottom feedback path (with $-a_1, -a_2$) processes the **output samples**. Each $z^{-1}$ block represents a **unit delay** (one sample period $T_s$).

---

### 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot Schematic

A **pole-zero plot** in the z-plane for a bandpass biomedical filter (e.g., for EMG band 20–200 Hz):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Unit circle in z plane"] --> B["Conjugate zero pair on unit circle at 50 Hz"]
    A --> C["Conjugate pole pair just inside unit circle near 50 Hz"]
    A --> D["Conjugate pole pair near origin for low cutoff"]
    A --> E["Conjugate zero pair near origin for DC rejection"]
    B --> F["Sharp notch at 50 Hz powerline interference"]
    C --> F
    D --> G["Highpass behavior at low frequencies"]
    E --> G
    F --> H["Clean EMG signal output"]
    G --> H
    classDef c1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef c2 fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef c3 fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef c4 fill:#fdfd96,stroke:#d4a017,color:#000
    class A c1
    class B,C c2
    class D,E c3
    class F,G,H c4
```

> **Engineering insight:** Zeros on the unit circle cause **exact nulls** (infinite attenuation) at that frequency, while poles just inside the unit circle create a **narrow resonance** that sharpens the transition. The further inside the unit circle, the wider the resonance; the closer, the sharper.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Biomedical Filtering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw["Raw ECG signal x of n"] --> preamp["Analog pre-amplifier and anti-alias filter"]
    preamp --> adc["ADC sampler at F sub s"]
    adc --> notch["50 Hz notch filter H sub 1 of z"]
    notch --> hp["Highpass filter for baseline wander H sub 2 of z"]
    hp --> lp["Lowpass filter for muscle noise H sub 3 of z"]
    lp --> clean["Clean ECG signal y of n"]
    notch -.->|"Poles inside, zeros on unit circle"| notch
    hp -.->|"Removes frequencies below 0.5 Hz"| hp
    lp -.->|"Removes frequencies above 40 Hz"| lp
    classDef bioNode fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef filtNode fill:#fdfd96,stroke:#d4a017,color:#000
    class raw,clean bioNode
    class preamp,adc procNode
    class notch,hp,lp filtNode
```

> **Note:** The **overall transfer function** of the cascade is the product of the individual transfer functions:

$$H_{\text{total}}(z) \;=\; H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z) \cdot H_3(z)$$

This multiplicative property is one of the most powerful features of z-domain analysis — a complex filter is built by cascading simple ones.
