# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Passband Ripple and Stopband Attenuation in dB

**Problem:** Given $\delta_p = 0.05$ and $\delta_s = 0.01$, convert to dB form for KTU specification writing.

### Step 1 — Identify the definitions
The amplitude response in the passband is bounded below by $(1 - \delta_p)$ because the peak-to-peak ripple $\delta_p$ is centered around unity. The amplitude response in the stopband is bounded above by $\delta_s$.

### Step 2 — Express the magnitude in linear scale

$$
1 - \delta_p \leq \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \leq 1 \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \leq \omega \leq \omega_p
$$

$$
\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \leq \delta_s \quad \text{for} \quad \omega_s \leq \omega \leq \pi
$$

### Step 3 — Convert ripple to dB using $20 \log_{10}$

$$
A_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \delta_p}\right) = -20 \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)
$$

$$
A_s = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\delta_s}\right) = -20 \log_{10}(\delta_s)
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values

$$
A_p = -20 \log_{10}(1 - 0.05) = -20 \log_{10}(0.95)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(0.95) = -0.02228
$$

$$
A_p = -20 \times (-0.02228) = 0.445 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the stopband attenuation

$$
A_s = -20 \log_{10}(0.01) = -20 \times (-2) = 40 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the result

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The filter must keep the passband ripple below $\mathbf{0.445 \text{ dB}}$ and provide at least $\mathbf{40 \text{ dB}}$ attenuation in the stopband. The $40$ dB figure means unwanted signals are reduced to $\mathbf{1/100^{th}}$ of their original amplitude — a clinically safe level for 50 Hz notch filters in ECG.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Bilinear Transform with Pre-Warping

**Problem:** Design a digital Butterworth lowpass filter with $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample, $f_s = 1000$ Hz, from a prototype with $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/s analog cutoff.

### Step 1 — Establish the bilinear substitution

The bilinear transform maps the analog $s$-plane to the digital $z$-plane via:

$$
s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}
$$

The frequency mapping is:

$$
\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the sampling period

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{1000} = 0.001 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the pre-warped analog cutoff

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right) = \frac{2}{0.001} \tan\!\left(\frac{0.4\pi}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\Omega_c = 2000 \cdot \tan(0.2\pi)
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the tangent

$$
0.2\pi = 0.6283 \text{ rad} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan(0.6283) = 0.7265
$$

### Step 5 — Final numerical result

$$
\Omega_c = 2000 \times 0.7265 = 1453.0 \text{ rad/s}
$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** To get a digital cutoff of $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample, the analog Butterworth prototype must be designed with $\Omega_c = 1453$ rad/s — *not* the naively scaled $0.4\pi \cdot 1000 = 1256.6$ rad/s. The $15.6\%$ discrepancy is the bilinear warping effect and is the most common source of design error in KTU exam problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Linear-Phase FIR Group Delay

**Problem:** For a linear-phase FIR filter of length $N = 51$ (order $M = 50$), show that the group delay is $N/2$ samples.

### Step 1 — Symmetric impulse response condition

A Type-I linear-phase FIR filter has $h[n] = h[N-1-n]$. Substituting:

$$
H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[n] e^{-j\omega n}
$$

### Step 2 — Pair the symmetric terms

$$
H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \sum_{k=0}^{(N-1)/2} a_k \cos(\omega k)
$$

where $a_k$ are real coefficients derived from $h[n]$.

### Step 3 — Identify the phase

$$
\phi(\omega) = -\omega \cdot \frac{N-1}{2} \quad \text{(a linear function of } \omega \text{)}
$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate to get group delay

$$
\tau_g(\omega) = -\frac{d\phi(\omega)}{d\omega} = \frac{N-1}{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical substitution

$$
\tau_g = \frac{51 - 1}{2} = 25 \text{ samples}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to seconds (assuming $f_s = 360$ Hz)

$$
\tau_g = \frac{25}{360} = 69.4 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Every frequency component is delayed by exactly $69.4$ ms — the entire signal waveform is shifted in time *uniformly*, preserving shape. This is why FIR filters are preferred for diagnostic ECG where QRS morphology matters.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — FIR vs IIR

| Design Constraint | Choose FIR if… | Choose IIR if… |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase linearity required** | ✓ Yes (always) | ✗ No (or use all-pass equalizer) |
| **Strict stability guarantee** | ✓ Yes (BIBO automatic) | ✗ Requires pole inspection |
| **Low computational budget** | ✗ No (N large) | ✓ Yes (low order) |
| **Sharp transition band** | ✗ Needs high N | ✓ Yes (4th–8th order suffices) |
| **Coefficient precision (8-bit MCU)** | ✓ Tolerant | ✗ Sensitive to quantization |
| **Real-time latency critical** | ✗ High $\tau_g = N/2$ | ✓ Low $\tau_g$ possible |
| **Offline batch processing (Holter)** | ✓ Excellent | Acceptable |
| **Powerline notch (50 Hz, $f_s$=500 Hz)** | ✓ Equiripple Parks-McClellan | ✓ 2nd-order IIR notch (2 mul/sample) |

---

## 3.5 The Stability Test — Worked Example

**Problem:** A 2nd-order IIR filter has transfer function:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 1.6 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-2}}
$$

Determine if it is BIBO stable.

### Step 1 — Find the poles by solving the denominator

$$
1 - 1.6 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-2} = 0
$$

Multiply by $z^2$:

$$
z^2 - 1.6 z + 0.7 = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the quadratic formula

$$
z = \frac{1.6 \pm \sqrt{1.6^2 - 4(0.7)}}{2} = \frac{1.6 \pm \sqrt{2.56 - 2.8}}{2} = \frac{1.6 \pm \sqrt{-0.24}}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Complex conjugate poles

$$
z_{1,2} = \frac{1.6 \pm j\sqrt{0.24}}{2} = 0.8 \pm j0.2449
$$

### Step 4 — Compute pole magnitudes

$$
\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert = \sqrt{0.8^2 + 0.2449^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 0.06} = \sqrt{0.70} = 0.8367
$$

### Step 5 — Apply stability criterion

$$
\vert z_1 \vert = \vert z_2 \vert = 0.8367 < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{STABLE} \checkmark
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the impulse response decay envelope

The impulse response decays as $0.8367^n$. After $n = 20$ samples, residual amplitude is $0.8367^{20} = 0.0263$ (2.6% of initial) — practical decay in $\approx 30$ samples.

---

## 3.6 Derivation — Filter Order via Kaiser Formula (FIR)

**Problem:** Estimate the FIR filter order for: $f_p = 40$ Hz, $f_s = 50$ Hz, $f_{sample} = 360$ Hz, $A_p = 1$ dB, $A_s = 50$ dB.

### Step 1 — Compute normalized transition width

$$
\Delta f = f_s - f_p = 10 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta f}{f_{sample}} = \frac{10}{360} = 0.02778
$$

### Step 2 — Kaiser empirical formula

$$
N \approx \frac{A_s - 7.95}{14.36 \cdot (\Delta f / f_{sample})} \quad \text{(when } A_s > 21 \text{ dB)}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$
N \approx \frac{50 - 7.95}{14.36 \times 0.02778} = \frac{42.05}{0.3989} = 105.4
$$

### Step 4 — Round up to nearest integer

$$
N = 106 \text{ taps}
$$

### Step 5 — Group delay

$$
\tau_g = \frac{N-1}{2} = 52.5 \text{ samples} = \frac{52.5}{360} = 145.8 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Designing a 50 dB-stopband FIR filter with only 10 Hz transition width at 360 Hz sampling rate requires **106 taps** and introduces a **145.8 ms delay** — about 1.5 typical QRS widths. This is *clinically significant* and one of the key trade-offs students must justify in design reports.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Digital Filter Design Pipeline (Preliminary Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomedical Signal Source] --> B[Identify Application]
    B --> C[Set Magnitude Specs]
    C --> D{Phase Linearity Needed?}
    D -- Yes --> E[FIR Filter Path]
    D -- No --> F[IIR Filter Path]
    E --> G[Choose Window or Frequency Sampling]
    F --> H[Choose Prototype: Butterworth Chebyshev Elliptic]
    G --> I[Compute Coefficients]
    H --> J[Apply Bilinear Transform with Pre Warping]
    I --> K[Verify Stability and Group Delay]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final Design Validated]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The pipeline begins with the application context (e.g., ECG denoising), passes through specification setting, then forks based on the phase-linearity requirement. Both paths converge at the verification stage before producing a finalized filter.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — FIR vs IIR Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FIR [FIR Filter Architecture]
        F1[X n] --> F2[Delay z1]
        F2 --> F3[Delay z2]
        F3 --> F4[Delay zN]
        F1 --> F5[Multiply b0]
        F2 --> F6[Multiply b1]
        F3 --> F7[Multiply b2]
        F4 --> F8[Multiply bN]
        F5 --> F9[Summation]
        F6 --> F9
        F7 --> F9
        F8 --> F9
        F9 --> F10[Y n]
    end
    subgraph IIR [IIR Filter Architecture]
        I1[X n] --> I2[Multiply b0]
        I2 --> I3[Summation]
        I4[Y n delayed 1] --> I5[Multiply a1]
        I5 --> I3
        I6[Y n delayed 2] --> I7[Multiply a2]
        I7 --> I3
        I3 --> I8[Y n]
        I8 --> I4
        I8 --> I6
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** FIR is a *pure feed-forward* tapped delay line (no feedback loops, hence always stable). IIR *feeds back* the output through delay elements, creating poles — hence the stability risk that must be checked by pole-location inspection.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Magnitude Specification Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPEC [Ideal Lowpass Filter Specification]
        S0[Frequency Axis omega 0 to pi]
        S1[Passband Region 0 to omega_p]
        S2[Transition Band omega_p to omega_s]
        S3[Stopband Region omega_s to pi]
    end
    S0 --- S1
    S0 --- S2
    S0 --- S3
    S1 --> S4[Allowed gain 1 minus delta_p to 1]
    S3 --> S5[Allowed gain 0 to delta_s]
    S2 --> S6[Smooth Monotonic Roll off]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The four "preliminary" parameters ($\omega_p, \omega_s, \delta_p, \delta_s$) carve the frequency axis into three zones: passband (unity gain with small ripple), transition band (controlled roll-off), and stopband (near-zero gain with bounded leakage).

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Preliminary Choice Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start] --> Q2{Need Linear Phase?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Choose FIR]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Need Low Order Sharp Cutoff?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Choose IIR Butterworth]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[Reassess Specifications]
    Q3 --> Q7{Real time or Offline?}
    Q7 -- Real time --> Q8[Consider Short FIR or Zero Phase via filtfilt]
    Q7 -- Offline --> Q9[Use Parks McClellan Equiripple]
    Q5 --> Q10{Continuous Frequency Mapping OK?}
    Q10 -- Yes --> Q11[Impulse Invariance]
    Q10 -- No --> Q12[Bilinear with Pre Warping]
    Q8 --> R[Design Complete]
    Q9 --> R
    Q11 --> R
    Q12 --> R
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the KTU-typical reasoning chain. Each decision node corresponds to one of the preliminary considerations. The final output ("Design Complete") is reached only after all four preliminary questions are answered.
