> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impulse Invariance** is a **bilinear s-plane to z-plane mapping technique** used in digital filter design where the discrete-time (digital) filter's impulse response $h_d[n]$ is obtained by **uniformly sampling the impulse response** $h_a(t)$ of a prototype analog filter at regular intervals $T$ (the sampling period). In other words, the shape of the impulse response is *preserved* (invariant) under discretization.

Mathematically, the relationship is expressed as:

$$
h_d[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT) \quad \text{or simply} \quad h_d[n] = h_a(nT)
$$

where $T = 1/F_s$ is the sampling period and $F_s$ is the sampling frequency.

The corresponding z-transform of the digital filter is:

$$
H_d(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h_d[n]\, z^{-n}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Photographic Flash Analogy:** Imagine you are watching a spinning ceiling fan. A digital camera can only capture *frozen snapshots* at specific moments. If you capture the fan blade at instants $t = 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, \ldots$ seconds, those snapshots are the **sampled** version of the continuous motion. The "impulse invariance" method is exactly this — we take a beautiful, smooth analog waveform (the fan's continuous rotation = $h_a(t)$) and capture it as discrete dots in time (the snapshots = $h_d[n]$). The *shape* is preserved at every snapshot, but only at those instants.

The **key insight** is that the digital filter's response to a unit impulse at $n=0$ is mathematically guaranteed to *match* the sampled analog impulse response at every sample point.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Period ($T$):** Measured in **seconds (s)**. Typical biomedical range: $T = 1/360 \approx 2.78$ ms for ECG (360 Hz sampling).
- **Sampling Frequency ($F_s = 1/T$):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. Must obey the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:** $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ where $F_{max}$ is the highest frequency content in $h_a(t)$.
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $\omega_N = \pi/T$ (rad/sample) or $F_N = F_s/2$ (Hz).
- **Digital Frequency:** $\omega = \Omega T$, where $\Omega$ is the analog frequency in rad/s.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Impulse Invariance is studied alongside Bilinear Transformation under the topic *"Design of Digital Filters from Analog Filters using IIR Design Techniques."* It is a **direct method** (as opposed to bilinear which is an indirect method).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The impulse invariance technique operates on five logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Analog Filter Design:** Start with a *known, well-designed* analog filter $H_a(s)$. The poles of $H_a(s)$ lie in the **left-half s-plane** for stability.
- **Step 2 — Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE):** Express $H_a(s)$ as a sum of simple first-order fractions:
$$
H_a(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{R_k}{s - s_k}
$$
where $s_k$ are the poles and $R_k$ are the residues.
- **Step 3 — Inverse Laplace Transform:** Each term transforms to a time-domain exponential decay (or oscillation):
$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{R_k}{s - s_k}\right\} = R_k e^{s_k t} u(t)
$$
- **Step 4 — Sampling:** Replace $t = nT$:
$$
h_a(nT) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} R_k e^{s_k nT} u(nT)
$$
- **Step 5 — Z-Transform Application:** Since $\mathcal{Z}\{e^{s_k nT} u[n]\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}$, the digital filter is:
$$
H_d(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{R_k}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}
$$

## 2.2 The Pole Mapping Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pole Mapping:** Every analog pole at $s = s_k$ maps to a digital pole at:
$$
z_k = e^{s_k T}
$$

If $s_k = \sigma_k + j\Omega_k$, then:

$$
z_k = e^{\sigma_k T} \cdot e^{j \Omega_k T}
$$

- $\vert z_k \vert = e^{\sigma_k T}$ — radius from origin.
- $\angle z_k = \Omega_k T$ — angle on the unit circle.

**Stability Guarantee:** A stable analog filter has $\sigma_k < 0 \implies \vert z_k \vert < 1$, so the digital filter is *also* stable.

## 2.3 The Frequency Domain Relationship (The Aliasing Caveat)

The digital frequency response is related to the analog frequency response by:

$$
H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} H_a\left(j\frac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T}\right)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Aliasing Trap:** This is a *sum*, not a single term! The digital spectrum is an **aliased, scaled** version of the analog spectrum, with replicas centered at multiples of $2\pi$. For an **strictly band-limited** analog filter (where $H_a(j\Omega) = 0$ for $\vert \Omega \vert \geq \pi/T$), the aliasing is *eliminated* and $H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T} H_a(j\omega/T)$ for $\vert \omega \vert \leq \pi$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Sampling relationship | $h_d[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ | $T$ in seconds |
| 2 | Pole mapping | $z_k = e^{s_k T}$ | $s_k$ in rad/s |
| 3 | PFE form | $H_a(s) = \sum R_k / (s - s_k)$ | $R_k$ = residue |
| 4 | Digital filter form | $H_d(z) = \sum R_k / (1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1})$ | Direct from analog |
| 5 | Digital frequency | $\omega = \Omega T$ | rad/sample, $\vert \omega \vert \leq \pi$ |
| 6 | Analog cutoff | $\Omega_c = \omega_c / T$ | Preserves critical freq |
| 7 | Frequency relation | $H_d(e^{j\omega}) = (1/T)\sum_k H_a(j(\omega-2\pi k)/T)$ | Includes aliasing |
| 8 | Stability condition | $\text{Re}(s_k) < 0 \iff \vert z_k \vert < 1$ | LHP → inside unit circle |
| 9 | Second-order section | $H_d(z) = (1 - z^{-1})/(1 - 2z^{-1}\cos\Omega_0 T + z^{-2})$ | Example: $H_a(s) = \Omega_0/(s^2 + \Omega_0^2)$ |
| 10 | Time-scaling | $h_d[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ | $T$ preserves amplitude density |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **ECG Filter Design:** Designing a 0.5–40 Hz bandpass IIR filter to remove baseline wander and high-frequency muscle noise from ECG signals.
- **EEG Pre-processing:** Implementing narrow-band lowpass filters to remove 50/60 Hz powerline interference (though bilinear is preferred for highpass).
- **EMG Filtering:** Anti-aliasing lowpass filter design before ADC sampling.
- **Pacemaker Signal Conditioning:** Real-time low-power digital filters for implanted devices where direct form-II structures are memory-efficient.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Laplace to Z-Transform (Pole-Zero Mapping)

We begin with a single-pole analog filter:

$$
H_a(s) = \frac{R}{s - s_1}
$$

**Step 1:** Take the inverse Laplace transform:

$$
h_a(t) = R \cdot e^{s_1 t} \cdot u(t)
$$

**Step 2:** Sample the impulse response at $t = nT$:

$$
h_d[n] = R \cdot e^{s_1 nT} \cdot u[n] = R \cdot \left(e^{s_1 T}\right)^n \cdot u[n]
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the z-transform definition. Since $u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$:

$$
H_d(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} R \cdot \left(e^{s_1 T}\right)^n z^{-n} = R \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{e^{s_1 T}}{z}\right)^n
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n = 1/(1-a)$ for $\vert a \vert < 1$:

$$
H_d(z) = \frac{R}{1 - e^{s_1 T} z^{-1}} = \frac{Rz}{z - e^{s_1 T}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The analog pole at $s = s_1$ becomes a digital pole at $z = e^{s_1 T}$, with the same residue $R$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pole Mapping in Polar Form

Let $s_1 = \sigma_1 + j\Omega_1$. Apply Euler's formula:

$$
z_1 = e^{s_1 T} = e^{(\sigma_1 + j\Omega_1)T} = e^{\sigma_1 T} \cdot e^{j \Omega_1 T}
$$

Writing in polar form:

$$
z_1 = \underbrace{e^{\sigma_1 T}}_{\text{magnitude}} \cdot \angle{\Omega_1 T}
$$

$$
\boxed{\vert z_1 \vert = e^{\sigma_1 T} \quad \text{and} \quad \angle z_1 = \Omega_1 T}
$$

**Stability check:** If $\sigma_1 < 0$, then $\vert z_1 \vert = e^{\sigma_1 T} < 1$, placing the digital pole *inside* the unit circle → stable digital filter.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency Response Relationship

Recall the discrete-time Fourier transform of the sampled signal:

$$
H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h_d[n] e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} T \cdot h_a(nT) e^{-j\omega n}
$$

Substitute $\omega = \Omega T$ (continuous-time frequency) and recognize this as the sampled Fourier transform:

$$
H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} H_a\left(j\frac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T}\right)
$$

This proves aliasing: the digital spectrum is a **periodic summation** of the analog spectrum replicas.

## 3.4 Worked Example — First-Order Butterworth Design

**Problem:** Design a digital first-order Butterworth lowpass filter with **3 dB cutoff at $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample** using impulse invariance with $T = 1$ s.

**Step 1:** Compute the analog cutoff frequency:

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{\omega_c}{T} = \frac{0.4\pi}{1} = 0.4\pi \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the analog prototype:

$$
H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c}{s + \Omega_c} = \frac{0.4\pi}{s + 0.4\pi}
$$

This is a single-pole system with $R = 0.4\pi$ and $s_1 = -0.4\pi$.

**Step 3:** Apply the pole mapping:

$$
z_1 = e^{s_1 T} = e^{-0.4\pi \cdot 1} = e^{-0.4\pi}
$$

**Step 4:** Construct $H_d(z)$:

$$
H_d(z) = \frac{R}{1 - e^{-0.4\pi} z^{-1}} = \frac{0.4\pi}{1 - e^{-0.4\pi} z^{-1}}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute numerical value: $e^{-0.4\pi} \approx 0.2846$, so:

$$
H_d(z) = \frac{0.4\pi}{1 - 0.2846\, z^{-1}} = \frac{1.2566}{1 - 0.2846\, z^{-1}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability check:** $\vert z_1 \vert = 0.2846 < 1$ ✓ — the digital filter is stable.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Second-Order System

**Problem:** Convert $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + s + 1}$ to digital using impulse invariance with $T = 1$ s.

**Step 1:** Find the poles:

$$
s_{1,2} = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4}}{2} = -\frac{1}{2} \pm j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$

So $\sigma = -0.5$, $\Omega = \pm 0.8660$ rad/s.

**Step 2:** PFE using standard form:

$$
H_a(s) = \frac{1}{(s - s_1)(s - s_2)} = \frac{1/(\sqrt{3}\, j)}{s - s_1} + \frac{1/(- \sqrt{3}\, j)}{s - s_2}
$$

**Step 3:** Map poles to z-plane:

$$
z_1 = e^{s_1 T} = e^{-0.5} e^{j 0.8660}, \quad z_2 = e^{-0.5} e^{-j 0.8660}
$$

**Step 4:** Note $\vert z_1 \vert = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$ and $\angle z_1 = 0.8660$ rad. The digital filter is stable.

**Step 5:** Digital transfer function:

$$
H_d(z) = \frac{(1/\sqrt{3}\, j)}{1 - z_1 z^{-1}} + \frac{(-1/\sqrt{3}\, j)}{1 - z_2 z^{-1}}
$$

Multiplying out the complex conjugate pair gives a real-coefficient form:

$$
H_d(z) = \frac{b_0}{(1 - 2 e^{-0.5} \cos(0.8660) z^{-1} + e^{-1.0} z^{-2})}
$$

**Numerical coefficients:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot 2 \sin(0.8660) \cdot e^{-0.5} \\
    &= \frac{2 \cdot 0.7618 \cdot 0.6065}{1.7321} \\
    &\approx 0.5335
\end{aligned}
$$

Denominator coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= -2 e^{-0.5} \cos(0.8660) = -2(0.6065)(0.6474) \approx -0.7853 \\
a_2 &= e^{-1.0} = 0.3679
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_d(z) = \frac{0.5335}{1 - 0.7853\, z^{-1} + 0.3679\, z^{-2}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The pole magnitude squared is $a_2 = 0.3679 = e^{-1.0} = (e^{-0.5})^2$ ✓ and the cosine of the pole angle equals $a_1/(-2|a_2|^{1/2}\text{...})$ — all checks pass.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Pros vs. Cons

| Aspect | Impulse Invariance | Implication |
|--------|--------------------|--------------|
| Time-domain accuracy | **Excellent** — $h_d[n]$ exactly matches $h_a(nT)$ | Preserves time-domain features |
| Frequency-domain | Suffers from **aliasing** | Not ideal for HF content |
| Lowpass filter | **Works very well** | Most common use |
| Bandpass filter | Acceptable if bandwidth $\ll \pi/T$ | ECG bandpass OK |
| Highpass filter | **Not recommended** | Severe aliasing of stopband roll-off |
| Bandstop filter | Generally **not used** | Reject-band content aliases |
| Critical frequency preservation | **Exact** at $\omega = \Omega T$ | Designers' favorite |
| Stability preservation | **Guaranteed** | $\text{LHP} \to \text{Inside unit circle}$ |
| Computational complexity | Low — PFE is straightforward | Good for real-time DSP |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Impulse Invariance Design Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram traces the entire design pipeline from analog specification to digital realization:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Analog Filter Specification H_a s"] --> B["Step 1 Obtain Prototype H_a s in s domain"]
    B --> C["Step 2 Partial Fraction Expansion of H_a s"]
    C --> D["Step 3 Inverse Laplace Transform to Get h_a t"]
    D --> E["Step 4 Sample h_a t at nT to Get h_d n"]
    E --> F["Step 5 Apply Z Transform to Get H_d z"]
    F --> G["Step 6 Verify Stability via Pole Magnitude Check"]
    G --> H["Step 7 Realize via Direct Form I or II Structure"]

    I["Input: Desired Digital Cutoff omega_c"] --> J["Compute Analog Cutoff Omega_c equals omega_c over T"]
    J --> B
```

## 4.2 The s-Plane to z-Plane Pole Mapping Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S_PLANE["S PLANE CONTINUOUS TIME"]
        A1["Analog Pole s_k = sigma + j Omega"]
        A2["Left Half Plane sigma less than 0 = STABLE"]
        A3["Imag Axis sigma equals 0 = MARGINAL"]
        A4["Right Half Plane sigma greater than 0 = UNSTABLE"]
    end

    subgraph Z_PLANE["Z PLANE DISCRETE TIME"]
        B1["Digital Pole z_k = exp s_k T"]
        B2["Inside Unit Circle magnitude less than 1 = STABLE"]
        B3["On Unit Circle magnitude equals 1 = MARGINAL"]
        B4["Outside Unit Circle magnitude greater than 1 = UNSTABLE"]
    end

    A1 -->|"Mapping z = exp sT"| B1
    A2 -->|"Preserved"| B2
    A3 -->|"Preserved"| B3
    A4 -->|"Preserved"| B4
```

## 4.3 The Aliasing Phenomenon — Frequency-Domain Replica Summation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Analog Spectrum H_a j Omega"] --> P2["Replicate Periodically at Multiples of 2 pi over T"]
    P2 --> P3["Scale by 1 over T"]
    P3 --> P4["Sum All Replicas to Obtain H_d exp j omega"]
    P4 --> P5{"Are Replicas Overlapping?"}
    P5 -- NO --> P6["No Aliasing Ideal Case H_d = 1 over T times H_a"]
    P5 -- YES --> P7["Aliasing Occurs Distortion of Frequency Response"]
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow Inside the Digital Filter Realization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Input x n"] --> SUM["Summer Node"]
    SUM --> DELAY1["Z to Power minus 1 Delay"]
    DELAY1 --> DELAY2["Z to Power minus 1 Delay"]
    DELAY2 --> GAIN_A1["Multiply by a1"]
    GAIN_A1 --> SUM
    DELAY2 --> GAIN_A2["Multiply by a2"]
    GAIN_A2 --> SUM
    SUM --> GAIN_B0["Multiply by b0"]
    GAIN_B0 --> Y["Output y n"]

    X --> GAIN_B0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The "Z⁻¹" block represents a unit delay element in the Direct Form-II realization of $H_d(z) = b_0 / (1 - a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2})$. The negative feedback through $a_1$ and $a_2$ implements the poles.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Impulse Invariance Method. State its primary limitation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Impulse Invariance is a technique for designing an IIR digital filter from a prototype analog filter, in which the digital filter's impulse response $h_d[n]$ is obtained by sampling the analog filter's impulse response $h_a(t)$ at uniform time intervals $T$, such that $h_d[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$. The z-transform $H_d(z)$ of the sampled sequence gives the digital filter transfer function.

**Limitation (1 mark):** The primary limitation is **aliasing** in the frequency domain. Since $H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_k H_a(j(\omega - 2\pi k)/T)$, the digital spectrum is a periodic summation of analog spectrum replicas, which overlap if the analog filter is not strictly bandlimited. Hence, the method is **unsuitable for highpass and bandstop filters**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*An analog filter has a transfer function $H_a(s) = \dfrac{2}{s+2}$. Using the impulse invariance method with $T = 0.5$ s, determine the digital filter $H_d(z)$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify pole and residue: $R = 2$, $s_1 = -2$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Map pole: $z_1 = e^{s_1 T} = e^{-2 \times 0.5} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Construct $H_d(z)$:

$$
H_d(z) = \frac{R}{1 - e^{-1} z^{-1}} = \frac{2}{1 - 0.3679\, z^{-1}}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Re-modeled]**
*Explain the Impulse Invariance method of designing IIR digital filters from analog filters. Derive the relationship between analog and digital poles. Discuss the effect of this mapping on stability.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of Impulse Invariance (2 marks):**

Impulse Invariance maps an analog filter $H_a(s)$ to a digital filter $H_d(z)$ by sampling the analog impulse response. If $h_a(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{H_a(s)\}$, then $h_d[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$

**2. Design Procedure (2 marks):**

The standard design steps are:
- Express $H_a(s)$ in partial fraction form.
- Find the inverse Laplace transform $h_a(t)$.
- Sample $h_a(t)$ at $t = nT$ to get $h_d[n]$.
- Apply the z-transform to get $H_d(z)$.

**3. Pole Mapping Derivation (2 marks):**

Starting from a single-pole section $H_a(s) = R/(s - s_k)$:

$$
h_a(t) = R e^{s_k t} u(t) \implies h_d[n] = R e^{s_k nT} u[n] = R (e^{s_k T})^n u[n]
$$

$$
H_d(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} R (e^{s_k T})^n z^{-n} = \frac{R}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}
$$

Thus the analog pole $s_k$ maps to $z_k = e^{s_k T}$.

**4. Stability (1 mark):**

A stable analog filter has $\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$, giving $\vert z_k \vert = e^{\text{Re}(s_k) T} < 1$, so the digital pole lies *inside* the unit circle. **Stability is preserved.**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Re-modeled]**
*Convert the analog filter $H_a(s) = \dfrac{0.5}{(s+0.5)^2 + 4}$ to a digital filter using the impulse invariance method with $T = 1$ s. Show that the digital filter is stable.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Identify poles:**

$$
(s + 0.5)^2 + 4 = 0 \implies s + 0.5 = \pm j 2 \implies s_{1,2} = -0.5 \pm j 2
$$

So $\sigma = -0.5$, $\Omega = \pm 2$ rad/s.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — PFE and inverse Laplace:**

Using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/((s+a)^2 + b^2)\} = (1/b) e^{-at} \sin(bt) u(t)$:

$$
h_a(t) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{2} e^{-0.5 t} \sin(2t) u(t) = 0.25 e^{-0.5 t} \sin(2t) u(t)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Sample and apply z-transform:**

Using $\mathcal{Z}\{e^{-anT} \sin(\Omega_0 nT) u[n]\} = \dfrac{e^{-aT} \sin(\Omega_0 T) z^{-1}}{1 - 2 e^{-aT} \cos(\Omega_0 T) z^{-1} + e^{-2aT} z^{-2}}$:

With $a = 0.5$, $\Omega_0 = 2$, $T = 1$:

- $e^{-aT} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$
- $\sin(\Omega_0 T) = \sin(2) \approx 0.9093$
- $\cos(\Omega_0 T) = \cos(2) \approx -0.4161$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_d(z) &= 0.25 \cdot \frac{0.6065 \cdot 0.9093 \cdot z^{-1}}{1 - 2(0.6065)(-0.4161) z^{-1} + (0.6065)^2 z^{-2}} \\
       &= \frac{0.1379\, z^{-1}}{1 + 0.5048\, z^{-1} + 0.3679\, z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Stability check:**

Pole magnitudes: $\vert z_k \vert = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065 < 1$ for both poles. **The digital filter is stable.** ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Re-modeled]**
*Discuss the aliasing problem in the impulse invariance method. Why is this method preferred for lowpass filter design but not for highpass filter design? Justify with the frequency-domain relationship.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Frequency Relationship (3 marks):**

$$
H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} H_a\left(j\frac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T}\right)
$$

This shows that the digital spectrum is an **infinite periodic sum** of analog spectrum replicas, each shifted by $2\pi/T$ in analog frequency and scaled by $1/T$.

**2. Aliasing Mechanism (2 marks):**

If $H_a(j\Omega)$ is **not strictly bandlimited** to $\vert \Omega \vert < \pi/T$, the shifted replicas overlap, distorting the digital frequency response. The overlap (aliasing) is most severe where the analog filter has significant *transition band* and *stopband* energy.

**3. Why Lowpass Works, Highpass Fails (2 marks):**

- **Lowpass filters** have a stopband starting at $\Omega_c \ll \pi/T$. Beyond $\pi/T$, $H_a(j\Omega) \approx 0$, so replicas don't overlap → *no aliasing in passband*.
- **Highpass filters** have a stopband at $\Omega = 0$ and passband extending to $\infty$. The replicas overlap heavily, distorting the passband shape.

**Conclusion:** Impulse Invariance is mathematically ideal *only* for bandlimited lowpass and bandpass designs.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Re-modeled]**
*A digital Butterworth lowpass filter is required to have a 3 dB cutoff at $\omega_c = 0.2\pi$ rad/sample. Using the impulse invariance method with $T = 1$ s and a first-order prototype $H_a(s) = \Omega_c / (s + \Omega_c)$, design $H_d(z)$ and plot the pole-zero diagram description.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Analog cutoff:**

$$
\Omega_c = \omega_c / T = 0.2\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 0.6283 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Analog prototype:**

$$
H_a(s) = \frac{0.2\pi}{s + 0.2\pi}
$$

Pole: $s_1 = -0.2\pi \approx -0.6283$. Residue: $R = 0.2\pi \approx 0.6283$.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Pole mapping:**

$$
z_1 = e^{s_1 T} = e^{-0.2\pi} \approx e^{-0.6283} \approx 0.5335
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Digital transfer function:**

$$
H_d(z) = \frac{0.2\pi}{1 - 0.5335\, z^{-1}} = \frac{0.6283}{1 - 0.5335\, z^{-1}}
$$

**Pole-Zero Description:**
- **Pole:** $z_1 = 0.5335$ (on the real axis, inside the unit circle).
- **Zero:** $z = 0$ (from the $z^{-1}$ structure of the numerator after division).
- The pole is at distance 0.5335 from origin, on the positive real axis → stable filter. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Impulse Invariance problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the PFE step:** Many students directly substitute $s = (1-z^{-1})/T$ (which is bilinear, not impulse invariance!). This is a *fatal conceptual error* and earns 0 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the $T$ factor:** The amplitude scaling $h_d[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ is often missed, leading to incorrect gain. Examiners will deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **No stability verification:** Always check $\vert z_k \vert < 1$ and write it down explicitly. KTU expects it.
> 4. **Aliasing ignored:** In design questions, students must comment on whether the analog filter is bandlimited. If not, state that impulse invariance is *not* the right method.
> 5. **Sign errors in $\cos(\Omega_0 T)$ term:** For complex conjugate poles, a sign mistake in $a_1 = -2e^{-aT}\cos(\Omega_0 T)$ propagates everywhere. Double-check with a calculator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Impulse Invariance:**

- **Definition (must memorize):** $h_d[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ — *sample the analog impulse response*.
- **Pole Mapping Rule:** $z_k = e^{s_k T}$ — *exponential map from s-plane to z-plane*.
- **Stability:** LHP poles ($\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$) → poles inside unit circle ($\vert z_k \vert < 1$).
- **Frequency Response:** $H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_k H_a(j(\omega - 2\pi k)/T)$ — *aliased, scaled sum*.
- **Best Use Case:** **Lowpass** and **narrow-band bandpass** filters with bandwidth $\ll \pi/T$.
- **Avoid For:** Highpass, bandstop, and wideband bandpass designs.
- **Design Procedure:** PFE → Inverse Laplace → Sample at $nT$ → Apply Z-transform.
- **Critical Frequency:** $\omega = \Omega T$ — *exact preservation of digital cutoff frequency*.
- **Difference from Bilinear:** Impulse Invariance is a *direct time-domain sampling* method; Bilinear is a *frequency-warping algebraic substitution*. They are **NOT interchangeable**.
- **For 2nd-order sections with complex poles:** Use the formula
  $H_d(z) = \dfrac{b_0 z^{-1}}{1 - 2e^{-aT}\cos(\Omega_0 T) z^{-1} + e^{-2aT} z^{-2}}$
  for terms like $R/((s+a)^2 + \Omega_0^2)$.
- **Biomedical relevance:** ECG, EEG, EMG filtering — all benefit from this technique for narrowband lowpass stages.
- **Key pitfall:** Always verify the analog filter is **strictly bandlimited** before claiming no aliasing.
- **Exam tip:** When asked to "convert" or "design using impulse invariance," always write the 5-step procedure, then perform the pole mapping, then verify stability — this captures full marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Takeaway:** Impulse Invariance is a *physically intuitive*, *time-domain-faithful*, and *computationally simple* IIR filter design method — but its *aliasing vulnerability* restricts it to **narrowband, lowpass, and bandpass applications**, which fortunately dominate the front-end stages of biomedical signal acquisition chains.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IIR Digital Filters for ECG Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IIR Digital Filters for ECG Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) Digital Filter** is a recursive discrete-time linear shift-invariant (LTI) system whose output depends on both the present and past input samples *and* the past output samples. Mathematically, it is described by the linear constant-coefficient difference equation:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k] \; - \; \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k]$$

where the presence of the feedback terms $a_k y[n-k]$ is what creates the *infinite* duration impulse response (theoretically non-zero for all $n \geq 0$ even after a single unit impulse input).

In the context of **Electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis**, IIR filters are the workhorse of the front-end signal conditioning chain. A raw ECG trace, sampled typically at **f_s = 360 Hz to 1000 Hz**, is contaminated by baseline wander (< **0.5 Hz**), powerline interference (**50 Hz** in India, 60 Hz in the US), high-frequency EMG/muscle noise, and electrode motion artefacts. IIR filters are deployed to isolate the diagnostic QRS complex band (roughly **0.5 Hz – 40 Hz** clinical, **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** wideband per AHA recommendations) by rejecting all out-of-band components with sharp roll-off and minimal computational load — a critical requirement for wearable Holter monitors and real-time ICU telemetry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim expected in answers):**
> An IIR filter is a recursive digital filter characterized by a rational system function $H(z) = \dfrac{B(z)}{A(z)}$ where the denominator polynomial $A(z) \neq 1$, giving it feedback, infinite memory, and a magnitude response that can be made sharper (with fewer coefficients) than its FIR counterpart at the cost of non-linear phase.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Echo-Cancelling Bathroom":**
> 
> Imagine a microphone in a tiled bathroom. When you clap once, you hear one sharp clap followed by a long, decaying *echo* bouncing off the walls. That echo is the *impulse response* — and it never fully ends. This is exactly how an **IIR filter** behaves mathematically: one input event (a single QRS spike) generates an output that "rings" forever (in theory) as the energy circulates through the feedback path.
> 
> Now contrast this with a **FIR filter** (the "dead room"): one clap produces exactly one clap, and then absolute silence. No memory, no echo, no feedback. FIR filters are great for phase-sensitive applications (like audio mastering), but IIR filters are preferred for **ECG** because they deliver the **same roll-off steepness with 5–10× fewer multiplications** — vital for low-power battery-operated wearable patches.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics for ECG

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| ECG Sampling Rate (f_s) | **360 Hz** (clinical) / **500 Hz** (research) | Nyquist limit = 180 Hz / 250 Hz |
| Mains Powerline Frequency (India) | **50 Hz** | Notch filter center frequency |
| Diagnostic ECG Bandwidth | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** | AHA standard (1990) |
| Clinical Monitoring Bandwidth | **0.5 Hz – 40 Hz** | Adult diagnostic standard |
| QRS Dominant Frequency | **8 Hz – 20 Hz** | Energy concentration |
| P-Wave Frequency | **0.7 Hz – 10 Hz** | Slow atrial depolarization |
| T-Wave Frequency | **0.7 Hz – 10 Hz** | Ventricular repolarization |
| Max ECG Amplitude (R-wave) | **± 5 mV** | Patient safety (defibrillation) |
| ADC Resolution | **≥ 12 bits** | To resolve 10 µV diagnostic detail |

## 1.4 Why IIR (and not FIR) for ECG? — The Design Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Cognitive Level: Understand (L2) & Compare (L4)**
> 
> The selection of IIR over FIR for ECG is a classic *engineering trade-off* between **transition band width (steepness)** and **computational complexity (power consumption)**. The relative comparison is:

| Property | IIR Filter | FIR Filter |
|----------|------------|------------|
| Phase Response | **Non-linear (distortion)** | **Linear (delay only)** |
| Stability | Conditional (poles inside unit circle) | Always stable (no poles) |
| Multiplications per sample | Few (5–10 for 2nd-order) | Many (50–100 for equivalent) |
| Roll-off steepness per order | High | Low |
| Memory length | Short | Long (≈ 10–20× IIR) |
| Suitability for ECG | **Excellent (front-end cleaning)** | Excellent (diagnostic post-processing) |
| Battery cost on wearable | **Low** | **High** |

For real-time **R-peak detection** on a smartwatch, the IIR's low computational footprint dominates the design decision, even though its non-linear phase is acceptable because the diagnostic QRS is robust to mild phase distortion (whereas ST-segment morphology — used for ischemia detection — is *not*, and there FIR or zero-phase IIR is mandatory).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IIR Difference Equation — Anatomy of Recursion

The general **Nth-order IIR difference equation** is:

$$y[n] = \underbrace{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]}_{\text{Feed-Forward (FIR Part)}} \; - \; \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k]}_{\text{Feedback (IIR Part)}}$$

**Operational interpretation of each term:**

- $b_0\,x[n]$ — the direct (un-delayed) input term, like the direct path in a signal flow graph.
- $b_k\,x[n-k]$ — the *non-recursive* part; this is essentially a FIR filter. It is responsible for adding **zeros** to the system function.
- $a_k\,y[n-k]$ — the *recursive* part; this introduces the feedback that gives the filter its **infinite memory** and its **poles**. Without any $a_k \neq 0$, the filter degenerates to FIR.
- The minus sign in front of the feedback sum is a **convention** inherited from analog prototype analysis; in some textbooks, the equation is written as $y[n] = \sum b_k x[n-k] + \sum a_k y[n-k]$ with $a_k$ carrying the negative sign. Always check the sign convention!

## 2.2 Transfer Function & Z-Domain Analysis

Taking the Z-transform of the difference equation (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

**Key properties of $H(z)$:**

1. **Poles** are the roots of $A(z) = 0$. For stability in a causal filter, **every pole must lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($|z_k| < 1$ for all $k$).
2. **Zeros** are the roots of $B(z) = 0$. They may lie anywhere in the z-plane; their positions shape the stop-band.
3. The **frequency response** is obtained by evaluating $H(z)$ on the unit circle: $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z)\big|_{z = e^{j\omega}}$.
4. The **magnitude response** $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ gives the gain (attenuation or amplification) at each digital frequency $\omega \in [0, \pi]$.

## 2.3 Stability Criterion — Pole Location Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) Stability Condition:**
> 
> A causal IIR filter is BIBO-stable if and only if **all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e. $|p_k| < 1$ for every pole $p_k$.
> 
> **Why?** Each pole $p_k$ contributes a term $C_k p_k^n$ to the impulse response. If $|p_k| < 1$, this term decays geometrically to zero. If $|p_k| \geq 1$, the impulse response grows unboundedly and the filter "explodes" — fatal in real-time ECG where a single noisy sample could crash the output permanently.

A useful companion criterion: for a 2nd-order IIR section with denominator $1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}$, the Schur-Cohn stability triangle is:

$$|a_2| < 1, \quad a_1 + a_2 < 1, \quad -a_1 + a_2 < 1$$

## 2.4 IIR Design Workflow for ECG Applications

The standard KTU-prescribed workflow for designing an IIR filter for ECG is:

1. **Specify** the desired digital passband edge $\omega_p$ and stopband edge $\omega_s$ (in rad/sample) using the ECG clinical bandwidth.
2. **Pre-warp** the digital frequencies to analog frequencies $\Omega_p, \Omega_s$ using the frequency mapping of the chosen transformation.
3. **Design an analog prototype** filter $H_a(s)$ (Butterworth / Chebyshev / Elliptic) meeting the analog specifications.
4. **Apply the transformation** (Bilinear / Impulse Invariance) to map $H_a(s)$ to $H(z)$.
5. **Validate stability** by checking pole locations.
6. **Test on a real ECG recording** (e.g., MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database) and measure SNR improvement.

## 2.5 The Two KTU-Mandated Transformation Methods

### Method A: Bilinear Transformation (BZT) — *Preferred for ECG*

The bilinear transformation maps the *entire* left-half s-plane onto the interior of the unit circle via the conformal map:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad z = \frac{1 + \frac{T}{2}s}{1 - \frac{T}{2}s}$$

where $T = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period. The corresponding **frequency warping** relation is:

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \omega = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{\Omega T}{2}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Frequency Warping is Non-Linear!** Low analog frequencies map approximately linearly, but high analog frequencies are severely compressed. *Always pre-warp* the critical digital cutoff frequencies *before* designing the analog prototype, never after.

### Method B: Impulse Invariance Method (IIM)

The impulse invariance method samples the impulse response of the analog prototype at the digital sampling instants:

$$h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad H(z) = T \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{\text{Res}\{H_a(s)\}_{s=p_k}}{1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1}}$$

It preserves the *time-domain shape* of the impulse response but suffers from **aliasing** of frequency content — making it *unsuitable* for high-frequency ECG components and high-Q notch filters. KTU typically marks IIM as "inappropriate for ECG above ~ 100 Hz" in the 14-mark design questions.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]$ | General IIR difference equation | $M \leq N$ for typical use |
| 2 | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Transfer function in z-domain | Rational, causal |
| 3 | $\lvert p_k \rvert < 1$ for all $k$ | BIBO stability condition (causal case) | Strict inequality |
| 4 | $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z)\big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ | Frequency response on unit circle | $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ |
| 5 | $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$ | Digital angular frequency | rad/sample |
| 6 | $s = \dfrac{2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | Bilinear transformation | $T = 1/f_s$ |
| 7 | $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | Frequency warping (BZT) | Apply **before** analog design |
| 8 | $h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ | Impulse invariance | $T \to 0$ ideal |
| 9 | $\lvert H_a(j\Omega) \rvert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}}$ | Butterworth magnitude squared | $N$ = order, $\Omega_c$ = cutoff |
| 10 | $p_k = \Omega_c \exp\!\left[j\dfrac{\pi}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2k-1}{N}\right)\right]$, $k = 1,\dots,N$ | Butterworth left-half-plane poles | Equally spaced on circle |
| 11 | $A_p = -20\log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)$, $A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$ | Passband and stopband ripple in dB | $A_p$, $A_s$ in dB |
| 12 | $N \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}\right)}{2 \log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$ | Minimum Butterworth filter order | Round up to integer |
| 13 | $H_{\text{notch}}(z) = \dfrac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | 2nd-order digital notch filter | $r \approx 0.95$ – $0.99$ for sharp notch |
| 14 | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s$ | Notch angular frequency | $f_0 = 50$ Hz (India) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of IIR Filters in the Real World:**
> - **Wearable ECG patches (Apple Watch, Fitbit Sense):** Use IIR notch + low-pass to remove 50/60 Hz hum before R-peak detection.
> - **Defibrillators & Patient Monitors:** Use IIR bandpass (0.5–40 Hz) to display clean ECG on the LCD.
> - **Cardiac MRI gating:** IIR filters remove gradient-switching artefacts before R-wave triggering.
> - **Implantable pacemakers:** IIR filters isolate the intracardiac electrogram (IEGM) from myopotentials.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Design Example A — 50 Hz Notch IIR Filter for ECG Mains Rejection (Full Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Style: 14-mark design problem with full step-by-step evaluation. This is the *exact* structure KTU examiners expect.**

**Problem Statement:**
Design a 2nd-order IIR notch filter to remove **50 Hz** powerline interference from an ECG signal sampled at **f_s = 250 Hz**. Use the Bilinear Transformation method with a Butterworth prototype. The notch should have a quality factor $Q = 30$ (sharp, narrow notch) and provide at least **40 dB** attenuation at 50 Hz.

### Step 1 — Convert the notch frequency to digital angular frequency

We use the relation between the physical frequency $f_0$ (Hz) and the digital angular frequency $\omega_0$ (radians/sample):

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{250} = \frac{100\pi}{250} = \frac{2\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample}$$

Numerically:

$$\omega_0 = 0.4\pi \approx 1.2566 \text{ rad/sample}$$

### Step 2 — Pre-warp the digital frequency to the analog prototype domain

The bilinear transformation requires pre-warping *before* analog filter design because the non-linear mapping $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$ distorts the frequency axis. Here $T = 1/f_s = 1/250 = 0.004$ s.

$$\Omega_0 = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_0}{2}\right) = 2 \times 250 \times \tan\!\left(\frac{0.4\pi}{2}\right) = 500 \times \tan(0.2\pi)$$

Evaluating the tangent:

$$\tan(0.2\pi) = \tan(36^{\circ}) \approx 0.7265$$

Therefore:

$$\Omega_0 \approx 500 \times 0.7265 = 363.27 \text{ rad/s}$$

This is the analog frequency at which the *analog prototype* notch must be centred to yield a digital notch at exactly 50 Hz after bilinear mapping.

### Step 3 — Design the Analog Notch Prototype (2nd-Order Butterworth)

A 2nd-order analog notch filter has the canonical transfer function:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{s^2 + \dfrac{\Omega_0}{Q}\,s + \Omega_0^2}$$

Substituting $\Omega_0 = 363.27$ rad/s and $Q = 30$:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{s^2 + (363.27)^2}{s^2 + \dfrac{363.27}{30}\,s + (363.27)^2} = \frac{s^2 + 131{,}965}{s^2 + 12.11\,s + 131{,}965}$$

where $(363.27)^2 = 131{,}965$ (rounded), and $\Omega_0 / Q = 363.27 / 30 = 12.11$ rad/s.

### Step 4 — Apply the Bilinear Transformation

Substitute $s = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ with $2/T = 500$:

$$s = 500 \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$$

To do this systematically, multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + z^{-1})^2$ to clear the squared denominator:

**Numerator:**

$$s^2 + \Omega_0^2 = \left(500\right)^2 \frac{(1 - z^{-1})^2}{(1 + z^{-1})^2} + 131{,}965$$

$$= \frac{250{,}000\,(1 - z^{-1})^2 + 131{,}965\,(1 + z^{-1})^2}{(1 + z^{-1})^2}$$

Expanding $(1 - z^{-1})^2 = 1 - 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}$ and $(1 + z^{-1})^2 = 1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}$:

$$= \frac{250{,}000(1 - 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}) + 131{,}965(1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2})}{(1+z^{-1})^2}$$

$$= \frac{(250{,}000 + 131{,}965) + (-500{,}000 + 263{,}930)z^{-1} + (250{,}000 + 131{,}965)z^{-2}}{(1+z^{-1})^2}$$

$$= \frac{381{,}965 - 236{,}070\,z^{-1} + 381{,}965\,z^{-2}}{(1+z^{-1})^2}$$

**Denominator:**

$$s^2 + \tfrac{\Omega_0}{Q}s + \Omega_0^2 = \left(500\right)^2 \frac{(1 - z^{-1})^2}{(1+z^{-1})^2} + 12.11 \cdot 500 \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} + 131{,}965$$

Multiply through by $(1 + z^{-1})^2$:

$$= 250{,}000(1 - z^{-1})^2 + 6055(1 - z^{-1})(1 + z^{-1}) + 131{,}965(1 + z^{-1})^2$$

Note that $(1 - z^{-1})(1 + z^{-1}) = 1 - z^{-2}$:

$$= 250{,}000(1 - 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}) + 6055(1 - z^{-2}) + 131{,}965(1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2})$$

Combining like powers of $z^{-1}$:

- $z^0$: $250{,}000 + 6055 + 131{,}965 = 388{,}020$
- $z^{-1}$: $-500{,}000 + 263{,}930 = -236{,}070$
- $z^{-2}$: $250{,}000 - 6055 + 131{,}965 = 375{,}910$

So:

$$\text{Denominator} = 388{,}020 - 236{,}070\,z^{-1} + 375{,}910\,z^{-2}$$

### Step 5 — Form the Digital Transfer Function $H(z)$

The $(1 + z^{-1})^2$ factor cancels between numerator and denominator, giving:

$$H(z) = \frac{381{,}965 - 236{,}070\,z^{-1} + 381{,}965\,z^{-2}}{388{,}020 - 236{,}070\,z^{-1} + 375{,}910\,z^{-2}}$$

Normalizing by the leading denominator coefficient (so the constant term of $A(z) = 1$, the standard form):

$$H(z) = \frac{0.9843 - 0.6083\,z^{-1} + 0.9843\,z^{-2}}{1 - 0.6083\,z^{-1} + 0.9687\,z^{-2}}$$

**Normalisation verification:**
- $381{,}965 / 388{,}020 = 0.9843$ ✓
- $-236{,}070 / 388{,}020 = -0.6083$ ✓
- $381{,}965 / 388{,}020 = 0.9843$ ✓
- $-236{,}070 / 388{,}020 = -0.6083$ ✓
- $375{,}910 / 388{,}020 = 0.9687$ ✓

### Step 6 — Verify Stability by Computing Pole Locations

Poles are roots of $1 - 0.6083\,z^{-1} + 0.9687\,z^{-2} = 0$, equivalently $z^2 - 0.6083\,z + 0.9687 = 0$. Using the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{0.6083 \pm \sqrt{(-0.6083)^2 - 4(1)(0.9687)}}{2} = \frac{0.6083 \pm \sqrt{0.3700 - 3.8748}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{0.6083 \pm j\,1.863}{2} = 0.3042 \pm j\,0.9315$$

Magnitude of each pole:

$$|p| = \sqrt{(0.3042)^2 + (0.9315)^2} = \sqrt{0.0925 + 0.8677} = \sqrt{0.9602} \approx 0.9799$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Check Passed:** $|p| = 0.9799 < 1$ — the pole lies *just inside* the unit circle, exactly as expected for a sharp, high-Q notch filter (poles approach the unit circle as $Q \to \infty$).

### Step 7 — Frequency Response Verification

At the notch frequency $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $\omega_0 = 0.4\pi$, so $z = e^{j0.4\pi} = \cos(0.4\pi) + j\sin(0.4\pi) = 0.309 + j\,0.951$.

Substituting into the numerator $(1 - e^{-j\omega_0})^2 + (\Omega_0 T / 2)^2 (1 + e^{-j\omega_0})^2$:

This is the design of a *perfect* digital notch — by construction, the magnitude response is exactly zero at $\omega_0$, i.e. $|H(e^{j0.4\pi})| = 0$, providing theoretically **infinite attenuation** at 50 Hz. The 3-dB bandwidth of the notch is $\Delta\omega \approx \omega_0 / Q = 0.4\pi / 30 \approx 0.0419$ rad/sample, which is approximately $\Delta f \approx 1.67$ Hz — narrow enough to preserve ECG spectral content but deep enough to kill the 50 Hz line hum.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Note:** In real hardware, finite word-length (e.g., 16-bit fixed-point arithmetic) reduces the practical notch depth to about 40–60 dB. In software implementations with 32-bit float, depths > 100 dB are routinely achieved.

## 3.2 Design Example B — Low-Pass Butterworth for ECG Baseline-Wander Removal

**Problem:** Design a digital IIR low-pass Butterworth filter to remove baseline wander (cutoff = **0.5 Hz**) from ECG sampled at **f_s = 500 Hz**, with $A_p = 1$ dB passband ripple and $A_s = 40$ dB stopband attenuation at $\omega_s = 0.04\pi$ rad/sample (i.e., 10 Hz).

### Step 1 — Convert digital frequencies to Hz and then to rad/s

$$\omega_p = 2\pi(0.5)/500 = 0.002\pi \text{ rad/sample} \quad \rightarrow \quad f_p = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\omega_s = 0.04\pi \text{ rad/sample} \quad \rightarrow \quad f_s^{\text{analog}} = 10 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2 — Pre-warp using bilinear transform

For a low-pass with $2/T = 2 \times 500 = 1000$:

$$\Omega_p = 1000 \tan(0.001\pi) \approx 1000 \times 0.00314 = 3.14 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Omega_s = 1000 \tan(0.02\pi) \approx 1000 \times 0.0629 = 62.9 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the filter order

Using the Butterworth order formula:

$$N \geq \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}\right)}{2 \log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)} = \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{4} - 1}{10^{0.1} - 1}\right)}{2 \log_{10}(62.9/3.14)}$$

Numerator: $\log_{10}(9999 / 0.2589) = \log_{10}(38{,}622) = 4.587$

Denominator: $2 \log_{10}(20.03) = 2 \times 1.3017 = 2.603$

$$N \geq 4.587 / 2.603 = 1.762 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = 2$$

### Step 4 — Build the 2nd-order Butterworth analog prototype

The normalized Butterworth polynomial for $N = 2$ has left-half-plane poles at $s = -0.7071 \pm j\,0.7071$. Scaling to cutoff $\Omega_c = \Omega_p = 3.14$ rad/s:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\Omega_c\,s + \Omega_c^2} = \frac{9.86}{s^2 + 4.44\,s + 9.86}$$

### Step 5 — Apply bilinear transform

After substitution $s = 1000(1 - z^{-1})/(1 + z^{-1})$ and clearing the common denominator (procedure identical to Example A), the final digital filter is:

$$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{0.000247 + 0.000494\,z^{-1} + 0.000247\,z^{-2}}{1 - 1.987\,z^{-1} + 0.9872\,z^{-2}}}$$

**Pole locations:** $z = 0.9935 \pm j\,0.0167$, magnitude $|p| = 0.9936$ — comfortably inside the unit circle, confirming stability.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — IIR Prototype Selection for ECG

| ECG Artifact / Feature | Frequency Range (Hz) | IIR Filter Type | Recommended Order | Approximation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Baseline wander (respiration) | 0.05 – 0.5 | High-pass | 2 | Butterworth (maximally flat) |
| P-wave preservation | 0.7 – 10 | Low-pass (or bandpass) | 2 | Butterworth |
| QRS detection | 8 – 20 | Bandpass | 4 | Chebyshev Type I (steeper) |
| Powerline interference (50 Hz India) | 50 ± 2 | Notch | 2 | Butterworth (Q ≈ 25–35) |
| High-frequency EMG noise | > 40 | Low-pass | 4 | Elliptic (sharpest roll-off) |
| Pacemaker spike detection | ~ 1 ms pulse | High-pass | 1 | Bessel (linear phase) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ECG Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Patient Electrodes] --> A2[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]
    A2 --> A3[Analog Anti-Alias LPF fc 150 Hz]
    A3 --> A4[ADC 12 bit fs 500 Hz]
    A4 --> B1[Digital IIR High Pass fc 0.5 Hz Baseline Removal]
    B1 --> B2[Digital IIR Notch f0 50 Hz Mains Rejection]
    B2 --> B3[Digital IIR Low Pass fc 40 Hz EMG Suppression]
    B3 --> C1[QRS R Peak Detector Pan Tompkins]
    C1 --> C2[Heart Rate BPM Display]
    C2 --> D1[Telemetry BLE to Smartphone]
    B3 --> D2[ST Segment Morphology Analysis FIR Post Processing]
    D2 --> D3[Ischemia Alarm Trigger]
```

**Reading guide:** The signal flows top-to-bottom from the patient to the diagnostic output. Notice the **two-stage IIR cleaning** (B1 → B2 → B3) followed by a *switch in filter class* to **FIR** (D2) for the ST-segment analysis, where linear phase is mandatory to preserve the subtle ST-elevation/depression morphology used to diagnose myocardial infarction.

## 4.2 IIR Filter Structure — Direct Form II (Transposed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[Input x n] --> sum1((Sum +))
    sum1 --> delay1[/z⁻¹/]
    delay1 --> delay2[/z⁻¹/]
    delay2 --> sum1
    sum1 --> gain1[/a1/]
    sum1 --> gain2[/a2/]
    gain1 --> sum1
    gain2 --> sum1
    sum1 --> gain3[/b0/]
    sum1 --> gain4[/b1/]
    sum1 --> gain5[/b2/]
    gain4 --> delay3[/z⁻¹/]
    delay5[/z⁻¹/] --> gain5
    delay3 --> delay5
    gain3 --> sum2((Sum +))
    gain4 --> sum2
    gain5 --> sum2
    sum2 --> outNode[Output y n]
```

**Reading guide:** The two delay blocks at the top form the **state line** shared by the feedback and feed-forward paths — this is the defining feature of *Direct Form II*. Notice that:

- The feedback coefficients $a_1$ and $a_2$ inject energy *back* into the summer (note the arrows returning to `sum1`).
- The feed-forward coefficients $b_0, b_1, b_2$ tap off the same state line (after a small delay chain) and sum into `sum2` to produce $y[n]$.
- Direct Form II uses **only N delay elements** (here N = 2) regardless of M — the most *memory-efficient* canonical form.

## 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot — 50 Hz Notch Filter (Designed in Section 3.1)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PoleZeroPlot
        direction TB
        uc[Unit Circle radius 1]
        pole1((Pole 0.98 at angle 0.4 pi))
        pole2((Pole 0.98 at angle minus 0.4 pi))
        zero1((Zero 1.0 at angle 0.4 pi))
        zero2((Zero 1.0 at angle minus 0.4 pi))
    end
    pole1 -.- zero1
    pole2 -.- zero2
```

**Reading guide:** In this schematic, the four critical points are:

- **Two poles** at magnitude $r = 0.98$, angles $\pm 0.4\pi$ — placed *very close* to the unit circle, *inside*, to create a sharp resonance (the inverse of the notch — a peak in the *denominator* that creates a *dip* in the magnitude response).
- **Two zeros** at magnitude $1.0$, angles $\pm 0.4\pi$ — placed *exactly* on the unit circle at the notch frequency, forcing $|H(e^{j0.4\pi})| = 0$ regardless of pole placement.
- The pole-zero *alignment* on the radial spokes at $\pm 0.4\pi$ guarantees the notch is at exactly 50 Hz, while their *separation* (zeros on the circle, poles just inside) controls the *sharpness* of the notch.

## 4.4 Frequency Response of Cascaded ECG Filter (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    f0[Frequency Hz] --> g1[HPF fc 0.5 Hz]
    g1 --> g2[Notch f0 50 Hz Q 30]
    g2 --> g3[LPF fc 40 Hz]
    g3 --> out1[Clean ECG Output]
    subgraph Spectrum
        direction LR
        bw[Baseline Wander 0 to 0.5 Hz] -.removed.-> g1
        pl[Powerline 50 Hz Tone] -.removed.-> g2
        emg[EMG Noise above 40 Hz] -.removed.-> g3
    end
```

**Reading guide:** The pipeline operates sequentially in frequency: the high-pass first removes the slow baseline drift (caused by respiration and electrode polarization), the notch then excises the 50 Hz mains hum, and the low-pass finally smooths out the high-frequency muscle noise — leaving a clean ECG with sharp QRS complexes and faithfully preserved ST segments. This *cascaded IIR* structure is the de-facto industry standard in clinical patient monitors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
> 
> **Define an IIR digital filter. Why is it preferred over an FIR filter for real-time ECG signal processing on a wearable device?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for ECG context, 1 for comparison):**

An IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) digital filter is a recursive discrete-time system whose output $y[n]$ depends on both current/past inputs *and* past outputs, described by the difference equation $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]$. Its impulse response has theoretically infinite duration due to the recursive feedback terms. **(1 mark)**

In ECG processing on a wearable device, IIR filters are preferred because they require **5–10× fewer coefficients** than an FIR filter to achieve an equivalent magnitude response, drastically reducing the number of multiply-accumulate operations per sample. This translates directly to lower CPU clock cycles, lower battery drain, and longer operating life — critical constraints for a Holter monitor or smartwatch. **(1 mark)**

While IIR filters have non-linear phase, the **QRS complex** used for heart-rate calculation is robust to mild phase distortion, so the IIR's computational advantage outweighs the phase linearity disadvantage. **(1 mark)**

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`
> 
> **State the BIBO stability condition for a causal IIR digital filter. What is the role of frequency pre-warping in the bilinear transformation method of IIR filter design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A causal IIR digital filter is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if **all poles of its transfer function $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane, i.e. $|p_k| < 1$ for every pole $p_k$. **(1 mark)**

Equivalently, for a 2nd-order section with denominator $1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}$, the **Schur-Cohn stability triangle** requires $|a_2| < 1$, $a_1 + a_2 < 1$, and $-a_1 + a_2 < 1$. **(0.5 mark bonus for 2nd-order criterion)**

The bilinear transformation $s = (2/T)(1 - z^{-1})/(1 + z^{-1})$ maps the analog frequency $\Omega$ to the digital frequency $\omega$ via the non-linear warping relation $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$. **Frequency pre-warping** is the inverse step: given a desired digital cutoff $\omega_d$, we compute the analog cutoff $\Omega_a = (2/T)\tan(\omega_d/2)$ *first*, design the analog prototype to meet this *pre-warped* specification, and only then apply the bilinear substitution. **(1 mark)**

Pre-warping compensates for the compression of high frequencies inherent in the bilinear map, ensuring that the final digital filter has its passband/stopband edges at the *exact* digital frequencies specified — critical for ECG filters where the 0.5 Hz and 40 Hz cutoffs must be precise. **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Examination]`
> 
> **(a)** Design a 2nd-order IIR notch filter using the bilinear transformation method to remove **50 Hz** powerline interference from an ECG signal sampled at **f_s = 250 Hz**. The notch should have a quality factor **Q = 25**. Determine the digital transfer function $H(z)$ and verify its stability. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram how IIR filters are cascaded in a typical ECG signal conditioning chain. Specify the type and cutoff frequency of each filter. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A(a) — Complete Design

**Step 1 — Compute digital notch frequency:**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{250} = 0.4\pi \text{ rad/sample} \approx 1.2566 \text{ rad/sample} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Pre-warp to analog domain** (with $T = 0.004$ s, $2/T = 500$):

$$\Omega_0 = 500 \tan\!\left(\frac{0.4\pi}{2}\right) = 500 \tan(0.2\pi) = 500 \times 0.7265 \approx 363.27 \text{ rad/s} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Form analog prototype notch** (2nd-order, $Q = 25$):

$$H_a(s) = \frac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{s^2 + \dfrac{\Omega_0}{Q}\,s + \Omega_0^2} = \frac{s^2 + 131{,}965}{s^2 + 14.53\,s + 131{,}965} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply bilinear transformation** $s = 500(1 - z^{-1})/(1 + z^{-1})$. After clearing the $(1 + z^{-1})^2$ factor:

$$H(z) = \frac{0.9891 - 0.6162\,z^{-1} + 0.9891\,z^{-2}}{1 - 0.6162\,z^{-1} + 0.9782\,z^{-2}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Verify stability** — pole magnitudes:

$$z = 0.3081 \pm j\,0.9462 \quad \Rightarrow \quad |p| = \sqrt{0.0949 + 0.8953} = \sqrt{0.9902} \approx 0.9951$$

Since $|p| = 0.9951 < 1$, the filter is **BIBO stable**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Frequency response at notch:** by construction, $H(e^{j\omega_0}) = 0$, providing theoretically infinite attenuation at 50 Hz. The 3-dB bandwidth is $\Delta f = f_0/Q = 50/25 = 2$ Hz, ensuring that ECG spectral content above and below 50 Hz is preserved. **(1 mark)**

### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Cascade Architecture

A typical ECG signal conditioning chain comprises **three cascaded IIR stages** in the following order:

| Stage | Filter Type | Cutoff / Notch | Purpose | Order |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IIR High-pass** (Butterworth) | $f_c = 0.5$ Hz | Remove baseline wander (respiration, electrode drift) | 2 |
| 2 | **IIR Notch** (Butterworth) | $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $Q = 30$ | Remove powerline interference | 2 |
| 3 | **IIR Low-pass** (Butterworth or Chebyshev) | $f_c = 40$ Hz | Remove EMG noise, high-freq interference | 4 |

**Block diagram (as drawn in Section 4.1):**

```
Electrodes → Instrumentation Amp → Anti-alias LPF → ADC → [IIR HPF 0.5 Hz]
→ [IIR Notch 50 Hz] → [IIR LPF 40 Hz] → R-peak detector / ST analyzer
```

**Cascade rationale (3 marks for the explanation):**

1. **Order matters:** high-pass first removes the slow DC-like drift, which would otherwise saturate the subsequent stages. Notch in the middle isolates the 50 Hz tone. Low-pass last smooths the high-frequency muscle artefacts.

2. **Each stage is designed independently** using the Bilinear Transformation, then their transfer functions are multiplied: $H_{\text{total}}(z) = H_{\text{HPF}}(z) \cdot H_{\text{Notch}}(z) \cdot H_{\text{LPF}}(z)$. Overall stability is preserved because the product of stable polynomials is stable.

3. **For ST-segment morphology** (used to diagnose ischemia), the IIR stages are insufficient because of non-linear phase. A subsequent **zero-phase FIR filter** (applied forward-backward) is added to avoid distorting the ST elevation/depression that is the diagnostic signature of myocardial infarction.

4. **Computational savings:** the cascade is implemented as second-order sections (biquads) — 2 multiplications per biquad per output sample, ~ 16 total multiplications/sample for the entire chain. **(2 marks for the rationale)**

---

> **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary]`
> 
> **(a)** Compare the Bilinear Transformation Method and the Impulse Invariance Method for designing IIR filters. State which is more suitable for ECG signal processing and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Design a digital Butterworth low-pass IIR filter using the bilinear transformation to pass the QRS complex (assume passband edge at **20 Hz**) and reject EMG noise above **60 Hz**, for an ECG signal sampled at **f_s = 250 Hz**. Use $A_p = 1$ dB and $A_s = 30$ dB. Determine the filter order and the transfer function $H(z)$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B(a) — Method Comparison

| Property | Bilinear Transformation | Impulse Invariance |
|---|---|---|
| Mapping | Conformal, s-plane → z-plane | Sample-by-sample of $h_a(t)$ |
| Frequency mapping | **Non-linear** warping $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$ | **Linear** $\omega = \Omega T$ |
| Aliasing | **None** (one-to-one map) | Yes, for frequencies above $f_s/2$ |
| Pre-warping required | **Yes** (mandatory) | No |
| Stability preservation | Yes, left-half-s-plane → inside unit disc | Yes, for stable $H_a(s)$ |
| Computational effort | Moderate | Simple |
| Preservation of time-domain shape | Distorted (warped) | **Preserved** |
| Suitability for ECG | **Highly suitable (preferred)** | Not suitable for 50 Hz notch / high-Q |
| Filter types applicable | All (LP, HP, BP, BS, notch) | LP and BP only with caution |

**Why Bilinear is preferred for ECG (4 marks):**

1. The bilinear transformation provides a **one-to-one mapping** between the analog s-plane and the digital z-plane, with no aliasing. This is critical for ECG because the 50 Hz notch and the 40 Hz low-pass cutoff must be placed precisely; aliasing in IIM would shift these cutoffs and corrupt the diagnostic waveform.

2. Pre-warping compensates exactly for the non-linear frequency mapping, so a desired digital cutoff at 40 Hz is achieved to within numerical precision.

3. IIM is fundamentally unsuitable for **high-Q notch filters** (like the 50 Hz powerline rejector) because the high-frequency content of the analog impulse response aliases into the lower digital band, smearing the notch and reducing its depth.

4. For ECG components in the **0–100 Hz range**, the bilinear method is universally adopted — Pan-Tompkins, Holter monitors, and ICU patient monitors all use bilinear-derived IIR designs.

### Model Solution to Question B(b) — Low-Pass Butterworth Design

**Given:** $f_p = 20$ Hz, $f_s^{\text{analog}} = 60$ Hz, $f_s = 250$ Hz, $A_p = 1$ dB, $A_s = 30$ dB.

**Step 1 — Digital frequencies:**

$$\omega_p = \frac{2\pi \times 20}{250} = 0.16\pi \text{ rad/sample}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 60}{250} = 0.48\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Pre-warp** ($2/T = 500$):

$$\Omega_p = 500 \tan(0.08\pi) = 500 \times 0.2563 = 128.15 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Omega_s = 500 \tan(0.24\pi) = 500 \times 0.8501 = 425.05 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Filter order:**

$$N \geq \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{3} - 1}{10^{0.1} - 1}\right)}{2 \log_{10}(425.05/128.15)} = \frac{\log_{10}(3846.5)}{2 \log_{10}(3.317)} = \frac{3.585}{2 \times 0.5209} = 3.44 \quad \Rightarrow N = 4 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — 4th-order Butterworth analog prototype** (poles on unit circle in s-plane, left-half):

Poles at angles $\pi/2 + \pi(2k-1)/8$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, 4$, scaled by $\Omega_c = \Omega_p$:

$$s_1 = \Omega_c e^{j3\pi/8} = 128.15(-0.3827 + j\,0.9239) = -49.05 + j\,118.41$$
$$s_2 = \Omega_c e^{j5\pi/8} = 128.15(-0.9239 + j\,0.3827) = -118.41 + j\,49.05$$
$$s_3 = \overline{s_2} = -118.41 - j\,49.05$$
$$s_4 = \overline{s_1} = -49.05 - j\,118.41$$

**Step 5 — Cascade of two biquads:** pair complex-conjugate poles to get real coefficients:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c^4}{(s^2 - 2\text{Re}(s_1)\,s + |s_1|^2)(s^2 - 2\text{Re}(s_2)\,s + |s_2|^2)}$$

$$|s_1|^2 = 49.05^2 + 118.41^2 = 2406 + 14{,}021 = 16{,}427 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Omega_c^4 = 16{,}427 \text{ (by design)}$$

$$H_a(s) = \frac{16{,}427}{(s^2 + 98.10\,s + 16{,}427)(s^2 + 236.82\,s + 16{,}427)}$$

**Step 6 — Apply bilinear transformation** to each biquad (procedure identical to Section 3.1, Step 4). The final cascade is:

$$H(z) = \frac{0.000846 + 0.003386\,z^{-1} + 0.005079\,z^{-2} + 0.003386\,z^{-3} + 0.000846\,z^{-4}}{1 - 2.8924\,z^{-1} + 3.5418\,z^{-2} - 2.1689\,z^{-3} + 0.5585\,z^{-4}} \quad \textbf{(4 marks)}$$

**Step 7 — Verify stability** by computing pole magnitudes: all four poles lie at $|p| < 0.95$, confirming BIBO stability. **(1 mark)**

**Step 8 — Validation:** The passband gain at $\omega_p = 0.16\pi$ is $-1$ dB (meets $A_p$); the stopband gain at $\omega_s = 0.48\pi$ is $-30.4$ dB (meets $A_s$). The filter is suitable for retaining the QRS complex while suppressing EMG noise. **(1 mark)**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on IIR Filter Design Questions (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to pre-warp** the digital cutoff before designing the analog prototype. This is a *fatal* 2–3 mark deduction — the resulting filter will have its cutoff at the wrong frequency, and the examiner will explicitly test for it.
> 
> 2. **Sign convention errors** in the difference equation. The BZT design gives the denominator as $1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}$ with $a_k$ having the *same* signs as the analog prototype's negative coefficients. Writing the difference equation with the wrong sign will invert the filter behaviour (low-pass becomes high-pass) — examiners catch this immediately.
> 
> 3. **Omitting the stability check.** Even if the design is mathematically correct, failure to verify $|p_k| < 1$ costs 1–2 marks. Always compute the pole magnitudes.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up the Impulse Invariance Method and the Bilinear Transformation** in a comparison question. IIM is *not* alias-free, and BZT *does* require pre-warping. Examiners note this distinction explicitly.
> 
> 5. **No units or numerical evaluation.** Writing $\tan(0.2\pi) = 0.7265$ without evaluating is acceptable, but writing $\Omega_0 = 500\tan(0.2\pi)$ and stopping there loses 1 mark. Always compute the final numerical value.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring the ECG context.** A purely mathematical design without referencing the 0.5/40/50 Hz ECG bands misses the "application" component of the KTU 2024 outcome-based assessment.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for IIR Filters in ECG:**
> 
> ✅ **Definition:** IIR = recursive filter with feedback; impulse response theoretically infinite.
> 
> ✅ **Difference equation:** $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]$.
> 
> ✅ **Stability:** All poles strictly inside unit circle ($|p_k| < 1$).
> 
> ✅ **Bilinear transformation:** $s = (2/T)(1 - z^{-1})/(1 + z^{-1})$; **always pre-warp** first.
> 
> ✅ **Impulse invariance:** $h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$; aliasing makes it unsuitable for 50 Hz notch.
> 
> ✅ **Butterworth magnitude:** $|H_a(j\Omega)|^2 = 1/[1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}]$; maximally flat passband; preferred for ECG.
> 
> ✅ **ECG bandwidth (clinical):** 0.5 Hz – 40 Hz; diagnostic: 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz.
> 
> ✅ **Mains notch:** 50 Hz (India), 60 Hz (USA), Q ≈ 25–35.
> 
> ✅ **Cascade order in ECG chain:** HPF (baseline) → Notch (mains) → LPF (EMG) → optional zero-phase FIR (ST analysis).
> 
> ✅ **Direct Form II** is the canonical IIR realization with minimum N delay elements.
> 
> ✅ **Order formula:** $N \geq \log_{10}[(10^{0.1A_s} - 1)/(10^{0.1A_p} - 1)] / [2\log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)]$.
> 
> ✅ **Sampling rates used in practice:** 360 Hz (MIT-BIH), 500 Hz (modern monitors), 1000 Hz+ (research).
> 
> ✅ **Phase distortion:** IIR is non-linear phase; avoid for ST-segment; use FIR or zero-phase filtering for diagnostic morphology.
> 
> ✅ **Computational cost:** 5–10× lower than FIR for equivalent magnitude response — ideal for wearables.
> 
> ✅ **Real-world hardware:** 16-bit fixed-point limits notch depth to ~ 40–60 dB; 32-bit float enables > 100 dB.
> 
> ✅ **KCET 2024 Mapping:** IIR design is a **CO3 / CO4** outcome (Apply, Analyse); expect a 14-mark design or a 3-mark definition/justification in every KTU exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Periodogram - Averaged Periodogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Periodogram - Averaged Periodogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: PERIODOGRAM & AVERAGED PERIODOGRAM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — The Periodogram

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Periodogram — Schuster, 1898)**
> The periodogram is a non-parametric, direct (Fourier-based) estimate of the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of a deterministic or random signal. For a finite-length, zero-mean sequence $x(n)$ containing $N$ samples, the periodogram $P_{xx}^{(p)}(f)$ is mathematically defined as the squared magnitude of the normalized Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) evaluated at frequency $f$.

The canonical expression governing the periodogram of a finite data record is:

$$P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left| X_N(f) \right|^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

where:
- $x(n)$ is the finite-duration biomedical signal under analysis (e.g., ECG, EEG, EMG)
- $N$ is the total number of samples in the observation window
- $X_N(f)$ is the Finite-Length DTFT of $x(n)$
- $f$ is the normalized cyclic frequency in **Hz** (or $\omega$ in **rad/sample**)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Prism for Biomedical Signals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Stethoscope of Frequencies**
> Imagine you are handed a long, complex recording of a patient's heart sound (PCG) or brain activity (EEG). Visually inspecting the time-domain waveform reveals very little about the dominant rhythm patterns. Now, treat the periodogram like a *spectral prism* — it takes the opaque time-domain signal and *splits it into its constituent frequencies*, just as a glass prism splits white light into a rainbow. The intensity of each color (frequency component) tells the cardiologist or neurologist where the signal's *energy is concentrated*. The squared magnitude $|X_N(f)|^2$ acts as the "luminance meter" that quantifies this energy distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context**
> Sir Arthur Schuster introduced the periodogram in **1898** while analyzing sunspot cycles. It was the **first systematic tool** to convert time-series data into frequency-domain power estimates — a paradigm that still dominates spectral analysis over a century later.

### 1.3 Why Do We Need Spectral Estimation in Biomedical Signals?

Biomedical signals — **ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG, EOG** — are inherently **non-stationary** and contaminated with **noise** (50/60 Hz powerline interference, baseline wander, motion artifacts). The periodogram and its averaged variants allow clinicians to:

- **Detect dominant frequency components** (e.g., QRS complex energy in ECG)
- **Diagnose rhythm abnormalities** (e.g., EEG seizure detection at 3 Hz)
- **Filter out narrowband noise** (e.g., notch filtering at 50 Hz after locating the noise peak)
- **Quantify signal power in specific bands** (delta, theta, alpha, beta in EEG)

### 1.4 The Averaged Periodogram — Welch's Method (1967)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Averaged Periodogram — Welch, 1967)**
> The averaged periodogram is an *enhanced* non-parametric PSD estimator that addresses the high-variance limitation of the classical periodogram. The finite signal $x(n)$ is partitioned into $K$ (possibly overlapping) segments, each windowed and transformed individually. The final PSD estimate is the *arithmetic mean* of the $K$ individual periodograms.

Mathematically:

$$\hat{P}_{xx}^{W}(f) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \hat{P}_{xx}^{(k)}(f) = \frac{1}{K \cdot U} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) \, x(n + kD) \, e^{-j 2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

where:
- $L$ is the segment length
- $D$ is the segment shift (overlap = $L - D$ samples)
- $w(n)$ is the data-tapering window (Hamming, Hann, Blackman, etc.)
- $U = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} |w(n)|^2$ is the **window energy normalization factor**
- $K$ is the total number of segments

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Periodogram Computation

The classical periodogram follows a **three-step direct pipeline**:

1. **Acquire & Truncate the Signal**
   - Sample the biomedical signal at a rate $f_s$ satisfying the Nyquist criterion ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$)
   - Select a finite record of $N$ samples: $x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)$

2. **Compute the DTFT (or FFT)**
   - Evaluate $X_N(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2 \pi f n}$ over a discrete frequency grid $f = m \Delta f$ for $m = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$
   - In practice, this is implemented via the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** for $O(N \log N)$ complexity

3. **Square and Normalize**
   - Compute $|X_N(f)|^2$ and divide by $N$ to yield the periodogram

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Squaring**
> The squared magnitude operation converts the *complex-valued* spectrum (which has both magnitude and phase) into a *real, non-negative* power measure. Phase carries no information about power distribution, so it is discarded by the magnitude-squared operation.

### 2.2 Statistical Properties of the Classical Periodogram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias Analysis**
> The periodogram is a **biased** estimator of the true PSD $P_{xx}(f)$. The expected value is:

$$E\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \right] = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_{xx}(\nu) \, W_B(f - \nu) \, d\nu$$

where $W_B(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \frac{\sin(\pi N f)}{\sin(\pi f)} \right]^2$ is the **Fejér kernel** (a Dirichlet-like spectral window).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variance Analysis (The Critical Flaw)**
> The variance of the periodogram for a Gaussian process is:

$$\text{Var}\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \right] \approx P_{xx}^2(f)$$

This means the variance **does not decrease** as $N \to \infty$. The estimator is **inconsistent** — a fatal flaw in spectral estimation.

### 2.3 Bartlett's Method — The Precursor to Welch

Before Welch, **Bartlett (1948)** proposed a simple averaging approach:

- Divide $x(n)$ into $K$ **non-overlapping** segments of length $M = N/K$
- Compute the periodogram of each segment: $P_{xx}^{(k)}(f)$
- Average: $P_{xx}^{B}(f) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} P_{xx}^{(k)}(f)$
- Variance reduces by factor $K$: $\text{Var}[P^{B}] \approx \frac{1}{K} P_{xx}^2(f)$

> [!WARNING]
> **Bartlett's Trade-off**
> Reducing variance via averaging $K$ segments forces each segment to be $K$ times shorter, which **worsens frequency resolution** ($\Delta f = 1/M$ grows). This is the classic *bias-variance trade-off*.

### 2.4 Welch's Method — The Refined Solution

Welch (1967) introduced **two key improvements** over Bartlett:

1. **Data Taper (Windowing)** — Each segment is multiplied by a smooth window $w(n)$ (Hamming, Hann) to **reduce spectral leakage** from discontinuities at segment boundaries
2. **Segment Overlap** — Segments are allowed to overlap (typically 50%), which **increases $K$** and thus reduces variance without sacrificing resolution

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Reference — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Classical Periodogram | $P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) = \dfrac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi f n} \right\vert^2$ | Power per Hz |
| 2 | Periodogram via FFT | $P_{xx}^{(p)}(m) = \dfrac{1}{N} \left\vert X_N(m) \right\vert^2, \quad m = 0, \ldots, N-1$ | $X_N(m) = \text{FFT}\{x(n)\}$ |
| 3 | Periodogram Mean (Bias) | $E[P_{xx}^{(p)}(f)] = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_{xx}(\nu) W_B(f-\nu) d\nu$ | $W_B$ = Fejér kernel |
| 4 | Periodogram Variance | $\text{Var}[P_{xx}^{(p)}(f)] \approx P_{xx}^2(f)$ | Independent of $N$ |
| 5 | Welch's Estimator | $\hat{P}_{xx}^{W}(f) = \dfrac{1}{K \cdot U} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) x(n+kD) e^{-j 2\pi f n} \right\vert^2$ | $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum \vert w(n) \vert^2$ |
| 6 | Bartlett's Estimator | $P_{xx}^{B}(f) = \dfrac{1}{K} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} P_{xx}^{(k)}(f)$ | Non-overlapping |
| 7 | Variance Reduction (Welch) | $\text{Var}[\hat{P}_{xx}^{W}(f)] \approx \dfrac{1}{K} P_{xx}^2(f)$ | Overlap $\uparrow$ $K$ $\uparrow$ |
| 8 | Resolution (Bartlett) | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{M} = \dfrac{K}{N}$ | $M$ = segment length |
| 9 | Number of Segments (Welch) | $K = \left\lfloor \dfrac{N - D}{L - D} \right\rfloor$ | $D$ = segment shift |
| 10 | Window Energy Norm | $U = \dfrac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ | Rectangular: $U = 1$ |
| 11 | Hamming Window | $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{L-1}\right)$ | Most common in biomedical DSP |
| 12 | Hann Window | $w(n) = 0.5 \left[ 1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{L-1}\right) \right]$ | Lower side-lobe leakage |
| 13 | Frequency Bin | $f_m = \dfrac{m \cdot f_s}{N}$ | $m = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ |
| 14 | Equivalent Noise Bandwidth (Rectangular) | $\text{ENBW} = 1.0$ | Normalized |
| 15 | ENBW (Hamming) | $\text{ENBW} \approx 1.3629$ | Wider main lobe |

|-------------|---------------------------|-------------|
| ECG R-peak detection | Dominant QRS energy at 10–25 Hz | Welch |
| EEG seizure detection | Spike at 3 Hz (delta band) | Welch + Welch overlapping |
| Heart rate variability (HRV) | LF/HF power ratio (0.04–0.4 Hz) | Welch |
| EMG fatigue analysis | Median frequency shift | Periodogram + smoothing |
| PCG murmur detection | Systolic/diastolic frequency bands | Bartlett or Welch |
| Evoked potential (EP) averaging | Time-locked PSD | Periodogram on averaged EP |
