## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Periodogram from Parseval's Theorem

**Starting Point:** The total energy of a finite-duration signal is given by Parseval's theorem.

**Step 1 — Express Total Energy in Time Domain:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} |x(n)|^2$$

**Step 2 — Express Total Energy in Frequency Domain (Parseval):**

$$E_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} |X_N(k)|^2$$

where $X_N(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2 \pi k n / N}$ is the $N$-point DFT.

**Step 3 — Interpret Energy as a Density:**

The average power of a length-$N$ signal is $P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{N}$. Substituting:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} |X_N(k)|^2$$

**Step 4 — Define Power Spectral Density:**

Recognizing that each DFT bin $k$ contributes power $\frac{|X_N(k)|^2}{N^2}$ at frequency $f_k = \frac{k}{N}$, we define:

$$P_{xx}^{(p)}(f_k) = \frac{1}{N} |X_N(k)|^2$$

Summing the per-bin contributions recovers $P_{\text{avg}}$ (up to a scaling), which is the defining property of a spectral density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The periodogram $P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X_N(f) \vert^2$ is the natural finite-sample estimator of the true Power Spectral Density, obtained by interpreting the squared magnitude of the DTFT as energy per frequency bin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Periodogram Bias

**Step 1 — Express the Periodogram via Convolution:**

It is a classical result that the squared magnitude of the DTFT equals the DTFT of the autocorrelation sequence $r_{xx}(\ell)$:

$$\frac{1}{N} \left| X_N(f) \right|^2 = \sum_{\ell = -(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}(\ell) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f \ell}$$

where the *biased* autocorrelation estimate is:

$$\hat{r}_{xx}(\ell) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1 - |\ell|} x(n) \, x(n + |\ell|)$$

**Step 2 — Take the Expectation:**

$$E\left[ \hat{r}_{xx}(\ell) \right] = \left( 1 - \frac{|\ell|}{N} \right) r_{xx}(\ell)$$

The triangular weighting factor $\left( 1 - \frac{|\ell|}{N} \right)$ is the *Bartlett window* in the lag domain.

**Step 3 — Apply the Convolution Property:**

In the frequency domain, multiplication by $\left( 1 - \frac{|\ell|}{N} \right)$ in the lag domain corresponds to *convolution* with its DTFT — the **Fejér kernel**:

$$E\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \right] = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_{xx}(\nu) \, W_B(f - \nu) \, d\nu$$

where

$$W_B(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \frac{\sin(\pi N f)}{\sin(\pi f)} \right]^2$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Bias:**

The expected periodogram is the *true PSD* smeared (convolved) with the Fejér kernel. As $N \to \infty$, $W_B(f)$ approaches a Dirac delta, and the bias vanishes. Hence, the periodogram is **asymptotically unbiased**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Periodogram Variance (Asymptotic Case)

**Step 1 — Assumptions:**

Assume $x(n)$ is a *zero-mean, wide-sense stationary Gaussian process* with true PSD $P_{xx}(f)$.

**Step 2 — Use the Chi-Squared Property:**

For a Gaussian process, the periodogram at any frequency $f$ is *asymptotically* distributed as a scaled chi-squared random variable with **2 degrees of freedom** (one for real part, one for imaginary part):

$$P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \sim \frac{P_{xx}(f)}{2} \chi^2_2$$

**Step 3 — Compute Mean and Variance of $\chi^2_2$:**

For a chi-squared distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom:

$$E[\chi^2_\nu] = \nu, \quad \text{Var}[\chi^2_\nu] = 2\nu$$

For $\nu = 2$:

$$E\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \right] = P_{xx}(f), \quad \text{Var}\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \right] = P_{xx}^2(f)$$

**Step 4 — Critical Observation:**

$$\text{Var}\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \right] = P_{xx}^2(f) \neq 0 \text{ as } N \to \infty$$

The variance does **not vanish** as the data length grows — the periodogram is a **statistically inconsistent** estimator. This is the fundamental motivation for averaging methods.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Welch's Variance Reduction

**Step 1 — Setup:**

Let $K$ windowed segments of length $L$ be processed. Each segment yields a *modified periodogram*:

$$\hat{P}_{xx}^{(k)}(f) = \frac{1}{L \cdot U} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) \, x_k(n) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f n} \right|^2$$

**Step 2 — Welch's Estimator (Average):**

$$\hat{P}_{xx}^{W}(f) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \hat{P}_{xx}^{(k)}(f)$$

**Step 3 — Variance Computation:**

Assuming adjacent segments are *approximately uncorrelated* (true for non-overlap; approximate for overlap), the variance of the mean is:

$$\text{Var}\left[ \hat{P}_{xx}^{W}(f) \right] = \frac{1}{K^2} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \text{Var}\left[ \hat{P}_{xx}^{(k)}(f) \right] = \frac{1}{K} \text{Var}\left[ \hat{P}_{xx}^{(k)}(f) \right]$$

For each modified periodogram (using window $w(n)$), the variance is approximately:

$$\text{Var}\left[ \hat{P}_{xx}^{(k)}(f) \right] \approx P_{xx}^2(f)$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}\left[ \hat{P}_{xx}^{W}(f) \right] \approx \frac{1}{K} P_{xx}^2(f)$$

**Step 4 — Resolution Penalty:**

The frequency resolution of each segment is:

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{L} = \frac{1}{N/K_{\text{eff}}}$$

where $K_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective number of segments after accounting for overlap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welch's Key Insight**
> Overlap (typically 50%) effectively *doubles* the number of segments $K$ (from $K = N/L$ to roughly $K \approx 2N/L$) *without* shortening each segment. This yields variance reduction **without** sacrificing resolution — a strict improvement over Bartlett.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Example — Periodogram vs. Welch

**Problem:** Consider an ECG-like signal $x(n) = \sin(2\pi \cdot 0.2 \cdot n) + 0.5 \sin(2\pi \cdot 0.35 \cdot n) + v(n)$, where $v(n)$ is AWGN with variance $\sigma^2 = 0.1$. Total samples $N = 1024$, $f_s = 1$ Hz (normalized).

**Step 1 — Classical Periodogram ($K=1$):**
- Compute $X_N(m)$ via 1024-point FFT
- Periodogram: $P^{(p)}(m) = \frac{1}{1024} |X_N(m)|^2$
- Result: Two distinct peaks at $m \approx 205$ ($f = 0.2$ Hz) and $m \approx 358$ ($f = 0.35$ Hz)
- Spectrum is **noisy** with high ripple

**Step 2 — Welch with $L = 256$, $D = 128$ (50% overlap):**
- Number of segments: $K = \lfloor (1024 - 128) / (256 - 128) \rfloor = \lfloor 896/128 \rfloor = 7$
- Each segment: Apply Hamming window, compute 256-point FFT, accumulate
- Welch's average: $\hat{P}^{W} = \frac{1}{7 \cdot U_H} \sum_{k=0}^{6} |X_k|^2$
- $U_H = 0.397$ (Hamming window energy norm)
- Result: **Smoother** spectrum, two peaks clearly visible, variance reduced by factor $\approx 7$

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away:** The peaks remain at the same frequency (resolution preserved), but the spectral floor is significantly less noisy.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam Model Question]**

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**Define the periodogram. Why is it called a non-parametric PSD estimator? Mention its major limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

The periodogram is a non-parametric estimate of the power spectral density (PSD) of a signal, defined as the squared magnitude of the normalized discrete-time Fourier transform (DTFT) of a finite data record:

$$P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

It is called *non-parametric* because it does not assume any specific parametric model (e.g., AR, MA, ARMA) for the underlying signal-generating process — the estimate is computed directly from the data via the Fourier transform.

**Major Limitation:** The variance of the periodogram does not decrease as the data length $N$ increases. It remains approximately equal to $P_{xx}^2(f)$ even as $N \to \infty$. Hence, the periodogram is a **statistically inconsistent** estimator, producing noisy and unreliable spectral estimates for long data records.

> *(3 marks: Definition 1m + Non-parametric reason 1m + Limitation 1m)*

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**Differentiate between Bartlett's method and Welch's method of PSD estimation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bartlett's Method | Welch's Method |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| Segment Overlap | No overlap | Overlap allowed (typically 50%) |
| Data Windowing | Optional; usually rectangular | Mandatory taper (Hamming/Hann) |
| Number of Segments $K$ | $K = N/M$ | $K = \lfloor (N - D)/(L - D) \rfloor$ |
| Variance | Reduced by $1/K$ | Reduced by $1/K$; $K$ is larger |
| Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = 1/(N/K)$ | $\Delta f = 1/L$ (preserved) |
| Spectral Leakage | Higher (rectangular window) | Lower (smooth window) |

The key innovation of **Welch** is the use of *windowing* to suppress spectral leakage and *overlapping segments* to increase $K$ without sacrificing segment length $L$.

> *(3 marks: 3 distinct differences)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Typical Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the classical periodogram from Parseval's theorem and explain why it is asymptotically unbiased but statistically inconsistent. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A stationary random signal $x(n)$ with true PSD $P_{xx}(f) = \sigma^2$ (white noise, $\sigma^2 = 1$) is sampled to obtain $N = 1024$ points. The periodogram is computed at frequency $f_0 = 0.25$ Hz. Compute: (i) the expected value, (ii) the variance, and (iii) the standard deviation of the periodogram at $f_0$. Comment on the consistency of the estimator. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Parseval's Theorem Application:**

For a finite sequence $x(n)$, $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} |x(n)|^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} |X_N(k)|^2$$

where $X_N(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$.

**Step 2 — Define the Periodogram as Energy per Frequency Bin:**

Dividing both sides of Parseval's identity by $N$ and interpreting $\frac{1}{N}|X_N(k)|^2$ as the power at frequency $f_k = k/N$:

$$P_{xx}^{(p)}(f_k) = \frac{1}{N} |X_N(k)|^2$$

Continuous extension yields:

$$P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

**Step 3 — Show Asymptotic Unbiasedness:**

Taking the expectation:

$$E\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \right] = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_{xx}(\nu) W_B(f - \nu) d\nu$$

where $W_B(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left[\frac{\sin(\pi N f)}{\sin(\pi f)}\right]^2$. As $N \to \infty$, $W_B(f)$ becomes a Dirac delta $\delta(f - \nu)$, and so:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} E\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \right] = P_{xx}(f)$$

This establishes **asymptotic unbiasedness**.

**Step 4 — Show Statistical Inconsistency:**

For a Gaussian process, the periodogram at $f \neq 0$ is asymptotically chi-squared with 2 degrees of freedom:

$$\frac{2 P_{xx}^{(p)}(f)}{P_{xx}(f)} \sim \chi^2_2$$

Hence:

$$\text{Var}\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) \right] = P_{xx}^2(f) \neq 0 \text{ as } N \to \infty$$

Since the variance does not vanish, the mean-square error cannot converge to zero. The estimator is **statistically inconsistent**.

> *(7 marks: Parseval derivation 2m + Periodogram formula 1m + Asymptotic unbiasedness 2m + Inconsistency 2m)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $P_{xx}(f) = \sigma^2 = 1$, $N = 1024$, $f_0 = 0.25$ Hz.

**(i) Expected Value:**

Since the periodogram is asymptotically unbiased:

$$E\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f_0) \right] = P_{xx}(f_0) = \sigma^2 = 1$$

**(ii) Variance:**

$$\text{Var}\left[ P_{xx}^{(p)}(f_0) \right] = P_{xx}^2(f_0) = (1)^2 = 1$$

**(iii) Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_{P} = \sqrt{\text{Var}} = \sqrt{1} = 1$$

**Comment on Consistency:**

The coefficient of variation is:

$$CV = \frac{\sigma_P}{E[P^{(p)}]} = \frac{1}{1} = 1 = 100\%$$

The standard deviation is *as large as* the mean. This means the periodogram fluctuates wildly from realization to realization. If $N$ were increased to $N = 4096$, the expected value would still be 1, but the standard deviation would still be 1. This conclusively demonstrates that **the periodogram is inconsistent** — the noise floor does not decrease with more data.

> *(7 marks: (i) 2m + (ii) 2m + (iii) 1m + Consistency comment 2m)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain Welch's method of averaged periodogram estimation in detail. Discuss the role of windowing and overlapping in reducing variance and spectral leakage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-second EEG signal is sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz. Welch's method is applied with segment length $L = 256$ samples and 50% overlap using a Hamming window. Compute: (i) the total number of samples $N$, (ii) the number of segments $K$, (iii) the frequency resolution $\Delta f$, and (iv) the variance reduction factor relative to the classical periodogram. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup:**

Welch's method addresses two critical deficiencies of the classical periodogram: (1) high variance, and (2) spectral leakage due to finite-length truncation.

**Step 2 — Segmentation:**

The signal of length $N$ is divided into $K$ segments of length $L$, with adjacent segments overlapping by $L - D$ samples (where $D$ is the shift). The $k$-th segment is:

$$x_k(n) = x(n + kD), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, L-1, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, K-1$$

**Step 3 — Windowing:**

Each segment is multiplied point-wise by a smooth data-tapering window $w(n)$ (Hamming, Hann, Blackman):

$$x_k^{(w)}(n) = w(n) \cdot x_k(n)$$

**Why windowing matters:** The finite truncation of a signal is equivalent to multiplying by a rectangular window in the time domain. In the frequency domain, this is a convolution with the Dirichlet kernel (sinc-like), producing high side-lobes (spectral leakage) that can mask weak spectral components. A smooth window (Hamming) trades a slightly wider main lobe for **dramatically lower side-lobes** (e.g., -43 dB vs. -13 dB for rectangular).

**Step 4 — Periodogram Computation:**

For each windowed segment, compute the modified periodogram:

$$\hat{P}_{xx}^{(k)}(f) = \frac{1}{L \cdot U} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) x_k(n) e^{-j 2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

where $U = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ is the window-energy normalization (rectangular: $U = 1$; Hamming: $U \approx 0.397$).

**Step 5 — Averaging:**

$$\hat{P}_{xx}^{W}(f) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \hat{P}_{xx}^{(k)}(f)$$

**Why overlap helps:** Overlap (typically 50%) increases the number of segments $K$ from $N/L$ to nearly $2N/L$. Since variance scales as $1/K$, doubling $K$ **halves the variance**. Crucially, overlap does *not* reduce segment length $L$, so frequency resolution $\Delta f = 1/L$ is preserved.

**Step 6 — Variance Reduction Summary:**

$$\text{Var}\left[ \hat{P}_{xx}^{W}(f) \right] \approx \frac{1}{K} P_{xx}^2(f)$$

> *(7 marks: Setup 1m + Windowing role 2m + Overlap role 2m + Variance formula 2m)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** Duration $T = 4$ s, $f_s = 256$ Hz, $L = 256$, overlap = 50%, Hamming window.

**(i) Total Number of Samples:**

$$N = T \cdot f_s = 4 \times 256 = 1024 \text{ samples}$$

**(ii) Number of Segments:**

With 50% overlap, shift $D = L/2 = 128$. The number of segments is:

$$K = \left\lfloor \frac{N - D}{L - D} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1024 - 128}{256 - 128} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{896}{128} \right\rfloor = 7 \text{ segments}$$

**(iii) Frequency Resolution:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{L} = \frac{256}{256} = 1 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iv) Variance Reduction Factor:**

$$VRF = K = 7$$

The variance is reduced by a factor of 7 relative to the classical periodogram. Equivalently:

$$\text{Var}\left[ \hat{P}_{xx}^{W}(f) \right] = \frac{P_{xx}^2(f)}{7}$$

If 75% overlap were used ($D = 64$), then $K = 13$, and the variance would be reduced by a factor of 13.

> *(7 marks: (i) 1m + (ii) 2m + (iii) 2m + (iv) 2m)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Periodogram Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/N$ Normalization** — Students often write $P_{xx}^{(p)}(f) = |X_N(f)|^2$ without dividing by $N$. The normalization is *mandatory* for a true PSD estimate. **Loss: 1 mark per occurrence.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Biased vs. Inconsistent** — The periodogram is *asymptotically unbiased* (bias $\to 0$ as $N \to \infty$) but *inconsistent* (variance does $\not\to 0$). Many students write "biased" alone. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Confusing Bartlett with Welch** — Both use averaging, but only Welch uses *overlap* and *windowing*. Writing "Welch = no overlap + rectangular window" is wrong. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Window Energy Normalization $U$** — Welch's formula requires $U$ in the denominator. Writing $\hat{P}_W$ without $U$ is technically incorrect. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Miscalculating Number of Segments** — The formula $K = \lfloor (N - D)/(L - D) \rfloor$ is not $K = N/L$. The shift $D$ must be accounted for. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Ignoring the Resolution Penalty in Bartlett** — Students often say "Bartlett reduces variance" without mentioning that resolution degrades by factor $K$. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Periodogram & Averaged Periodogram**

- **Classical Periodogram** is the **squared magnitude of the normalized DTFT**: $P^{(p)}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left| X_N(f) \right|^2$
- It is **non-parametric** (no model assumption) and **asymptotically unbiased**
- It is **statistically inconsistent** — variance $\approx P_{xx}^2(f)$ does *not* decrease with $N$
- The **bias** is governed by convolution with the **Fejér kernel** $W_B(f)$
- The periodogram follows a **chi-squared distribution** with 2 degrees of freedom (for Gaussian signals)
- **Bartlett's method** averages $K$ non-overlapping periodograms: variance $\downarrow$ by $1/K$, but resolution $\downarrow$ by $K$
- **Welch's method** averages $K$ overlapping, windowed periodograms: variance $\downarrow$ by $1/K$, resolution **preserved**
- **Windowing** (Hamming, Hann) reduces **spectral leakage** at the cost of a slightly wider main lobe
- **Overlap** (typically 50%) effectively *doubles* $K$ without shortening segments
- **Window energy normalization** $U = \frac{1}{L} \sum w^2(n)$ ensures unbiased PSD magnitude
- **Frequency resolution** is determined by segment length $L$: $\Delta f = f_s / L$
- **Equivalent Noise Bandwidth (ENBW)** captures window-induced resolution loss: ENBW$_{\text{Hamming}} \approx 1.36$
- **Clinical applications**: EEG band power (delta/theta/alpha/beta), ECG QRS detection, EMG median frequency, PCG murmur analysis
- **Direct implementation** via FFT: $O(N \log N)$ complexity makes it suitable for real-time biomedical DSP
- **Arthur Schuster** (1898) introduced the periodogram; **Bartlett** (1948) and **Welch** (1967) refined it
- The **trade-off triangle**: *resolution* ↔ *variance* ↔ *leakage* — Welch optimally balances all three

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mnemonic for the Exam Hall**
> **"PeriodoGRAM is a GRAM of energy per frequency bin — square the FFT, divide by N, and you have the periodogram. Welch averages many such GRAMS into one smooth spectrum."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

topics.title: Blackman-Tukey Spectral Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Blackman-Tukey Spectral Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Blackman-Tukey Spectral Estimation** is a classical *non-parametric* power spectral density (PSD) estimation technique, formally described in 1958 by R. B. Blackman and J. W. Tukey. It belongs to the family of **indirect (correlogram) methods**, wherein the PSD of a stationary random process is obtained by computing the **windowed Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of an estimated autocorrelation sequence**.

Mathematically, the Blackman-Tukey PSD estimator is expressed as:

$$
\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = T \cdot \sum_{m=-M}^{M} \hat{r}_{xx}(m) \, w(m) \, e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

where the symbols carry the following exact KTU 2024 meanings:
- $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ — **biased (or unbiased) sample autocorrelation estimate** at lag $m$
- $w(m)$ — **symmetric lag window** of length $(2M+1)$ with $w(0) = 1$
- $M$ — **maximum lag** (window truncation point), with $M \ll N$
- $T$ — **sampling interval** in seconds
- $N$ — **total number of data samples**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Blackman-Tukey method is *indirect* because the computation flows: **Data $\rightarrow$ Autocorrelation $\rightarrow$ PSD**. This contrasts with *direct* methods (Periodogram) that compute: **Data $\rightarrow$ PSD directly via FFT**. The windowing of the autocorrelation is the unique feature distinguishing this method from the raw correlogram.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sliding Memory Window"

Imagine you are trying to figure out the dominant **rhythms hidden in an EEG (electroencephalogram) recording** of a sleeping patient. The raw periodogram (squaring the FFT) gives a very jagged, noisy spectrum — like trying to identify a melody while standing next to a jackhammer.

The **autocorrelation function** $r_{xx}(m)$ tells you *"how similar the signal is to a delayed copy of itself after $m$ samples."* If the brain oscillates at 10 Hz (alpha rhythm), $r_{xx}(m)$ will show a strong wave-like pattern at lag values corresponding to 100 ms (one alpha period). Far-apart lags (large $m$) carry less reliable rhythm information because the patient might have moved, blinked, or the signal has become statistically independent.

**Blackman-Tukey insight:** Instead of using the *entire* autocorrelation (which has noisy tails), apply a **tapering window** $w(m)$ that **emphasizes reliable, short-lag values** and **de-emphasizes unreliable, long-lag values**. The DTFT then converts this cleaned-up correlation into a smooth PSD. Think of it as **wearing noise-cancelling headphones that get progressively less effective the further away the sound source moves** — the lag window $w(m)$ does exactly this with autocorrelation samples.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Metrics for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Frequency Resolution** $\Delta f \approx \dfrac{1}{MT}$ — improves linearly as lag $M$ increases.
> - **Variance of estimate** $\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} \approx P_{xx}^{2}(f) \cdot \dfrac{1}{M}$ — decreases as $M$ increases.
> - **Bias** depends on the **sidelobe level** of the window's frequency response $W(f)$.
> - **Trade-off Rule:** Choosing $M \approx N/5$ to $N/10$ is a common KTU-approved practical guideline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theorem — The Blackman-Tukey Approach

The method rests on the **Wiener-Khinchin theorem** which states that for a wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process, the PSD and the autocorrelation function form a **Fourier Transform pair**:

$$
P_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{r_{xx}(m)\} = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m) \, e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

In practice, we only have a *finite* observation of length $N$, so $r_{xx}(m)$ is replaced by the **sample autocorrelation** $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$, computed only over lags $|m| \le M$ where $M \ll N$.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Acquire $N$ samples** $x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]$ of the WSS biomedical signal (e.g., ECG, EEG, EMG).
2. **Estimate the autocorrelation** $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ for lags $m = -M, \dots, 0, \dots, +M$ using:
   - *Biased estimator:* $\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-|m|} x[n] x[n+|m|]$
   - *Unbiased estimator:* $\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \dfrac{1}{N-|m|} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-|m|} x[n] x[n+|m|]$
3. **Select a symmetric lag window** $w(m)$ with $w(m) = w(-m)$ and $w(0) = 1$, and $w(m) = 0$ for $|m| > M$.
4. **Multiply point-wise** to obtain the windowed autocorrelation $c_{xx}(m) = \hat{r}_{xx}(m) \cdot w(m)$.
5. **Take the DTFT** of $c_{xx}(m)$ to obtain $\hat{P}_{BT}(f)$. In practice, an FFT of length $L \ge 2M+1$ is used.
6. **Normalize** and interpret the resulting magnitude squared as the estimated PSD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "Why" and "How" matter for KTU 2024:**
> - The windowing in the *lag domain* corresponds to a **convolution in the frequency domain**: $W(f) * P_{xx}(f)$. This is the source of *bias*.
> - The use of a *finite* window reduces the *number of independent estimates*, which is the source of *variance reduction*.
> - The *bias-variance trade-off* is the central engineering theme in all non-parametric spectral estimation.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Window Selection

The choice of lag window $w(m)$ is critical. Popular choices in KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Window Type | Time-Domain Formula $w(m), \vert m \vert \le M$ | Mainlobe Width | Sidelobe Level (dB) |
|-------------|--------------------------------------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Rectangular** | $1$ | $2/M$ | $-13$ dB |
| **Bartlett (Triangular)** | $1 - \dfrac{\vert m \vert}{M}$ | $4/M$ | $-26$ dB |
| **Hann (Hanning)** | $0.5 + 0.5 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi m}{M}\right)$ | $4/M$ | $-31$ dB |
| **Hamming** | $0.54 + 0.46 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi m}{M}\right)$ | $4/M$ | $-43$ dB |
| **Blackman** | $0.42 + 0.5 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi m}{M}\right) + 0.08 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m}{M}\right)$ | $6/M$ | $-58$ dB |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|-------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Blackman-Tukey PSD | $\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = T \sum_{m=-M}^{M} \hat{r}_{xx}(m) w(m) e^{-j2\pi f m T}$ | Windowed DTFT of autocorrelation |
| Biased Autocorrelation | $\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x[n]\, x[n+\vert m \vert]$ | Used for guaranteed PSD non-negativity |
| Unbiased Autocorrelation | $\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \dfrac{1}{N-\vert m \vert} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x[n]\, x[n+\vert m \vert]$ | Higher variance at large lags |
| Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{MT}$ | Smaller $M$ → broader spectral features merged |
| Variance of $\hat{P}_{BT}(f)$ | $\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} \approx \dfrac{P_{xx}^{2}(f)}{M}$ | Variance drops as window length grows |
| Bias Expression | $\text{Bias} \approx P_{xx}(f) * W(f)$ | Convolution in freq. domain |
| Spectral Quality (Bartlett) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{M} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\int_{-1/2}^{1/2} W^{2}(f)\, df}$ | Higher $Q$ = better statistical reliability |
| Energy Conservation (Parseval) | $\sum_{m=-M}^{M} w^{2}(m) \hat{r}_{xx}^{2}(m) = \int_{-1/2T}^{1/2T} \hat{P}_{BT}^{2}(f)\, df$ | Window must preserve total power |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **biomedical signal processing**, the Blackman-Tukey method remains a *gold-standard* for analysing:
- **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** spectra extracted from R-R intervals of ECG.
- **EEG band-power estimation** (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma) in polysomnography.
- **Vocal-tract formant analysis** in speech-audiology.
- **EMG median frequency** tracking for muscle fatigue studies.

The advantage of Blackman-Tukey in clinical applications is the **smooth spectral estimate** it provides, free of the high-variance fluctuations of the raw periodogram, while still being computationally simple (single FFT of the autocorrelation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Wiener-Khinchin Theorem to Blackman-Tukey Estimator

We begin with the **true** PSD of a WSS process, given by the Wiener-Khinchin relation:

$$
P_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m) \, e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

### Step 1 — Replace the True Autocorrelation with Its Estimate

Since we have only $N$ data samples, the *true* $r_{xx}(m)$ is replaced by the *sample* autocorrelation $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$. This is the **first estimation step**, and it alone yields the *correlogram*:

$$
\hat{P}_{c}(f) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}(m) \, e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

### Step 2 — Truncate the Lag Domain Using a Window

The estimate $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ for large $|m|$ is **statistically unreliable** (very few overlapping products contribute to the sum). Blackman and Tukey proposed to multiply by a window $w(m)$ that smoothly decays to zero at $\vert m \vert = M$:

$$
c_{xx}(m) = \hat{r}_{xx}(m) \cdot w(m)
$$

with $w(m) = 0$ for $\vert m \vert > M$. Therefore the lag-domain truncation becomes:

$$
\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = \sum_{m=-M}^{M} \hat{r}_{xx}(m)\, w(m)\, e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

### Step 3 — Multiplication in Time $\Leftrightarrow$ Convolution in Frequency

The product in the lag domain corresponds to a **circular convolution** in the frequency domain (denoted $*$):

$$
\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = P_{xx}(f) * W(f)
$$

**Why this happens:** By the convolution theorem of the Fourier transform, multiplication in one domain equals convolution in the dual domain. Here, $W(f)$ is the DTFT of $w(m)$.

### Step 4 — Statistical Analysis: Mean of the Estimator

Taking the expectation operator on both sides:

$$
\mathbb{E}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} = \mathbb{E}\{\hat{r}_{xx}(m)\} * W(f) \approx r_{xx}(m) * W(f)
$$

For large $N$, the sample autocorrelation is approximately unbiased, so:

$$
\mathbb{E}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} = \sum_{m=-M}^{M} r_{xx}(m) \, w(m) \, e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

The **bias** is therefore:

$$
\text{Bias}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} = \mathbb{E}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} - P_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-M}^{M} r_{xx}(m) w(m) e^{-j2\pi f m T} - \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m) e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

### Step 5 — Variance of the Estimator

For a Gaussian WSS process, the variance is given by:

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} = P_{xx}^{2}(f) \left[ 1 + 2 \sum_{m=1}^{M} \left( \dfrac{w(m)}{w(0)} \right)^{2} \right]
$$

Since $w(0) = 1$, for large $M$ this simplifies to:

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} \approx \dfrac{P_{xx}^{2}(f)}{M} \cdot \left[ \sum_{m=-M}^{M} w^{2}(m) \right]
$$

If the window energy is normalized to $\sum w^{2}(m) = 1$, the canonical KTU result is:

$$
\boxed{\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} \approx \dfrac{P_{xx}^{2}(f)}{M}}
$$

This shows the **inverse-M reduction in variance** that makes the method statistically robust.

### Step 6 — Energy Preservation Property

Because the window $w(m)$ is real and even, $W(f)$ is real and even at $f=0$. Integrating the estimator over the Nyquist band:

$$
\int_{-1/(2T)}^{1/(2T)} \hat{P}_{BT}(f) \, df = \hat{r}_{xx}(0) \cdot w(0) = \hat{r}_{xx}(0)
$$

This is the **sample signal power** — confirming Parseval's theorem is satisfied.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A biomedical signal $x[n]$ of length $N=5$ has samples: $x[0]=1, x[1]=2, x[2]=3, x[3]=2, x[4]=1$. Compute the biased autocorrelation $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ for $m = 0, 1, 2$. Using a rectangular window with $M=2$, find $\hat{P}_{BT}(f=0)$ and $\hat{P}_{BT}(f=0.5/T)$.

### Step 1 — Compute Biased Autocorrelation

For $m=0$:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(0) = \dfrac{1}{5} \sum_{n=0}^{4} x^{2}[n] = \dfrac{1}{5}(1+4+9+4+1) = \dfrac{19}{5} = 3.8
$$

For $m=1$:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(1) = \dfrac{1}{5} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] x[n+1] = \dfrac{1}{5}(1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 1) = \dfrac{16}{5} = 3.2
$$

For $m=2$:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(2) = \dfrac{1}{5} \sum_{n=0}^{2} x[n] x[n+2] = \dfrac{1}{5}(1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 1) = \dfrac{10}{5} = 2.0
$$

For negative lags: $\hat{r}_{xx}(-1) = 3.2$ and $\hat{r}_{xx}(-2) = 2.0$ (autocorrelation is even).

### Step 2 — Apply Rectangular Window

With $M=2$ and rectangular $w(m) = 1$ for $\vert m \vert \le 2$:

$$
c_{xx}(0)=3.8, \quad c_{xx}(\pm 1)=3.2, \quad c_{xx}(\pm 2)=2.0
$$

### Step 3 — Compute PSD at $f=0$

$$
\hat{P}_{BT}(0) = T \sum_{m=-2}^{2} c_{xx}(m) = T (3.8 + 2 \cdot 3.2 + 2 \cdot 2.0) = T (3.8 + 6.4 + 4.0) = 14.2 \, T
$$

### Step 4 — Compute PSD at $f=0.5/T$ (Nyquist)

Using $e^{-j2\pi (0.5/T) m T} = e^{-j\pi m} = (-1)^{m}$:

$$
\hat{P}_{BT}(0.5/T) = T \sum_{m=-2}^{2} c_{xx}(m) (-1)^{m}
$$

$$
= T \left[ 3.8 - 3.2 + 2.0 - 2.0 + 3.2 \right] = T (3.8)
$$

So $\hat{P}_{BT}(0.5/T) = 3.8 \, T$ (equal to $\hat{r}_{xx}(0)$ — the DC power dominates, indicating low high-frequency content, consistent with a smooth biomedical-like waveform).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The PSD at DC ($14.2T$) is approximately 3.7 times larger than at Nyquist ($3.8T$), confirming the signal has dominant low-frequency energy — typical of ECG, EEG, and respiratory waveforms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Blackman-Tukey Estimation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Biomedical Signal xn, N samples] --> B[Mean Removal and Detrending]
    B --> C[Compute Biased Autocorrelation rxxm, lags -M to +M]
    C --> D{Window Selection}
    D --> D1[Rectangular]
    D --> D2[Bartlett Triangular]
    D --> D3[Hann or Hamming]
    D --> D4[Blackman]
    D1 --> E[Multiply rxxm by wm Pointwise]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Zero-Pad to L greater than 2M+1]
    F --> G[FFT Operation, length L]
    G --> H[PSD Magnitude, PBTf]
    H --> I{Smoothing and Interpretation}
    I --> J[ECG HRV Analysis]
    I --> K[EEG Band Power Extraction]
    I --> L[EMG Median Frequency]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Mathematical Pipeline Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Input Data xn, length N] --> M2[Estimate rxxm]
    M2 --> M3[Window Design wm, length 2M+1]
    M3 --> M4[Pointwise Multiplication]
    M4 --> M5[FFT of length L]
    M5 --> M6[Output PSD PBTf]
    M6 --> M7[Statistical Analysis: Bias and Variance]
    M7 --> M8[Clinical Decision Support]
```

## 4.3 Bias-Variance Trade-off Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[Increase M, larger window] --> N2[Better Frequency Resolution]
    N1 --> N3[Lower Variance]
    N1 --> N4[Higher Bias from mainlobe broadening]
    N5[Decrease M, smaller window] --> N6[Worse Frequency Resolution]
    N5 --> N7[Higher Variance]
    N5 --> N8[Lower Bias from sidelobe leakage]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Diagram Reading:** The two competing forces of **bias** and **variance** are fundamental to all non-parametric spectral estimators. The optimal $M$ balances these for the specific biomedical application — typically $M \approx N/5$ for HRV and $M \approx N/4$ for EEG band power.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — *Define Blackman-Tukey method of power spectral estimation. State any two advantages of using a lag window in this method.*

**Model Answer:**

The Blackman-Tukey method is a non-parametric PSD estimation technique that computes the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform of the **windowed (tapered) sample autocorrelation sequence** of a finite-length signal.

**Two advantages of lag windowing:**

1. **Variance reduction:** By truncating the unreliable high-lag components of the sample autocorrelation, the variance of the PSD estimate is reduced approximately by a factor of $M$ (window length), producing smoother spectra suitable for clinical interpretation.

2. **Bias control via window choice:** Windows such as Hamming or Blackman suppress spectral leakage caused by the implicit rectangular truncation, reducing the bias introduced by energy from adjacent frequencies — critical when detecting closely-spaced biomedical rhythms (e.g., alpha and theta EEG bands).

> [!TIP]
> **Mark allocation (KTU pattern):** Definition: 1 mark; Advantage 1: 1 mark; Advantage 2: 1 mark. Use a diagram or equation in part (b) for partial credit recovery.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — *What is the role of the maximum lag $M$ in Blackman-Tukey estimation? State the relationship between $M$ and the statistical properties of the estimate.*

**Model Answer:**

The maximum lag $M$ controls the **length of the lag window** $w(m)$ and directly determines both the **resolution** and **variance** of the Blackman-Tukey PSD estimate.

**Relationship:**

- **Frequency Resolution:** $\Delta f \approx \dfrac{1}{MT}$ — larger $M$ yields finer frequency discrimination, enabling separation of closely-spaced spectral peaks (e.g., 9 Hz alpha vs. 10 Hz alpha variant).
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} \approx \dfrac{P_{xx}^{2}(f)}{M}$ — larger $M$ reduces variance, producing a more statistically stable estimate suitable for averaging across patient cohorts.
- **Bias:** Larger $M$ increases bias because the window's mainlobe broadens, smearing adjacent spectral features.

Thus $M$ embodies the **bias-variance trade-off**, and a common KTU-recommended choice is $M \approx N/5$ to $N/10$.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]** — *With a neat block diagram, explain the Blackman-Tukey method of spectral estimation. Derive the expression for the mean and variance of the estimator and discuss the bias-variance trade-off.*

#### (a) Block Diagram and Method Explanation [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (describe in words as per KTU answer sheet):**

The Blackman-Tukey method consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Signal Acquisition:** Collect $N$ samples $x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]$ of the stationary biomedical signal.
2. **Autocorrelation Computation:** Calculate $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ for lags $m = -M, -M+1, \dots, 0, \dots, M-1, M$ using either the biased or unbiased estimator.
3. **Window Multiplication:** Multiply each $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ by the symmetric lag window $w(m)$ to obtain $c_{xx}(m) = \hat{r}_{xx}(m) w(m)$.
4. **DTFT (or FFT):** Compute the DTFT (in practice, zero-padded FFT) of $c_{xx}(m)$ to obtain $\hat{P}_{BT}(f)$.
5. **Interpretation:** Display $\vert \hat{P}_{BT}(f) \vert$ as the estimated power spectral density.

**Key Mathematical Expression:**

$$
\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = T \sum_{m=-M}^{M} \hat{r}_{xx}(m) w(m) e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

**Why this works (intuitive explanation):** The Wiener-Khinchin theorem states that PSD is the Fourier transform of the true autocorrelation. With finite data, we estimate the autocorrelation. Windowing suppresses unreliable large-lag estimates, smoothing the resulting spectrum.

#### (b) Derivation of Mean, Variance, and Trade-off Discussion [7 Marks]

**Mean of the Estimator:**

For a WSS process with true autocorrelation $r_{xx}(m)$:

$$
\mathbb{E}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} = \sum_{m=-M}^{M} \mathbb{E}\{\hat{r}_{xx}(m)\} w(m) e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

Since $\mathbb{E}\{\hat{r}_{xx}(m)\} \approx r_{xx}(m)$ for the biased estimator and large $N$:

$$
\mathbb{E}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} = \sum_{m=-M}^{M} r_{xx}(m) w(m) e^{-j2\pi f m T}
$$

This is the convolution $P_{xx}(f) * W(f)$ in the frequency domain. The **bias** is the difference:

$$
\text{Bias} = P_{xx}(f) * W(f) - P_{xx}(f) = \int_{-1/(2T)}^{1/(2T)} P_{xx}(\nu) \left[ W(f-\nu) - \delta(f-\nu) \right] d\nu
$$

**Variance of the Estimator:**

For a Gaussian WSS process, the variance is given by:

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} \approx P_{xx}^{2}(f) \left[ 1 + 2 \sum_{m=1}^{M} w^{2}(m) \right]
$$

For large $M$ with $w(0) = 1$ and normalized window energy:

$$
\boxed{\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} \approx \dfrac{P_{xx}^{2}(f)}{M}}
$$

**Bias-Variance Trade-off Discussion:**

- As $M$ increases, **variance decreases** (smoother estimate) but **bias increases** (smeared peaks).
- As $M$ decreases, **bias decreases** (sharper peaks) but **variance increases** (noisy estimate).
- Optimal $M$ balances these for the specific application:
  - $M \approx N/10$ for stationary ECG HRV analysis.
  - $M \approx N/5$ for EEG band-power estimation.
  - $M \approx N/4$ for narrow-band EMG fatigue analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing the $T$ factor** in the PSD formula — KTU deducts **1 mark** if students write $\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = \sum \hat{r}_{xx}(m) w(m) e^{-j2\pi f m T}$ without the sampling interval $T$.
> 2. **Confusing bias direction** — students often claim "larger $M$ reduces bias." The correct statement: "larger $M$ reduces variance but increases bias via mainlobe broadening."
> 3. **Forgetting that windowing introduces bias** — the *raw* correlogram is unbiased; it is the *windowing* that introduces bias. This conceptual point is worth **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — failing to mention "WSS process" and "Gaussian assumption for variance derivation" costs **1 mark**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Alternative]** — *Explain the concept of lag windows in Blackman-Tukey spectral estimation. Compare Rectangular, Bartlett, Hann, and Blackman windows with respect to mainlobe width, sidelobe level, and their effect on bias and variance.*

#### (a) Concept of Lag Windows and Their Role [7 Marks]

A **lag window** $w(m)$ is a symmetric, finite-length weighting sequence applied to the sample autocorrelation before computing the DTFT. It is a real, even function with $w(0) = 1$ and $w(m) = 0$ for $|m| > M$.

**Role of lag windows:**

1. **Tapering unreliable estimates:** The sample autocorrelation $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ for large $|m|$ has high variance (fewer products contribute). Windows smoothly attenuate these values.
2. **Bias control via spectral smoothing:** Multiplication in the lag domain corresponds to convolution with $W(f)$ in the frequency domain. A well-designed $W(f)$ should have a narrow mainlobe and low sidelobes.
3. **Variance reduction:** The number of effective degrees of freedom in the estimate is approximately $2M/N$, which limits variance.
4. **Ensuring non-negative PSD:** For windows with non-negative Fourier transforms (e.g., Bartlett, Hann), the estimated PSD is guaranteed to be non-negative at all frequencies — important for KTU signal processing applications.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$
w(m) =
\begin{cases}
\text{specified function of } m, & |m| \le M \\[4pt]
0, & |m| > M
\end{cases}
$$

with symmetry $w(m) = w(-m)$.

#### (b) Comparative Analysis of Windows [7 Marks]

| **Window** | **Time-Domain Expression** | **Mainlobe Width** | **Sidelobe Level (dB)** | **Effect on Bias** | **Effect on Variance** |
|------------|----------------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Rectangular | $1$ | Narrowest ($2/M$) | High ($-13$ dB) | Lowest (sharp peaks) | Highest (noisy) |
| Bartlett | $1 - \vert m \vert / M$ | Moderate ($4/M$) | Moderate ($-26$ dB) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Hann | $0.5 + 0.5 \cos(\pi m / M)$ | Moderate ($4/M$) | Low ($-31$ dB) | Slightly higher | Lower than Bartlett |
| Blackman | $0.42 + 0.5 \cos(\pi m / M) + 0.08 \cos(2\pi m / M)$ | Widest ($6/M$) | Very low ($-58$ dB) | Highest (smeared peaks) | Lowest (smoothest) |

**Discussion:**

- **Rectangular Window** is the *no-window* baseline; the periodogram is the special case $M = N-1$. It has the best resolution but worst leakage, making it unsuitable for detecting weak biomedical rhythms adjacent to strong ones (e.g., a small alpha spike near a large delta wave).
- **Bartlett (Triangular)** offers a good compromise and is the historical baseline of Welch's method (overlapping segments).
- **Hann** is widely used in KTU 2024 biomedical applications for its smooth sidelobe decay, ideal for HRV analysis.
- **Blackman** provides the best dynamic range ($-58$ dB), excellent for spectral analysis of signals with large amplitude differences, such as ECG with prominent QRS complex near low-amplitude P and T waves.

**Key Engineering Insight:** The choice of window is governed by the **dynamic range of the signal of interest.** For typical ECG/EEG where peak-to-pedestal ratios are 20–40 dB, Hann or Hamming windows are preferred. For signals with very large dynamic range, Blackman is optimal at the cost of resolution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. **Students often confuse "mainlobe width" with "sidelobe level."** Mainlobe width controls *resolution*; sidelobe level controls *leakage/bias*. KTU deducts 1 mark for conflating them.
> 2. **Forgetting symmetry $w(m) = w(-m)$** — this is essential to guarantee the PSD is real. Lose 1 mark if omitted.
> 3. **Not mentioning the role of the FFT length $L \ge 2M+1$** when implementing the windowed correlation in practice. The FFT length must be at least $2M+1$ to avoid circular correlation artifacts.
> 4. **Confusing Hann with Hamming** — the cosine coefficients differ (0.5/0.5 vs. 0.54/0.46). A 1-mark penalty applies if the formulas are interchanged.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Examination:**

- [ ] **Blackman-Tukey** is an *indirect* (correlogram) non-parametric PSD estimator using the windowed DTFT of the sample autocorrelation.
- [ ] Core formula: $\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = T \sum_{m=-M}^{M} \hat{r}_{xx}(m) w(m) e^{-j2\pi f m T}$.
- [ ] Window $w(m)$ must be real, even, with $w(0) = 1$ and $w(m) = 0$ for $|m| > M$.
- [ ] **Biased autocorrelation** uses divisor $N$ — preferred for guaranteed non-negative PSD.
- [ ] **Frequency resolution** $\Delta f \approx 1/(MT)$ — improves with larger $M$.
- [ ] **Variance** $\approx P_{xx}^{2}(f) / M$ — decreases with larger $M$.
- [ ] **Bias** arises from $W(f)$ sidelobes (leakage) and mainlobe broadening — grows with $M$.
- [ ] Recommended practical choice: $M \approx N/5$ to $N/10$.
- [ ] Rectangular window has narrowest mainlobe (best resolution) but highest sidelobes (worst leakage).
- [ ] Blackman window has widest mainlobe (worst resolution) but lowest sidelobes (best leakage suppression at $-58$ dB).
- [ ] For Gaussian processes, variance formula holds; for non-Gaussian, it is an approximation.
- [ ] The estimator preserves total power: $\int \hat{P}_{BT}(f) df = \hat{r}_{xx}(0)$.
- [ ] Multiplication in lag domain = convolution in frequency domain (the source of bias).
- [ ] Implementation requires FFT of length $L \ge 2M+1$ after zero-padding the windowed autocorrelation.
- [ ] In **biomedical applications**: Blackman-Tukey is the gold standard for *ECG HRV*, *EEG band power*, and *EMG median frequency* due to its smooth, low-variance output.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: QRS Detection in ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** QRS Detection in ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of QRS Detection in ECG

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**QRS Detection** is defined as the algorithmic process of identifying the temporal location, amplitude, and morphology of the **QRS complex** — the most prominent and diagnostically significant waveform within the electrocardiogram (ECG) signal, which corresponds to the electrical depolarization of the ventricles of the human heart.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, QRS detection is formally classified as a **feature extraction problem** in the time-domain analysis of biomedical signals, forming the foundational step upon which all subsequent cardiac diagnostics — heart rate variability (HRV) analysis, arrhythmia classification, R-R interval tachogram generation, and beat-to-beat interval analysis — are constructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBBRT504 – Module 4):**
> *QRS detection in ECG refers to the reliable identification of the R-peak of each cardiac cycle using linear and non-linear digital signal processing techniques applied to the preprocessed ECG waveform.*

## 1.2 The QRS Complex — Origin and Morphology

The QRS complex is generated when the **Bundle of His**, the **bundle branches**, and the **Purkinje fibres** transmit a synchronized depolarization wave through the ventricular myocardium. This produces a sharp, high-amplitude, high-frequency deflection typically lasting between **80 ms and 120 ms** in a healthy adult, with dominant spectral energy concentrated in the **5 Hz – 15 Hz** band.

A standard lead-II ECG beat comprises the following fiducial points:

| Fiducial Point | Physiological Meaning | Typical Duration | Typical Amplitude (Lead II) |
|---|---|---|---|
| P-wave | Atrial depolarization | 60 – 100 ms | < 0.25 mV |
| **Q-wave** | Initial septal depolarization (downward) | < 40 ms | < 0.1 mV (25% of R) |
| **R-peak** | Main ventricular depolarization (upward) | < 50 ms | **0.5 – 2.0 mV** |
| **S-wave** | Late ventricular depolarization (downward) | < 50 ms | < 0.5 mV |
| T-wave | Ventricular repolarization | 100 – 250 ms | < 0.5 mV |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heart-Beat Ear"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Drummer in an Orchestra**
>
> Imagine an ECG recording as a long recording of a **40-piece orchestra**. Among the flutes, violins, and cellos (which represent the P-wave, T-wave, and baseline noise), the **QRS complex** is the loud, sharp **strike of a big bass drum**. Every time the drummer hits, you can unmistakably identify that exact moment by simply *listening for the loudest, sharpest sound* and ignoring the rest.
>
> QRS detection is exactly this — designing a digital "ear" that listens to the noisy ECG waveform and locates every sharp "drum hit" (the R-peak) with surgical precision, even when the orchestra (the patient's heart) is playing in an irregular or noisy fashion.

## 1.4 Why QRS Detection is the Cornerstone of ECG Analysis

The R-peak serves as a **cardiac time-stamp**. Once the R-peak location is known:

- **Heart Rate (HR)** is computed directly as $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{\text{interval (s)}}}$ beats per minute.
- **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** is derived from successive R-R intervals.
- **Arrhythmia classification** (e.g., PVC, AFib, Bradycardia, Tachycardia) is performed using R-R interval patterns.
- **Signal averaging** techniques (e.g., SAECG) require R-peak alignment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics for QRS Detectors (per ANSI/AAMI EC38:1998):**
> - **Sensitivity (Se)** = TP / (TP + FN)
> - **Positive Predictivity (+P)** = TP / (FP + TP)
> - **Detection Accuracy (Acc)** = TP / (Total actual QRS complexes)
> - Where **TP = True Positives**, **FP = False Positives**, **FN = False Negatives**.
> - A clinically acceptable detector must achieve **Se > 99%** and **+P > 99%**.

## 1.5 Challenges in Robust QRS Detection

1. **Baseline wander** (0.1 – 0.5 Hz) from respiration and patient movement.
2. **Powerline interference (PLI)** at **50 Hz / 60 Hz**.
3. **Muscle artifact (EMG noise)** in the **20 – 300 Hz** band.
4. **High-amplitude T-waves** that can be falsely detected as R-peaks.
5. **Pathological QRS morphologies** (e.g., wide QRS in bundle branch blocks).
6. **Low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** in ambulatory / Holter recordings.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of QRS Detection Algorithms

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups QRS detection algorithms under three broad categories:

### Category A — Non-Adaptive (Threshold-Based) Methods
- **Amplitude-threshold method**: Detect R-peak as the sample exceeding a fixed voltage threshold.
- **First-derivative method**: Detect the zero-crossing of the first derivative (steepest slope of QRS).
- **Second-derivative method**: Detect the maximum of $d^{2}x/dt^{2}$ during the QRS.

### Category B — Adaptive (Real-Time) Methods
- **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (1985)** — the gold standard taught in KTU.
- **Hamilton-Tompkins Algorithm** — an optimized variant.
- **Adaptive thresholding** — dynamically updates thresholds based on running signal and noise statistics.

### Category C — Transform / Learning-Based Methods
- **Wavelet-transform-based** detection using modulus maxima.
- **Neural-network-based** and **Deep-Learning (CNN, LSTM)** detection.
- **Matched filtering** with template libraries (e.g., MIT-BIH database templates).

## 2.2 The Pan-Tompkins Algorithm — The KTU Gold Standard

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm is a **real-time, adaptive, six-stage linear filter chain** specifically engineered to detect QRS complexes in noisy ECG. It is the most heavily weighted topic in Module 4 of PBBRT504.

### Stage 1: Bandpass Filtering (5 – 15 Hz)

A **cascaded low-pass + high-pass filter** is used to isolate the QRS spectral band while suppressing baseline wander (< 5 Hz) and high-frequency noise (> 15 Hz).

**Low-Pass Filter (2nd-order Butterworth, cutoff ≈ 11 Hz):**

$$
H_{LP}(z) = \frac{(1 - z^{-6})^{2}}{(1 - z^{-1})^{2}}
$$

**High-Pass Filter (1st-order, cutoff ≈ 5 Hz):**

$$
H_{HP}(z) = \frac{z^{-16} - \frac{1}{32}(1 + z^{-1})^{16}}{1 - z^{-1}}
$$

The combined **bandpass filter** is:

$$
H_{BP}(z) = H_{LP}(z) \cdot H_{HP}(z)
$$

### Stage 2: Derivative Operator

A **five-point derivative** emphasizes the high slew rate of the QRS:

$$
H_{D}(z) = \frac{1}{8T}\left(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z^{1} + z^{2}\right)
$$

where $T$ is the sampling period. This operator doubles the suppression of low-frequency components (P, T waves) and amplifies the steep QRS slopes.

### Stage 3: Squaring Function

A **point-by-point squaring** is applied:

$$
y[n] = (x[n])^{2}
$$

This makes all values strictly positive and emphasizes large differences — large QRS amplitudes are amplified, while small noise contributions are squashed.

### Stage 4: Moving-Window Integration

A **moving average** smooths the squared signal and produces a waveform with a single rising-falling edge per QRS:

$$
y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]
$$

where $N \approx 0.15 \times f_{s}$ samples (typical: $N = 30$ for $f_s = 200$ Hz).

### Stage 5: Adaptive Thresholding

Two thresholds — **signal level threshold ($THR_{sig}$)** and **noise level threshold ($THR_{noise}$)** — are dynamically updated:

$$
THR_{sig} = NOISE_{level} + 0.25 \cdot (SIGNAL_{level} - NOISE_{level})
$$

$$
THR_{noise} = NOISE_{level} + 0.50 \cdot (SIGNR_{level} - NOISE_{level})
$$

Whenever a peak exceeds $THR_{sig}$, it is classified as a **QRS peak**; otherwise it is treated as noise, and the running $NOISE_{level}$ is updated.

### Stage 6: Search Back & T-Wave Discrimination

If no QRS is detected for **1.66 × current R-R interval**, the algorithm performs a *search-back* using $THR_{noise}$. T-waves are rejected by enforcing a **refractory period of 200 ms** — any peak within 200 ms of the previous R-peak is rejected.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Table for QRS Detection**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heart Rate | $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}}$ | $RR_{interval}$ in seconds | **beats/min (bpm)** |
| 2 | QRS Bandwidth | $\Delta f = f_{high} - f_{low}$ | Spectral extent | **Hz** |
| 3 | Pan-Tompkins LPF | $H_{LP}(z) = \frac{(1-z^{-6})^{2}}{(1-z^{-1})^{2}}$ | 2nd-order Butterworth | — |
| 4 | Pan-Tompkins HPF | $H_{HP}(z) = \frac{z^{-16}-\tfrac{1}{32}(1+z^{-1})^{16}}{1-z^{-1}}$ | 1st-order all-pole | — |
| 5 | Derivative Operator | $H_D(z) = \tfrac{1}{8T}(-z^{-2}-2z^{-1}+2z^{1}+z^{2})$ | Five-point differentiator | — |
| 6 | Squaring Function | $y[n] = (x[n])^{2}$ | Nonlinear amplification | — |
| 7 | Moving Average | $y[n] = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$ | $N$ = window length | samples |
| 8 | Sensitivity | $Se = \tfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Detection completeness | **%** |
| 9 | Positive Predictivity | $+P = \tfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Detection precision | **%** |
| 10 | Detection Error Rate | $DER = \tfrac{FP+FN}{Total\ QRS}$ | Combined error | **%** |
| 11 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\tfrac{\sigma_{signal}^{2}}{\sigma_{noise}^{2}}$ | Signal quality metric | **dB** |
| 12 | Refractory Period | $t_{ref} = 200$ ms | T-wave rejection | **ms** |
| 13 | Search-Back Window | $t_{sb} = 1.66 \cdot \overline{RR}$ | Missed-beat recovery | **ms** |
| 14 | Adaptive Threshold | $THR = N_{lvl} + \alpha(S_{lvl}-N_{lvl})$ | $\alpha = 0.25$ signal, $0.50$ noise | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm is the **backbone of every commercial Holter monitor, ICU bedside monitor, wearable fitness tracker, and implantable cardiac defibrillator (ICD)** manufactured in the last three decades. It is embedded in:

- **Patient monitoring systems** (Philips IntelliVue, GE CARESCAPE).
- **Ambulatory Holter recorders** (Schiller, Mortara).
- **Wearable ECG patches** (iRythm Zio, Apple Watch ECG, Fitbit).
- **Automated external defibrillators (AEDs)** — R-peak detection is critical for shock-synchronization.

A delay of even **50 ms** in R-peak detection can cause a defibrillator to deliver a shock during the **T-wave vulnerable period**, inducing ventricular fibrillation — a fatal iatrogenic arrhythmia. Hence, QRS detection is not just an academic exercise but a **life-critical signal processing pipeline**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pan-Tompkins Low-Pass Filter (2nd-Order Butterworth)

We begin with the **bilinear-transformed** analog Butterworth prototype. For a 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter with cutoff $\Omega_{c}$, the analog transfer function is:

$$
H_{a}(s) = \frac{\Omega_{c}^{2}}{s^{2} + \sqrt{2}\,\Omega_{c}\,s + \Omega_{c}^{2}}
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the **bilinear transform** $s = \frac{2}{T_{b}}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ where $T_{b}$ is the time constant chosen such that the cutoff aligns to **11 Hz** at the sampling rate $f_{s} = 200$ Hz.

**Step 2:** Substitute and simplify to obtain the digital filter:

$$
H_{LP}(z) = \frac{(1 - z^{-6})^{2}}{(1 - z^{-1})^{2}}
$$

This can be expanded for clarity:

$$
H_{LP}(z) = \frac{1 - 2z^{-6} + z^{-12}}{1 - 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The numerator $(1 - z^{-6})^{2}$ represents **two cascaded moving average filters of length 6 samples each**, which in the time-domain is equivalent to averaging over a 12-sample window. This corresponds to $\frac{12}{f_s} = \frac{12}{200} = 60$ ms — slightly longer than a QRS — providing smooth low-pass response.

## 3.2 Derivation of the High-Pass Filter (1st-Order)

The analog high-pass prototype is:

$$
H_{a}(s) = \frac{s}{s + \Omega_{c}}
$$

**Step 1:** After bilinear transform:

$$
H_{HP,a}(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + (2T_{b}\Omega_{c} - 1)z^{-1}}
$$

**Step 2:** To achieve a very sharp roll-off at 5 Hz while using integer arithmetic, Pan-Tompkins introduce a **delay term $z^{-16}$** and an **all-pass compensation term** $\frac{1}{32}(1+z^{-1})^{16}$:

$$
H_{HP}(z) = \frac{z^{-16} - \frac{1}{32}(1 + z^{-1})^{16}}{1 - z^{-1}}
$$

This realizes a filter with a 3-dB cutoff at **5 Hz** and a sharp attenuation of **35 dB** at **0 Hz** (DC), which effectively eliminates baseline wander.

## 3.3 Magnitude Response Verification

The combined bandpass filter magnitude response is:

$$
\vert H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) \vert \cdot \vert H_{HP}(e^{j\omega}) \vert
$$

The **passband** is approximately **[5 Hz, 12 Hz]**, achieving maximum gain near **10 Hz** — the dominant QRS spectral peak.

$$
\omega_{peak} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_{peak}}{f_{s}} \quad \text{rad/sample}
$$

$$
\boxed{\vert H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) \vert_{max} \approx 1.0 \quad \text{at} \quad f = 10 \text{ Hz}}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Five-Point Derivative Operator

The ideal differentiator in the discrete domain is:

$$
H_{ideal}(z) = \frac{1}{T}(1 - z^{-1})
$$

To improve robustness against high-frequency noise, Pan-Tompkins use a **5-point central difference** approximation:

$$
y[n] = \frac{1}{8T}\bigl(x[n+2] + 2x[n+1] - 2x[n-1] - x[n-2]\bigr)
$$

In the Z-domain:

$$
H_{D}(z) = \frac{1}{8T}\left(z^{2} + 2z^{1} - 2z^{-1} - z^{-2}\right)
$$

The **magnitude response** of this differentiator is:

$$
\vert H_{D}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{4T}\bigl(\sin(2\omega) + 2\sin(\omega)\bigr)
$$

This response provides **monotonic gain increase** in the QRS band (5–15 Hz) while attenuating very low frequencies (P, T waves) and very high frequencies (noise).

## 3.5 Derivation of the Squaring Stage

The squaring stage output is:

$$
y[n] = \bigl(x[n]\bigr)^{2}
$$

For an input $x[n] = A\cos(\omega n) + n[n]$ (signal + noise), the squared output is:

$$
y[n] = A^{2}\cos^{2}(\omega n) + 2A\cos(\omega n)n[n] + n^{2}[n]
$$

$$
= \frac{A^{2}}{2} + \frac{A^{2}}{2}\cos(2\omega n) + 2A\cos(\omega n)n[n] + n^{2}[n]
$$

The squaring:
- **Eliminates negative values** (all outputs ≥ 0).
- **Doubles the signal frequency** (the $\cos(2\omega n)$ term) — pushes the energy further into the band where the moving integrator operates.
- **Suppresses low-amplitude noise** (the $n^{2}[n]$ term is much smaller when $n \ll A$).

## 3.6 Moving Window Integration — Time Response

For a QRS event approximated as a triangular pulse of base $B$ samples, the integrator output is:

$$
y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x[n-k]
$$

The peak of $y[n]$ occurs at the **center of the QRS window** with a value:

$$
y_{peak} = \frac{A}{2}\left(1 + \frac{B}{N}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad N \geq B
$$

The choice $N = 30$ (at $f_s = 200$ Hz) corresponds to a **150 ms** integration window — wide enough to fully encompass a QRS (80–120 ms) and to bridge any brief gaps within the QRS, but narrow enough to remain distinct from adjacent P and T waves.

## 3.7 Adaptive Threshold Update Equations

The running signal and noise levels are updated by **exponential smoothing**:

$$
SIGNAL_{level}[n] = 0.125 \cdot y_{peak}[n] + 0.875 \cdot SIGNAL_{level}[n-1]
$$

$$
NOISE_{level}[n] = 0.125 \cdot y_{peak}[n] + 0.875 \cdot NOISE_{level}[n-1]
$$

The two thresholds are then:

$$
THR_{signal} = NOISE_{level} + 0.25(SIGNAL_{level} - NOISE_{level})
$$

$$
THR_{noise} = NOISE_{level} + 0.50(SIGNAL_{level} - NOISE_{level})
$$

The forgetting factor **0.875** corresponds to a time constant of approximately **8 samples** at $f_s = 200$ Hz — i.e., the algorithm "remembers" the past **~40 ms** of activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 0.875?** The exponential decay weight is chosen such that the algorithm adapts to slow baseline shifts (e.g., respiration-induced gain changes) but remains fast enough to track abrupt amplitude changes (e.g., sudden PVCs).

## 3.8 Refractory Period and T-Wave Rejection — Analytical Proof

A T-wave typically occurs **300 ms** after the R-peak. If the refractory period is set to **200 ms**, any peak occurring within this window is automatically rejected as a T-wave.

The minimum RR-interval corresponding to a heart rate of **300 bpm** is:

$$
RR_{min} = \frac{60}{300} = 0.2\ \text{s} = 200\ \text{ms}
$$

Thus, the refractory period also doubles as the **maximum-allowed heart-rate guard**, preventing the algorithm from double-counting during extreme tachycardia.

## 3.9 Final Performance Numbers (Reported by Pan & Tompkins, 1985)

On the **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database (48 records, ~116,000 beats)**:

$$
Se = 99.75\% \quad ; \quad +P = 99.54\%
$$

$$
DER = \frac{FP + FN}{Total\ QRS} \approx 0.71\%
$$

These numbers remain the **benchmark** against which every new QRS detector (including modern deep-learning models) is validated.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal flow of the Pan-Tompkins algorithm, from raw ECG input to detected R-peak output. Each block represents a discrete processing stage with a defined mathematical transformation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ECG Signal xn] --> B[Bandpass Filter 5 to 15 Hz]
    B --> C[Derivative Operator Five Point]
    C --> D[Squaring Function y equals x squared]
    D --> E[Moving Window Integrator N equals 30]
    E --> F[Adaptive Threshold Comparator]
    F --> G{Peak above THRsig}
    G -- Yes --> H[Update SIGNAL level]
    G -- No --> I[Update NOISE level]
    H --> J[Apply 200 ms Refractory Rule]
    I --> F
    J --> K[Output R Peak Timestamp]
    F --> L{No QRS for 1.66 RR}
    L -- Yes --> M[Search Back using THRnoise]
    M --> K
    L -- No --> F
```

## 4.2 Detailed Stage-by-Stage Mermaid Block Diagram

The following diagram elaborates the **internal sub-blocks** of the bandpass filter and adaptive threshold stages, since these are the most heavily tested in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BPF["STAGE 1 BANDPASS FILTER 5 to 15 Hz"]
        A1[Raw ECG Input] --> A2[Low Pass Butterworth Order 2 Fc 11 Hz]
        A2 --> A3[High Pass All Pole Fc 5 Hz]
        A3 --> A4[Bandpass Output yLPF n and yHPF n]
    end

    subgraph DIFSQ["STAGE 2 AND 3 DERIVATIVE AND SQUARING"]
        A4 --> B1[Five Point Derivator HD z]
        B1 --> B2[Squaring y equals x squared]
    end

    subgraph MAW["STAGE 4 MOVING WINDOW INTEGRATION"]
        B2 --> C1[Sliding Window Length N equals 30]
        C1 --> C2[Integrated Waveform yInt n]
    end

    subgraph THR["STAGE 5 ADAPTIVE THRESHOLDING"]
        C2 --> D1{Peaks above THRsig}
        D1 -- Yes --> D2[Classify as QRS Update SIGNAL level]
        D1 -- No --> D3[Classify as Noise Update NOISE level]
    end

    subgraph RFR["STAGE 6 REFRACTORY AND SEARCH BACK"]
        D2 --> E1[Apply 200 ms Refractory Period]
        E1 --> E2{No QRS for 1.66 RR}
        E2 -- Yes --> E3[Search Back at THRnoise]
        E2 -- No --> E4[Output R Peak Timestamp]
        E3 --> E4
```

## 4.3 Adaptive Threshold — Decision Logic Block Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart zooms into the **adaptive threshold decision logic**, which is the most commonly asked diagrammatic question in KTU Module 4 examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[New Peak Detected] --> Q{Refractory Active}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Discard Peak]
    Q -- No --> S{Peak above THRsig}
    S -- Yes --> T[Confirm QRS Update SIGNAL level]
    S -- No --> U{Peak above THRnoise}
    U -- Yes --> V[Classify as Possible QRS Wait for Confirmation]
    U -- No --> W[Classify as Noise Update NOISE level]
    T --> X[Emit R Peak Time]
    V --> Y{Confirmation in 360 ms}
    Y -- Yes --> T
    Y -- No --> W
```

## 4.4 Schematic Annotation Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-by-Block Functional Summary**

| Block # | Block Name | Function | KTU Key Equation | Output Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bandpass Filter | Isolate 5–15 Hz QRS band | $H_{BP}(z) = H_{LP}(z) \cdot H_{HP}(z)$ | Filtered ECG |
| 2 | Derivative | Emphasize QRS slope | $H_D(z) = \tfrac{1}{8T}(-z^{-2}-2z^{-1}+2z^{1}+z^{2})$ | Slope waveform |
| 3 | Squaring | Nonlinear amplification | $y[n] = (x[n])^{2}$ | All-positive energy |
| 4 | Moving Average | Smooth into single peak/beat | $y[n] = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum x[n-k]$ | Integrated waveform |
| 5 | Adaptive Threshold | Distinguish QRS from noise | $THR = N_{lvl} + 0.25(S_{lvl} - N_{lvl})$ | Binary decision |
| 6 | Refractory + Search | Reject T-waves; recover missed beats | $t_{ref} = 200$ ms | R-peak timestamps |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Model Answer Style:** Each answer is structured into *Definition → Key Points → Final Result* to match the KTU board valuation rubric.

---

### Question A1: Define QRS complex. Why is its detection important in ECG signal processing? [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The QRS complex is the waveform in an ECG that represents the electrical depolarization of the ventricles of the heart. It consists of three deflections — Q (downward), R (upward, dominant), and S (downward) — and typically lasts between **80 ms and 120 ms** in a healthy adult.

**Importance (2 marks):**
- The R-peak serves as the most prominent and easily identifiable fiducial point in the ECG.
- Heart rate is computed directly from the **R-R interval**: $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}\ (s)}$.
- Heart Rate Variability (HRV), arrhythmia classification, and signal-averaged ECG all depend on accurate R-peak detection.

---

### Question A2: List any four limitations of simple amplitude-threshold-based QRS detection. [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sensitivity to baseline wander** — the threshold may be exceeded by slow baseline shifts rather than true R-peaks.
2. **Inability to handle T-wave overshoot** — high-amplitude T-waves may be falsely detected as R-peaks (false positives).
3. **No fixed threshold works across patients** — R-wave amplitude varies by a factor of 10× between patients.
4. **Failure during arrhythmias** — flipped polarity, PVCs, and low-amplitude QRS complexes cause missed detections.
5. **Poor performance in noisy ambulatory recordings** — the threshold is overwhelmed by EMG and motion artifacts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. An internal choice between Question A and Question B is provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): With a neat block diagram, explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for QRS detection in ECG. [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm is a real-time, six-stage digital signal processing chain designed for robust QRS detection in noisy ECG. The block diagram is:

$$
\text{ECG} \rightarrow \text{BPF} \rightarrow \text{Derivative} \rightarrow \text{Squaring} \rightarrow \text{MWI} \rightarrow \text{Adaptive Threshold} \rightarrow \text{R-peak}
$$

**Stage 1 — Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz):** A cascade of a 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter (cutoff 11 Hz) and a 1st-order high-pass filter (cutoff 5 Hz). The transfer functions are:

$$
H_{LP}(z) = \frac{(1 - z^{-6})^{2}}{(1 - z^{-1})^{2}}, \quad H_{HP}(z) = \frac{z^{-16} - \frac{1}{32}(1 + z^{-1})^{16}}{1 - z^{-1}}
$$

This filter removes baseline wander (< 5 Hz) and high-frequency noise (> 15 Hz) while preserving the dominant QRS energy.

**Stage 2 — Derivative Operator:** A five-point differentiator that emphasizes the steep slopes of the QRS:

$$
H_D(z) = \frac{1}{8T}\left(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z^{1} + z^{2}\right)
$$

**Stage 3 — Squaring Function:** $y[n] = (x[n])^{2}$. This makes all values positive and amplifies large QRS amplitudes while suppressing small noise contributions.

**Stage 4 — Moving Window Integrator:** $y[n] = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$ with $N = 30$ (at 200 Hz). This produces a single smooth peak per QRS.

**Stage 5 — Adaptive Thresholding:** Two thresholds — $THR_{sig}$ and $THR_{noise}$ — are dynamically updated using exponential smoothing with forgetting factor 0.875. The signal level is updated only when a QRS is detected; the noise level otherwise.

**Stage 6 — Refractory Period & Search-Back:** A 200 ms refractory window rejects T-waves; a 1.66 × RR search-back window recovers missed beats.

**Reported performance:** Se = 99.75%, +P = 99.54% on MIT-BIH database.

---

#### Part (b): Compute the heart rate in bpm given five successive R-peak timestamps at t = 0.50, 1.20, 1.85, 2.50, 3.15 s. Comment on the variability. [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute the four R-R intervals:**

$$
RR_1 = 1.20 - 0.50 = 0.70\ \text{s}
$$

$$
RR_2 = 1.85 - 1.20 = 0.65\ \text{s}
$$

$$
RR_3 = 2.50 - 1.85 = 0.65\ \text{s}
$$

$$
RR_4 = 3.15 - 2.50 = 0.65\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean R-R interval:**

$$
\overline{RR} = \frac{0.70 + 0.65 + 0.65 + 0.65}{4} = \frac{2.65}{4} = 0.6625\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean heart rate:**

$$
HR_{mean} = \frac{60}{\overline{RR}} = \frac{60}{0.6625} = 90.57\ \text{bpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute SDNN (Standard Deviation of NN intervals) for variability comment:**

$$
SDNN = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{4}(RR_i - \overline{RR})^{2}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\bigl[(0.70-0.6625)^{2} + 3(0.65-0.6625)^{2}\bigr]}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\bigl[0.00141 + 3(0.000156)\bigr]} = \sqrt{0.000470} \approx 0.0217\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — RMSSD (successive differences):**

$$
RMSSD = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}\sum_{i=1}^{3}(RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}\bigl[(-0.05)^{2} + 0^{2} + 0^{2}\bigr]} = \sqrt{0.000833} \approx 0.0289\ \text{s}
$$

**Comment:** The heart rate of **~90.6 bpm** lies within the normal sinus rhythm range (60–100 bpm). The SDNN of **21.7 ms** indicates very low beat-to-beat variability — possibly indicating a regular rhythm with one slightly longer interval at the start.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a): Explain the first-derivative-based QRS detection method. Discuss its advantages and limitations. [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (3 marks):** The first derivative of the ECG, $y[n] = \tfrac{1}{T}(x[n] - x[n-1])$, is computed. The R-peak is identified as the **zero-crossing** of the first derivative — that is, the point where the slope changes sign from positive to negative (peak of the R-wave). Equivalently, the algorithm can detect the **maximum of the absolute derivative** $|y[n]|$ within each QRS window.

**Mathematical basis (2 marks):** Near the R-peak, the ECG signal can be approximated as a triangular pulse of width $W$ and peak amplitude $A$. The first derivative yields a constant slope $\tfrac{2A}{W}$ over the rising edge, and $-\tfrac{2A}{W}$ over the falling edge. The zero-crossing pinpoints the exact R-peak location.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Simple to implement; computationally inexpensive.
- Robust to baseline wander since derivative suppresses low-frequency drift.
- More accurate R-peak localization than pure amplitude thresholding.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Sensitive to high-frequency noise; derivative amplifies high-frequency artifacts.
- Cannot distinguish R-peak from S-peak (both produce zero-crossings).
- Fails in low-amplitude QRS or PVCs.
- Requires post-processing (refractory period) to reject T-waves.

---

#### Part (b): A 200 Hz sampled ECG yields R-peaks at samples 200, 380, 560, 745, 925. Compute (i) the instantaneous heart rate at each beat, (ii) the mean heart rate, and (iii) classify the rhythm. [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert sample indices to time (T = 1/200 = 0.005 s):**

$$
t_1 = 200 \times 0.005 = 1.00\ \text{s}
$$

$$
t_2 = 380 \times 0.005 = 1.90\ \text{s}
$$

$$
t_3 = 560 \times 0.005 = 2.80\ \text{s}
$$

$$
t_4 = 745 \times 0.005 = 3.725\ \text{s}
$$

$$
t_5 = 925 \times 0.005 = 4.625\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — R-R intervals:**

$$
RR_1 = 0.90\ \text{s},\ RR_2 = 0.90\ \text{s},\ RR_3 = 0.925\ \text{s},\ RR_4 = 0.90\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous heart rate:** $HR_i = \tfrac{60}{RR_i}$

$$
HR_1 = \frac{60}{0.90} = 66.67\ \text{bpm}
$$

$$
HR_2 = \frac{60}{0.90} = 66.67\ \text{bpm}
$$

$$
HR_3 = \frac{60}{0.925} = 64.86\ \text{bpm}
$$

$$
HR_4 = \frac{60}{0.90} = 66.67\ \text{bpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Mean heart rate:**

$$
\overline{HR} = \frac{66.67 + 66.67 + 64.86 + 66.67}{4} = 66.22\ \text{bpm}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**
The mean HR of **~66.2 bpm** lies within the **normal sinus rhythm range (60–100 bpm)**. The inter-beat variability is small (SDNN < 0.05 s), indicating a **regular normal sinus rhythm**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in QRS Detection Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing Pan-Tompkins with simple thresholding (–2 marks):** Many students write "R-peak is detected when amplitude exceeds a threshold" — this is **NOT** Pan-Tompkins. Always mention the **six-stage filter chain**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in HR calculation (–1 mark):** $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{interval}}$ is valid **only** when RR is in **seconds**. If the RR is in samples, divide by $f_s$ first: $RR_{sec} = RR_{samples} / f_s$.
>
> 3. **Wrong refractory value (–1 mark):** The standard Pan-Tompkins refractory period is **200 ms**, not 360 ms. The 360 ms window is for *learning-level updates*, not the refractory gate.
>
> 4. **Omitting squaring stage (–1 mark):** The squaring block is essential — without it, the moving integrator cannot separate QRS from P/T waves.
>
> 5. **Stating Se and +P without the database (–1 mark):** Always cite: "On the **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** (48 records, ~116,000 beats): Se = 99.75%, +P = 99.54%."
>
> 6. **Confusing detection with classification (–2 marks):** QRS detection identifies the *time of occurrence* of the R-peak. Classification (normal vs. PVC vs. AFib) is a *separate downstream step*.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning search-back (–1 mark):** Always mention the 1.66 × RR search-back rule as part of the Pan-Tompkins algorithm — it is a key differentiator from simpler methods.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Points for KTU Module 4 — QRS Detection**
>
> - **QRS complex** = ventricular depolarization; duration **80–120 ms**; spectral peak at **10 Hz** (band 5–15 Hz).
> - **Pan-Tompkins** is a **6-stage algorithm**: BPF → Derivative → Square → MWI → Adaptive Threshold → Refractory.
> - **Bandpass filter**: $H_{LP}(z) = \tfrac{(1-z^{-6})^{2}}{(1-z^{-1})^{2}}$; $H_{HP}(z) = \tfrac{z^{-16} - \tfrac{1}{32}(1+z^{-1})^{16}}{1-z^{-1}}$.
> - **Derivative operator**: 5-point differentiator; $H_D(z) = \tfrac{1}{8T}(-z^{-2}-2z^{-1}+2z^{1}+z^{2})$.
> - **Squaring**: $y[n] = (x[n])^{2}$ — all-positive output; doubles signal frequency.
> - **Moving Window Integrator**: $N = 30$ at $f_s = 200$ Hz; equals 150 ms.
> - **Adaptive Thresholds**: $THR_{sig} = N_{lvl} + 0.25(S_{lvl} - N_{lvl})$; $THR_{noise} = N_{lvl} + 0.50(S_{lvl} - N_{lvl})$.
> - **Forgetting factor**: 0.875 for both signal and noise level updates.
> - **Refractory period**: 200 ms — rejects T-waves and prevents double-counting.
> - **Search-back window**: 1.66 × current R-R interval.
> - **Performance on MIT-BIH**: Se = **99.75%**, +P = **99.54%**, DER = **0.71%**.
> - **Heart rate formula**: $HR = \tfrac{60}{RR_{interval}\ (s)}$ bpm.
> - **HRV metrics**: SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50 — all derived from successive R-R intervals.
> - **Alternative methods**: First-derivative (zero-crossing), template matching (matched filter), wavelet transform (modulus maxima), Hilbert transform (envelope), and deep learning (CNN/LSTM).
> - **Clinical gold standard database**: **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** — 48 records, 30-minute duration, 360 Hz sampling.
> - **Engineered applications**: Holter monitors, ICU monitors, AEDs, ICDs, wearable ECG patches.
> - **ANSI/AAMI EC38 standard** mandates Se > 99% and +P > 99% for clinically acceptable QRS detectors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Heart Rate Variability Using Periodogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Heart Rate Variability Using Periodogram # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Heart Rate Variability (HRV) — Formal Definition **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** is the physiological phenomenon of variation in the time interval between consecutive heartbeats. More precisely, it is the variation in the **R-R intervals** (the time between successive R-peaks of the QRS complex in an Electrocardiogram (ECG) signal).
