HRV is a non-invasive quantitative marker of the **Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)** activity, reflecting the dynamic interplay between the sympathetic (accelerator) and parasympathetic (brake) nervous systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBBRT504 Module 4):** > HRV refers to the beat-to-beat alterations in heart rate. A healthy heart is not a metronome — the intervals between beats are constantly modulated by respiratory sinus arrhythmia, baroreflex, thermoregulation, and circadian rhythms. Reduced HRV indicates pathology; elevated HRV (within physiological limits) indicates robust autonomic adaptability. ## 1.2 The Periodogram — Formal Definition The **Periodogram** is a non-parametric (classical) method of estimating the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of a deterministic signal or a finite-length sample of a stationary random process. It was introduced by **Arthur Schuster in 1898** for detecting hidden periodicities in time series data. Mathematically, the periodogram of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ of length $N$ is defined as the squared magnitude of its Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT), normalized by the number of samples: $$I_N(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$ For computational efficiency, the DTFT is replaced by the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** computed via the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm, yielding the same estimator at discrete frequencies $f_k = k/N$. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Heart as a Drummer in a Jazz Band:** > Imagine the heart is a drummer in a jazz band. A *metronomic* (rigidly regular) drummer represents a diseased or stressed heart. A *jazz* drummer constantly varies tempo, dynamics, and rhythm in response to the music — this is **HRV**. Now imagine recording the drummer's tempo with a microphone and running it through a **spectrum analyzer** (the periodogram). The analyzer decomposes the variability into frequency components: > - **Very slow oscillations** (VLF band) → long-term trends, thermoregulation, hormonal cycles. > - **Medium oscillations** (LF band) → blood pressure regulation, sympathetic activity. > - **Fast oscillations** (HF band) → breathing, parasympathetic (vagal) activity. > > The **periodogram** is precisely this spectrum analyzer for the heart's rhythm. ## 1.4 Standard HRV Metrics (Bold Constants & Bands) | Metric | Frequency Range | Physiological Correlate | Standard Duration | |---|---|---|---| | **VLF (Very Low Frequency)** | **0.003 – 0.04 Hz** | Thermoregulation, hormonal, slow autonomic | 24 h | | **LF (Low Frequency)** | **0.04 – 0.15 Hz** | Sympathetic + Parasympathetic (baroreflex) | 24 h / 5 min | | **HF (High Frequency)** | **0.15 – 0.40 Hz** | Parasympathetic (RSA, respiration) | 24 h / 5 min | | **LF/HF Ratio** | dimensionless | Sympathovagal Balance | 24 h / 5 min | | **Total Power (TP)** | $\leq$ **0.40 Hz** | Overall autonomic modulation | 24 h | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference:** The frequency band boundaries above are adopted from the **Task Force of the European Society of Cardiology and the North American Society of Pacing and Electrophysiology (1996)** — universally accepted in clinical HRV analysis and expected in KTU 2024 Scheme answers. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Spectral Analysis for HRV? Time-domain metrics (SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50) provide a single global index of variability but **conceal the underlying physiological mechanisms**. Different oscillatory components at distinct frequencies are governed by distinct branches of the ANS: 1. **High-Frequency (HF, 0.15–0.40 Hz):** Coupled to the respiratory cycle (~9–24 breaths/min). Known as **Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA)**. Mediated almost exclusively by the **vagus nerve (parasympathetic)**. Disappears with vagal blockade (atropine). 2. **Low-Frequency (LF, 0.04–0.15 Hz):** Associated with the **baroreflex loop** (~6 sec cycle). Jointly mediated by sympathetic and parasympathetic systems. Reflects blood pressure regulation. 3. **Very Low Frequency (VLF, 0.003–0.04 Hz):** Long-term regulators — thermoregulation, renin-angiotensin system, circadian rhythms. The **Periodogram** quantifies the power in each band, allowing clinical separation of autonomic contributions. ## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Periodogram-based HRV Analysis ### Pillar 1: Signal Acquisition and Pre-processing - **ECG Sampling:** typically $f_s = 500$ Hz or $1000$ Hz. - **R-peak Detection:** Pan-Tompkins algorithm, Hilbert transform, or wavelet-based detection. - **R-R Tachogram Construction:** Successive R-R intervals plotted as a function of beat number (or time) → discrete-time series $d[k]$, unevenly sampled. - **Resampling (Interpolation):** Tachogram is **cubically spline-interpolated** and **uniformly resampled** (typically at $f_s = 4$ Hz) because the FFT requires uniform sampling. Common methods: linear, cubic spline, Lomb-Scargle (avoids resampling). ### Pillar 2: Periodogram Computation The squared magnitude of the normalized DFT is the **raw (direct) periodogram**: $$P_{xx}(f_k) = \frac{1}{N} \left| X(f_k) \right|^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f_k n} \right|^2, \quad f_k = \frac{k}{N}$$ where $X(f_k) = \text{FFT}\{x[n]\}$ and $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$. The **direct periodogram is biased and inconsistent**: as $N \to \infty$, the variance of the estimator does **not** vanish — it remains constant. To overcome this, two variance-reduction techniques are standard in HRV analysis. ### Pillar 3: Variance Reduction — Welch's Method (Industry Standard) **Welch's Method** (1967) is the de-facto standard for HRV spectral estimation. It addresses the inconsistency of the raw periodogram through three operations: 1. **Segmentation:** Divide $x[n]$ into $L$ overlapping segments $x_i[n]$, each of length $M$ with **overlap** of $50\%$ (typical) or $75\%$. 2. **Windowing:** Apply a smooth tapering window $w[n]$ (typically **Hann** or **Hamming**) to each segment to reduce spectral leakage. 3. **Averaging:** Compute the periodogram of each windowed segment, then average: $$\hat{P}_{Welch}(f) = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} \frac{1}{M U} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w[n]\, x_i[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$ where $U = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w^2[n]$ is the **window energy normalization factor**. > [!NOTE] > **Why Welch works:** The variance of the averaged estimator is reduced by a factor of $L$ (number of segments) compared to the raw periodogram. The trade-off is a slight loss of frequency resolution (mainlobe widens by the window's bandwidth) and a small bias. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I_N(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert X(f) \right\vert^2$ | Raw (direct) Periodogram | Power / Hz | | 2 | $\hat{P}_{Welch}(f) = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} P_i(f)$ | Welch's averaged periodogram | Power / Hz | | 3 | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ | DFT (FFT) of segment | — | | 4 | $P_{VLF} = \int_{0.003}^{0.04} \hat{P}(f)\, df$ | VLF band power | ms² | | 5 | $P_{LF} = \int_{0.04}^{0.15} \hat{P}(f)\, df$ | LF band power | ms² | | 6 | $P_{HF} = \int_{0.15}^{0.40} \hat{P}(f)\, df$ | HF band power | ms² | | 7 | $P_{TP} = \int_{0.003}^{0.40} \hat{P}(f)\, df$ | Total Power | ms² | | 8 | $R_{LF/HF} = \frac{P_{LF}}{P_{HF}}$ | Sympathovagal Ratio | dimensionless | | 9 | $L \approx \frac{N - M}{M(1 - \text{overlap})} + 1$ | Number of segments | — | | 10 | $f_{res} = \frac{f_s}{M}$ | Frequency resolution | Hz | | 11 | $U = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w^2[n]$ | Window energy | — | | 12 | $\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N}$ | Frequency bin spacing | Hz | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units in clinical practice:** When the tachogram is expressed in **milliseconds (ms)**, the spectral power is reported in **ms²/Hz** and integrated band power in **ms²**. When expressed in **seconds (s)**, units are **s²/Hz** and **s²**. KTU answers must explicitly state units to earn full marks. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cardiology:** Risk stratification after myocardial infarction (low HRV = high mortality). - **Diabetology:** Early detection of diabetic autonomic neuropathy. - **Stress & Fatigue Monitoring:** Wearable HRV biosensors (Apple Watch, Fitbit, Garmin) use Welch's periodogram in real-time. - **Sports Physiology:** Tracking overtraining and recovery via LF/HF trends. - **Neonatal / Fetal Monitoring:** HRV of fetal ECG predicts acidosis and preterm outcomes. - **Neurodegenerative Disease:** Parkinson's, Alzheimer's autonomic dysregulation. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Periodogram from the Autocorrelation Function The periodogram is the **Fourier transform of the biased sample autocorrelation sequence**. We derive this rigorously because it is a **frequently asked 7-mark derivation in KTU Module 4**. **Step 1 — Define the biased autocorrelation estimator:** For a finite-length signal $x[n]$, $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, the biased sample autocorrelation is: $$\hat{r}_{xx}[m] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-|m|-1} x[n]\, x[n+|m|], \quad m = -(N-1), \ldots, (N-1)$$ **Step 2 — Compute the DTFT of the autocorrelation:** By the **Wiener-Khinchin theorem**, the true PSD and the true autocorrelation are Fourier pairs: $$P_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}[m]\, e^{-j2\pi f m}$$ Replacing the true $r_{xx}[m]$ with the estimate $\hat{r}_{xx}[m]$ over the finite lag range gives the **periodogram estimator**: $$\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}[m]\, e^{-j2\pi f m}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the autocorrelation and exchange summation order:** $$\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left[ \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-|m|-1} x[n]\, x[n+|m|] \right] e^{-j2\pi f m}$$ **Step 4 — Re-index with $k = n + |m|$:** When $m \geq 0$, let $k = n + m$, so $n = k - m$, with $k$ ranging from $m$ to $N-1$. When $m < 0$, let $|m| = -m$ and similarly. After re-indexing and exploiting the symmetry of the autocorrelation, both half-axes combine into a single sum: $$\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$ **Step 5 — Conclude:** $$\boxed{\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(f) \right\vert^2 = I_N(f)}$$ This is the **periodogram**, now shown to be the DTFT of the biased autocorrelation estimate. ## 3.2 Derivation of Welch's Variance Reduction **Step 1 — Variance of a single raw periodogram:** For a real Gaussian zero-mean process with true PSD $P(f)$, the periodogram at frequency $f_k$ (excluding DC and Nyquist) follows an **exponential distribution** with mean $P(f_k)$ and variance $P^2(f_k)$. Hence the **coefficient of variation is 1** — the estimate is *inconsistent* (variance does not decrease with $N$). **Step 2 — Welch's segmentation:** Divide $x[n]$ of length $N$ into $L$ segments of length $M$ each, with overlap $O \in [0,1)$: $$L = \left\lfloor \frac{N - O\cdot M}{M - O\cdot M} \right\rfloor$$ **Step 3 — Window each segment:** The $i$-th windowed segment is $x_i[n] = x[n + iD]\, w[n]$, where $D = M(1-O)$ is the hop size. **Step 4 — Compute periodogram of each segment:** $$P_i(f) = \frac{1}{M U} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w[n]\, x_i[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$ **Step 5 — Average across segments:** $$\hat{P}_{Welch}(f) = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} P_i(f)$$ **Step 6 — Variance of Welch's estimator:** If segments are independent (no overlap), $\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{Welch}\} = \text{Var}\{P_i\}/L = P^2(f)/L$. Overlap introduces correlation, slightly degrading the variance reduction. **Step 7 — Frequency resolution:** Because each segment has length $M$, the frequency resolution becomes: $$\Delta f_{Welch} = \frac{f_s}{M}$$ > This is *coarser* than the raw periodogram's resolution $f_s/N$ by a factor of $N/M$. Welch trades resolution for variance reduction. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Band Power Computation > [!NOTE] > **Problem (typical KTU numerical):** A 5-minute ECG is sampled at $f_s = 4$ Hz after R-peak detection and resampling. The Welch periodogram of the resulting tachogram yields 1024 frequency bins. Compute the VLF, LF, HF, and total power (in ms²) and the LF/HF ratio, given the following summed spectral values in each band (assume rectangular window normalization): VLF sum = 800 units, LF sum = 1200 units, HF sum = 1000 units, Total sum = 3000 units. Each unit corresponds to 0.001 ms². **Step 1 — Convert each band sum to power:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{VLF} &= 800 \times 0.001 = 0.800 \text{ ms}^2 \\ P_{LF} &= 1200 \times 0.001 = 1.200 \text{ ms}^2 \\ P_{HF} &= 1000 \times 0.001 = 1.000 \text{ ms}^2 \\ P_{TP} &= 3000 \times 0.001 = 3.000 \text{ ms}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the LF/HF ratio:** $$R_{LF/HF} = \frac{P_{LF}}{P_{HF}} = \frac{1.200}{1.000} = 1.20$$ **Step 3 — Interpret clinically (for viva):** An LF/HF ratio of 1.20 is within the normal adult resting range (**0.5 – 2.0**), suggesting balanced sympathovagal tone. **Step 4 — Express as normalized units (nu):** Often required in KTU problems: $$LF_{nu} = \frac{P_{LF}}{P_{TP} - P_{VLF}} \times 100 = \frac{1.200}{3.000 - 0.800} \times 100 = \frac{1.200}{2.200} \times 100 = 54.5\%$$ $$HF_{nu} = \frac{P_{HF}}{P_{TP} - P_{VLF}} \times 100 = \frac{1.000}{2.200} \times 100 = 45.5\%$$ ## 3.4 The Bias-Variance Trade-off in HRV Periodogram — Analytical Matrix | **Method** | **Resolution** | **Variance** | **Bias** | **HRV Use Case** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Raw Periodogram | Highest $f_s/N$ | Highest (constant) | Lowest | Long, clean recordings | | Bartlett (non-overlapping) | $f_s/M$ | Reduced by $L$ | Moderate | Quick estimates | | **Welch (50% overlap, Hann)** | $f_s/M$ | Reduced by $\approx L$ | Moderate | **Standard for HRV** | | Welch (75% overlap, Hamming) | $f_s/M$ | Further reduced | Slightly higher | Short recordings | | Blackman-Tukey (smoothed correlogram) | Variable | Tunable | Tunable | Parametric flexibility | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End HRV Analysis Pipeline (Block Diagram) The complete periodogram-based HRV analysis chain is rendered below as a Mermaid block-level architecture flow. Each stage maps to a discrete processing step in MATLAB, Python (MNE/scipy-signals), or LabVIEW. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ECG Signal Acquisition] --> B[Bandpass Filtering 0.5 to 40 Hz] B --> C[R peak Detection
Pan Tompkins or Hilbert] C --> D[RR Interval Extraction
dT k = R k+1 minus R k] D --> E[Artifact Correction
PhysioNet Rules and Median Filter] E --> F[RR Tachogram Construction
Unevenly Sampled] F --> G[Interpolation
Cubic Spline at fs equals 4 Hz] G --> H[Detrending
Remove Mean and Linear Trend] H --> I[Welch Periodogram
Hann Window 256 s 50 percent Overlap] I --> J[PSD Estimate P hat f] J --> K[Band Integration
VLF LF HF] K --> L[Compute LF over HF Ratio] L --> M[Clinical Decision Support
Normal vs Pathological HRV] style A fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#000 style C fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000 style I fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000 style L fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000 style M fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Frequency Band Decomposition Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FREQ[Power Spectral Density of RR Tachogram] VLF[Band 1 VLF
0.003 to 0.04 Hz
Thermoregulation and Hormonal] LF[Band 2 LF
0.04 to 0.15 Hz
Baroreflex and Sympathetic plus Vagal] HF[Band 3 HF
0.15 to 0.40 Hz
Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia Vagal] end VLF --> P1[Integral P VLF] LF --> P2[Integral P LF] HF --> P3[Integral P HF] P1 --> SUM[Total Power] P2 --> SUM P3 --> SUM P2 --> RATIO[LF over HF Ratio] P3 --> RATIO style VLF fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,color:#000 style LF fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,color:#000 style HF fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,color:#000 style RATIO fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Periodogram Computation Sequence (Data-Flow View) ```mermaid flowchart TD X[Input Tachogram x n length N] --> WIN[Apply Tapering Window w n
Hann or Hamming] WIN --> SEG[Segment with Overlap
50 percent typical] SEG --> FFT[Compute FFT per Segment] FFT --> MAG[Square Magnitude divided by MU] MAG --> AVG[Average across L Segments] AVG --> OUT[Welch PSD Estimate P hat f] OUT --> INT[Numerical Integration over Bands] INT --> BP[Band Powers and LF over HF] style X fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#000 style FFT fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000 style AVG fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000 style OUT fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000 style BP fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,color:#000 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **Define Heart Rate Variability (HRV). List the standard frequency bands used in its spectral analysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Heart Rate Variability (HRV) is the variation in the time intervals between successive heartbeats, quantified as the beat-to-beat fluctuations of the R-R intervals extracted from an ECG signal. It is a non-invasive marker of autonomic nervous system (ANS) activity, specifically the balance between sympathetic and parasympathetic (vagal) modulation. **Frequency Bands (1.5 marks):** The three standard frequency bands defined by the Task Force (1996) are: | Band | Range | Physiological Origin | |---|---|---| | VLF | 0.003 – 0.04 Hz | Thermoregulation, hormonal | | LF | 0.04 – 0.15 Hz | Baroreflex, mixed autonomic | | HF | 0.15 – 0.40 Hz | Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (vagal) | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **What is a periodogram? State its mathematical expression.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A periodogram is a **non-parametric estimator of the Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of a discrete-time signal, defined as the squared magnitude of the signal's Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), normalized by the signal length. **Mathematical Expression (2 marks):** $$I_N(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$ For practical computation using the FFT at frequency bins $f_k = k \cdot f_s / N$: $$I_N(f_k) = \frac{1}{N}\left| X[k] \right|^2, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$ The periodogram is the **Fourier transform of the biased sample autocorrelation sequence** and is asymptotically unbiased but **not consistent** (its variance does not vanish as $N \to \infty$) — necessitating Welch's averaging method in practice. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, subdivided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer **one** of two choices. ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Suggested]** **Q. (a)** Derive the periodogram as the Fourier transform of the biased autocorrelation estimate. Show explicitly that the periodogram is asymptotically unbiased but not consistent. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure for the analysis of Heart Rate Variability (HRV) using Welch's periodogram method. List the standard frequency bands and their physiological significance. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(a):** **Derivation (5 marks):** The biased sample autocorrelation of a length-$N$ sequence $x[n]$ is: $$\hat{r}_{xx}[m] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-|m|-1} x[n]\, x[n+m], \quad m = -(N-1), \ldots, N-1$$ By the Wiener-Khinchin theorem, the true PSD is the DTFT of the true autocorrelation. Substituting the estimate: $$\hat{P}(f) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}[m]\, e^{-j2\pi f m}$$ Substituting the autocorrelation and interchanging summations (valid for finite sums): $$\hat{P}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-|m|-1} x[n]\, x[n+m]\, e^{-j2\pi f m}$$ Re-index with $k = n + m$ (for $m \geq 0$ and $m < 0$ separately) and combine the two half-planes of $m$: $$\hat{P}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2 = \frac{1}{N}\left| X(f) \right|^2$$ **Asymptotic bias and consistency (2 marks):** - **Bias:** $E\{\hat{P}(f)\} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_{true}(\nu)\, W_B(f-\nu)\, d\nu$ where $W_B$ is the Fejér kernel. As $N \to \infty$, the mainlobe of $W_B$ narrows $\Rightarrow$ the convolution converges to $P_{true}(f)$. So the periodogram is **asymptotically unbiased**. - **Consistency:** The variance $\text{Var}\{\hat{P}(f)\} \to P_{true}^2(f) \neq 0$ as $N \to \infty$. Hence it is **not consistent** — averaging (Welch, Bartlett) is mandatory. --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(b):** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the pipeline shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes (ECG → Filter → R-peak → RR → Interpolate → Welch → Band integration → LF/HF → Clinical interpretation). **Step-by-step procedure (2 marks):** 1. Acquire ECG at $f_s \geq 500$ Hz; bandpass filter 0.5–40 Hz. 2. Detect R-peaks; compute successive R-R intervals → unevenly sampled tachogram. 3. Remove ectopic/missed beats; detrend; cubic-spline interpolate to uniform 4 Hz. 4. Segment into overlapping 256-sample windows (Hann, 50% overlap); FFT each. 5. Compute periodogram per segment, normalize, and average → Welch PSD. 6. Integrate PSD over VLF (0.003–0.04), LF (0.04–0.15), HF (0.15–0.40) Hz. 7. Compute LF/HF ratio; compare to normative ranges. **Physiological significance (2 marks):** - **VLF:** Thermoregulation, renin-angiotensin, circadian rhythm. - **LF:** Baroreflex-mediated; reflects both sympathetic and parasympathetic tone. - **HF:** Respiratory sinus arrhythmia; pure vagal (parasympathetic) marker. - **LF/HF ratio:** Sympathovagal balance (high → sympathetic dominance; low → vagal dominance). --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Q. (a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the periodogram method of PSD estimation. Explain its limitations and how Welch's method overcomes them. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Compute the VLF, LF, HF, and total spectral power and the LF/HF ratio for the given Welch periodogram (problem-data as in Section 3.3 above). Comment on the clinical interpretation. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(a):** **Periodogram Flowchart (3 marks):** Input signal $x[n]$ → Compute $X[k] = \text{FFT}\{x[n]\}$ → Compute magnitude squared $\vert X[k]\vert^2$ → Divide by $N$ → Plot vs. frequency $f_k = k f_s / N$ → Output: PSD estimate $\hat{P}(f)$. **Mathematical expression (1.5 marks):** $$\hat{P}(f_k) = \frac{1}{N}\left| X[k] \right|^2$$ **Limitations (1.5 marks):** 1. **High variance (inconsistency):** Var $\{\hat{P}\} \to P^2$ as $N \to \infty$ — does not vanish. 2. **Spectral leakage:** Finite windowing causes sidelobes; periodogram assumes rectangular window implicit truncation → high leakage. 3. **Resolution vs. variance trade-off:** Cannot improve both simultaneously with a single estimator. **How Welch's method overcomes them (1 mark):** Welch segments the signal, applies a smooth tapering window to reduce leakage, computes a periodogram per segment, and **averages** them — reducing variance by a factor of $L$ at the cost of coarser frequency resolution $f_s/M$. --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(b):** Using the numerical values from Section 3.3: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{VLF} &= 0.800 \text{ ms}^2 \\ P_{LF} &= 1.200 \text{ ms}^2 \\ P_{HF} &= 1.000 \text{ ms}^2 \\ P_{TP} &= 3.000 \text{ ms}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **LF/HF ratio:** $$R_{LF/HF} = \frac{1.200}{1.000} = 1.20$$ **Normalized units (bonus, 1 mark):** $$LF_{nu} = \frac{1.200}{2.200} \times 100 = 54.5\%, \quad HF_{nu} = \frac{1.000}{2.200} \times 100 = 45.5\%$$ **Clinical interpretation:** An LF/HF ratio of **1.20** falls within the typical adult resting range of **0.5 – 2.0**, indicating **balanced sympathovagal tone**. The dominant LF component suggests mild sympathetic predominance consistent with awake, restful, but alert state. No autonomic dysfunction is indicated. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting normalization:** The $1/N$ factor in $I_N(f)$ must appear explicitly; omitting it loses 1 mark. > 2. **Mixing units:** HRV band powers in **ms²** vs. **s²** vs. **normalized units (nu)** must be clearly labeled. > 3. **Confusing overlap with hop size:** $50\%$ overlap means $D = M/2$, not $D = M$. > 4. **Wrong band limits:** Using 0.04–0.4 instead of 0.04–0.15 for LF (or 0.15–0.4 for HF) is a **fatal** error — always cite the **Task Force 1996** limits. > 5. **Skipping the resampling step:** Periodogram requires uniform sampling; tachogram must be interpolated first. > 6. **Calling periodogram "consistent":** It is *unbiased* but NOT *consistent* — examiners trap students here every year. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Periodogram-based HRV Analysis (KTU 2024 Module 4):** - ✅ **HRV** = variation in successive R-R intervals; marker of **autonomic nervous system** balance. - ✅ **R-R tachogram** is the input to spectral analysis; must be **uniformly resampled** (typically 4 Hz) before FFT. - ✅ **Periodogram** = $(1/N)\vert X(f) \vert^2$ = DTFT of biased autocorrelation. - ✅ **Direct periodogram is asymptotically unbiased but NOT consistent** (variance does not decay with $N$). - ✅ **Welch's method** = segment + window (Hann/Hamming) + average → variance $\downarrow$ by factor $L$. - ✅ **Frequency bands (Task Force 1996):** VLF 0.003–0.04, LF 0.04–0.15, HF 0.15–0.40 Hz. - ✅ **HF = vagal (parasympathetic) marker; LF = mixed; LF/HF = sympathovagal balance.** - ✅ **Units:** ms²/Hz for PSD, ms² for integrated band power, dimensionless for LF/HF. - ✅ **Resolution** of Welch = $f_s / M$ (segment length, not total length). - ✅ **Variance reduction** = factor of $L$ (number of segments); 50% overlap is standard. - ✅ **Total Power** $P_{TP} = P_{VLF} + P_{LF} + P_{HF}$ (integrated over 0.003–0.40 Hz). - ✅ **Normalized Units (nu):** $LF_{nu} = P_{LF}/(P_{TP}-P_{VLF}) \times 100$. - ✅ **Clinical relevance:** Risk stratification post-MI, diabetic autonomic neuropathy, stress/fatigue, fetal monitoring, sports physiology. - ✅ **Common KTU traps:** Confusing *unbiased* with *consistent*; mixing units; wrong band limits; missing $1/N$ normalization.

topics.title: Adaptive Filters - Principle of Wiener Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Filters - Principle of Wiener Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wiener Filter (Formal Definition):**
> The **Wiener filter** is the optimal *linear*, *time-invariant* (LTI) filter that minimizes the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** between the filter's output and a desired (reference) signal, given the second-order statistics (autocorrelation and cross-correlation) of the involved stochastic processes. It is the *optimum linear estimator* in the **L² (least-squares)** sense.

In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing (Module 4, KTU 2024)**, the Wiener filter is the *theoretical benchmark* against which all practical adaptive filters (LMS, RLS, NLMS) are compared, because:

1. It produces the **Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE)** achievable by any linear filter.
2. It is obtained by solving the **Wiener–Hopf equation**: $\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$.
3. It requires *a priori* knowledge of $\mathbf{R}$ (input autocorrelation) and $\mathbf{p}$ (cross-correlation) — which is *rarely* available in real biosignals → hence the need for *adaptive* filters that *learn* $\mathbf{w}_o$ on-the-fly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Filter (Companion Definition):**
> An *adaptive filter* is a self-adjusting digital filter whose coefficients $\mathbf{w}(n)$ are updated recursively using an *error signal* $e(n)$, such that a cost function (usually MSE) is progressively minimized. It is a **practical real-time implementation of the Wiener filter** for *non-stationary, statistics-unknown* signals like ECG, EEG, and EMG.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Noisy Hospital Room"

Imagine you are a doctor trying to listen to a patient's faint heartbeat with a stethoscope, but the air-conditioner, ventilator, and family chatter are drowning it out.

| Element in the Analogy | Biomedical Signal Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Faint heartbeat (signal of interest) | Clean ECG / EEG waveform |
| Air-conditioner hum (noise) | 50 Hz powerline interference / baseline wander |
| Your brain *intuitively* filtering the noise | **Wiener filter** (mathematically optimal) |
| Your brain *learning* the noise pattern over time | **Adaptive filter** (e.g., LMS) |

> **The Key Insight:** A *Wiener filter* is the *mathematical proof* of what the ideal listener *would* do if they knew every statistical detail of both the signal and the noise. An *adaptive filter* is a *learning machine* that *approaches* this ideal by trial-and-error using only a reference noise source.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Adaptive"?**
> Because real biomedical signals are **non-stationary** (their statistics change with time, e.g., heart rate variability, sleep-stage transitions in EEG), the optimum filter $\mathbf{w}_o$ is itself *time-varying*. An adaptive filter continuously *tracks* this changing optimum — a fixed Wiener filter cannot.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Sampling frequency ($f_s$):** Typically $\mathbf{250}$–$\mathbf{1000}$ Hz for ECG; up to $\mathbf{10}$ kHz for EMG.
- **Powerline interference frequency:** $\mathbf{50}$ Hz (India/Europe) or $\mathbf{60}$ Hz (USA).
- **Filter order ($M$):** Number of taps; typical range $\mathbf{8}$–$\mathbf{64}$ for biosignals.
- **Step-size ($\mu$):** LMS convergence parameter; bounded by $\mathbf{0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{max}}}$ where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$.
- **Excess MSE ($\zeta$):** Steady-state error above the Wiener minimum; a direct quality measure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Wiener Filter Problem Statement

Consider a discrete-time FIR filter of order $M$ with input $x(n)$ and output $y(n)$:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k \, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$$

where $\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} w_0 & w_1 & \cdots & w_{M-1} \end{bmatrix}^{T}$ is the *tap-weight vector* and $\mathbf{x}(n) = \begin{bmatrix} x(n) & x(n-1) & \cdots & x(n-M+1) \end{bmatrix}^{T}$ is the *input vector*.

Let $d(n)$ be the *desired response*. Define the *error signal*:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$$

The **cost function** (instantaneous squared error) is $e^2(n)$, and the **Mean Square Error** is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}$$

Expanding using the orthogonality principle:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$$

where:
- $\sigma_d^2 = E\{d^2(n)\}$ is the variance (power) of the desired signal,
- $\mathbf{p} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n)\}$ is the **cross-correlation vector** (size $M \times 1$),
- $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\}$ is the **input autocorrelation matrix** (size $M \times M$, Toeplitz, positive-definite).

---

## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Minimization

1. **Formulate** the quadratic cost $J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$.
2. **Differentiate** w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ (gradient operator): $\nabla J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$.
3. **Set gradient to zero** (first-order optimality / stationarity condition).
4. **Solve** for the optimal weight vector $\mathbf{w}_o$ — this is the **Wiener–Hopf equation**.
5. **Verify** the *orthogonality principle*: $E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,e_o(n)\} = \mathbf{0}$ at the optimum.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Wiener Filter & Adaptive Filters**

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Key |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k\,x(n-k)$ | FIR filter output (linear combiner) |
| 2 | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | Error signal definition |
| 3 | $J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\} = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$ | MSE cost function (quadratic) |
| 4 | $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\}$ | Input autocorrelation matrix ($M \times M$) |
| 5 | $\mathbf{p} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n)\}$ | Cross-correlation vector ($M \times 1$) |
| 6 | $\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$ | **Wiener–Hopf equation** (canonical) |
| 7 | $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | **Wiener filter solution** (optimal weights) |
| 8 | $J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{w}_o = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | Minimum achievable MSE |
| 9 | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,[-\nabla J(n)]$ | **Steepest-descent update** |
| 10 | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | **LMS algorithm** (Widrow–Hoff) |
| 11 | $\nabla J(n) = -2\,E\{e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} \;\approx\; -2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | True gradient vs. LMS instantaneous estimate |
| 12 | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ (mean-square stable) ; $\;0<\mu<\dfrac{2}{\lambda_{max}}$ (LMS convergence in mean) | **Step-size bounds** |
| 13 | $\zeta(n) = J(\infty) - J_{min} = \dfrac{\mu}{2}\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})\,J_{min}$ | **Excess MSE** (steady-state) |
| 14 | $y_o(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_{o,k}\,x(n-k)$ | Optimal Wiener-filter output |
| 15 | $H_o(z) = \dfrac{P_{xd}(z)}{P_{xx}(z)}$ | **Wiener filter in Z-domain** (non-causal form) |

> **Notation Key:** $\lambda_{max}$ = largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$, $\text{tr}(\cdot)$ = matrix trace, $P_{xx}(z)$ = input power spectral density, $P_{xd}(z)$ = cross-power spectral density.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Wiener / Adaptive Filter |
|---|---|
| **Fetal ECG monitoring** | Cancels maternal ECG from abdominal recordings |
| **Powerline interference (50 Hz) removal** | Adaptive notch filter |
| **ECG baseline wander correction** | High-pass adaptive filter (breathing artefact) |
| **EEG artifact removal (EOG, EMG)** | Reference-noise adaptive cancellation |
| **Cochlear implants & hearing aids** | Adaptive noise suppression |
| **Evoked-potential averaging** | Wiener-type matched filter for SNR enhancement |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wiener–Hopf Equation from First Principles

We start with the *primary* cost function:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the square inside the expectation:**

$$
e^2(n) = d^2(n) - 2\,d(n)\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n) + [\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)]^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the expectation operator $E\{\cdot\}$ term by term:**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{d^2(n)\} - 2\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\,E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n)\} + E\{\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{w}\}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the standard statistical definitions:**

$$
\sigma_d^2 = E\{d^2(n)\}, \quad \mathbf{p} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n)\}, \quad \mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\}
$$

and use the scalar property $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{w}$ (valid for symmetric $\mathbf{R}$):

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w} \qquad \text{(canonical quadratic form)}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$:**

Using the matrix-calculus identities $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{p}$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ (for symmetric $\mathbf{R}$):

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 5 — Set the gradient to zero (stationary / minimum point):**

$$
-2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p} \qquad \text{(Wiener–Hopf Equation)}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\mathbf{w}_o$ (assuming $\mathbf{R}$ is non-singular):**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Minimum MSE substitution:**

Substitute $\mathbf{w}_o$ back into $J(\mathbf{w})$:

$$
J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}_o^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}_o^{T}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_o
$$

Using $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$, the last two terms combine as $-\mathbf{w}_o^{T}\mathbf{p}$:

$$
\boxed{\;J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}\;}
$$

> **Physical meaning:** $J_{min}$ is the *irreducible* error floor — the noise that *no* linear filter can remove. It quantifies the *quality limit* of optimal estimation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LMS Algorithm (Widrow–Hoff, 1960)

The LMS algorithm *replaces* the unknown true gradient $\nabla J = -2E\{e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\}$ by its *instantaneous unbiased estimate*:

$$
\hat{\nabla}J(n) = -2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) \quad \text{(single-sample estimate, no expectation)}
$$

**Step 1 — Steepest-descent recursion (general):**

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \tfrac{1}{2}\mu\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}J
$$

(where the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ cancels the $2$ from the gradient.)

**Step 2 — Substitute the LMS gradient estimate:**

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \tfrac{1}{2}\mu\,(-2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n))
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the constants:**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\;}
$$

> This is the **LMS update equation** — by far the most widely deployed adaptive algorithm due to its extreme simplicity (no matrix inversion, no correlation estimation, $O(M)$ computation per sample).

**Step 4 — Algorithm summary (in execution order, for KTU practical exam):**

| Step | Operation |
|---|---|
| 1 | Read $x(n)$ and $d(n)$ |
| 2 | Compute filter output $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ |
| 3 | Compute error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ |
| 4 | Update weights $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ |
| 5 | Set $n \leftarrow n+1$ and repeat |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stability Bound for Step-Size $\mu$

The mean-weight behavior $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}(n)\}$ follows:

$$
\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}(n+1)\} = (\mathbf{I} - 2\mu\mathbf{R})\,\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}(n)\} + 2\mu\,\mathbf{p}
$$

For convergence in the mean, every eigenvalue of $(\mathbf{I} - 2\mu\mathbf{R})$ must lie strictly inside the unit circle, i.e., $\vert 1 - 2\mu\lambda_i \vert < 1$ for all $i$. This gives:

$$
0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}
$$

For *mean-square convergence* (more stringent), a tighter condition is $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{max}}$ and additionally $\sum_{i}\dfrac{\mu\lambda_i}{2 - \mu\lambda_i} < 1$ — usually satisfied if $\mu \ll 1/\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 2-tap adaptive filter receives $x(n) = [x(n),\,x(n-1)]^{T}$. Given:
> $\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix},\;\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$
> Find the **Wiener solution** $\mathbf{w}_o$ and the **minimum MSE** $J_{min}$. Assume $\sigma_d^2 = 4$.

**Step 1 — Compute the inverse of $\mathbf{R}$:**

$$
\det(\mathbf{R}) = (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 3
$$

$$
\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$:**

$$
\mathbf{w}_o = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 - 0.5 \\ -1 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $J_{min}$:**

$$
J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{w}_o = 4 - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 4 - 0.5 = 3.5
$$

> **Interpretation:** The optimum filter has $w_0 = 0.5,\, w_1 = 0$ — only the *current* input sample matters; the past sample carries no additional information. The smallest achievable MSE is $3.5$.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Adaptive Filter (LMS) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inX["Input x of n"] --> delay1["Delay z^-1"]
    inX --> sumY["Multiplier w0 n"]
    delay1 --> delay2["Delay z^-1"]
    delay2 --> sumY2["Multiplier w1 n"]
    sumY --> adderY["Adder"]
    sumY2 --> adderY
    adderY --> outY["Output y of n"]
    inD["Desired d of n"] --> diffPos["Positive input"]
    outY --> diffNeg["Negative input"]
    diffPos --> adderE["Adder"]
    diffNeg --> adderE
    adderE --> errSig["Error e of n"]
    errSig --> gainMu["Gain mu"]
    errSig --> outE["e of n to next stage"]
    gainMu --> mulX1["Multiply with x of n"]
    inX --> mulX1
    mulX1 --> newW["Weight Update w of n plus 1"]
    newW --> sumY
    newW --> sumY2

    style newW fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The primary path (top) is the FIR filter producing $y(n)$; the lower loop subtracts $y(n)$ from the reference $d(n)$ to form the error $e(n)$, which is then *fed back* to adjust the tap weights $\mathbf{w}(n+1)$ via the LMS recursion.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Wiener Filter Convergence via MSE Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start w at 0 vector"] --> B["Read x of n and d of n"]
    B --> C["Compute y of n = wT x of n"]
    C --> D["Compute e of n = d of n minus y of n"]
    D --> E{"Is J w below threshold or n at N"}
    E -->|"No"| F["Update w of n plus 1 = w of n plus mu e of n x of n"]
    F --> B
    E -->|"Yes"| G["Converged w of n approximately equals w of optimum"]
    G --> H["Steady state J w of n = J min plus Excess MSE"]
    H --> I["Output clean estimated signal"]
    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style G fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style H fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style I fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual MSE Performance Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Weight w1"] --> B["MSE J as quadratic bowl"]
    A2["Weight w2"] --> B
    B --> C["Unique global minimum at w of optimum"]
    B --> D["Elliptical contours around minimum"]
    D --> E["Steepest descent path spirals to center"]
    C --> F["J min achieved"]
    style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style C fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style E fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style F fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2
```

> **Intuition for the surface:** The MSE $J(\mathbf{w})$ is a *convex paraboloid* in weight-space. Its *unique global minimum* is the Wiener point $\mathbf{w}_o$. The level-curves are concentric ellipses (because $\mathbf{R}$ is symmetric positive-definite). Steepest-descent/LMS moves *downhill* along the gradient — perpendicular to the contours — converging to $\mathbf{w}_o$.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Wiener Filter in a Biomedical Noise-Cancellation Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECGplusN["Primary Input s n plus n0 n = ECG plus noise"] --> subtractor["Subtractor"]
    refNoise["Reference Noise Channel n1 n correlated with n0 n"] --> adaptFilt["Adaptive Filter W z of n"]
    adaptFilt --> subtractor
    subtractor --> outClean["Clean ECG estimate s of n"]
    refNoise --> feedbackPath["Feedback to LMS update"]
    outClean --> errorSig["Error e of n equals s of n residual"]
    errorSig --> adaptFilt
```

> **Application (Widrow et al., 1975):** This is the canonical *ANC* (Adaptive Noise Canceller) topology — the gold standard for interference removal in biomedical signal processing (e.g., 50 Hz mains, maternal ECG in fetal monitoring).
