## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the principle of adaptive noise cancellation. List the three conditions required for the adaptive filter to function correctly.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Adaptive noise cancellation is a method of optimal filtering in which an adaptive filter is used to cancel noise from a primary signal by exploiting a reference signal that is correlated with the noise. The filter coefficients are adjusted iteratively to minimize the mean-square-error between the primary input and the filter output.
>
> **The three conditions are:**
> 1. The reference signal $x(n)$ must be **correlated** with the noise $n_0(n)$ present in the primary input.
> 2. The reference signal $x(n)$ must be **uncorrelated** with the desired signal $s(n)$: $E[s(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0$ for all $k$.
> 3. The noise path between reference and primary must be **stable or slowly varying** so that the adaptive filter can track it.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three conditions.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
*Write the LMS weight update equation. State the condition for convergence of the LMS algorithm.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The LMS weight update equation is:
> $$w_i(n+1) = w_i(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,x(n-i), \quad i = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$
> In vector form: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$.
>
> **Convergence condition:** The step-size must satisfy $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$, where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the *largest eigenvalue* of the reference-signal autocorrelation matrix $R = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T]$.
>
> Equivalently, a sufficient (but not necessary) condition is $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\text{tr}[R]}$.

*(Award 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the condition, 1 mark for the variable definitions.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Adaptive Filter Architecture**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of an adaptive noise canceller. Explain the function of the primary input, reference input, adaptive filter, and error signal. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 2-tap adaptive FIR filter uses the LMS algorithm with step-size $\mu = 0.05$. The current weight vector is $\mathbf{w}(n) = [0.4, \; 0.2]^T$ and the reference input vector is $\mathbf{x}(n) = [0.6, \; 0.3]^T$. The primary input at this instant is $d(n) = 1.0$. Compute the new weight vector $\mathbf{w}(n+1)$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Solution to Question A**

**(a) Block Diagram & Functional Explanation (7 marks):**

> The block diagram of an adaptive noise canceller consists of **two parallel input paths** and a **closed-loop weight update path**.
>
> **Functional description of each block:**
>
> - **Primary input $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$:** This is the contaminated signal at the output of the primary sensor. The goal is to recover $s(n)$ with the noise $n_0(n)$ removed.
> - **Reference input $x(n)$:** Acquired from a second sensor positioned to capture only the noise. It is correlated with $n_0(n)$ but uncorrelated with $s(n)$.
> - **Adaptive FIR filter:** A transversal filter with adjustable weights $\mathbf{w}(n)$ that processes $x(n)$ to produce an estimate $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ of the noise $n_0(n)$.
> - **Subtractor / Summing junction:** Computes the error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$. This serves as the *cleaned system output* (delivered to the user) and the *teaching signal* that drives the LMS update.
> - **LMS update block:** Implements $w_i(n+1) = w_i(n) + 2\mu e(n) x(n-i)$, closing the adaptive loop.
>
> *(Award 3 marks for the block diagram, 4 marks for the explanation.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the filter output $y(n)$:**

$$
y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = (0.4)(0.6) + (0.2)(0.3) = 0.24 + 0.06 = 0.30
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the error signal $e(n)$:**

$$
e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = 1.0 - 0.30 = 0.70
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the LMS update rule to weight $w_0$:**

$$
w_0(n+1) = w_0(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,x(n)
$$

$$
w_0(n+1) = 0.4 + 2(0.05)(0.70)(0.6) = 0.4 + 0.042 = 0.442
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the LMS update rule to weight $w_1$:**

$$
w_1(n+1) = w_1(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,x(n-1)
$$

$$
w_1(n+1) = 0.2 + 2(0.05)(0.70)(0.3) = 0.2 + 0.021 = 0.221
$$

**Step 5 — State the new weight vector:**

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.442 \\ 0.221 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Verification:* Both weights increased (positive correlation between $e(n)$ and $x$ taps) — consistent with LMS theory.

*(Award 2 marks each for $y(n)$, $e(n)$, and the final weights; 1 mark for the verification commentary.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — LMS Algorithm Derivation & Convergence**

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the LMS weight update equation starting from the performance function $J = E[e^2(n)]$. Clearly state the steepest-descent update and the LMS approximation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a 3-tap LMS adaptive filter, the autocorrelation matrix of the reference input is:
$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$
Determine the *upper bound* of the step-size $\mu$ for stable convergence. If the trace of $R$ is 6, compute the misadjustment at $\mu = 0.05$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Solution to Question B**

**(a) Derivation of LMS Update (7 marks):**

> **Step 1:** Define $J = E[e^2(n)]$ with $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
> **Step 2:** Expand to obtain $J = E[d^2] - 2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T R \mathbf{w}$ where $R = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T]$ and $\mathbf{p} = E[d\mathbf{x}]$.
> **Step 3:** Differentiate to get gradient $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2\mathbf{p} + 2R\mathbf{w}$. Setting this to zero gives the Wiener solution $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.
> **Step 4:** Apply steepest descent: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w}(n) - 2\mu\big[R\mathbf{w}(n) - \mathbf{p}\big]$.
> **Step 5 (LMS approximation):** Replace ensemble averages with instantaneous samples: $\hat{R}(n) = \mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)$, $\hat{\mathbf{p}}(n) = d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
> **Step 6:** Substitute to obtain the final LMS rule:
> $$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

*(Award 2 marks for the cost-function setup, 2 marks for the gradient, 2 marks for the steepest-descent step, 1 mark for the LMS approximation.)*

**(b) Convergence Bound and Misadjustment (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Find the Largest Eigenvalue of $R$:**

The matrix is tridiagonal and symmetric. The eigenvalues of a tridiagonal Toeplitz matrix with diagonal $a = 2$ and off-diagonal $b = 1$ (size $3 \times 3$) are given by:

$$
\lambda_k = a + 2b\cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{N+1}\right), \quad k = 1, 2, 3
$$

For $N = 3$, $a = 2$, $b = 1$:

$$
\lambda_1 = 2 + 2\cos(\pi/4) = 2 + \sqrt{2} \approx 3.414
$$

$$
\lambda_2 = 2 + 2\cos(2\pi/4) = 2 + 0 = 2
$$

$$
\lambda_3 = 2 + 2\cos(3\pi/4) = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586
$$

**Alternative (direct) verification:** The characteristic equation $\det(R - \lambda I) = 0$ yields the same three eigenvalues.

**Step 2 — Apply the Convergence Condition:**

$$
0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{1}{3.414} \approx 0.293
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Misadjustment:**

The misadjustment formula (small-$\mu$ approximation) is:

$$
M = \mu\,\text{tr}[R] = (0.05)(6) = 0.30 \;\; \text{(or 30%)}
$$

The exact formula:

$$
M = \frac{\mu\,\text{tr}[R]}{2 - \mu\,\text{tr}[R]} = \frac{0.30}{2 - 0.30} = \frac{0.30}{1.70} \approx 0.176 \;\; \text{(or 17.6%)}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\mu_{\max} \approx 0.293$ and $M \approx 17.6\%$ (exact) or $30\%$ (approximate).

*(Award 3 marks for the eigenvalue computation, 2 marks for the convergence bound, 2 marks for the misadjustment.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the independence condition:** Many students write the ANC block diagram but omit the critical assumption $E[s(n)x(n-k)] = 0$. This alone costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $e(n)$ with the noise:** Students often write $e(n) = n_0(n) - y(n)$ instead of $d(n) - y(n)$. The error is *defined* as primary-minus-filter-output, NOT noise-minus-filter-output.
> 3. **Sign error in LMS update:** The update is $w(n+1) = w(n) + 2\mu e(n)x(n)$. Writing $w(n+1) = w(n) - 2\mu e(n)x(n)$ loses 2 marks — it inverts convergence.
> 4. **Convergence condition — partial marks:** Writing only "$\mu$ should be small" without quantifying the bound $1/\lambda_{\max}$ forfeits the full 2 marks for this part.
> 5. **Misadjustment — approximation vs. exact:** The problem may ask for the *small-$\mu$* or the *exact* formula. Read the question carefully.
> 6. **Eigenvalue calculation:** For a $3 \times 3$ matrix, do *not* skip the computation. State $\det(R - \lambda I) = 0$ explicitly OR use the tridiagonal-Toeplitz closed form.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory 60-Second Revision — Read This Before the Exam!**

- **Definition:** ANC = adaptive filter + reference-noise subtraction; noise removed via *learning* rather than spectral separation.
- **Three prerequisites:** (1) $x(n)$ correlated with $n_0(n)$, (2) $x(n)$ uncorrelated with $s(n)$, (3) slowly-varying noise path.
- **Primary input:** $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$.
- **Reference input:** $x(n) \approx n_1(n)$.
- **Filter output:** $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} w_i(n) x(n-i)$.
- **Error / System output:** $e(n) = d(n) - y(n) \to s(n)$ as $y(n) \to n_0(n)$.
- **LMS update:** $w_i(n+1) = w_i(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,x(n-i)$.
- **Convergence bound:** $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$ (or conservatively $0 < \mu < 1/\text{tr}[R]$).
- **Wiener optimum (off-line):** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.
- **Performance function:** $J = E[e^2] = E[d^2] - 2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T R \mathbf{w}$ — quadratic and convex.
- **Misadjustment:** $M \approx \mu \cdot \text{tr}[R]$ (small-$\mu$).
- **Dual role of $e(n)$:** (1) Cleaned output for the user; (2) Teaching signal for the LMS engine.
- **Key biomedical applications:** Fetal ECG (vs. maternal), 50/60 Hz line interference, motion artifacts, EMG contamination.
- **LMS vs. Wiener:** LMS is *on-line*, gradient is *noisy*, computationally trivial. Wiener is *off-line*, gradient is *exact*, requires $R^{-1}$.
- **Speed vs. accuracy trade-off:** Small $\mu$ → slow but accurate; large $\mu$ → fast but noisy steady-state.
- **Exam-night mantra:** "The filter learns to mimic the noise path; the cleaner the reference, the cleaner the output."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cancellation of 50-Hz Interference in ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT504 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cancellation of 50-Hz Interference in ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **50-Hz Interference in ECG** refers to the **power-line interference (PLI)** that contaminates the recorded Electrocardiogram (ECG) signal due to electromagnetic coupling from the **50 Hz** (or **60 Hz** in the Americas) AC mains supply. In India and Europe, the dominant interference frequency is **f₀ = 50 Hz**, which often has an amplitude several times larger than the ECG signal itself (which lies in the **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** bandwidth with peak QRS amplitude ~**1 mV**).

The **Cancellation of 50-Hz Interference** is the process of removing this narrowband sinusoidal noise using either:
1. **Fixed (Non-Adaptive) Notch Filtering** — a band-stop filter with a sharp null at 50 Hz.
2. **Adaptive Noise Cancellation (ANC)** — a self-adjusting system that uses a reference noise input and the **Widrow-Hoff Least Mean Squares (LMS) Algorithm** to estimate and subtract the interference in real time.

The **Sampling Frequency** of clinical ECG digitizers is typically **f_s = 360 Hz, 500 Hz, or 1000 Hz**, so the digital notch frequency is:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 50}{f_s}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Subtractive Headphones"**
>
> Imagine you are trying to listen to a quiet flute player (the **ECG signal**) in a room where an air-conditioner hums loudly at a single low pitch (the **50 Hz noise**). 
>
> - **Notch Filter approach**: You build a special pair of headphones that physically blocks *only* the exact frequency of the AC hum. Everything else passes through. It is a "surgical mute button" tuned to one frequency.
> - **Adaptive Filter approach**: You place a *second microphone* near the AC unit (the **reference input**) and an *electronic ear* listens to it. A tiny computer figures out *exactly* how the hum is mixing into the flute's microphone, plays back an **anti-hum** (180° out of phase), and subtracts it — leaving only the clean flute. As the room temperature changes and the AC speed changes, the system *automatically re-tunes* itself. This is **adaptive noise cancellation**.

## 1.3 Why 50 Hz is Particularly Troublesome in ECG

| Source of 50-Hz Pickup | Physical Mechanism | Typical Amplitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Mains wiring near patient cables** | Capacitive (electric-field) coupling | 0.1 – 5 mV |
| **Patient's own body acting as an antenna** | Stray capacitance to ground | Up to 10 mV |
| **Ground-loop currents** | Resistive coupling due to multiple grounds | 1 – 20 mV |
| **Electromagnetic induction** (50 Hz magnetic field from transformers) | Inductive coupling | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **QRS complex** (largest ECG feature) typically peaks at only ~**1 mV**. A 50-Hz interference of even **0.5 mV** can severely distort ST-segment and T-wave morphology, leading to **misdiagnosis of ischemia, MI, or arrhythmia**. Hence, KTU examiners consider 50-Hz cancellation a **high-weightage clinical-grade signal-processing problem**.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Module-4 Context

Within **Module 4 (Filtering for Removal of Artifacts and Interference)**, the topic bridges:
- **Module 3** (Filter design — IIR/FIR structures, frequency transformations)
- **Module 5** (Event detection — R-peak, since clean ECG is a prerequisite)

The two canonical KTU-asked techniques are **(i) IIR Notch Filter** and **(ii) Adaptive Filter using LMS**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Cancellation Strategies — Logic Steps

### Strategy A — Fixed IIR Notch Filter

**Step 1:** Place a pair of complex-conjugate zeros *exactly* on the unit circle at angle $\omega_0$ to create a sharp null at 50 Hz.

**Step 2:** Place a pair of complex-conjugate poles *inside* the unit circle at the *same* angle $\omega_0$ (but at radius $r < 1$) to **sharpen the notch** (high Q) without affecting the flat passband elsewhere.

**Step 3:** Scale the gain so the filter has unity magnitude at DC and at Nyquist.

### Strategy B — Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC) — Widrow & Hoff (1975)

**Step 1:** Acquire **two** signals simultaneously:
- **Primary input** $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ — ECG + 50 Hz noise.
- **Reference input** $x(n)$ — pure 50 Hz noise picked up from mains (e.g., a coil near the power cable, or a separate "noise-only" electrode).

**Step 2:** Filter $x(n)$ through an **adaptive FIR filter** with weights $\mathbf{w}(n) = [w_0, w_1, \dots, w_{N-1}]^T$ to produce an estimate $\hat{n}_0(n)$.

**Step 3:** Subtract the estimate from the primary: the **error** $e(n) = d(n) - \hat{n}_0(n)$ becomes the *clean* ECG estimate.

**Step 4:** Use $e(n)$ and $x(n)$ to **update the weights** via the LMS rule so that mean-squared error $E[e^2(n)]$ is minimized. The filter *learns* the amplitude, phase, and any harmonics of the interference.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbols | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Digital notch frequency: $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s$ | $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $f_s$ = sampling rate | rad/sample |
| 2 | IIR Notch Transfer Function: $H(z) = \dfrac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + r^{2}z^{-2}}$ | $r = 1 - (\pi B / f_s)$, $B$ = 3-dB bandwidth | Dimensionless |
| 3 | 3-dB Bandwidth: $B \approx f_s (1-r) / \pi$ | $r < 1$ typically $0.9 \le r < 1$ | Hz |
| 4 | Quality factor: $Q = f_0 / B$ | Higher $Q$ → narrower notch | Dimensionless |
| 5 | FIR filter output: $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n)\, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | $\mathbf{w}$ = weight vector, $N$ = filter order | — |
| 6 | LMS Error: $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | $d(n)$ = primary input | volts / mV |
| 7 | LMS Weight Update: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | $\mu$ = step-size | $0 < \mu < 1 / (N P_x)$ |
| 8 | Stability bound: $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{N \sigma_x^2}$ | $\sigma_x^2$ = reference-signal variance | — |
| 9 | Excess MSE: $J_{ex} \approx \mu\, N\, \sigma_n^2$ | For small $\mu$ | Watts |
| 10 | Misadjustment: $M \approx \mu\, N$ | Trade-off with convergence speed | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Time constant (convergence): $\tau_{mse} \approx 1 / (4 \mu \lambda_m)$ | $\lambda_m$ = largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ | samples |
| 12 | Notch pole-radius design: $r = 1 - (\pi B / f_s)$ | Direct mapping | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic**: For KTU, the "magical trio" to memorize is **(1) $\omega_0$ formula, (2) $H(z)$ of IIR notch, (3) LMS weight update**. A question worth 7 marks is usually built from these three.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Holter Monitors & ICU Bedsides**: Real-time ANC in commercial ECG chips (e.g., Texas Instruments ADS129x, Analog Devices ADAS1000).
- **Defibrillators & Stress-Test Systems**: 50-Hz cancellation prevents false arrhythmia alarms during treadmill exercise.
- **Telemedicine / Wearables (Apple Watch, AliveCor)**: On-device adaptive notch + LMS hybrid.
- **Magnetocardiography (MCG)** and **fetal ECG from maternal abdominal leads**: The same ANC structure is used to remove maternal 50-Hz pickup and maternal ECG.
- **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database preprocessing** — a standard dataset where 50-Hz cancellation is a must.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight**: The **adaptive** approach is preferred in clinical practice because the actual 50-Hz pickup is *not a pure sinusoid* — it has harmonics (100 Hz, 150 Hz), amplitude drift, and frequency jitter (±0.5 Hz). A fixed notch cannot track these, but an adaptive filter with $N \ge 32$ taps can.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — IIR Notch Filter Transfer Function

**Goal:** Design a stable, causal, real-coefficient digital filter $H(z)$ that has a sharp null at $\omega = \omega_0$ and unity gain everywhere else.

**Step 1:** To produce a zero of magnitude response at exactly $\omega_0$, place two complex-conjugate zeros on the unit circle:

$$z_{z1,2} = e^{\pm j\omega_0} = \cos\omega_0 \pm j\sin\omega_0$$

The numerator polynomial is therefore:

$$N(z) = (1 - e^{j\omega_0}z^{-1})(1 - e^{-j\omega_0}z^{-1})$$

**Step 2:** Expand using $(a-b)(a-b^*) = a^2 - 2\text{Re}(b) a + \vert b \vert^2$:

$$N(z) = 1 - (e^{j\omega_0}+e^{-j\omega_0})z^{-1} + e^{j\omega_0}e^{-j\omega_0}z^{-2} = 1 - 2\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$

**Step 3:** Place poles at the *same* angles but at radius $r < 1$ to sharpen the notch (without making it a true resonator, which would be unstable):

$$z_{p1,2} = r\, e^{\pm j\omega_0}$$

The denominator polynomial becomes:

$$D(z) = 1 - 2r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + r^{2}z^{-2}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the IIR notch transfer function is:

$$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + r^{2}z^{-2}}}$$

**Step 5:** Stability check — all poles must lie **inside** the unit circle:

$$\vert z_{p1,2} \vert = r < 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{System is BIBO stable.}$$

**Step 6:** Verification of unity gain at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi$:

$$H(e^{j0}) = \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0 + 1}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 + r^{2}} = \frac{(1-\cos\omega_0)\cdot 2}{(1-r\cos\omega_0)\cdot 2 - r^{2}+\dots} \rightarrow 1 \text{ as } r \rightarrow 1$$

## 3.2 Derivation — LMS Weight Update Rule (Widrow-Hoff, 1960)

**Step 1:** Define the **instantaneous squared error** as the cost function to be minimized:

$$J(n) = e^{2}(n)$$

**Step 2:** Express the error in terms of weights:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

where $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), \dots, x(n-N+1)]^{T}$ and $\mathbf{w}(n) = [w_0(n), w_1(n), \dots, w_{N-1}(n)]^{T}$.

**Step 3:** Apply the **method of steepest descent** by taking the gradient of $J$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = \frac{\partial\, e^{2}(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = 2 e(n)\, \frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -2 e(n)\, \mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 4:** Update weights in the **negative gradient direction**:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\, \mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 5:** This is the **LMS algorithm** — a stochastic-gradient approximation that avoids computing $E[\cdot]$ (expectation), making it real-time and computationally cheap.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Table for KTU Answers

| Property | Fixed IIR Notch Filter | Adaptive LMS Filter (ANC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of inputs needed** | 1 (ECG only) | 2 (Primary + Reference noise) |
| **Tracks frequency drift of mains** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (if reference is real) |
| **Tracks amplitude/phase change** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (via weight adaptation) |
| **Removes harmonics (100, 150 Hz)** | ❌ Requires extra notches | ✅ Possible with longer $N$ |
| **Computational cost per sample** | Very low (2 mults + 2 adds) | $2N$ mults + $2N$ adds (per $N$ taps) |
| **Convergence behaviour** | Instantaneous | Needs $\sim 1000$–$5000$ samples |
| **Steady-state residual error** | Zero (theoretically) | Excess MSE = $\mu N \sigma_n^2$ |
| **Choice of $\mu$** | Not applicable | $0 < \mu < 1/(N\sigma_x^2)$ |
| **KTU typical marks** | 7 (design) | 7 (derivation + block diagram) |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** An ECG is sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz. Design an IIR notch filter to cancel 50-Hz interference with a 3-dB bandwidth of 2 Hz.

**Step 1:** Compute digital notch frequency:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 50}{200} = \frac{\pi}{2} = 1.5708 \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\cos\omega_0$:

$$\cos(1.5708) = 0 \quad \text{(exact, since } \pi/2 \text{)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute pole radius from the bandwidth relation:

$$r = 1 - \frac{\pi B}{f_s} = 1 - \frac{\pi \cdot 2}{200} = 1 - 0.03142 = 0.9686$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the transfer function. Since $\cos\omega_0 = 0$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + z^{-2}}{1 + r^{2} z^{-2}} = \frac{1 + z^{-2}}{1 + 0.9382\, z^{-2}}$$

**Step 5:** Difference equation (by inspection, since the middle terms vanish):

$$y(n) = x(n) + x(n-2) - 0.9382\, y(n-2)$$

**Step 6:** Quality factor check:

$$Q = \frac{f_0}{B} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \quad \text{(sharp, clinically acceptable)}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC) for 50-Hz Removal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Body - ECG Source s of n] --> C[Primary Sensor Electrode]
    AC1[50 Hz Mains Interference n0 of n] --> C
    C --> P[Primary Input d of n equals s plus n0]
    P --> SUM((Sum junction -))

    AC1 -.-> REF[Reference Noise Sensor Near Mains]
    REF --> X[Reference Input x of n - pure 50 Hz]

    X --> ADAP[Adaptive FIR Filter - N taps]
    W[Weight Vector w of n - LMS updated] --> ADAP

    ADAP --> Y[Noise Estimate y of n]
    Y --> SUM

    SUM --> E[Error e of n equals d minus y equals clean ECG estimate]
    E --> OUT[Clean ECG Output]

    E --> LMS[LMS Update Algorithm - mu step size]
    X --> LMS
    LMS --> W

    style P fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style X fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style ADAP fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f7a1f
    style E fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000
    style OUT fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333333
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — LMS Algorithm Execution Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start - Initialise weights w of 0 equals 0 - Step size mu] --> S1[Sample new reference vector x of n]
    S1 --> S2[Compute filter output y of n equals w transpose x]
    S2 --> S3[Read primary sample d of n]
    S3 --> S4[Compute error e of n equals d of n minus y of n]
    S4 --> S5[Update weights w of n plus 1 equals w of n plus 2 mu e of n x of n]
    S5 --> S6[Output clean ECG sample e of n]
    S6 --> S7{All samples processed?}
    S7 -- No --> S1
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Stop - Steady state reached]

    style S4 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style S5 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style S6 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f7a1f
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — IIR Notch Filter Realization (Direct Form II)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input ECG x of n] --> A1((+))
    A1 --> D1[Delay z to power minus 1]
    D1 --> D2[Delay z to power minus 1]
    D2 --> A2((+))

    A2 --> OUT[Output y of n]

    D1 --> MUL1[Multiply by b1 equals minus 2 cos omega0]
    A1 --> MUL2[Multiply by b0 equals 1]
    A2 --> MUL3[Multiply by a2 equals r squared]
    A2 --> MUL4[Multiply by minus a1 equals 2 r cos omega0]
    A1 --> MUL5[Multiply by minus a2 equals minus r squared]

    MUL1 --> FB1((Sum feedback))
    MUL2 --> FF1((Sum feedforward))
    MUL3 --> FB1
    MUL4 --> FB1
    MUL5 --> FB1
    FF1 --> OUT
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]
**What is 50-Hz interference in ECG? Mention two sources.**

**Model Answer (≈ 60 words):**
50-Hz interference is the sinusoidal noise from the AC mains supply that couples into the ECG recording, with amplitude (0.1 – 10 mV) often comparable to or larger than the ECG itself (~1 mV). It corrupts ST-segment and QRS morphology. **Sources:** (i) capacitive coupling between mains wiring and patient cables, (ii) ground loops between multiple grounded instruments. It can be removed using a **notch filter at 50 Hz** or by **adaptive noise cancellation using the LMS algorithm**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**State the LMS weight-update equation and explain the role of step size μ.**

**Model Answer (≈ 60 words):**
The **LMS (Least Mean Squares) update equation** is:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\, \mathbf{x}(n)$$

The **step size μ** controls the convergence rate and steady-state error. A larger μ gives faster convergence but larger misadjustment and possible instability; a smaller μ gives slower but more accurate convergence. The stability bound is $0 < \mu < 1/(N\sigma_x^2)$, where $N$ is the filter order and $\sigma_x^2$ is the reference input variance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### 📌 QUESTION A — 14 Marks [Adaptive Filter Path]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Model Paper]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the principle of adaptive noise cancellation (ANC) for removing 50-Hz interference from an ECG signal. Discuss why adaptive cancellation is preferred over a fixed notch filter. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Block diagram** — see Section 4.1 above. The two inputs are:
- **Primary $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$** — ECG + 50-Hz noise from the chest electrode.
- **Reference $x(n)$** — 50-Hz noise picked up independently (e.g., from a coil placed near the mains cable, but *uncorrelated* with the ECG).

(ii) **Working principle:** The adaptive FIR filter of order $N$ produces an output $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ that *estimates* the noise $n_0(n)$. The **error** $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ is taken as the clean ECG output (since $s(n)$ and $n_0(n)$ are uncorrelated, the LMS algorithm drives $y(n) \to n_0(n)$ and thus $e(n) \to s(n)$).

(iii) **LMS update** (see derivation in Section 3.2):
$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.

(iv) **Why adaptive is preferred over fixed notch:**
- Mains frequency drifts by ±0.5 Hz; a fixed 50-Hz notch becomes misaligned.
- Amplitude and phase of the interference change with patient movement and electrode impedance.
- An LMS filter continuously tracks these changes by adjusting $\mathbf{w}(n)$.
- With sufficient taps ($N \ge 32$), it can also cancel 50-Hz *harmonics* (100, 150 Hz) simultaneously.

---

**(b) Derive the LMS weight-update rule from the principle of steepest descent. Mention the stability condition on μ. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Cost function** (instantaneous squared error):
$J(n) = e^2(n) = [d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]^2$.

(ii) **Gradient** with respect to the weight vector:
$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = \dfrac{\partial e^2(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = 2 e(n) \dfrac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -2 e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$.

(iii) **Steepest-descent update** (move opposite to gradient):
$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$.

(iv) **Stability condition:** For mean-square convergence of the LMS, the step size must satisfy:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{N \sigma_x^2}$$

where $N$ is the filter order and $\sigma_x^2$ is the variance (power) of the reference input. If $\mu$ is too large, the algorithm diverges; if too small, convergence becomes impractically slow.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B — 14 Marks [Fixed Notch Filter Path]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Model Paper]**

**(a) Design a second-order IIR notch filter to remove 50-Hz interference from an ECG sampled at f_s = 250 Hz. Choose a 3-dB bandwidth of 5 Hz. Show the transfer function, pole-zero plot, and difference equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Digital notch frequency:**
$\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s = 2\pi(50)/250 = 0.4\pi = 1.2566$ rad/sample.
$\cos\omega_0 = \cos(0.4\pi) = 0.3090$.

(ii) **Pole radius from bandwidth relation:**
$r = 1 - \pi B/f_s = 1 - \pi(5)/250 = 1 - 0.0628 = 0.9372$.

(iii) **Transfer function:**

$$H(z) = \frac{1 - 2(0.3090) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2(0.9372)(0.3090) z^{-1} + (0.9372)^{2} z^{-2}} = \frac{1 - 0.6180\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5790\, z^{-1} + 0.8783\, z^{-2}}$$

(iv) **Pole-zero plot (conceptual):** Zeros at $e^{\pm j 0.4\pi}$ on the unit circle. Poles at $0.9372 e^{\pm j 0.4\pi}$ inside the unit circle, very close to the zeros — giving a narrow, sharp notch.

(v) **Difference equation** (Direct Form I):

$$y(n) = 0.5790\, y(n-1) - 0.8783\, y(n-2) + x(n) - 0.6180\, x(n-1) + x(n-2)$$

(vi) **Quality factor:** $Q = f_0/B = 50/5 = 10$ (clinically adequate).

---

**(b) Compare the IIR notch filter and the adaptive LMS filter for 50-Hz cancellation. In which clinical scenario would you choose each? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | IIR Notch | LMS Adaptive |
|---|---|---|
| **Inputs required** | 1 | 2 (primary + reference) |
| **Frequency tracking** | Fixed 50 Hz | Tracks drift and harmonics |
| **Computational load** | Very low | $O(N)$ per sample |
| **Convergence** | Instantaneous | Needs $\sim 1000$–$5000$ samples |
| **Residual noise** | Very small at 50 Hz | Excess MSE = $\mu N \sigma_n^2$ |
| **Phase distortion** | Possible (nonlinear phase) | Minimal (FIR structure) |
| **Robust to harmonics** | No | Yes (with sufficient $N$) |
| **Robust to patient movement** | Poor | Good (reference tracks too) |

**Clinical scenarios:**
- **Choose IIR notch** when the ECG is recorded in a *controlled laboratory or operating room* with a stable mains, single-input data-logger, and where phase distortion is acceptable (e.g., simple heart-rate monitors).
- **Choose LMS adaptive** when the ECG is recorded in *ambulatory, ICU, or stress-test environments* where mains frequency drifts, electrode impedance changes, and patient movement cause amplitude/phase variations. Also preferred for *fetal ECG from abdominal leads* where maternal ECG and 50-Hz noise are mixed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (where KTU examiners deduct marks):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing notch frequency $\omega_0$ with the analog frequency $f_0$** — Forgetting to multiply by $2\pi/f_s$. A 7-mark question is reduced to 3 marks if $\omega_0$ is wrong.
> 
> 2. **In LMS, omitting the factor of 2** — Writing $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$ instead of $+2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$. The 2 comes from differentiating $e^2(n)$.
> 
> 3. **Pole placement outside the unit circle** — If $r > 1$, the IIR notch is **unstable** and the examiner will mark zero for the design part.
> 
> 4. **No explanation of "Why adaptive over fixed"** — Simply drawing the block diagram is not enough; KTU requires the *reason* (mains drift, harmonics, patient movement).
> 
> 5. **Confusing "primary" and "reference" inputs** — Primary is $d(n)$ (ECG + noise); reference is $x(n)$ (noise alone). Reversing them invalidates the entire ANC structure.
> 
> 6. **Using only the FIR output $y(n)$ as the "clean ECG"** — Wrong. The **error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$** is the clean ECG, not $y(n)$.
> 
> 7. **Choosing μ too large** — A common mistake is to write $\mu = 0.5$ without checking $1/(N\sigma_x^2)$. Always verify the bound.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets — Read in 60 Seconds**
> 
> ✅ **50-Hz interference** is the **power-line mains pickup** that overwhelms the ECG (typical noise 0.1 – 10 mV vs. ECG ~1 mV). 
> 
> ✅ **Two solutions**: (1) **IIR Notch Filter** (fixed, single input), (2) **Adaptive LMS Filter** (ANC, two inputs). 
> 
> ✅ **Notch frequency**: $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0/f_s$. **Pole radius**: $r = 1 - \pi B/f_s$ where $B$ = 3-dB bandwidth. 
> 
> ✅ **Notch transfer function**: $H(z) = (1 - 2\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + z^{-2}) / (1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2})$. 
> 
> ✅ **Stability requires** $r < 1$. **Q-factor** $Q = f_0/B$. 
> 
> ✅ **LMS update**: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$. 
> 
> ✅ **Stability bound**: $0 < \mu < 1/(N\sigma_x^2)$. **Misadjustment** $M \approx \mu N$. 
> 
> ✅ **Primary input** $d(n) = $ ECG + noise. **Reference input** $x(n) = $ noise alone (uncorrelated with ECG). 
> 
> ✅ **Filter output** $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$. **Error (clean ECG)** $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$. 
> 
> ✅ Adaptive filter is preferred for **fetal ECG, ICU, ambulatory Holter**; notch for **fixed laboratory / single-channel monitors**. 
> 
> ✅ **KTU mnemonic "MAINS"**: **M**ains, **A**daptive, **I**IR, **N**otch, **S**teepest-descent. 
> 
> ✅ **Forgetting the factor 2 in LMS = automatic 1-mark cut**. **Wrong sign of gradient = full zero on derivation**. 
> 
> ✅ For 50 Hz at $f_s = 200$ Hz, $\omega_0 = \pi/2$ and $\cos\omega_0 = 0$ — a special case often asked in KTU.
